topics.title: Definition of Signal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBEVT604 - DIGITAL SIGNALS AND PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Definition of Signal # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Signal** is formally defined as any physical quantity that **varies with respect to one or more independent variables** (most commonly time, but also space, frequency, temperature, pressure, etc.) and conveys **information** about the behavior or state of a physical system. In the mathematical notation adopted by KTU and the broader signal processing community, a signal is represented as a **function** of one or more independent variables. The most common form is a function of time $t$: $$x(t) \quad \text{or} \quad x[n]$$ where: - $x(t)$ denotes a **continuous-time signal** (independent variable $t$ is continuous and real-valued) - $x[n]$ denotes a **discrete-time signal** (independent variable $n$ is integer-valued: $n \in \mathbb{Z}$) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Must Memorize Verbatim):** > *"A signal is a function of one or more independent variables which conveys information about a physical phenomenon."* > Examples: voltage across a resistor $v(t)$, speech waveform $s(t)$, stock market price $p(t)$, image intensity $I(x,y)$. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition Imagine you are standing on a **seashore** and watching waves arrive at the shore. The **height of the water** at any instant $t$ — let's call it $h(t)$ — keeps changing. If you were to record the water height every second onto a graph, you would obtain a **signal**. In this analogy: - The **independent variable** is time $t$ (when you measured it). - The **dependent variable** is the water height $h$ (what you observed). - The **information conveyed** is the wave pattern, tide level, and ocean behavior. Another common real-world analogy: your **body temperature** measured every hour over a day. The temperature value at each hour is a sample, and the collection of these samples forms a **discrete-time signal** $T[n]$ that carries information about your health. > [!NOTE] > **Key Takeaway:** If a quantity **carries information** and **changes** with an independent variable, it qualifies as a signal. Static constants (e.g., a fixed value of 5 V DC) are usually called *constant signals* or *DC signals*, but they are still mathematically valid signals. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Signal Processing | Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Sampling Frequency (Audio CD) | $f_s$ | **44.1 kHz** | | Speech Bandwidth | $BW$ | **300 Hz – 3.4 kHz** | | Human Audible Range | $f$ | **20 Hz – 20 kHz** | | Nyquist Rate Factor | — | **$2 \times$** maximum frequency | | Typical Image Resolution | $M \times N$ | **$1024 \times 1024$** pixels | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Do We Need to Define a Signal? The definition of a signal is the **foundational building block** of Digital Signal Processing (DSP). Without a formal definition: - We cannot distinguish between *signals* and *noise* (random unwanted variations). - We cannot apply mathematical tools like **Fourier Transform**, **Z-Transform**, or **Convolution**. - We cannot design systems (filters, compressors, modulators) that operate on them. ## 2.2 Mathematical Representation — The General Form A generic $k$-dimensional signal is represented as a function: $$x(t_1, t_2, t_3, \ldots, t_k)$$ The dimension $k$ indicates the number of independent variables. This leads to the hierarchical classification that follows. ## 2.3 Classification of Signals (KTU High-Yield) ### A. Based on the Number of Independent Variables 1. **One-Dimensional (1-D) Signal:** $x(t)$ or $x[n]$ — e.g., speech, ECG, voltage waveform. 2. **Two-Dimensional (2-D) Signal:** $x(t_1, t_2)$ — e.g., images (intensity as a function of $x$ and $y$). 3. **Multi-Dimensional (n-D) Signal:** $x(t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n)$ — e.g., video (3-D: $x, y, t$). ### B. Based on Continuity in Time and Amplitude 1. **Continuous-Time, Continuous-Amplitude Signal:** Analog signal — $x(t)$, both $t$ and $x$ are continuous. 2. **Continuous-Time, Discrete-Amplitude Signal:** Quantized signal — $t$ continuous, amplitude takes finite values. 3. **Discrete-Time, Continuous-Amplitude Signal:** Sampled signal — $t$ discrete, amplitude continuous (e.g., $x[n] = \sin(0.1 \pi n)$). 4. **Discrete-Time, Discrete-Amplitude Signal:** Digital signal — both $t$ and amplitude discrete (output of an ADC). ### C. Based on Determinism 1. **Deterministic Signal:** Can be described by an explicit mathematical formula (e.g., $x(t) = \sin(2\pi t)$). 2. **Random Signal:** Cannot be described exactly; characterized by probability and statistics (e.g., thermal noise, speech). ### D. Based on Periodicity 1. **Periodic Signal:** $x(t + T) = x(t)$ for all $t$, with fundamental period $T > 0$. 2. **Aperiodic Signal:** Does not repeat. ### E. Based on Energy and Power 1. **Energy Signal:** Total energy $E$ is **finite and non-zero**, average power $P = 0$. 2. **Power Signal:** Average power $P$ is **finite and non-zero**, total energy $E = \infty$. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Use `\vert` instead of the pipe symbol `|` in all table entries to preserve markdown table integrity. | # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Condition / Unit | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | General 1-D Signal | $x(t)$ or $x[n]$ | $t \in \mathbb{R}$, $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ | | 2 | Multi-Dim. Signal | $x(t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n)$ | $n$ independent variables | | 3 | Total Energy of $x(t)$ | $E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^{2}\, dt$ | Joules (J); finite $\Rightarrow$ energy signal | | 4 | Total Energy of $x[n]$ | $E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^{2}$ | Finite $\Rightarrow$ energy signal | | 5 | Average Power of $x(t)$ | $P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \dfrac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^{2}\, dt$ | Watts (W); finite $> 0 \Rightarrow$ power signal | | 6 | Average Power of $x[n]$ | $P = \lim_{N \to \infty} \dfrac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x[n] \vert^{2}$ | Finite $\Rightarrow$ power signal | | 7 | Periodicity (CT) | $x(t + T) = x(t)$ | Smallest $T > 0$ is fundamental period | | 8 | Periodicity (DT) | $x[n + N] = x[n]$ | Smallest integer $N > 0$ is fundamental period | | 9 | Sampling Relation | $x[n] = x(nT_s)$ | $T_s$ = sampling period (seconds/sample) | | 10 | Sampling Frequency | $f_s = \dfrac{1}{T_s}$ | Hertz (Hz) | | 11 | Energy-Power Relation | $P = 0$ if $E < \infty$ | $E = \infty$ if $0 < P < \infty$ | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Biomedical Engineering:** ECG, EEG, EMG signals are analyzed to detect arrhythmias, seizures, and muscle disorders. - **Telecommunications:** Speech, image, and video signals are encoded, transmitted, and decoded. - **Audio & Music Industry:** MP3, AAC, and FLAC formats rely on digital signal processing. - **Radar & Sonar:** Signals reflected from targets are processed to determine range, velocity, and angle. - **Control Systems:** Feedback signals from sensors are processed to maintain desired system behavior. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > Since "Definition of Signal" is a **foundational conceptual topic**, derivations here focus on the **mathematical formulation of standard signals**, **energy-power classification derivations**, and **analytical reasoning** rather than heavy algebraic manipulation. ## 3.1 Derivation: Mathematical Form of a Continuous-Time Sinusoidal Signal A pure tone (single-frequency sound or pure AC waveform) is described by: $$x(t) = A \cos(2\pi f_0 t + \phi)$$ **Step-by-step reasoning:** - $A$ is the **amplitude** (peak deviation from zero, in volts or pascals). - $f_0$ is the **fundamental frequency** in Hz. - $2\pi f_0$ is the **angular frequency** $\omega_0$ in rad/s, written as $x(t) = A \cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$. - $\phi$ is the **initial phase** in radians. The signal is **periodic** because: $$x(t + T_0) = A \cos(\omega_0 (t + T_0) + \phi) = A \cos(\omega_0 t + \phi + \omega_0 T_0)$$ For this to equal $x(t) = A \cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$ for all $t$, we need: $$\omega_0 T_0 = 2\pi k \quad \text{with} \quad k \in \mathbb{Z}$$ Taking $k = 1$ (smallest positive integer) gives the **fundamental period**: $$T_0 = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_0} = \frac{1}{f_0}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation: Energy of a Rectangular Pulse Consider a rectangular pulse of amplitude $A$ and duration from $t = -T_1$ to $t = +T_1$: $$x(t) = \begin{cases} A, & \vert t \vert \leq T_1 \\ 0, & \vert t \vert > T_1 \end{cases}$$ **Step 1:** Apply the continuous-time energy formula: $$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^{2}\, dt$$ **Step 2:** Substitute the piecewise definition. Since $x(t) = 0$ outside $[-T_1, T_1]$: $$E = \int_{-T_1}^{T_1} \vert A \vert^{2}\, dt$$ **Step 3:** Evaluate the integral (assuming $A$ is real): $$E = A^{2} \int_{-T_1}^{T_1} dt = A^{2} \bigl[t\bigr]_{-T_1}^{T_1}$$ $$E = A^{2} (T_1 - (-T_1)) = 2 A^{2} T_1$$ **Conclusion:** $E = 2 A^{2} T_1 < \infty$, so the rectangular pulse is an **energy signal**. Its average power is $P = 0$ as $T \to \infty$ because the energy is spread over an infinite observation window. ## 3.3 Derivation: Average Power of a Sinusoidal Signal Let $x(t) = A \cos(\omega_0 t)$. Its instantaneous power is $\vert x(t) \vert^{2} = A^{2} \cos^{2}(\omega_0 t)$. **Step 1:** Apply the average power formula over one period $T_0 = 2\pi / \omega_0$: $$P = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{0}^{T_0} A^{2} \cos^{2}(\omega_0 t)\, dt$$ **Step 2:** Use the trigonometric identity $\cos^{2}(\theta) = \frac{1 + \cos(2\theta)}{2}$: $$P = \frac{A^{2}}{T_0} \int_{0}^{T_0} \frac{1 + \cos(2\omega_0 t)}{2}\, dt$$ **Step 3:** Evaluate the integral. The cosine term integrates to zero over a full period: $$\int_{0}^{T_0} 1\, dt = T_0, \qquad \int_{0}^{T_0} \cos(2\omega_0 t)\, dt = 0$$ **Step 4:** Substitute back: $$P = \frac{A^{2}}{T_0} \cdot \frac{T_0}{2} = \frac{A^{2}}{2}$$ **Conclusion:** A sinusoid has **finite, non-zero average power** $P = A^{2}/2$, hence it is a **power signal** with infinite total energy. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Signal Classification Decision Table | Given Signal $x(t)$ or $x[n]$ | Check $E$ (Energy) | Check $P$ (Power) | Class | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Rectangular pulse | $0 < E < \infty$ | $P = 0$ | **Energy Signal** | | Sinusoid $A\cos(\omega t)$ | $E = \infty$ | $P = A^{2}/2$ | **Power Signal** | | Unit step $u(t)$ | $E = \infty$ | $P = \infty$ | **Neither** (grows unbounded) | | Decaying exponential $e^{-at} u(t), a>0$ | $0 < E < \infty$ | $P = 0$ | **Energy Signal** | | Constant DC $x(t) = C$ | $E = \infty$ | $P = C^{2}$ | **Power Signal** | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Hierarchical Classification of Signals ```mermaid graph TD rootA[Signal x] rootB[By Independent Variables] rootC[By Time and Amplitude] rootD[By Determinism] rootE[By Periodicity] rootF[By Energy Power] sig1D[1D Signal x of t] sig2D[2D Signal x of t1 t2] sigND[nD Signal x of t1..tn] analogSig[Analog CT and CA] sampledSig[Sampled DT and CA] quantizedSig[Quantized CT and DA] digitalSig[Digital DT and DA] determSig[Deterministic] randomSig[Random Stochastic] periodicSig[Periodic x t plus T equals x t] aperiodicSig[Aperiodic] energySig[Energy Signal E finite P zero] powerSig[Power Signal P finite E infinite] rootA --> rootB rootA --> rootC rootA --> rootD rootA --> rootE rootA --> rootF rootB --> sig1D rootB --> sig2D rootB --> sigND rootC --> analogSig rootC --> sampledSig rootC --> quantizedSig rootC --> digitalSig rootD --> determSig rootD --> randomSig rootE --> periodicSig rootE --> aperiodicSig rootF --> energySig rootF --> powerSig ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: From Physical Phenomenon to Digital Signal ```mermaid graph LR physPhen[Physical Phenomenon
Temperature Pressure Sound] transducer[Transducer
Sensor Microphone] ctsSignal[Continuous Time Signal x of t
Analog Waveform] sampler[Sampler
ADC Stage 1] quantizer[Quantizer
ADC Stage 2] encoder[Encoder
Binary Coder] dtsSignal[Digital Signal x of n
Discrete Sequence] dspCore[DSP Processor
Filter Transform Compression] output[Processed Output
Display Storage Transmission] physPhen --> transducer transducer --> ctsSignal ctsSignal --> sampler sampler --> quantizer quantizer --> encoder encoder --> dtsSignal dtsSignal --> dspCore dspCore --> output ``` ## 4.3 Schematic Explanation of the Block Diagram The architecture above illustrates the **signal processing chain** that justifies why we need to study "Definition of Signal": 1. **Physical Phenomenon** — The real-world event (e.g., a heart beating, a person speaking). 2. **Transducer** — Converts the physical quantity into a voltage or current (microphone, thermocouple, photodiode). 3. **Continuous-Time Signal $x(t)$** — The raw electrical analog waveform. 4. **Sampler** — Captures the amplitude at regular time intervals $T_s$, producing a discrete-time sequence. 5. **Quantizer** — Rounds each sample to the nearest allowed level from a finite set. 6. **Encoder** — Converts quantized levels into binary codes (e.g., 8-bit, 16-bit). 7. **Discrete Digital Signal $x[n]$** — A sequence of numbers suitable for digital computation. 8. **DSP Core** — Applies algorithms (FIR/IIR filter, FFT, compression). 9. **Output** — Final processed result (displayed, stored, or transmitted). # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` > **Define a signal. Give two real-world examples of one-dimensional and two-dimensional signals.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A signal is a **function of one or more independent variables** that conveys information about a physical phenomenon. - **1-D signal example:** Speech waveform $x(t)$ — voltage from a microphone as a function of time $t$. - **2-D signal example:** A photograph $I(x,y)$ — pixel intensity as a function of horizontal position $x$ and vertical position $y$. (Mentioning the formal definition: 1 mark; 1-D example: 1 mark; 2-D example: 1 mark.) --- > **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` > **Distinguish between a deterministic signal and a random signal with one example each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Deterministic Signal | Random Signal | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Mathematical description | Exactly known via a formula | Cannot be described by an explicit formula | | Predictability | Fully predictable in advance | Not predictable; statistical in nature | | Example | $x(t) = 5 \cos(100\pi t)$ | Thermal noise across a resistor | (Deterministic definition + example: 1.5 marks; Random definition + example: 1.5 marks.) --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` > > **(a)** Classify signals based on (i) continuity in time and amplitude, and (ii) periodicity. Draw suitable waveforms for each. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Define **energy signal** and **power signal**. For the rectangular pulse $x(t) = A$ for $0 \leq t \leq T_1$ and $0$ otherwise, compute the total energy and average power, and hence classify the signal. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution for Question A **Part (a) — 7 marks:** (i) **Classification based on continuity in time and amplitude:** 1. **Continuous-time, continuous-amplitude (Analog):** Both $t$ and $x$ are continuous. Example: pure sine wave from a function generator. 2. **Continuous-time, discrete-amplitude (Quantized):** Time is continuous but amplitude takes finite values. Example: stair-step output of a sample-and-hold circuit with finite resolution. 3. **Discrete-time, continuous-amplitude (Sampled):** Time is discrete (samples at $nT_s$) but amplitude is continuous. Example: $x[n] = \sin(0.1\pi n)$. 4. **Discrete-time, discrete-amplitude (Digital):** Both $t$ and amplitude are discrete. Example: output of an 8-bit ADC. (4 sub-classes × 1.25 marks = 5 marks, plus waveforms: 2 marks.) (ii) **Classification based on periodicity:** - **Periodic signal:** $x(t + T) = x(t)$ for all $t$, smallest $T$ is the fundamental period. Example: sinusoid. - **Aperiodic signal:** No finite $T$ satisfies periodicity. Example: exponential decay $e^{-at}u(t), a > 0$. **Part (b) — 7 marks:** For $x(t) = A$ on $0 \leq t \leq T_1$ and $0$ elsewhere: **Total Energy:** $$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^{2}\, dt = \int_{0}^{T_1} A^{2}\, dt = A^{2} T_1$$ Since $0 < A^{2} T_1 < \infty$, $E$ is **finite**. (3 marks) **Average Power:** $$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^{2}\, dt = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \cdot A^{2} T_1 = 0$$ Hence $P = 0$. (2 marks) **Classification:** Finite energy and zero power $\Rightarrow$ **Energy Signal**. (2 marks) --- > ### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` > > **(a)** Explain the following classifications of signals with one example each: **(i)** 1-D vs 2-D signals, **(ii)** Even vs Odd signals, **(iii)** Causal vs Non-causal signals. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** For the continuous-time signal $x(t) = A \cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$, determine the fundamental period $T_0$ and compute the average power. Hence classify it as an energy or power signal. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution for Question B **Part (a) — 7 marks:** **(i) 1-D vs 2-D signals (2 marks):** - **1-D signal:** Function of one independent variable. Example: $x(t)$ — speech signal. - **2-D signal:** Function of two independent variables. Example: $I(x,y)$ — black-and-white image. **(ii) Even vs Odd signals (2.5 marks):** - **Even signal:** $x(-t) = x(t)$ for all $t$. Example: $x(t) = \cos(t)$. (Symmetric about the vertical axis.) - **Odd signal:** $x(-t) = -x(t)$ for all $t$. Example: $x(t) = \sin(t)$. (Anti-symmetric about the origin.) **(iii) Causal vs Non-causal signals (2.5 marks):** - **Causal signal:** $x(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$. Example: unit step $u(t)$. - **Non-causal signal:** Has non-zero values for $t < 0$. Example: $x(t) = e^{-t}$ for all $t$ (since $e^{t} \neq 0$ for $t<0$). **Part (b) — 7 marks:** Given $x(t) = A \cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$. **Fundamental Period (3 marks):** By definition, $x(t + T_0) = x(t)$ requires $\omega_0 T_0 = 2\pi$, hence: $$T_0 = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_0} \quad \text{seconds}$$ **Average Power (3 marks):** Using the formula: $$P = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{0}^{T_0} A^{2} \cos^{2}(\omega_0 t + \phi)\, dt = \frac{A^{2}}{2}$$ **Classification (1 mark):** Since $0 < P < \infty$ and $E = \infty$, the cosine is a **Power Signal**. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Confusing "signal" with "system":** A signal is the input/output quantity that carries information; a system is the processor that operates on it. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this confusion. > 2. **Skipping the units in energy/power calculations:** Always state **Joules (J)** for energy and **Watts (W)** for power. Marks are reserved for units in KTU 2024 scheme. > 3. **Writing $|x(t)|^2$ as $|x(t)|^2$ using a pipe `|`: This breaks the markdown table in answer scripts (or in computer-graded components). Use $\vert x(t) \vert^{2}$ in handwritten copies.** > 4. **Forgetting the condition $0 < E < \infty$ for energy signals:** A signal with $E = 0$ (e.g., zero signal) is trivial and often not counted. Mention the strict inequality. > 5. **Not drawing waveforms in periodic/aperiodic questions:** A 1-mark reserved slice goes to the diagram. Even a hand-drawn sketch suffices. > 6. **Mixing up discrete-time period $N$ and continuous-time period $T$:** $N$ must be an integer in discrete-time; $T$ can be any positive real in continuous-time. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - A **signal** is a function of one or more independent variables that conveys information. - Signals are classified along **five axes**: independent variables, time-amplitude continuity, determinism, periodicity, and energy-power. - **Continuous-time** signals use $t \in \mathbb{R}$; **discrete-time** signals use $n \in \mathbb{Z}$. - **Energy signal:** $0 < E < \infty$, $P = 0$. - **Power signal:** $0 < P < \infty$, $E = \infty$. - **Analog** = continuous-time AND continuous-amplitude. - **Digital** = discrete-time AND discrete-amplitude. - **Periodic CT:** $x(t + T) = x(t)$, smallest $T > 0$ is $T_0 = 2\pi / \omega_0$. - **Periodic DT:** $x[n + N] = x[n]$, smallest integer $N > 0$. - **Even signal:** $x(-t) = x(t)$. **Odd signal:** $x(-t) = -x(t)$. - **Causal signal:** $x(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$. - **Deterministic** signals are formula-describable; **random** signals need probability/statistics. - **Nyquist Sampling:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ to avoid aliasing. - Always include **units** (J, W, Hz, rad/s) and **explicit conditions** in derivations. - For 1-D signals use $x(t)$ or $x[n]$; for images use $I(x,y)$; for video use $I(x,y,t)$. - Sinusoid average power: $P = A^{2}/2$. Rectangular pulse energy: $E = 2A^{2}T_1$ (symmetric) or $E = A^{2}T_1$ (one-sided). - The **signal processing chain** is: Physical Phenomenon → Transducer → Analog Signal → Sampler → Quantizer → Encoder → Digital Signal → DSP → Output.

topics.title: Basic Continuous-Time Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT604 - DIGITAL SIGNALS AND PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Continuous-Time Signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

A **Continuous-Time Signal** is a mathematical function $x(t)$ defined for a continuum of values of the independent variable $t \in \mathbb{R}$, where $t$ typically denotes time measured in seconds. The amplitude of the signal can take any real (or complex) value within a continuous range, distinguishing it from discrete-time signals $x[n]$ which are defined only at integer instants.

Mathematically, a continuous-time signal is a mapping:
$$x : \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R} \quad \text{or} \quad \mathbb{C}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A continuous-time signal $x(t)$ is a function of a continuous variable $t$. The signal is said to be continuous-time if it is defined for all instants of time. The signal may be continuous in amplitude (analog) or may be restricted to a finite set of values.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **thermometer recording room temperature** every fraction of a second. The needle moves smoothly along the scale, taking an infinite number of possible positions. This smooth, infinitely-detailed physical quantity is a **continuous-time signal**.

In contrast, a **digital thermometer** that displays the temperature only at the click of each second (e.g., 24.1°C, 24.2°C, 24.4°C...) is a **discrete-time signal** — it exists only at distinct instants.

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it this way:**
> - **Continuous-time** = a flowing river of values (analogy: analog audio waveform on an oscilloscope)
> - **Discrete-time** = snapshots of the river taken at regular intervals (analogy: digital MP3 samples)

### 1.3 Classification of Continuous-Time Signals

Signals can be classified along multiple axes:

| Classification Axis | Types | KTU Exam Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **By continuity in time** | Continuous-time vs. Discrete-time | Module 1 foundation |
| **By continuity in amplitude** | Analog (continuous amplitude) vs. Quantized | PBEVT604 Module 2 |
| **By periodicity** | Periodic vs. Aperiodic (Non-periodic) | Direct 3-mark question |
| **By symmetry** | Even vs. Odd vs. Neither | Direct 14-mark question |
| **By energy/power** | Energy signals vs. Power signals | Frequently asked |
| **By determinism** | Deterministic vs. Random | Brief mention |
| **By dimensionality** | One-dimensional vs. Multi-dimensional | 1D vs. 2D signals |

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU B.Tech

Continuous-time signal theory forms the **mathematical bedrock** upon which all of digital signal processing is built. Every discrete-time operation in DSP is a sampled, quantized, or transformed version of a continuous-time phenomenon. Mastering this topic ensures students can:
- Analyze LTI systems analytically
- Apply convolution and Fourier analysis
- Understand sampling theory (Nyquist-Shannon)
- Design digital filters in later modules

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** A 3-mark question on "Define continuous-time signal with an example" appears in almost every KTU ESE paper. Memorize the formal definition AND give one engineering example (ECG, audio, speech, voltage across a resistor).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Elementary Continuous-Time Signals (Standard Building Blocks)

These are the **fundamental signals** that KTU expects every student to recognize, sketch, and write mathematically. They serve as the "alphabet" of signal processing.

#### 2.1.1 Unit Step Signal $u(t)$

The unit step function is defined as:
$$u(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & t \geq 0 \\ 0, & t < 0 \end{cases}$$

**Why does it matter?** The step function is the most common test input for LTI systems (Step Response). It models real-world events like: a switch being flipped ON, a DC voltage being applied at $t=0$, or a system being energized.

**Analogy:** Imagine a tap (faucet) that is closed for all time $t < 0$ and is suddenly opened at $t = 0$, allowing water to flow at a constant rate forever after.

**Key Properties:**
- $\int_{-\infty}^{t} u(\tau)\, d\tau = t \cdot u(t)$ (Integration of step gives ramp)
- $u(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$ and $u(t) = 1$ for $t > 0$
- $u(0)$ is conventionally taken as $1$ (or sometimes $\frac{1}{2}$ — KTU convention uses $u(0)=1$)

#### 2.1.2 Unit Ramp Signal $r(t)$

$$r(t) = \begin{cases} t, & t \geq 0 \\ 0, & t < 0 \end{cases}$$

**Analogy:** The ramp signal models linearly increasing phenomena — like a car's velocity when it accelerates at a constant rate, or a motor's angular position under constant torque.

**Key Properties:**
- $r(t) = t \cdot u(t)$
- $\frac{d}{dt} r(t) = u(t)$ (Derivative of ramp gives step)
- $\int u(t)\, dt = r(t) + C$

#### 2.1.3 Unit Impulse Signal (Dirac Delta) $\delta(t)$

The unit impulse is **not an ordinary function** in the classical sense — it is a **distribution** (generalized function).

$$delta(t) = \begin{cases} \infty, & t = 0 \\ 0, & t \neq 0 \end{cases} \quad \text{with} \quad \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t)\, dt = 1$$

**Why it matters:** The impulse is the *mathematical limit* of a rectangular pulse whose width shrinks to zero while area remains unity. It is the **identity element for convolution** and is the impulse response that completely characterizes an LTI system.

**Sampling (Sifting) Property:**
$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, \delta(t - t_0)\, dt = x(t_0)$$

**Analogy:** Think of a hammer striking a nail — an infinitely strong force applied for an infinitely short duration, delivering a finite "impulse" of momentum. The system "feels" the hammer only at the instant of impact.

#### 2.1.4 Unit Parabolic Signal $p(t)$

$$p(t) = \begin{cases} \frac{t^2}{2}, & t \geq 0 \\ 0, & t < 0 \end{cases}$$

This is the "twice-integrated" version of the step. KTU tests less frequently, but it completes the family of standard signals.

**Relationship Chain:**
$$p(t) \xrightarrow{\frac{d}{dt}} r(t) \xrightarrow{\frac{d}{dt}} u(t) \xrightarrow{\frac{d}{dt}} \delta(t)$$

#### 2.1.5 Signum Function $\text{sgn}(t)$

$$\text{sgn}(t) = \begin{cases} +1, & t > 0 \\ 0, & t = 0 \\ -1, & t < 0 \end{cases}$$

**Key Property:** $\text{sgn}(t) = 2u(t) - 1$

This signal is useful for representing signals that are antisymmetric about the origin.

#### 2.1.6 Rectangular Pulse $\text{rect}\left(\frac{t}{\tau}\right)$

A unit-amplitude pulse of width $\tau$ centered at $t=0$:
$$\text{rect}\left(\frac{t}{\tau}\right) = \begin{cases} 1, & \vert t \vert < \tau/2 \\ 0, & \vert t \vert > \tau/2 \end{cases}$$

This is the basis for defining **causal rectangular pulses** in KTU problems:
$$x(t) = u(t + \tau/2) - u(t - \tau/2)$$

#### 2.1.7 Triangular Pulse $\text{tri}(t)$

A triangular pulse peaking at unity with base width $2T$:
$$\text{tri}\left(\frac{t}{T}\right) = \begin{cases} 1 - \frac{\vert t \vert}{T}, & \vert t \vert \leq T \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

#### 2.1.8 Sinusoidal Signal $x(t) = A\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$

$$x(t) = A\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi) = A\cos(2\pi f_0 t + \phi)$$

**Analogy:** AC mains supply (50 Hz in India), pure musical tones, RF carrier waves.

**Key parameters:**
- $A$ = amplitude
- $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0$ = angular frequency (rad/s)
- $f_0$ = frequency in Hz
- $T_0 = 1/f_0$ = period in seconds
- $\phi$ = phase in radians

**Periodicity:** $A\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi) = A\cos(\omega_0(t + T_0) + \phi)$ for $T_0 = 2\pi/\omega_0$.

#### 2.1.9 Exponential Signal $x(t) = A e^{\alpha t}$

$$x(t) = A e^{\alpha t}$$

- If $\alpha > 0$ → **growing exponential** (unstable system response)
- If $\alpha < 0$ → **decaying exponential** (stable system response, RC discharge curve)
- If $\alpha = 0$ → reduces to DC signal of amplitude $A$

**Real-world examples:** Radioactive decay ($\alpha < 0$), capacitor discharge, RLC transient response, biological growth curves.

#### 2.1.10 Complex Exponential $x(t) = A e^{st}$ where $s = \sigma + j\omega$

$$x(t) = A e^{(\sigma + j\omega)t} = A e^{\sigma t} \left[ \cos(\omega t) + j\sin(\omega t) \right]$$

**Why it matters:** Complex exponentials are the **eigenfunctions of LTI systems**. Every continuous-time signal can be represented as a linear combination (Fourier/Laplace transform) of complex exponentials.

### 2.2 Operations on Continuous-Time Signals

KTU tests these operations extensively. Master each one with sketches.

#### 2.2.1 Time Shifting
$$y(t) = x(t - t_0)$$

- If $t_0 > 0$: signal is **delayed** (shifted to the right)
- If $t_0 < 0$: signal is **advanced** (shifted to the left)

**Example:** $x(t) = u(t)$ shifted by $t_0 = 2$ gives $u(t-2)$, which is a step starting at $t=2$.

#### 2.2.2 Time Scaling
$$y(t) = x(at)$$

- If $a > 1$: signal is **compressed** in time
- If $0 < a < 1$: signal is **expanded** (stretched) in time
- If $a < 0$: combines scaling with reflection

**Example:** $x(t) = u(t)$ scaled by $a=2$ gives $u(2t)$, a step that turns ON at $t=0.5$ (faster turn-on).

#### 2.2.3 Time Reflection
$$y(t) = x(-t)$$

The signal is reflected (folded) about the vertical axis ($t=0$).

**Example:** $x(t) = u(t-1)$ reflected gives $u(-t-1)$, a step that turns OFF at $t=-1$.

#### 2.2.4 Amplitude Scaling
$$y(t) = A \cdot x(t)$$

Multiplies the signal's amplitude by constant $A$. In a circuit, this corresponds to amplification ($A>1$) or attenuation ($0<A<1$) or inversion ($A<0$).

#### 2.2.5 Addition and Multiplication
- **Addition:** $y(t) = x_1(t) + x_2(t)$ (superposition of signals)
- **Multiplication:** $y(t) = x_1(t) \cdot x_2(t)$ (modulation, gating, windowing)

### 2.3 Symmetry Properties — Even and Odd Signals

**Even Signal:** $x(-t) = x(t)$ for all $t$. Symmetric about the vertical axis.
- Examples: $\cos(\omega t)$, $t^2$, $|t|$, $\text{rect}(t/\tau)$

**Odd Signal:** $x(-t) = -x(t)$ for all $t$. Antisymmetric about the origin.
- Examples: $\sin(\omega t)$, $t^3$, $\text{sgn}(t)$, $t \cdot u(t)$

**Theorem (Any signal decomposition):** Every signal $x(t)$ can be written as a sum of its even and odd parts:
$$x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$$

where:
$$x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2} \quad \text{(even part)}$$
$$x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2} \quad \text{(odd part)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU frequently tests:** Given a sketch of $x(t)$, derive $x(-t)$, $x(t-2)$, $x(2t)$, and then identify even/odd parts. Practice this combination — it is a guaranteed 14-mark question.

### 2.4 Periodic and Aperiodic Signals

**Periodic Signal:** $x(t + T) = x(t)$ for all $t$ and some $T > 0$. The smallest such $T$ is called the **fundamental period** $T_0$.

For a sum of periodic signals $x(t) = x_1(t) + x_2(t)$ with periods $T_1$ and $T_2$:
- The sum is periodic **only if** $T_1/T_2$ is **rational** (a ratio of integers)
- The fundamental period is $T_0 = \text{LCM}(T_1, T_2)$

**Example:** $x(t) = \cos(2\pi t) + \sin(3\pi t)$ has $T_1 = 1$ s and $T_2 = 2/3$ s. Since $T_1/T_2 = 3/2$ is rational, the sum is periodic with $T_0 = 2$ s.

**Aperiodic Signal:** No finite $T$ satisfies $x(t+T) = x(t)$.

### 2.5 Energy and Power Signals

**Total Energy:**
$$E = \lim_{T \to \infty} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$$

**Average Power:**
$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$$

**Classification Rule:**
- If $0 < E < \infty$ and $P = 0$ → **Energy Signal** (e.g., decaying exponential, single pulse)
- If $0 < P < \infty$ and $E = \infty$ → **Power Signal** (e.g., sinusoid, periodic signal)
- Signals that are neither energy nor power signals exist (e.g., $x(t) = t$) but are rare.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Signal | Mathematical Definition | Key Property | Energy/Power |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Unit Step** $u(t)$ | $u(t)=1$ for $t\geq 0$, $0$ otherwise | $\int u(t)dt = t\cdot u(t)$ | Power (P=0.5) |
| 2 | **Unit Ramp** $r(t)$ | $r(t)=t$ for $t\geq 0$, $0$ otherwise | $\frac{d}{dt}r(t) = u(t)$ | Power (P=∞) |
| 3 | **Unit Impulse** $\delta(t)$ | $\delta(t)=0$ for $t\neq 0$, area $=1$ | $\int x(t)\delta(t-t_0)dt = x(t_0)$ | Energy (E=1) |
| 4 | **Signum** $\text{sgn}(t)$ | $+1$ for $t>0$, $-1$ for $t<0$ | $\text{sgn}(t) = 2u(t)-1$ | Power (P=1) |
| 5 | **Rectangular Pulse** | $1$ for $\vert t\vert < \tau/2$ | $= u(t+\tau/2)-u(t-\tau/2)$ | Energy (E=τ) |
| 6 | **Sinusoid** | $A\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$ | Period $T_0 = 2\pi/\omega_0$ | Power (P=A²/2) |
| 7 | **Exponential** | $A e^{\alpha t}$ | $\alpha<0$: decay; $\alpha>0$: grow | Power/Energy |
| 8 | **Triangular Pulse** | $1-\vert t\vert/T$ for $\vert t\vert \leq T$ | Symmetric, peak at $t=0$ | Energy |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strict KTU Notation Convention (Exam Hall):** When writing $u(t-a)$, the parameter $a$ represents the **time shift** in seconds. The signal turns ON at $t = a$. Many students confuse this and lose 2-3 marks per question.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Step function** $u(t)$ → circuit ON/OFF switching, digital clock edges
- **Impulse** $\delta(t)$ → mathematical model for lightning strike, sampling probe, system identification
- **Exponential decay** → capacitor discharge, radioactive decay, RC low-pass filter step response
- **Sinusoid** → AC power systems (50/60 Hz), audio signals, RF communication
- **Rectangular pulse** → digital clock signals, radar pulses, PWM control
- **Ramp** → frequency-swept signals, sawtooth waveform in oscillators

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Express a Rectangular Pulse Using Step Functions

**Problem:** A rectangular pulse $x(t)$ of amplitude $2$ extends from $t = -1$ to $t = 3$. Express $x(t)$ mathematically in terms of unit step functions.

**Step 1:** Identify the ON and OFF instants.
- The pulse is ON from $t = -1$
- The pulse is OFF from $t = 3$

**Step 2:** Write the contribution of the leading edge (rising edge at $t = -1$).
At $t = -1$, the signal jumps from $0$ to $2$, contributing $+2 \cdot u(t+1)$.

**Step 3:** Write the contribution of the trailing edge (falling edge at $t = 3$).
At $t = 3$, the signal drops from $2$ to $0$, contributing $-2 \cdot u(t-3)$.

**Step 4:** Combine the two contributions.
$$x(t) = 2\, u(t+1) - 2\, u(t-3)$$

**Step 5:** Verify by computing $x(0)$.
$$x(0) = 2\, u(1) - 2\, u(-3) = 2(1) - 2(0) = 2 \quad \checkmark$$

The pulse value at $t=0$ is indeed $2$, confirming the derivation.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Decompose a Signal into Even and Odd Parts

**Problem:** Given $x(t) = e^{at}$ where $a$ is a real constant, find the even and odd parts.

**Step 1:** Compute $x(-t)$.
$$x(-t) = e^{a(-t)} = e^{-at}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the even part.
$$x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2} = \frac{e^{at} + e^{-at}}{2} = \cosh(at)$$

**Step 3:** Compute the odd part.
$$x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2} = \frac{e^{at} - e^{-at}}{2} = \sinh(at)$$

**Step 4:** Verify the decomposition.
$$x_e(t) + x_o(t) = \cosh(at) + \sinh(at) = \frac{e^{at} + e^{-at}}{2} + \frac{e^{at} - e^{-at}}{2} = e^{at} = x(t) \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5:** Verify symmetry properties.
- $x_e(-t) = \cosh(-at) = \cosh(at) = x_e(t)$ ✓ (even)
- $x_o(-t) = \sinh(-at) = -\sinh(at) = -x_o(t)$ ✓ (odd)

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Fundamental Period of a Sum of Sinusoids

**Problem:** Find the fundamental period of $x(t) = 3\cos(5t) - 2\sin(10t + \pi/4)$.

**Step 1:** Identify the angular frequencies.
- $\omega_1 = 5$ rad/s → $T_1 = 2\pi/5$ s
- $\omega_2 = 10$ rad/s → $T_2 = 2\pi/10 = \pi/5$ s

**Step 2:** Compute the ratio $T_1/T_2$.
$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{2\pi/5}{\pi/5} = 2$$

The ratio is rational (an integer), so the signal is periodic.

**Step 3:** Compute the fundamental period.
$$T_0 = \text{LCM}(T_1, T_2) = 2 \cdot T_2 = 2 \cdot \frac{\pi}{5} = \frac{2\pi}{5} \text{ s}$$

**Step 4:** Verify by substitution.
$$x\left(t + \frac{2\pi}{5}\right) = 3\cos\left(5t + 2\pi\right) - 2\sin\left(10t + 4\pi + \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$$
$$= 3\cos(5t) - 2\sin\left(10t + \frac{\pi}{4}\right) = x(t) \quad \checkmark$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Energy of a Decaying Exponential Pulse

**Problem:** Compute the energy of $x(t) = e^{-2t}\, u(t)$.

**Step 1:** Write the energy integral.
$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the signal. Since $u(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$, the lower limit becomes $0$.
$$E = \int_{0}^{\infty} \left( e^{-2t} \right)^2 dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-4t} \, dt$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the integral.
$$E = \left[ \frac{e^{-4t}}{-4} \right]_{0}^{\infty}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the limits.
$$E = 0 - \left( \frac{e^{0}}{-4} \right) = \frac{1}{4}$$

**Step 5:** Conclude classification. Since $0 < E < \infty$, the signal is an **energy signal** with $E = 0.25$ J (assuming $1\Omega$ normalization).

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Power of a Sinusoid

**Problem:** Compute the average power of $x(t) = A\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$.

**Step 1:** Write the power formula.
$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} A^2 \cos^2(\omega_0 t + \phi)\, dt$$

**Step 2:** Apply the trigonometric identity $\cos^2(\theta) = \frac{1 + \cos(2\theta)}{2}$.
$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{A^2}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \frac{1 + \cos(2\omega_0 t + 2\phi)}{2}\, dt$$

**Step 3:** Split the integral.
$$P = \frac{A^2}{4T} \left[ \int_{-T}^{T} dt + \int_{-T}^{T} \cos(2\omega_0 t + 2\phi)\, dt \right]$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the first integral.
$$\int_{-T}^{T} dt = 2T$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the second integral. The integral of a cosine over a symmetric interval that spans a complete number of periods is zero (as $T \to \infty$).
$$\int_{-T}^{T} \cos(2\omega_0 t + 2\phi)\, dt = 0$$

**Step 6:** Combine.
$$P = \frac{A^2}{4T} \cdot 2T = \frac{A^2}{2}$$

**Conclusion:** The average power of any sinusoid depends only on amplitude, not frequency or phase. $P = A^2/2$.

### 3.6 Derivation 6: Relationship Between Step, Ramp, and Impulse

**Step 1:** Show that integrating step gives ramp.
$$\int_{-\infty}^{t} u(\tau)\, d\tau = \int_{0}^{t} 1\, d\tau = t \quad \text{for } t \geq 0$$
$$\therefore r(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} u(\tau)\, d\tau$$

**Step 2:** Show that differentiating step gives impulse (in distribution sense).
$$\frac{d}{dt} u(t) = \delta(t)$$

**Step 3:** Show that differentiating ramp gives step.
$$\frac{d}{dt} r(t) = \frac{d}{dt} (t \cdot u(t))$$

Apply the product rule:
$$= u(t) \cdot 1 + t \cdot \delta(t)$$

By the sifting property of impulse, $t \cdot \delta(t) = 0$:
$$= u(t)$$

**Step 4:** Generalization.
$$\delta(t) = \frac{du(t)}{dt}, \quad u(t) = \frac{dr(t)}{dt}, \quad r(t) = \frac{dp(t)}{dt}$$

where $p(t) = t^2 u(t)/2$ is the unit parabolic signal.

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix: Standard Signal Identification

For KTU exam speed, use this decision matrix to instantly identify signal properties:

| Property | $u(t)$ | $r(t)$ | $\delta(t)$ | $\cos(\omega_0 t)$ | $e^{-\alpha t}u(t)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Value at $t=0$** | $1$ | $0$ | $\infty$ | $A$ | $1$ |
| **Causal?** | Yes | Yes | Yes | No (bilateral) | Yes |
| **Energy** | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $1$ | $\infty$ | $\frac{1}{2\alpha}$ |
| **Power** | $0.5$ | $\infty$ | $0$ | $A^2/2$ | $0$ |
| **Even?** | No | No | Yes | Yes | No |
| **Odd?** | No | Yes | No | No | No |
| **Periodic?** | No | No | No | Yes ($T_0 = 2\pi/\omega_0$) | No |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define a continuous-time signal. Give two examples of continuous-time signals encountered in engineering applications.*

**Model Answer:**

A continuous-time signal is a mathematical function $x(t)$ defined for all values of the independent variable $t$ in the real line $t \in (-\infty, \infty)$. The signal amplitude can take any value in a continuous range, and the time axis is also continuous (not restricted to discrete instants).

Mathematically, it is a mapping: $x : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ or $\mathbb{C}$.

**Engineering Examples:**
1. **Voltage across a resistor** in an AC circuit: $v(t) = 230\sin(100\pi t)$ V (Indian mains)
2. **ECG (electrocardiogram) signal** measured continuously from a patient's heart
3. **Speech waveform** captured by a microphone
4. **Temperature** in a room measured by a mercury thermometer

**[3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for examples with explanation]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*State and explain the sifting property of the unit impulse function $\delta(t)$.*

**Model Answer:**

The sifting (or sampling) property of the unit impulse $\delta(t)$ states that when an impulse is multiplied with a continuous function $x(t)$ and integrated over all time, the result equals the value of the function at the location of the impulse.

Mathematically:
$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, \delta(t - t_0)\, dt = x(t_0)$$

**Explanation:** The impulse $\delta(t - t_0)$ is non-zero only at $t = t_0$. When multiplied with $x(t)$, only the value $x(t_0)$ contributes to the integral, since the impulse has unit area concentrated at $t_0$. This property is fundamental in LTI system analysis because it allows us to "sample" any function at a specific time instant.

**Special case:** When $t_0 = 0$,
$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, \delta(t)\, dt = x(0)$$

**[3 marks: 1 for statement, 1 for equation, 1 for explanation]**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]**
*For the continuous-time signal $x(t)$ shown below, sketch and label carefully:*
*(i) $x(t-2)$*
*(ii) $x(2t)$*
*(iii) $x(-t+1)$*

*Given:* $x(t) = \begin{cases} t+1, & -1 \leq t \leq 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$

**[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Sketch of $x(t-2)$ — Time Shift (Delay) by 2 units**

The operation $x(t-2)$ shifts the entire signal **2 units to the right** (delays it).

Original signal: non-zero from $t = -1$ to $t = 1$
Shifted signal: non-zero from $t = 1$ to $t = 3$

Mathematically:
$$x(t-2) = \begin{cases} (t-2)+1 = t-1, & 1 \leq t \leq 3 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

At $t = 1$: $x(-1) = 1 - 1 = 0$
At $t = 3$: $x(1) = 3 - 1 = 2$ (peak)

**Sketch:** A ramp segment starting at $(1, 0)$ and rising linearly to $(3, 2)$, then dropping to zero.

---

**(ii) Sketch of $x(2t)$ — Time Scaling by factor 2**

The operation $x(2t)$ **compresses** the signal in time by a factor of 2 (the signal appears twice as fast).

The condition $-1 \leq t \leq 1$ in $x(t)$ becomes $-1 \leq 2t \leq 1$, which means $-0.5 \leq t \leq 0.5$.

Mathematically:
$$x(2t) = \begin{cases} 2t+1, & -0.5 \leq t \leq 0.5 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

At $t = -0.5$: $x(-1) = 0$
At $t = 0$: $x(0) = 1$ (peak)
At $t = 0.5$: $x(1) = 2$ (peak value)

**Sketch:** A triangular peak rising from $(-0.5, 0)$ to $(0, 1)$ and then to $(0.5, 2)$, then dropping to zero. The duration is halved.

---

**(iii) Sketch of $x(-t+1)$ — Combined Reflection and Shift**

The operation $x(-t+1)$ involves reflection AND shifting. Rewrite as $x(-(t-1))$.

This means: first shift the signal right by 1, then reflect about the vertical axis.

OR equivalently: first reflect $x(t)$ to get $x(-t)$, then shift right by 1.

Step A: Reflect $x(t)$ to get $x(-t)$:
$$x(-t) = \begin{cases} -t+1, & -1 \leq t \leq 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Step B: Shift $x(-t)$ right by 1 to get $x(-(t-1)) = x(-t+1)$:
$$x(-t+1) = \begin{cases} -(t-1)+1 = -t+2, & 0 \leq t \leq 2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

At $t = 0$: $x(1) = -0 + 2 = 2$ (peak at the start)
At $t = 2$: $x(-1) = -2 + 2 = 0$ (zero at the end)

**Sketch:** A ramp descending from $(0, 2)$ to $(2, 0)$, then zero elsewhere. The original signal has been mirrored and shifted.

---

**Q3(b). [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]**
*For the continuous-time signal $x(t) = (t+1) [u(t+1) - u(t-1)]$, determine:*
*(i) Whether the signal is periodic or aperiodic*
*(ii) The even and odd components of $x(t)$*
*(iii) The total energy of the signal*

**[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Periodicity Analysis**

The signal $x(t) = (t+1)$ is non-zero only over the finite interval $-1 \leq t \leq 1$. Since the signal has finite support (it is zero outside a bounded region), it cannot be periodic. A periodic signal must repeat for ALL $t \in (-\infty, \infty)$.

**Conclusion:** $x(t)$ is **aperiodic**.

---

**(ii) Even and Odd Components**

The signal is non-zero only on $[-1, 1]$, so we only need to evaluate on this interval.

**Step 1:** Compute $x(-t)$.

For $-1 \leq t \leq 1$, we have $-1 \leq -t \leq 1$, so $x(-t) = (-t + 1) = (1 - t)$.

**Step 2:** Even part.
$$x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2} = \frac{(t+1) + (1-t)}{2} = \frac{2}{2} = 1 \quad \text{for } -1 \leq t \leq 1$$

$$x_e(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & -1 \leq t \leq 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

This is a **rectangular pulse of height 1 and width 2**.

**Step 3:** Odd part.
$$x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2} = \frac{(t+1) - (1-t)}{2} = \frac{2t}{2} = t \quad \text{for } -1 \leq t \leq 1$$

$$x_o(t) = \begin{cases} t, & -1 \leq t \leq 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

This is a **ramp passing through origin** on the interval $[-1, 1]$.

**Verification:** $x_e(t) + x_o(t) = 1 + t = x(t)$ for $-1 \leq t \leq 1$ ✓

---

**(iii) Total Energy**

$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2\, dt = \int_{-1}^{1} (t+1)^2\, dt$$

Expand the integrand:
$$(t+1)^2 = t^2 + 2t + 1$$

Compute each term:
$$\int_{-1}^{1} t^2\, dt = \left[ \frac{t^3}{3} \right]_{-1}^{1} = \frac{1}{3} - \left(-\frac{1}{3}\right) = \frac{2}{3}$$

$$\int_{-1}^{1} 2t\, dt = \left[ t^2 \right]_{-1}^{1} = 1 - 1 = 0 \quad \text{(odd function over symmetric interval)}$$

$$\int_{-1}^{1} 1\, dt = 2$$

Sum:
$$E = \frac{2}{3} + 0 + 2 = \frac{2 + 6}{3} = \frac{8}{3} \text{ J}$$

**Conclusion:** Total energy $E = \frac{8}{3} \approx 2.667$ J. Since $0 < E < \infty$, $x(t)$ is an **energy signal**.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q4(a). [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]**
*Express the following continuous-time signal as a sum of weighted and shifted step functions:*
$$x(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & t < 0 \\ 3, & 0 \leq t < 2 \\ -1, & 2 \leq t < 4 \\ 2, & 4 \leq t < 5 \\ 0, & t \geq 5 \end{cases}$$

*Also, sketch $x(t)$ and find its total energy.*

**[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify each transition.

| Transition | Time | Change in Amplitude |
|---|---|---|
| 0 → 3 | $t = 0$ | $+3$ |
| 3 → -1 | $t = 2$ | $-4$ |
| -1 → 2 | $t = 4$ | $+3$ |
| 2 → 0 | $t = 5$ | $-2$ |

**Step 2:** Express $x(t)$ as a sum of weighted, shifted step functions.

$$x(t) = 3\, u(t) - 4\, u(t-2) + 3\, u(t-4) - 2\, u(t-5)$$

**Step 3:** Verify at a few sample times.
- $x(1) = 3(1) - 4(0) + 3(0) - 2(0) = 3$ ✓
- $x(3) = 3(1) - 4(1) + 3(0) - 2(0) = -1$ ✓
- $x(4.5) = 3 - 4 + 3 - 0 = 2$ ✓

**Step 4:** Sketch the signal.

The signal is a piecewise-constant waveform with jumps at $t = 0, 2, 4, 5$ and values $3, -1, 2, 0$ in those intervals respectively.

**Step 5:** Calculate total energy.

$$E = \int_{0}^{2} (3)^2\, dt + \int_{2}^{4} (-1)^2\, dt + \int_{4}^{5} (2)^2\, dt$$

$$E = 9(2) + 1(2) + 4(1) = 18 + 2 + 4 = 24 \text{ J}$$

---

**Q4(b). [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]**
*For the signal $x(t) = 2\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{3}t\right) + 4\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{4}t\right)$:*
*(i) Determine whether the signal is periodic. If yes, find the fundamental period.*
*(ii) Calculate the average power of the signal.*

**[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Periodicity**

**Step 1:** Compute the period of each component.
- $\omega_1 = \pi/3$ rad/s → $T_1 = \frac{2\pi}{\pi/3} = 6$ seconds
- $\omega_2 = \pi/4$ rad/s → $T_2 = \frac{2\pi}{\pi/4} = 8$ seconds

**Step 2:** Compute the ratio.
$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{6}{8} = \frac{3}{4}$$

The ratio is **rational**, so the sum is **periodic**.

**Step 3:** Find the fundamental period.

The fundamental period is the LCM of $T_1$ and $T_2$. Since $T_1 = 6$ s and $T_2 = 8$ s, both in seconds:
$$T_0 = \text{LCM}(6, 8) = 24 \text{ seconds}$$

**Verification:** $T_0/T_1 = 24/6 = 4$ (integer) ✓ and $T_0/T_2 = 24/8 = 3$ (integer) ✓

---

**(ii) Average Power**

**Step 1:** Apply the power formula.

For a sum of sinusoids at **different frequencies**, the cross-product terms average to zero (orthogonality of sinusoids at different frequencies over a common period).

$$P = P_1 + P_2$$

**Step 2:** Compute power of each component.

For a sinusoid $A\cos(\omega t + \phi)$, average power is $A^2/2$.

- Power of $2\cos(\pi t/3)$: $P_1 = \frac{(2)^2}{2} = 2$ W
- Power of $4\sin(\pi t/4)$: $P_2 = \frac{(4)^2}{2} = 8$ W

**Step 3:** Total average power.
$$P = 2 + 8 = 10 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4:** Detailed verification using the integral form.

$$P = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{0}^{T_0} \left[ 2\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{3}t\right) + 4\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{4}t\right) \right]^2 dt$$

Expand:
$$= \frac{1}{24} \int_0^{24} \left[ 4\cos^2\left(\frac{\pi t}{3}\right) + 16\sin^2\left(\frac{\pi t}{4}\right) + 16\cos\left(\frac{\pi t}{3}\right)\sin\left(\frac{\pi t}{4}\right) \right] dt$$

The cross-product integral over an integer number of periods is zero, and $\int \cos^2 = \int \sin^2 = T/2$:
$$P = \frac{1}{24} \left[ 4 \cdot 12 + 16 \cdot 12 + 0 \right] = \frac{1}{24} (48 + 192) = \frac{240}{24} = 10 \text{ W} \quad \checkmark$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully Before Writing Your Exam!**
>
> **🚨 Critical Mistakes That Cause Mark Deductions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing shift direction:** $u(t-a)$ starts at $t = +a$ (delayed), not $t = -a$. Many students write $u(t+2)$ as starting at $t = -2$ on the time axis. **Rule:** $u(t-a)$ → step turns ON at $t = a$.
>
> 2. **Sign errors in step function combinations:** A pulse from $t=a$ to $t=b$ is $u(t-a) - u(t-b)$, NOT $u(t-b) - u(t-a)$. Always subtract the LATER step.
>
> 3. **Ignoring causality:** When asked "is the signal causal?", a signal is causal only if $x(t) = 0$ for all $t < 0$. Bilateral signals (like $e^{-\vert t\vert}$) are NOT causal even if they decay.
>
> 4. **Energy/Power classification errors:** A single pulse is ALWAYS an energy signal. A periodic non-zero signal is ALWAYS a power signal. A constant DC signal ($x(t) = C$) is a power signal with $P = C^2$.
>
> 5. **Period of sin/cos confusion:** $\sin(\omega t)$ and $\cos(\omega t)$ BOTH have period $T = 2\pi/\omega$. The phase $\phi$ does NOT change the period.
>
> 6. **Sum of sinusoids periodicity:** If $\omega_1/\omega_2$ is irrational, the sum is APERIODIC. Don't blindly apply the LCM formula.
>
> 7. **Even/odd verification:** Always verify by computing $x(-t)$ and checking. If $x(-t) = x(t)$, it's even. If $x(-t) = -x(t)$, it's odd. Otherwise, it's neither.
>
> 8. **Impulse at boundary:** $t \cdot \delta(t) = 0$ (sifting property), but $t^2 \cdot \delta(t) = 0$ as well. Only constants survive the impulse multiplication.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1: Basic Continuous-Time Signals**

**📌 Core Definitions:**
- Continuous-time signal: $x(t)$ defined for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$
- Amplitude can be continuous (analog) or discrete (quantized)
- Causal signal: $x(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$
- Periodic: $x(t + T) = x(t)$ for smallest $T > 0$

**📌 Standard Signals (must memorize):**
- $u(t)$: Step — unit value for $t \geq 0$
- $r(t) = t \cdot u(t)$: Ramp
- $\delta(t)$: Impulse — unit area, zero elsewhere, infinite peak
- $\text{sgn}(t) = 2u(t) - 1$
- Rectangular pulse: $u(t + \tau/2) - u(t - \tau/2)$

**📌 Differentiation/Integration Chain:**
$$\delta(t) \xrightarrow{\text{integrate}} u(t) \xrightarrow{\text{integrate}} r(t) \xrightarrow{\text{integrate}} p(t)$$
(And reverse for differentiation)

**📌 Symmetry Formulas:**
- Even part: $x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2}$
- Odd part: $x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}$

**📌 Period Formulas:**
- Sinusoid $A\cos(\omega t + \phi)$: $T = 2\pi/\omega$
- Sum of periodic signals: $T_0 = \text{LCM}(T_1, T_2)$ if $T_1/T_2$ is rational

**📌 Energy and Power:**
- Energy: $E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t)\vert^2 dt$ (Joules)
- Power: $P = \lim_{T\to\infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t)\vert^2 dt$ (Watts)
- Sinusoid power: $P = A^2/2$
- Energy signal: $0 < E < \infty$, $P = 0$
- Power signal: $0 < P < \infty$, $E = \infty$

**📌 Operations (transformation rules):**
- $x(t - t_0)$: shift right by $t_0$ (delay if $t_0 > 0$)
- $x(at)$: compress by factor $a$ (if $a > 1$)
- $x(-t)$: reflect about vertical axis
- Combination: $x(at - b)$ — shift first, then scale (or use chain rule on argument)

**📌 Sifting Property (Impulse):**
- $\int x(t)\delta(t - t_0) dt = x(t_0)$
- $x(t)\delta(t - t_0) = x(t_0)\delta(t - t_0)$

**📌 Unit Conversions & Important Numbers (in bold):**
- Frequency: $f$ (Hz) ↔ $\omega$ (rad/s): $\omega = 2\pi f$
- Period: $T = 1/f = 2\pi/\omega$ seconds
- Indian mains: **50 Hz** → $T = 0.02$ s, $\omega = 100\pi$ rad/s ≈ **314.16 rad/s**
- US mains: **60 Hz**

**📌 Examiner's Golden Rule for KTU Module 1:**
> If a question asks to "sketch and label" — ALWAYS mark axis values, signal amplitude at breakpoints, and discontinuities. **A correct answer without a proper sketch loses 2-3 marks** out of 7.

**📌 Common Pitfall to Avoid:**
- Don't confuse the **ramp signal** with a linear function. The ramp $r(t) = t \cdot u(t)$ is **zero for $t < 0$**, while $y(t) = t$ is non-zero everywhere.
- Don't assume $\delta(t)$ is a function. It is a **distribution** (generalized function).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Frequency and Angular Frequency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT604 - DIGITAL SIGNALS AND PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Frequency and Angular Frequency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition

In the context of **Digital Signal Processing (DSP)**, signals — whether continuous-time or discrete-time — are often represented as combinations of sinusoidal components. Two fundamental parameters that completely characterize the rate of oscillation of any periodic or sinusoidal signal are **Frequency** and **Angular Frequency**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Frequency (f):** The number of complete cycles (oscillations) a periodic signal executes per unit time. It is measured in **Hertz (Hz)** where 1 Hz = 1 cycle per second. Mathematically, it is the reciprocal of the fundamental time period $T$ of the signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Angular Frequency (ω):** The rate of change of the phase of a sinusoidal signal, expressed in **radians per second (rad/s)**. It represents the angular displacement covered per unit time by a rotating phasor. The standard symbol used in KTU DSP modules is the lower-case Greek letter **omega (ω)** for continuous-time and capital **Omega (Ω)** for discrete-time signals.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing on the equator of the Earth holding a long string attached to a ball that you swing in a circle at a constant rate.

- The number of **full circles** the ball completes around you every second is the **Frequency (f)**. If the ball completes 2 full circles each second, $f = 2$ Hz.
- However, each full circle sweeps an angle of $2\pi$ radians. So, in 1 second, the ball sweeps an angular distance of $2 \times 2\pi = 4\pi$ radians. This angular sweep per second is the **Angular Frequency (ω)**, and in this case, $\omega = 4\pi$ rad/s.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Mnemonic for KTU Exams:** Frequency counts *circles*, Angular Frequency counts *radians*. Since one full circle always equals $2\pi$ radians, $\omega$ is always $2\pi$ times larger than $f$.

### Key Distinctions at a Glance

- **Frequency ($f$)** is intuitive and human-friendly (Hz, kHz, MHz, GHz).
- **Angular Frequency ($\omega$)** is mathematically convenient because it absorbs the $2\pi$ factor, simplifying derivatives, integrals, and Fourier transform expressions.
- In DSP, when a signal is written as $x(t) = A\cos(\omega t + \phi)$, the parameter multiplying $t$ inside the cosine is **always** $\omega$ (not $f$).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often interchange $f$ and $\omega$ in differential equations and Fourier formulas. Always remember — if the formula contains $\pi$, use $\omega$; if it contains Hz or seconds directly, use $f$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Logical Breakdown of the Concept

- **Step 1 — Identify the Periodic Signal:** A signal $x(t)$ is periodic if $x(t + T) = x(t)$ for all $t$, where $T > 0$ is the smallest positive constant satisfying this. $T$ is the **Fundamental Time Period** measured in **seconds (s)**.
- **Step 2 — Count the Repetitions:** Count how many times the signal repeats its shape in one second. This count is the **Frequency $f$**, measured in Hz.
- **Step 3 — Convert to Angular Form:** Because sinusoidal functions are fundamentally built on the radian argument, mathematicians and DSP engineers prefer to work with $\omega$, where each cycle is mapped to $2\pi$ radians of phase.
- **Step 4 — Apply in DSP Context:** In any sinusoidal representation $x(t) = A\sin(\omega t + \phi)$ or $x(t) = A\cos(\omega t + \phi)$, the parameter $\omega$ directly controls the **rate of oscillation**. Higher $\omega$ means faster oscillation (higher pitch in audio, higher frequency in RF).
- **Step 5 — Discrete-Time Adaptation:** For discrete-time signals $x[n]$, the normalized angular frequency is $\Omega = \omega T_s$, where $T_s$ is the sampling period. $\Omega$ is in **radians per sample**.

### The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why use $\omega$ instead of $f$?** Because derivatives of $\sin(\omega t)$ and $\cos(\omega t)$ preserve the form and the $\omega$ factor (e.g., $\frac{d}{dt}\cos(\omega t) = -\omega \sin(\omega t)$). This makes differential equations and Fourier transforms cleaner.
- **Why is $2\pi$ involved?** Because the natural period of sine and cosine is $2\pi$ radians. One complete oscillation of $\sin(\theta)$ requires $\theta$ to change by $2\pi$.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $f = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Frequency from period | Hz |
| 2 | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi}{T}$ | Angular frequency from period | rad/s |
| 3 | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | Angular frequency from frequency | rad/s |
| 4 | $T = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | Period from frequency | s |
| 5 | $T = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega}$ | Period from angular frequency | s |
| 6 | $f = \dfrac{\omega}{2\pi}$ | Frequency from angular frequency | Hz |
| 7 | $\Omega = \omega T_s$ | Normalized discrete-time angular frequency | rad/sample |
| 8 | $\Omega = \dfrac{2\pi f}{f_s}$ | Discrete-time angular frequency | rad/sample |
| 9 | $x(t) = A\sin(\omega t + \phi)$ | Continuous-time sinusoid | — |
| 10 | $x[n] = A\sin(\Omega n + \phi)$ | Discrete-time sinusoid | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Whenever the question gives a frequency in Hz (e.g., 50 Hz), **multiply by $2\pi$** to convert to $\omega$ before substituting into sinusoidal or Fourier formulas. Examiners specifically test this conversion.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio & Music Processing:** Human hearing spans roughly 20 Hz to 20 kHz. Frequency determines musical pitch; angular frequency is used in equalizer filter design.
- **Telecommunications:** Carrier frequencies (e.g., 900 MHz, 2.4 GHz) for GSM, Wi-Fi, and 5G are quoted in $f$, but modulation and demodulation math operates in $\omega$.
- **Power Systems:** The Indian AC mains supply operates at $f = 50$ Hz, so $\omega = 100\pi \approx 314.16$ rad/s — a value that appears in nearly every electrical machine equation.
- **Biomedical Signals:** EEG, ECG, and EMG signals are analyzed across frequency bands (Delta, Theta, Alpha, Beta, Gamma) where $f$ is standard.
- **Radar & Sonar:** Doppler shift calculations require precise $\omega$ values to determine target velocity.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Relationship Between Frequency and Angular Frequency

**Given:** A sinusoidal signal $x(t) = A\sin(\theta(t))$, where $\theta(t)$ is the instantaneous phase in radians.

**Step 1 — Express the argument of sine in terms of time.**

A linear phase progression means $\theta(t) = \theta_0 + kt$, where $k$ is a constant slope. So:

$$x(t) = A\sin(\theta_0 + kt)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the periodic condition.**

By definition, the period $T$ is the smallest positive $T$ such that $x(t + T) = x(t)$ for all $t$. Substituting:

$$A\sin(\theta_0 + k(t + T)) = A\sin(\theta_0 + kt)$$

**Step 3 — Use the periodicity of the sine function.**

Since $\sin(\alpha + 2\pi) = \sin(\alpha)$, the smallest phase increment that leaves sine unchanged is $2\pi$. Therefore:

$$kT = 2\pi$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the constant $k$.**

$$k = \frac{2\pi}{T}$$

**Step 5 — Identify $k$ as the angular frequency.**

The rate of change of phase with time is, by definition, the angular frequency:

$$\omega = \frac{d\theta}{dt} = k = \frac{2\pi}{T}$$

**Step 6 — Express $T$ in terms of $f$ and substitute.**

Since $f = \frac{1}{T}$, substituting $T = \frac{1}{f}$:

$$\omega = 2\pi \cdot \frac{1}{T} = 2\pi \cdot f$$

$$\boxed{\omega = 2\pi f}$$

**Step 7 — Invert to obtain $f$ in terms of $\omega$.**

$$f = \frac{\omega}{2\pi}$$

This completes the derivation of the master relationship.

---

### Derivation 2: Derivation of Period from Angular Frequency

**Given:** A continuous-time cosine signal $x(t) = \cos(\omega t)$.

**Step 1 — Impose the periodic condition.**

We require the smallest $T > 0$ such that $\cos(\omega(t + T)) = \cos(\omega t)$ for all $t$.

**Step 2 — Apply the cosine periodicity identity.**

Since $\cos(\alpha + 2\pi) = \cos(\alpha)$, the phase shift must equal $2\pi$:

$$\omega T = 2\pi$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $T$.**

$$T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical example for KTU validation.**

If $\omega = 100\pi$ rad/s (Indian mains), then:

$$T = \frac{2\pi}{100\pi} = \frac{1}{50} = 0.02 \text{ s} = 20 \text{ ms}$$

This matches the 50 Hz AC supply, confirming the derivation.

---

### Derivation 3: Conversion of Discrete-Time Angular Frequency $\Omega$

**Given:** A continuous sinusoid $x(t) = \cos(\omega t)$ sampled uniformly at instants $t = nT_s$, where $T_s = \frac{1}{f_s}$ is the sampling period.

**Step 1 — Substitute the sampling instants.**

$$x[n] = x(nT_s) = \cos(\omega n T_s)$$

**Step 2 — Define the discrete-time normalized angular frequency $\Omega$.**

By definition, $\Omega$ absorbs the sampling period:

$$\Omega = \omega T_s = \frac{\omega}{f_s}$$

**Step 3 — Express the discrete-time sinusoid.**

$$x[n] = \cos(\Omega n)$$

**Step 4 — Express $\Omega$ in terms of $f$ and $f_s$.**

$$\Omega = 2\pi f T_s = \frac{2\pi f}{f_s}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the periodicity in $\Omega$.**

Because $\cos(\theta)$ is $2\pi$-periodic, $x[n]$ is periodic in $\Omega$ with period $2\pi$:

$$x[n] = \cos((\Omega + 2\pi k)n) = \cos(\Omega n), \quad k \in \mathbb{Z}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** Unlike continuous-time $\omega$ (which spans all of $\mathbb{R}$), discrete-time $\Omega$ is unique only within the principal interval $[-\pi, \pi]$ or $[0, 2\pi]$. Frequencies outside this range **alias** back into it.

---

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A signal $x(t) = 5\cos(200\pi t + \pi/4)$ is given. Find (a) the angular frequency $\omega$, (b) the ordinary frequency $f$ in Hz, (c) the period $T$ in seconds.

**Solution:**

**(a) Angular frequency:** Reading directly from the argument of cosine:

$$\omega = 200\pi \text{ rad/s}$$

**(b) Frequency in Hz:** Using $f = \omega / (2\pi)$:

$$f = \frac{200\pi}{2\pi} = 100 \text{ Hz}$$

**(c) Period:** Using $T = 1/f$:

$$T = \frac{1}{100} = 0.01 \text{ s} = 10 \text{ ms}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Shortcut:** When you see a coefficient like $200\pi$ in front of $t$, immediately cancel the $\pi$ with the $2\pi$ in the denominator. The result is a clean integer in Hz (here, 100 Hz).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: Conceptual Relationship Between Frequency and Angular Frequency

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Periodic Signal x of t] --> B[Identify Period T in seconds]
    B --> C["f = 1 divided by T"]
    B --> D["omega = 2 pi divided by T"]
    C --> E["omega = 2 pi f"]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Sinusoid x of t equals A cos omega t plus phi]
    F --> G[Continuous Time Domain]
    E --> H[Sample at t equals n Ts]
    H --> I["Omega equals omega Ts"]
    I --> J[Discrete Time Sinusoid x of n equals A cos Omega n]
    J --> K[Discrete Time Domain]
```

### Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Frequency Conversion in DSP

```mermaid
graph LR
    INPUT[Analog Signal Source] --> SAMPLE[Sampler at fs]
    SAMPLE --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter]
    ADC --> DSPUNIT[Digital Signal Processor Core]
    DSPUNIT --> FFTA[FFT Engine in omega domain]
    DSPUNIT --> FFTA2[FFT Engine in f domain with 2 pi normalization]
    FFTA --> OUT1[Spectral Output in rad per s]
    FFTA2 --> OUT2[Spectral Output in Hz]
    OUT1 --> SPEAKER[Audio Output Device]
    OUT2 --> DISPLAY[Frequency Display Module]
```

### Diagram 3: Phasor Rotation Mapping f to omega

```mermaid
graph TD
    P0[Phasor at angle 0] --> R1[Rotate by 2 pi radians in T seconds]
    R1 --> P1[One Complete Cycle Completed]
    P1 --> R2[f equals 1 over T cycles per second]
    R1 --> R3[Phase swept 2 pi radians in T seconds]
    R3 --> P2[omega equals 2 pi over T rad per s]
    R2 --> P3[Counting full circles]
    P2 --> P4[Counting radians swept]
    P3 --> EQ[omega equals 2 pi f]
    P4 --> EQ
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Diagram 1 establishes the *mathematical chain* linking period, frequency, and angular frequency. Diagram 2 shows the *engineering pipeline* of how a real analog signal is converted to digital form and analyzed in both $f$ and $\omega$ domains inside a DSP system. Diagram 3 visualizes the *physical meaning* of $f$ as circle-counting and $\omega$ as radian-counting.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define *frequency* and *angular frequency* of a continuous-time sinusoidal signal. State the mathematical relationship between them.

**Model Answer:**
Frequency $f$ of a periodic signal is the number of complete cycles executed per unit time, measured in Hertz (Hz). Angular frequency $\omega$ is the rate of change of phase of the sinusoid, measured in radians per second (rad/s). They are related by the equation:

$$\omega = 2\pi f = \frac{2\pi}{T}$$

where $T$ is the fundamental time period. Conceptually, $f$ counts cycles while $\omega$ counts the corresponding radians swept (one cycle $= 2\pi$ radians).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
A continuous-time signal is given by $x(t) = 10\sin(50\pi t)$. Calculate its (i) angular frequency, (ii) frequency in Hz, and (iii) period in milliseconds.

**Model Answer:**

(i) Angular frequency: $\omega = 50\pi$ rad/s.

(ii) Frequency: $f = \dfrac{\omega}{2\pi} = \dfrac{50\pi}{2\pi} = 25$ Hz.

(iii) Period: $T = \dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{25} = 0.04$ s $= 40$ ms.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 1 Part B questions carry 14 marks with internal choice. Each question has sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** A sinusoidal voltage signal is expressed as $v(t) = 155.6\sin(314\,t + \pi/6)$ volts. Determine:
- (i) The peak amplitude, angular frequency, frequency, and phase.
- (ii) The RMS value of the signal.
- (iii) The time instant (in ms) at which the signal first reaches its positive peak after $t = 0$.

**(b)** For a discrete-time signal $x[n] = 5\cos(0.4\pi n + \pi/3)$:
- (i) Identify the normalized angular frequency $\Omega$ and compute the ordinary frequency assuming $f_s = 8$ kHz.
- (ii) Determine the fundamental period $N$ of $x[n]$ and verify whether the signal is periodic.
- (iii) Comment on what happens to the perceived frequency if $\Omega$ is increased beyond $\pi$.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)**

(i) Reading off the standard form $A\sin(\omega t + \phi)$:
- Peak amplitude $A = 155.6$ V
- Angular frequency $\omega = 314$ rad/s
- Frequency $f = \omega / (2\pi) = 314 / (2\pi) = 50$ Hz
- Phase $\phi = \pi/6$ rad $= 30^\circ$

(ii) RMS value of a sinusoid is $V_{\text{rms}} = A / \sqrt{2}$:

$$V_{\text{rms}} = \frac{155.6}{\sqrt{2}} = 110.0 \text{ V}$$

(This matches the Indian 230 V mains — wait, the form is 110 V; the coefficient matches 110 V RMS, peak = $110\sqrt{2} = 155.56$ V.)

(iii) Positive peak occurs when $\omega t + \phi = \pi/2$:

$$314\,t + \frac{\pi}{6} = \frac{\pi}{2}$$

$$314\,t = \frac{\pi}{2} - \frac{\pi}{6} = \frac{3\pi - \pi}{6} = \frac{2\pi}{6} = \frac{\pi}{3}$$

$$t = \frac{\pi/3}{314} = \frac{\pi}{942} \approx 3.336 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 3.34 \text{ ms}$$

**(b)**

(i) The normalized angular frequency is $\Omega = 0.4\pi$ rad/sample. The continuous-time frequency is:

$$f = \frac{\Omega}{2\pi} \cdot f_s = \frac{0.4\pi}{2\pi} \cdot 8000 = 0.2 \times 8000 = 1600 \text{ Hz}$$

(ii) For a discrete sinusoid $\cos(\Omega n)$, the period $N$ is the smallest integer such that $\Omega N = 2\pi k$ for some integer $k$:

$$\Omega N = 2\pi k \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.4\pi \cdot N = 2\pi k \quad \Rightarrow \quad N = 5k$$

The smallest positive integer choice is $k = 1$, giving $N = 5$. So the signal is **periodic with fundamental period $N = 5$**.

(iii) If $\Omega > \pi$, the discrete-time sinusoid exhibits **aliasing** — the apparent frequency decreases as $\Omega$ increases from $\pi$ to $2\pi$, mirroring the behaviour in $[0, \pi]$. The unique baseband is $\Omega \in [0, \pi]$.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Derive the mathematical relationship $\omega = 2\pi f$ starting from the definition of the period $T$ of a continuous-time sinusoidal signal. State clearly the units of each quantity and explain the physical meaning of angular frequency with the help of a rotating phasor diagram.

**(b)** The discrete-time signal $x[n] = \cos(0.5\pi n + \pi/4)$ is sampled at $f_s = 1000$ Hz. Find:
- (i) The continuous-time frequency $f$ in Hz.
- (ii) The continuous-time angular frequency $\omega$.
- (iii) The continuous-time period $T$.
- (iv) The number of samples $N$ collected in one period of the continuous-time signal.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *Derivation:*

Let $x(t) = A\sin(\omega t + \phi)$. For periodicity, $x(t + T) = x(t)$ for all $t$. The smallest positive $T$ satisfying this is the period. Because $\sin(\theta + 2\pi) = \sin(\theta)$, the phase must advance by $2\pi$ over time $T$:

$$\omega(t + T) + \phi = \omega t + \phi + 2\pi$$

$$\omega T = 2\pi \quad \Rightarrow \quad T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega}$$

By definition, $f = 1/T$, so:

$$f = \frac{\omega}{2\pi} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega = 2\pi f$$

**Units:** $[f] = $ Hz (s$^{-1}$), $[\omega] = $ rad/s, $[T] = $ s, $[2\pi] = $ rad (dimensionless ratio).

**Phasor interpretation:** $\omega$ represents the constant angular velocity (in rad/s) of a phasor rotating counter-clockwise. One full rotation ($2\pi$ rad) takes time $T = 1/f$ seconds.

**(b)**

(i) $f = \dfrac{\Omega}{2\pi} \cdot f_s = \dfrac{0.5\pi}{2\pi} \cdot 1000 = 250$ Hz.

(ii) $\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi(250) = 500\pi$ rad/s.

(iii) $T = 1/f = 1/250 = 0.004$ s $= 4$ ms.

(iv) Number of samples per period: $N = f_s \cdot T = 1000 \times 0.004 = 4$ samples.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Omission:** Forgetting to write units (Hz, rad/s, s) in Part (a) costs 0.5–1 mark per sub-part.
> 2. **Direct Formula Substitution Without Derivation:** Writing $\omega = 2\pi f$ without deriving it from periodicity loses up to 3 marks in derivation-heavy questions.
> 3. **Confusing $\omega$ and $\Omega$:** $\omega$ is for continuous-time, $\Omega$ is for discrete-time. Interchanging them in sampling problems loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Not Verifying Periodicity in Discrete-Time:** In $x[n] = \cos(\Omega n)$, you must check whether $\Omega / (2\pi)$ is a rational number. If irrational, the signal is **aperiodic**.
> 5. **Sign Error in Phase Equations:** When computing peak times, students often drop the sign of $\phi$ — keep phase algebra strict.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Frequency $f$** = cycles per second (Hz). **Angular frequency $\omega$** = radians per second (rad/s).
- Master conversion: $\omega = 2\pi f$, $T = 1/f$, $T = 2\pi/\omega$.
- Continuous-time: $\omega$ in rad/s. Discrete-time: $\Omega = \omega T_s$ in rad/sample.
- Discrete-time frequencies are unique only in $[0, 2\pi]$ (or equivalently $[-\pi, \pi]$) due to aliasing.
- A discrete sinusoid $\cos(\Omega n)$ is periodic **only if** $\Omega / (2\pi$ is a **rational number** $p/q$ in lowest terms; the period is then $N = q$.
- A continuous sinusoid is **always periodic** for any real $\omega$.
- Always specify **units** in KTU answers: Hz, rad/s, s, ms, kHz, etc.
- For the Indian AC mains: $f = 50$ Hz $\Rightarrow$ $\omega = 100\pi \approx 314.16$ rad/s.
- One full cycle $= 2\pi$ radians. This is the heart of the $\omega$ vs $f$ relationship.
- In Fourier transforms, the convention uses $\omega$ (continuous) and $\Omega$ (discrete); the $2\pi$ factor location depends on the convention.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Operations on Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT604 - DIGITAL SIGNALS AND PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Operations on Signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Basic Signal Operations?

In **Digital Signal Processing (DSP)**, a **signal** is a mathematical function that conveys information about the behavior of a physical system. Signals are typically represented as functions of an independent variable, most commonly **time ($t$)** for continuous-time signals or **sample index ($n$)** for discrete-time signals.

**Basic operations on signals** are fundamental mathematical transformations applied to signals to generate new, modified signals. These operations form the building blocks of every DSP algorithm — from filtering and modulation to image processing and communications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Basic operations on signals refer to a set of elementary transformations — *time-shifting, time-scaling, time-reversal, amplitude-scaling, addition, and multiplication* — applied independently or in combination to generate a new signal from one or more parent signals.

---

## 1.2 Classification of Signals (Context Setting)

| Signal Type | Independent Variable | Notation | Example |
|-------------|---------------------|----------|---------|
| Continuous-Time Signal | $t \in \mathbb{R}$ | $x(t)$ | Audio waveform, ECG |
| Discrete-Time Signal | $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ | $x[n]$ | Digital audio sample, pixel intensity |
| Analog Signal | $t \in \mathbb{R}$, $x \in \mathbb{R}$ | $x(t)$ | Voltage across a resistor |
| Digital Signal | $n \in \mathbb{Z}$, $x \in$ finite set | $x[n]$ | MP3 audio, JPEG image |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Photo Editing" Viewpoint

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Students:**
> Imagine a signal as a **photograph of a sound wave** taken along the horizontal time axis.
> - **Shifting** the photo left or right = moving the waveform in time.
> - **Scaling** the photo horizontally = stretching or compressing the time axis.
> - **Flipping** the photo = mirroring the signal in time.
> - **Scaling vertically** = changing the loudness (amplitude).
> - **Adding two photos** = mixing two sounds together.
> - **Multiplying two photos** = modulating one signal by another.

Just as photo-editing tools allow you to manipulate images, signal operations allow engineers to manipulate waveforms for analysis, storage, and transmission.

---

## 1.4 Why Study These Operations? (Engineering Utility)

- **Delay lines** in radars and digital filters use *time-shifting*.
- **Audio pitch shifting** in music production uses *time-scaling*.
- **Echo generation** in audio engineering uses *time-shifting + addition*.
- **Amplitude Modulation (AM)** in radio broadcasting uses *signal multiplication*.
- **Sampling oscilloscopes** use **time-scaling** to zoom into signals.
- **Image mirroring** in medical imaging (MRI, CT) uses *time/space reversal*.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** KTU questions on this topic often test whether the student can correctly identify whether a transformation is *independent variable* based (time side) or *dependent variable* based (amplitude side). Keep this distinction crystal clear.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Core Operations — A Structured Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Time Shifting ($x(t - t_0)$ or $x[n - n_0]$)

**The "Why":** Many physical systems introduce **delays** (a sound echo, a reflected radar pulse, a pipeline lag). We mathematically represent this delay as a shift along the time axis.

**The "How":**
- Replace the independent variable $t$ (or $n$) with $(t - t_0)$ or $(n - n_0)$.
- If $t_0 > 0$ (or $n_0 > 0$): the signal is **delayed (right-shifted)** by $t_0$ units.
- If $t_0 < 0$ (or $n_0 < 0$): the signal is **advanced (left-shifted)** by $\vert t_0 \vert$ units.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick:** The signal **moves in the direction of the sign** of the shift constant.
> - $(t - 2)$: graph shifts to the **right** by 2.
> - $(t + 2)$: graph shifts to the **left** by 2.

---

### 2.1.2 Time Scaling ($x(at)$ or $x[an]$)

**The "Why":** When we compress or expand a recording (playback speed up or slow motion), we are time-scaling the signal.

**The "How":**
- Replace $t$ with $at$ (where $a > 0$).
