- If $a > 1$: the signal is **compressed** horizontally by factor $a$ (faster playback).
- If $0 < a < 1$: the signal is **expanded** horizontally by factor $1/a$ (slower playback).
- If $a < 0$: combines scaling with reversal (discussed below).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write $x(2t)$ and try to "stretch" the graph. The correct interpretation is that $x(2t)$ **compresses** the graph horizontally by factor 2.

---

### 2.1.3 Time Reversal / Folding ($x(-t)$ or $x[-n]$)

**The "Why":** Time reversal appears in radar signal processing (matched filters), audio phase inversion, and image mirroring.

**The "How":**
- Replace $t$ with $-t$ (or $n$ with $-n$).
- The signal is **reflected about the vertical axis** (the amplitude axis at $t = 0$ or $n = 0$).

---

### 2.1.4 Amplitude Scaling ($A \cdot x(t)$ or $A \cdot x[n]$)

**The "Why":** Volume control on a TV, gain control in amplifiers, brightness control in images — all are amplitude scaling.

**The "How":**
- Multiply every amplitude value by a constant $A$.
- If $A > 1$: signal is **amplified**.
- If $0 < A < 1$: signal is **attenuated**.
- If $A < 0$: signal is **inverted** (flipped vertically) **and** scaled by $\vert A \vert$.

---

### 2.1.5 Signal Addition ($y(t) = x_1(t) + x_2(t)$)

**The "Why":** Mixing two audio tracks, superposition of waves (physics), combining sensor outputs.

**The "How":**
- The amplitude values of both signals are **added point-by-point** at every value of the independent variable.
- $y(t) = x_1(t) + x_2(t)$ — defined only where both signals overlap.

---

### 2.1.6 Signal Multiplication ($y(t) = x_1(t) \cdot x_2(t)$)

**The "Why":** Amplitude modulation (AM radio), windowing in spectral analysis, gating a signal.

**The "How":**
- The amplitude values of both signals are **multiplied point-by-point**.
- $y(t) = x_1(t) \cdot x_2(t)$ — non-zero only where both signals are non-zero.

---

### 2.1.7 Differentiation & Integration (Advanced KTU Operations)

**Differentiation:**
$$y(t) = \frac{d}{dt}x(t)$$
Used to detect edges in images, compute rate of change (velocity from position).

**Integration:**
$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau)\, d\tau$$
Used to recover a signal from its derivative (position from velocity), signal smoothing.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Operation | Continuous-Time | Discrete-Time | Geometric Effect |
|---|-----------|-----------------|----------------|------------------|
| 1 | Time Shifting (Delay) | $x(t - t_0)$ | $x[n - n_0]$ | Shifts right by $t_0$ / $n_0$ if $t_0, n_0 > 0$ |
| 2 | Time Shifting (Advance) | $x(t + t_0)$ | $x[n + n_0]$ | Shifts left by $t_0$ / $n_0$ |
| 3 | Time Scaling | $x(at)$ | $x[an]$ | Compresses by $a$ if $a > 1$ |
| 4 | Time Reversal | $x(-t)$ | $x[-n]$ | Reflects about vertical axis |
| 5 | Combined: Reversal + Shift | $x(-t - t_0)$ | $x[-n - n_0]$ | Reflect, then shift; or shift, then reflect |
| 6 | Amplitude Scaling | $A \cdot x(t)$ | $A \cdot x[n]$ | Stretches/compresses vertically |
| 7 | Signal Addition | $x_1(t) + x_2(t)$ | $x_1[n] + x_2[n]$ | Point-wise sum |
| 8 | Signal Multiplication | $x_1(t) \cdot x_2(t)$ | $x_1[n] \cdot x_2[n]$ | Point-wise product |
| 9 | Differentiation | $\dfrac{dx(t)}{dt}$ | $x[n] - x[n-1]$ (approx.) | Slope of signal |
| 10 | Integration | $\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau)\,d\tau$ | $\displaystyle\sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x[k]$ | Cumulative area |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule for Combined Operations:**
> Apply operations **right-to-left** for the argument (time-side) transformations.
> Example: $x(-2t + 4) = x\bigl(-2(t - 2)\bigr)$
> Step 1: Time-scale by factor 2 (compress).
> Step 2: Time-reverse (fold).
> Step 3: Time-shift right by 2 units.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility Map

| Operation | Real-World System |
|-----------|-------------------|
| Time Shifting | Radar echo, audio delay effects (reverb) |
| Time Scaling | Variable-speed playback, slow-motion replay |
| Time Reversal | Matched filters, phase-inverting speakers |
| Amplitude Scaling | Volume knob, optical zoom |
| Addition | Audio mixing console, beamforming in mic arrays |
| Multiplication | AM radio, signal windowing in FFT |
| Differentiation | Edge detection in image processing |
| Integration | Charge calculation from current, position from velocity |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Identifying a Composite Operation

**Problem:** Given a signal $x(t)$, sketch the signal $y(t) = x(-2t + 6)$.

**Step 1 — Algebraic Rearrangement**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(t) &= x(-2t + 6) \\
     &= x\bigl(-2(t - 3)\bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

We factored out $-2$ from the expression. The three operations are now evident: **time-scale by 2, time-reverse, time-shift right by 3**.

**Step 2 — Define the Sequence of Operations**

Reading the parenthesis inside-out (right to left in terms of mathematical nesting):

| Step | Operation | Algebraic Form |
|------|-----------|----------------|
| 1 | Time scale by 2 | $x(2t)$ — compress horizontally by factor 2 |
| 2 | Time reverse | $x(-2t)$ — reflect about the y-axis |
| 3 | Time shift right by 3 | $x(-2(t-3)) = x(-2t+6)$ — move right by 3 |

**Step 3 — Numerical Verification at Key Points**

| Original Point $(t, x(t))$ | $y$-value at $t = ?$ | Check |
|----------------------------|----------------------|-------|
| $x(0) = 1$ | $y(3) = x(-2(3)+6) = x(0) = 1$ | ✅ Matches shift by 3 right |
| $x(2) = 3$ | $y(4) = x(-2(4)+6) = x(-2) = 3$ | ✅ Time-reversal: original $x(2)$ appears at $y(4)$ |
| $x(-1) = 2$ | $y(2.5) = x(-2(2.5)+6) = x(1) = 2$ | ✅ Compression + reversal |

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Computing a Combined Operation on a Discrete Signal

**Problem:** Given the discrete-time signal $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ defined for $n = 0, 1, 2, 3$. Compute and plot $y[n] = 2 \cdot x[3 - n] - x[n]$.

**Step 1 — Process $x[3 - n]$**

This is a time-reversal ($n \to -n$) followed by a right shift by 3 units.

$$
\begin{aligned}
x[n] &= \{1, 2, 3, 4\}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, 3 \\
x[-n] &= \{?, ?, ?, ?\} \to \text{reversed indices}
\end{aligned}
$$

We compute the reversed signal at the corresponding negative indices:

| $n$ | $x[3-n]$ | Value |
|-----|----------|-------|
| 0 | $x[3]$ | 4 |
| 1 | $x[2]$ | 3 |
| 2 | $x[1]$ | 2 |
| 3 | $x[0]$ | 1 |
| 4 | $x[-1]$ | 0 (undefined → 0) |
| $-1$ | $x[4]$ | 0 (undefined → 0) |

So $x[3-n] = \{0, 4, 3, 2, 1, 0\}$ at $n = \{-1, 0, 1, 2, 3, 4\}$.

**Step 2 — Apply Amplitude Scaling: $2 \cdot x[3-n]$**

| $n$ | $2 \cdot x[3-n]$ |
|-----|------------------|
| $-1$ | 0 |
| 0 | 8 |
| 1 | 6 |
| 2 | 4 |
| 3 | 2 |
| 4 | 0 |

**Step 3 — Form $x[n]$ at the same indices**

| $n$ | $x[n]$ |
|-----|--------|
| $-1$ | 0 |
| 0 | 1 |
| 1 | 2 |
| 2 | 3 |
| 3 | 4 |
| 4 | 0 |

**Step 4 — Compute $y[n] = 2 \cdot x[3-n] - x[n]$**

| $n$ | $2 \cdot x[3-n]$ | $x[n]$ | $y[n]$ |
|-----|------------------|--------|--------|
| $-1$ | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 0 | 8 | 1 | **7** |
| 1 | 6 | 2 | **4** |
| 2 | 4 | 3 | **1** |
| 3 | 2 | 4 | **-2** |
| 4 | 0 | 0 | **0** |

**Final Answer:**

$$y[n] = \{0, 7, 4, 1, -2, 0\}, \quad n = \{-1, 0, 1, 2, 3, 4\}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Operation Identification Table

| Given Expression | Sequence of Operations (right → left) |
|-----------------|---------------------------------------|
| $x(t - 5)$ | Time-shift right by 5 |
| $x(2t + 4)$ | Time-shift left by 2, then time-compress by 2 |
| $x(-t + 3)$ | Time-shift right by 3, then time-reverse |
| $x(-3t)$ | Time-compress by 3, then time-reverse |
| $3x(t/2)$ | Time-expand by 2, then amplify by 3 |
| $x(2t-4) + x(-t)$ | Two independent branches: compressed-delayed signal + reversed signal |
| $\dfrac{d}{dt}x(t-2)$ | Time-shift right by 2, then differentiate |

---

## 3.4 Differentiation and Integration — Worked Example

**Problem:** If $x(t) = u(t)$ (unit step), compute $y(t) = \dfrac{dx(t)}{dt}$.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(t) &= u(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & t < 0 \\ 1, & t \geq 0 \end{cases} \\
y(t) &= \frac{d}{dt}u(t) = \delta(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

The derivative of the unit step is the **unit impulse (Dirac delta)** — a foundational result in continuous-time signal processing.

**Problem:** If $x[n] = u[n]$ (unit step in discrete), compute $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x[k]$.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y[n] &= \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} u[k] = \sum_{k=0}^{n} 1 = n + 1, \quad n \geq 0 \\
y[n] &= 0, \quad n < 0
\end{aligned}
$$

This yields the **ramp signal** — another fundamental DSP building block.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Taxonomy of Basic Signal Operations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Basic Operations on Signals"] --> B["Time-Side Transformations"]
    A --> C["Amplitude-Side Transformations"]
    A --> D["Multi-Signal Operations"]
    A --> E["Calculus Operations"]

    B --> B1["Time Shifting x(t minus t0)"]
    B --> B2["Time Scaling x of a t"]
    B --> B3["Time Reversal x of minus t"]
    B --> B4["Combined Transformations"]

    B1 --> B1a["Delay t0 greater than 0 Right Shift"]
    B1 --> B1b["Advance t0 less than 0 Left Shift"]

    B2 --> B2a["Compression a greater than 1"]
    B2 --> B2b["Expansion a less than 1"]

    C --> C1["Amplitude Scaling A times x t"]
    C --> C2["Amplitude Offset x t plus B"]

    D --> D1["Signal Addition x1 plus x2"]
    D --> D2["Signal Multiplication x1 times x2"]

    E --> E1["Differentiation dx dt"]
    E --> E2["Integration integral x tau d tau"]

    B1a --> F["KTU 2024 Module 1 Topic"]
    B1b --> F
    B2a --> F
    B2b --> F
    B3 --> F
    B4 --> F
    C1 --> F
    C2 --> F
    D1 --> F
    D2 --> F
    E1 --> F
    E2 --> F
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Signal Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    In["Input Signal x of t"] --> Op1["Operation 1: Time Shifting"]
    Op1 --> Op2["Operation 2: Time Scaling"]
    Op2 --> Op3["Operation 3: Time Reversal"]
    Op3 --> Op4["Operation 4: Amplitude Scaling"]
    Op4 --> Out["Output Signal y of t"]

    Src1["Source x1 of t"] --> Add["Adder"]
    Src2["Source x2 of t"] --> Add
    Add --> Out2["Output z of t = x1 plus x2"]

    Src3["Source p of t"] --> Mul["Multiplier"]
    Src4["Source q of t"] --> Mul
    Mul --> Out3["Output w of t = p times q"]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Diagram — Time-Side Transformations on a Triangular Pulse

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Orig["Original x of t: Triangular pulse from 0 to 4, peak at 2"]
    Shift["Time Shift: x of t minus 2: Triangular pulse from 2 to 6, peak at 4"]
    Rev["Time Reversal: x of minus t: Triangular pulse from minus 4 to 0, peak at minus 2"]
    Scale["Time Scaling: x of 2t: Compressed pulse from 0 to 2, peak at 1"]
    Comb["Combined: x of minus 2t plus 4: Reversed, compressed, shifted pulse from 1 to 3, peak at 2"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Each branch in the diagram represents a unique transformation of the same parent signal. The horizontal extent (width) of the pulse visually indicates the effect of time-scaling, while the horizontal position shows the effect of time-shifting.

---

## 4.4 Table — Effect of Operations on Signal Properties

| Operation | Period | Energy | Symmetry | Duration |
|-----------|--------|--------|----------|----------|
| Time Shift | Unchanged | Unchanged | Unchanged | Unchanged |
| Time Scale by $a$ | $T/a$ | Unchanged | Unchanged (if no reversal) | $D/a$ |
| Time Reversal | Unchanged | Unchanged | Even → Even, Odd → Odd | Unchanged |
| Amplitude Scale by $A$ | Unchanged | $A^2 \cdot E$ | Unchanged | Unchanged |
| Addition | May change | Increases | Generally lost | May increase |
| Multiplication | Generally changed | Modulated | Generally lost | Generally reduced |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

### Question A1
**Q:** Differentiate between time-shifting and time-scaling of a continuous-time signal. Illustrate with an example.

**Model Answer:**

- **Time Shifting:** Replacing $t$ with $(t - t_0)$ shifts the signal along the time axis. If $t_0 > 0$, the signal is delayed (shifted right); if $t_0 < 0$, it is advanced (shifted left). The shape and width of the signal remain unchanged.
- **Time Scaling:** Replacing $t$ with $at$ compresses or expands the signal along the time axis. If $a > 1$, the signal is compressed (faster); if $0 < a < 1$, it is expanded (slower). The amplitude remains unchanged, but the duration changes.
- **Example:** $x(t) = \sin(t)$ from $t = 0$ to $2\pi$. Then $x(t - 1)$ is the same sinusoid starting at $t = 1$ and ending at $t = 2\pi + 1$ (time shift). $x(2t)$ completes two cycles in the time $x(t)$ would complete one (time scale/compression).

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

### Question A2
**Q:** What is the effect of the operation $x(-t)$ on a discrete-time signal? Show with a numerical example.

**Model Answer:**

The operation $x(-n)$ is called **time reversal** or **folding**. It mirrors the signal about the vertical axis ($n = 0$).

**Example:** Let $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ at $n = \{-2, -1, 0, 1, 2\}$.

Then $x[-n]$ at $n = \{-2, -1, 0, 1, 2\}$:
- $x[-(-2)] = x[2] = 5$
- $x[-(-1)] = x[1] = 4$
- $x[-(0)] = x[0] = 3$
- $x[-(1)] = x[-1] = 2$
- $x[-(2)] = x[-2] = 1$

Result: $x[-n] = \{5, 4, 3, 2, 1\}$ at $n = \{-2, -1, 0, 1, 2\}$.

The signal has been **reversed** in the amplitude order — a mirror image of the original.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]**

### Question A (14 Marks) — **OR** Question B (14 Marks)

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**Consider the continuous-time signal:**

$$x(t) = \begin{cases} t + 1, & 0 \leq t \leq 2 \\ 3, & 2 \leq t \leq 4 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**(a)** Sketch $x(t)$ and obtain an expression for $y(t) = x(2 - t)$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compute and plot the signal $z(t) = x(2t - 4) + 2x(-t + 1)$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Step 1 — Sketch $x(t)$**

| Interval | Value | Behavior |
|----------|-------|----------|
| $t < 0$ | 0 | Zero |
| $0 \leq t \leq 2$ | $t + 1$ | Line from $(0,1)$ to $(2,3)$ |
| $2 \leq t \leq 4$ | 3 | Constant at 3 |
| $t > 4$ | 0 | Zero |

**Step 2 — Compute $y(t) = x(2 - t)$**

We find the interval where the **argument** $2 - t$ lies in the support $[0, 4]$ of $x(\cdot)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 \leq 2 - t \leq 4 \\
-2 \leq -t \leq 2 \\
-2 \leq t \leq 2
\end{aligned}
$$

So $y(t)$ is non-zero only for $t \in [-2, 2]$.

**Sub-case 1:** $2 - t \in [0, 2] \Rightarrow 0 \leq t \leq 2$. Here $x(2-t) = (2-t) + 1 = 3 - t$.

**Sub-case 2:** $2 - t \in [2, 4] \Rightarrow -2 \leq t \leq 0$. Here $x(2-t) = 3$.

$$
y(t) = \begin{cases} 3, & -2 \leq t \leq 0 \\ 3 - t, & 0 \leq t \leq 2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

The signal is now a **reversed (folded)** version of $x(t)$, reflecting the $x(-t)$ nature of the transformation.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Branch 1: $w_1(t) = x(2t - 4)$**

Find the range: $0 \leq 2t - 4 \leq 4 \Rightarrow 2 \leq t \leq 4$.

**Sub-case A:** $0 \leq 2t-4 \leq 2 \Rightarrow 2 \leq t \leq 3$. Value: $(2t-4)+1 = 2t - 3$.

**Sub-case B:** $2 \leq 2t-4 \leq 4 \Rightarrow 3 \leq t \leq 4$. Value: $3$.

$$
w_1(t) = \begin{cases} 2t - 3, & 2 \leq t \leq 3 \\ 3, & 3 \leq t \leq 4 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Branch 2: $w_2(t) = 2x(-t + 1) = 2x(1 - t)$**

Range: $0 \leq 1 - t \leq 4 \Rightarrow -3 \leq t \leq 1$.

**Sub-case A:** $0 \leq 1-t \leq 2 \Rightarrow -1 \leq t \leq 1$. Value: $2(1 - t + 1) = 2(2 - t) = 4 - 2t$.

**Sub-case B:** $2 \leq 1-t \leq 4 \Rightarrow -3 \leq t \leq -1$. Value: $2 \cdot 3 = 6$.

$$
w_2(t) = \begin{cases} 6, & -3 \leq t \leq -1 \\ 4 - 2t, & -1 \leq t \leq 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Final Step — Add $w_1(t) + w_2(t)$**

These two branches have **disjoint supports** (one is on $[2, 4]$; the other is on $[-3, 1]$), so:

$$
z(t) = w_1(t) + w_2(t) = \begin{cases} 6, & -3 \leq t \leq -1 \\ 4 - 2t, & -1 \leq t \leq 1 \\ 2t - 3, & 2 \leq t \leq 3 \\ 3, & 3 \leq t \leq 4 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since the supports are disjoint, addition is trivial (no overlapping region).

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**For the discrete-time signal $x[n] = \{2, 1, -1, 3, 2\}$ defined for $n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4$:**

**(a)** Plot $x[n]$ and determine $y[n] = x[2 - n]$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Find the expression and sketch $w[n] = 3x[n - 2] - 2x[-n + 3]$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Step 1 — Original signal $x[n]$:**

| $n$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|-----|---|---|---|---|---|
| $x[n]$ | 2 | 1 | -1 | 3 | 2 |

**Step 2 — Compute $y[n] = x[2-n]$**

For each $n$, we need the value of $x$ at index $(2 - n)$:

| $n$ | $2 - n$ | $y[n] = x[2-n]$ |
|-----|---------|-----------------|
| $-2$ | $4$ | $x[4] = 2$ |
| $-1$ | $3$ | $x[3] = 3$ |
| 0 | 2 | $x[2] = -1$ |
| 1 | 1 | $x[1] = 1$ |
| 2 | 0 | $x[0] = 2$ |
| 3 | $-1$ | $x[-1] = 0$ (undefined) |
| 4 | $-2$ | $x[-2] = 0$ (undefined) |

**Result:**

$$y[n] = \{2, 3, -1, 1, 2, 0, 0\}, \quad n = \{-2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3, 4\}$$

This is a **time-reversed and shifted** version of $x[n]$.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Branch 1: $g_1[n] = 3x[n - 2]$ (Amplitude-scaled, delayed by 2)**

| $n$ | $n - 2$ | $x[n-2]$ | $g_1[n] = 3x[n-2]$ |
|-----|---------|----------|---------------------|
| 0 | $-2$ | 0 | 0 |
| 1 | $-1$ | 0 | 0 |
| 2 | 0 | 2 | 6 |
| 3 | 1 | 1 | 3 |
| 4 | 2 | -1 | -3 |
| 5 | 3 | 3 | 9 |
| 6 | 4 | 2 | 6 |

**Branch 2: $g_2[n] = 2x[-n + 3] = 2x[3-n]$ (Reversed, shifted)**

| $n$ | $3 - n$ | $x[3-n]$ | $g_2[n] = 2x[3-n]$ |
|-----|---------|----------|---------------------|
| $-1$ | 4 | 2 | 4 |
| 0 | 3 | 3 | 6 |
| 1 | 2 | -1 | -2 |
| 2 | 1 | 1 | 2 |
| 3 | 0 | 2 | 4 |
| 4 | $-1$ | 0 | 0 |

**Final Step: $w[n] = g_1[n] - g_2[n]$**

| $n$ | $g_1[n]$ | $g_2[n]$ | $w[n]$ |
|-----|----------|----------|---------|
| $-1$ | 0 | 4 | **-4** |
| 0 | 0 | 6 | **-6** |
| 1 | 0 | -2 | **2** |
| 2 | 6 | 2 | **4** |
| 3 | 3 | 4 | **-1** |
| 4 | -3 | 0 | **-3** |
| 5 | 9 | 0 | **9** |
| 6 | 6 | 0 | **6** |

$$w[n] = \{-4, -6, 2, 4, -1, -3, 9, 6\}, \quad n = \{-1, 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Valuation Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in Shift (–2 marks):** Students often write $x(2t + 4)$ and shift left by 4. The correct factoring is $x(2(t + 2))$ — shift left by **2** (after the compression by 2). The shift constant divides by the scale factor.
>
> 2. **Order of Operations Error (–3 marks):** When applying multiple operations, students frequently reverse the order. The correct method is: apply operations from the **innermost parenthesis outward**, OR equivalently, **read the transformation as a chain applied to the original signal**.
>
> 3. **Disregarding Domain Restrictions (–2 marks):** Failing to specify the intervals where the resulting signal is non-zero, or not accounting for zero-padding when the argument falls outside the original support.
>
> 4. **Confusing Time and Amplitude Transformations (–2 marks):** $A \cdot x(t)$ scales amplitude; $x(A \cdot t)$ scales time. Mixing these is a frequent error.
>
> 5. **Skipping the Sketch (–1 mark):** KTU examiners expect a clearly labeled sketch for any 7-mark sub-question. Always include a plot with axis labels, tick values, and clear endpoints.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📋 **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1**

- ✅ The **six basic operations** are: time-shift, time-scale, time-reverse, amplitude-scale, signal addition, signal multiplication.
- ✅ Time-side operations modify the **independent variable** ($t$ or $n$).
- ✅ Amplitude-side operations modify the **dependent variable** (the signal value).
- ✅ **Time shift rule:** $x(t - t_0)$ with $t_0 > 0$ → **right** (delay); $t_0 < 0$ → **left** (advance).
- ✅ **Time scale rule:** $x(at)$ with $a > 1$ → **compress** (faster); $0 < a < 1$ → **expand** (slower).
- ✅ **Time reversal:** $x(-t)$ reflects the signal about the **vertical axis** ($t = 0$).
- ✅ **Combined operations** must be applied as a **chain** in the correct sequence; factor the argument carefully.
- ✅ For $x(at + b)$, rewrite as $x\bigl(a(t + b/a)\bigr)$ — shift by $b/a$, then scale by $a$.
- ✅ For $x(-t + c)$, rewrite as $x\bigl(-(t - c)\bigr)$ — shift right by $c$, then reflect.
- ✅ **Differentiation** of unit step gives unit impulse; **integration** of unit step gives ramp.
- ✅ Always **sketch** the input and output signals with clear axis labels.
- ✅ Specify the **support (domain)** of every resulting signal explicitly.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Discrete-Time Signals - Frequency and Angular Frequency of Discrete-Time Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT604 - DIGITAL SIGNALS AND PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Discrete-Time Signals - Frequency and Angular Frequency of Discrete-Time Signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Discrete-Time Signal?

A **discrete-time signal** is a function defined only at discrete instants of time, typically indexed by an integer variable $n \in \mathbb{Z}$. Mathematically, it is represented as a sequence of numbers:

$$x[n] = \{x[0], x[1], x[2], \dots, x[-1], x[-2], \dots\}$$

Unlike continuous-time signals $x(t)$ which exist for every real value of $t$, a discrete-time signal is obtained by **sampling** a continuous signal at uniformly spaced intervals $T$ (sampling period), so that $x[n] = x(nT)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In DSP, we treat $n$ as a dimensionless integer index. The actual time variable is $t = nT$, where $T$ is the sampling period in seconds. This is a fundamental distinction from continuous-time systems.

## 1.2 Frequency of a Discrete-Time Sinusoidal Signal

A general **discrete-time sinusoidal signal** is expressed as:

$$x[n] = A \cos(\omega n + \phi)$$

where:
- $A$ is the **amplitude** (dimensionless or in signal units, e.g., Volts).
- $\omega$ is the **angular frequency** in **radians/sample** (rad).
- $\phi$ is the **phase** in radians.
- $n$ is the **discrete-time index** (integer).

If we substitute the actual time $t = nT$, we get:

$$x[n] = A \cos(\omega n + \phi) = A \cos(2\pi f \cdot nT + \phi)$$

This allows us to relate the **continuous-time frequency** $f$ (in **Hz**) to the **discrete-time frequency**:

$$\boxed{\omega = 2\pi f T = 2\pi \frac{f}{f_s}}$$

where $f_s = 1/T$ is the **sampling frequency** in Hz.

## 1.3 The Two Faces of Frequency: $f$ vs. $\omega$

In the KTU 2024 syllabus, you must clearly distinguish between:

| Symbol | Name | Unit | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $f$ | Frequency (Cyclic) | **Hz** (cycles/sample or cycles/sec) | Number of oscillations per second |
| $\omega$ | Angular Frequency | **rad/sample** (radians) | Rate of phase change per sample |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** When we say "frequency" in DSP, we usually mean the **digital frequency** $f$ measured in **cycles per sample** (dimensionless). The term "angular frequency" $\omega$ is measured in **radians per sample**. They are related by $\omega = 2\pi f$.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy: The Stroboscopic Wheel 🎡

Imagine a rotating wheel with a single dot on its edge, observed under a **stroboscope** (which flashes at a fixed rate $f_s$).

- **Continuous-time observer** sees the dot moving smoothly at angular velocity $\omega_c$ rad/sec.
- **Stroboscopic observer (discrete-time)** only sees the wheel's position at flash instants.

If the wheel completes exactly **1 full rotation** between two consecutive flashes, the discrete observer sees the dot in the **same position every time** — the digital frequency is **0 Hz**, even though the wheel is spinning fast!

This is the heart of the **aliasing phenomenon**: discrete-time frequencies are unique only in the range:

$$-\frac{1}{2} \leq f \leq \frac{1}{2} \quad \text{(cycles/sample)}$$

or equivalently,

$$-\pi \leq \omega \leq \pi \quad \text{(rad/sample)}$$

## 1.5 Normalized Digital Frequency

In most DSP textbooks and KTU problems, the **sampling period $T$ is normalized to 1** (i.e., $T = 1$ second/sample). Under this convention:

$$\omega = 2\pi f$$

This is why in standard DSP notation, $x[n] = A \cos(\omega n + \phi)$ is used directly, with $\omega$ in rad/sample, bypassing the $T$ parameter altogether.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Representation: Three Equivalent Forms

A discrete-time sinusoid can be written in three equivalent ways:

**Form 1: Cosine with angular frequency (Most common in KTU exams)**
$$x[n] = A \cos(\omega_0 n + \phi)$$

**Form 2: Cosine with cyclic frequency**
$$x[n] = A \cos(2\pi f_0 n + \phi)$$

**Form 3: Complex exponential form (Euler's formula)**
$$x[n] = A e^{j(\omega_0 n + \phi)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** The **complex exponential form** $e^{j\omega n}$ is the **fundamental building block** of DSP. Just as $e^{st}$ is fundamental to continuous-time LTI systems, $e^{j\omega n}$ is fundamental to discrete-time LTI systems. The **Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT)** is built using these exponentials.

## 2.2 Periodicity Properties of Discrete Sinusoids

Unlike continuous sinusoids, a discrete sinusoid $x[n] = \cos(\omega_0 n)$ is **periodic if and only if** the normalized frequency $f_0 = \omega_0 / 2\pi$ is a **rational number**:

$$f_0 = \frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{N}{M}$$

where $N, M$ are integers with no common factors. The **fundamental period** is:

$$N_0 = M \quad \text{(samples)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Concept:** If $\omega_0 / 2\pi$ is irrational, the discrete sinusoid is **aperiodic** (never repeats). This is a major departure from continuous-time signals where periodicity is guaranteed.

## 2.3 The Fundamental Range: Principal Frequency Interval

Discrete-time frequencies exhibit an **inherent periodicity of $2\pi$** in $\omega$ (or $1$ in $f$):

$$\cos((\omega_0 + 2\pi k) n) = \cos(\omega_0 n + 2\pi k n) = \cos(\omega_0 n) \quad \forall k \in \mathbb{Z}$$

Therefore, all discrete-time frequencies are **equivalent** to a unique frequency in the **principal range**:

$$-\pi < \omega \leq \pi \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad -\frac{1}{2} < f \leq \frac{1}{2}$$

Frequencies outside this range are **aliases** of frequencies inside it.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Application | Role of Discrete Frequency |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Digital Audio (MP3, CDs)** | Audio band (20 Hz – 20 kHz) mapped to digital frequencies via $f_d = f/f_s$ |
| **OFDM in 4G/5G/Wi-Fi** | Subcarrier frequencies are digital frequencies on $\left[0, \tfrac{1}{2}\right]$ |
| **Biomedical Signal Processing** | ECG/EEG sampled at 250–1000 Hz, frequencies analyzed in digital domain |
| **Image Processing** | Spatial frequency (cycles/pixel) — direct analog of digital frequency |
| **Radar & Sonar** | Doppler shifts converted to digital frequency for matched filtering |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet 📋

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $x[n] = A\cos(\omega_0 n + \phi)$ | Discrete sinusoid (canonical form) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $x[n] = A\cos(2\pi f_0 n + \phi)$ | Cyclic-frequency form | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0 T$ | Angular frequency ↔ Continuous frequency | rad/sample |
| 4 | $f_0 = \dfrac{\omega_0}{2\pi}$ | Normalized digital frequency (when $T=1$) | cycles/sample |
| 5 | $f_s = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Sampling frequency | Hz |
| 6 | $f_N = \dfrac{f_s}{2}$ | Nyquist frequency (maximum representable) | Hz |
| 7 | $\omega_N = \pi$ | Nyquist angular frequency | rad/sample |
| 8 | $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0$ | Angular–cyclic relation (normalized) | rad/sample |
| 9 | $\cos((\omega_0 + 2\pi k)n) = \cos(\omega_0 n)$ | Aliasing identity | dimensionless |
| 10 | Period $N = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_0} M$ (smallest integer) | Fundamental period condition | samples |
| 11 | $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{j\omega_0 n} = \dfrac{1 - e^{j\omega_0 N}}{1 - e^{j\omega_0 n}}$ | Geometric series for sinusoids | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Note:** When using the pipe symbol `$\vert \cdot \vert$` in solutions, **never write it inside a markdown table** — it breaks the table syntax. Use `$\lvert \cdot \rvert$` or `$\mid \cdot \mid$` instead.

## 2.6 The "Why" Behind Each Concept

- **Why normalize by $T$?** To make the math independent of physical time units, so that any DSP algorithm works at any sampling rate.
- **Why restrict to $\vert\omega\vert \leq \pi$?** Because $\omega$ and $\omega + 2\pi k$ produce **identical** discrete sequences — no new information is gained by going outside.
- **Why are irrational frequencies aperiodic?** Because $e^{j\omega_0 n}$ samples the unit circle at points that **never repeat exactly** (irrational angle increments on a circle never close).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Continuous Sinusoid to Discrete Sinusoid

**Start** with a continuous-time cosine:

$$x_c(t) = A \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi)$$

**Step 1:** Sample uniformly at $t = nT$:

$$x[n] = x_c(nT) = A \cos(2\pi f_c \cdot nT + \phi)$$

**Step 2:** Factor out $T$ inside the argument:

$$x[n] = A \cos\left(2\pi \left(\frac{f_c}{f_s}\right) n + \phi\right)$$

where $f_s = 1/T$ is the sampling frequency. Let $F = f_c / f_s$ be the **normalized digital frequency** (cycles/sample).

**Step 3:** Define the **digital angular frequency**:

$$\omega_0 = 2\pi F = 2\pi \frac{f_c}{f_s} = 2\pi f_c T$$

**Step 4:** Substitute back:

$$x[n] = A \cos(\omega_0 n + \phi) \quad \blacksquare$$

> This is the canonical DSP representation used throughout KTU textbooks and exams.

## 3.2 Derivation: Periodicity of Discrete Sinusoid

**Claim:** $x[n] = \cos(\omega_0 n)$ is periodic with period $N$ if and only if $\omega_0 N = 2\pi k$ for some integer $k$.

**Step 1 — Necessity ($\Rightarrow$):**
Assume $x[n+N] = x[n]$ for all $n$. Then:

$$\cos(\omega_0 (n+N)) = \cos(\omega_0 n)$$

Using the identity $\cos(\theta + 2\pi k) = \cos(\theta)$:

$$\omega_0 (n+N) = \omega_0 n + 2\pi k$$

Simplify:

$$\omega_0 N = 2\pi k \quad \Rightarrow \quad N = \frac{2\pi k}{\omega_0}$$

**Step 2 — Sufficiency ($\Leftarrow$):**
If $\omega_0 N = 2\pi k$, then:

$$\cos(\omega_0(n+N)) = \cos(\omega_0 n + \omega_0 N) = \cos(\omega_0 n + 2\pi k) = \cos(\omega_0 n) \quad \blacksquare$$

**Step 3 — Fundamental Period:**
The smallest positive $N$ requires the smallest integer $k$ such that $N$ is a positive integer. Thus:

$$\frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{k}{N} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} \text{ must be rational} = \frac{k}{N}$$

If $\omega_0 / 2\pi = p/q$ in lowest terms, the **fundamental period is $N = q$**.

## 3.3 Derivation: The $2\pi$ Periodicity of $\omega$

**Claim:** Discrete sinusoids with $\omega$ and $\omega + 2\pi k$ (for any integer $k$) are **identical**.

**Proof:**

$$x_1[n] = \cos(\omega n)$$
$$x_2[n] = \cos((\omega + 2\pi k)n)$$

Expand $x_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_2[n] &= \cos(\omega n + 2\pi k n) \\
&= \cos(\omega n)\cos(2\pi k n) - \sin(\omega n)\sin(2\pi k n)
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $k$ and $n$ are both integers, $2\pi k n$ is an integer multiple of $2\pi$:

$$\cos(2\pi k n) = 1, \quad \sin(2\pi k n) = 0$$

Therefore:

$$x_2[n] = \cos(\omega n) \cdot 1 - \sin(\omega n) \cdot 0 = \cos(\omega n) = x_1[n] \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** This is why we **never need** frequencies outside $\left[-\pi, \pi\right]$ in rad/sample (or $\left[-1/2, 1/2\right]$ in cycles/sample). Any frequency outside is just an "alias" of a frequency inside.

## 3.4 Worked Example: Computing Period from $\omega_0$

**Problem (KTU-style):** Find the fundamental period of $x[n] = 5\cos(0.4\pi n + \pi/3)$.

**Step 1 — Express as rational:**
$$\frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{0.4\pi}{2\pi} = \frac{0.4}{2} = \frac{2}{10} = \frac{1}{5}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify fraction:**
$$\frac{1}{5} \text{ is already in lowest terms} \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = 1, \; N = 5$$

**Step 3 — Verify:**
$$x[n+5] = 5\cos(0.4\pi(n+5) + \pi/3) = 5\cos(0.4\pi n + 2\pi + \pi/3) = 5\cos(0.4\pi n + \pi/3) = x[n]$$

**Answer:** $N = 5$ samples. ✅

## 3.5 Worked Example: Aliasing Demonstration

**Problem:** Show that $\cos(0.7\pi n)$ and $\cos(1.3\pi n)$ are indistinguishable as discrete signals.

**Step 1 — Difference in frequencies:**
$$\Delta\omega = 1.3\pi - 0.7\pi = 0.6\pi$$

But notice:
$$0.7\pi + 2\pi(1) = 0.7\pi + 2\pi = 2.7\pi$$

Subtracting $2\pi$:

$$2.7\pi - 2\pi = 0.7\pi$$

Now consider $1.3\pi$:
$$1.3\pi - 2\pi = -0.7\pi$$

And $\cos(-0.7\pi n) = \cos(0.7\pi n)$. So:

$$\cos(1.3\pi n) = \cos(-0.7\pi n) = \cos(0.7\pi n) \quad \blacksquare$$

**Engineering Takeaway:** A frequency of $0.7\pi$ rad/sample has infinitely many aliases: $\pm 0.7\pi, \pm 1.3\pi, \pm 2.7\pi, \dots$ All are the same discrete signal.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Frequency Conversions

| Continuous-time $f_c$ (Hz) | Sampling rate $f_s$ (Hz) | Digital frequency $f = f_c / f_s$ (cycles/sample) | Digital angular $\omega = 2\pi f$ (rad/sample) | Range Check |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 100 | 1000 | 0.1 | $0.2\pi$ | ✅ Within $\left[-1/2, 1/2\right]$ |
| 250 | 1000 | 0.25 | $0.5\pi$ | ✅ At Nyquist |
| 400 | 1000 | 0.4 | $0.8\pi$ | ✅ Within |
| 600 | 1000 | 0.6 | $1.2\pi$ | ❌ Aliases to $-0.4$ (or $0.4\pi$ rad/sample) |
| 750 | 1000 | 0.75 | $1.5\pi$ | ❌ Aliases to $-0.25$ (or $0.5\pi$ rad/sample) |

> [!NOTE]
> The "Range Check" column is the key takeaway: any $f$ outside $\left[-1/2, 1/2\right]$ is an alias and will be heard/seen as a different frequency. This is the basis of the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Frequency Conversion Pipeline

This diagram illustrates the conceptual flow of converting between continuous-time and discrete-time frequency representations — a process central to every DSP problem in the KTU syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Continuous Time Signal xc of t] --> B[Anti-Aliasing Filter LPF f less than fs by 2]
    B --> C[ADC Sampler T seconds]
    C --> D[Discrete Signal x of n with n integer]
    D --> E[Frequency Mapper omega equals 2 pi f T]
    E --> F[Digital Angular Frequency rad per sample]
    D --> G[Frequency Mapper f equals omega over 2 pi]
    G --> H[Normalized Digital Frequency cycles per sample]
    F --> I[Range Check minus pi to pi]
    H --> J[Range Check minus half to half]
    I --> K[Unique Representation Principal Domain]
    J --> K
    K --> L[DSP Processing DFT DTFT FFT Filter]
    L --> M[Output Analysis Frequency Response Magnitude Phase]
```

## 4.2 Concept Map: Discrete-Time Frequency Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InputDomain[Input Domain]
        C[Continuous Time Frequency fc in Hz]
        A[Amplitude A]
        P[Phase phi in radians]
    end
    subgraph Sampling[Conversion Block]
        T[Sampling Period T in seconds]
        FS[Sampling Frequency fs equals 1 by T]
    end
    subgraph DiscreteDomain[Discrete Domain]
        FD[Digital Cyclic Frequency f equals fc by fs cycles per sample]
        WD[Digital Angular Frequency omega equals 2 pi f rad per sample]
        XN[Discrete Signal x of n equals A cos omega n plus phi]
    end
    subgraph RangeLimits[Range Limits]
        R1[Range minus pi less than omega less than pi rad per sample]
        R2[Range minus half less than f less than half cycles per sample]
        R3[Aliases omega and omega plus 2 pi k identical]
    end
    C --> T
    T --> FS
    FS --> FD
    FD --> WD
    A --> XN
    WD --> XN
    P --> XN
    WD --> R1
    FD --> R2
    R1 --> R3
    R2 --> R3
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow: Determining Periodicity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Given x of n equals A cos omega n plus phi] --> Q1{Is omega divided by 2 pi rational?}
    Q1 -->|Yes Reduce to p by q| Compute[k equals p, N equals q]
    Q1 -->|No Irrational| Aperiodic[Signal is Aperiodic, never repeats]
    Compute --> Check{Q is smallest integer making N integer?}
    Check -->|Yes| Result[Fundamental Period equals N samples]
    Check -->|No| Refine[Simplify fraction p by q fully, then set N equals q]
    Refine --> Result
```

## 4.4 Frequency Spectrum: Visual Comparison of Two Frequencies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LowFreq[Low Frequency Signal omega equals 0 point 2 pi]
        L1[Sample 0: cos 0 equals 1]
        L2[Sample 1: cos 0 point 2 pi equals 0 point 81]
        L3[Sample 2: cos 0 point 4 pi equals 0 point 31]
        L4[Sample 3: cos 0 point 6 pi equals minus 0 point 31]
        L5[Sample 4: cos 0 point 8 pi equals minus 0 point 81]
        L6[Sample 5: cos 1 point 0 pi equals minus 1]
        L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5 --> L6
    end
    subgraph HighFreq[High Frequency Signal omega equals 0 point 8 pi]
        H1[Sample 0: cos 0 equals 1]
        H2[Sample 1: cos 0 point 8 pi equals minus 0 point 81]
        H3[Sample 2: cos 1 point 6 pi equals minus 0 point 81]
        H4[Sample 3: cos 2 point 4 pi equals minus 0 point 31]
        H5[Sample 4: cos 3 point 2 pi equals 0 point 31]
        H6[Sample 5: cos 4 point 0 pi equals 1]
        H1 --> H2 --> H3 --> H4 --> H5 --> H6
    end
```

> **Interpretation:** The **low frequency** (0.2π rad/sample) completes 1/10 of a cycle in 5 samples. The **higher frequency** (0.8π rad/sample) completes 2/5 of a cycle in 5 samples. Both are valid discrete sinusoids, and both are within the principal range $\left[-\pi, \pi\right]$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**
> Define a discrete-time signal. With a neat sketch, represent the unit step sequence $u[n]$ and the unit ramp sequence $r[n]$.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **discrete-time signal** $x[n]$ is a sequence of values defined only at integer indices $n \in \mathbb{Z}$. It is obtained by sampling a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ at uniform intervals $t = nT$.

- **Unit step:** $u[n] = \begin{cases} 1, & n \geq 0 \\ 0, & n < 0 \end{cases}$
- **Unit ramp:** $r[n] = \begin{cases} n, & n \geq 0 \\ 0, & n < 0 \end{cases}$ (equivalently, $r[n] = n \cdot u[n]$)

> *(Examiner expects: clear definition + either algebraic form or 1-mark sketch showing $u[n]$ as a step and $r[n]$ as a linearly increasing sequence for $n \geq 0$.)*

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**
> State the relationship between continuous-time frequency $f$ (in Hz) and discrete-time angular frequency $\omega$ (in rad/sample) for a signal sampled at $f_s$ Hz.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The relationship is:

$$\omega = 2\pi f T = \frac{2\pi f}{f_s}$$

where $T = 1/f_s$ is the sampling period. In **normalized form** (with $T = 1$ s):

$$\omega = 2\pi f \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad f = \frac{\omega}{2\pi}$$

**Key limits:** The maximum unambiguous digital frequency is the **Nyquist frequency**:

$$f_{\max} = \frac{f_s}{2} \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad \omega_{\max} = \pi \text{ rad/sample}$$

> *(Examiner expects: formula + units + Nyquist boundary. 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for units, 1 mark for Nyquist statement.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**
>
> **(a)** For the discrete-time signal $x[n] = 4\cos(0.3\pi n - \pi/4) + 2\sin(0.6\pi n + \pi/6)$:
> - (i) Find the fundamental period $N$ of each component and the overall signal. **(4 marks)**
> - (ii) Determine whether the signal is periodic, and if so, find its overall period. **(3 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A continuous-time signal $x_c(t) = 10\cos(200\pi t)$ is sampled at $f_s = 400$ Hz.
> - (i) Determine the discrete-time signal $x[n]$ obtained after sampling. **(3 marks)**
> - (ii) Find the normalized digital frequency $f$ and digital angular frequency $\omega$. **(2 marks)**
> - (iii) Determine the Nyquist frequency and state whether aliasing occurs. **(2 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)(i) Period of each component:**

For $x_1[n] = 4\cos(0.3\pi n - \pi/4)$:

$$\frac{\omega_1}{2\pi} = \frac{0.3\pi}{2\pi} = \frac{0.3}{2} = \frac{3}{20}$$

In lowest terms, $3/20$ is already reduced. So $N_1 = 20$ samples.

For $x_2[n] = 2\sin(0.6\pi n + \pi/6)$:

$$\frac{\omega_2}{2\pi} = \frac{0.6\pi}{2\pi} = \frac{0.6}{2} = \frac{3}{10}$$

In lowest terms, $3/10$ is already reduced. So $N_2 = 10$ samples.

**(a)(ii) Overall periodicity:**

The overall signal is periodic if $\text{lcm}(N_1, N_2)$ exists (it always does for rationals):

$$N = \text{lcm}(20, 10) = 20 \text{ samples}$$

So the overall signal $x[n]$ is periodic with fundamental period $N = 20$ samples. ✅

**(b)(i) Discrete signal after sampling:**

Sampling at $f_s = 400$ Hz gives $T = 1/400$ s. Substituting $t = nT$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x[n] &= 10\cos(200\pi \cdot nT) \\
&= 10\cos\!\left(200\pi \cdot \frac{n}{400}\right) \\
&= 10\cos(0.5\pi n)
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b)(ii) Normalized digital frequencies:**

$$f = \frac{f_c}{f_s} = \frac{100}{400} = 0.25 \text{ cycles/sample}$$

$$\omega = 2\pi f = 0.5\pi \text{ rad/sample}$$

**(b)(iii) Nyquist check:**

$$f_N = \frac{f_s}{2} = 200 \text{ Hz}, \quad \omega_N = \pi \text{ rad/sample}$$

Since $f_c = 100 \text{ Hz} < 200 \text{ Hz}$ and $\omega = 0.5\pi < \pi$, **no aliasing occurs**. The signal is faithfully represented. ✅

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the concept of angular frequency $\omega$ of a discrete-time signal. How is it different from the continuous-time frequency? Show that two sinusoids $\cos(\omega_1 n)$ and $\cos(\omega_2 n)$ are identical if $\omega_2 = \omega_1 + 2\pi k$, $k \in \mathbb{Z}$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A signal $x[n] = 3\cos(0.5\pi n + \pi/3) + 5\sin(0.75\pi n)$ is sampled from a system with $T = 0.5$ ms.
> - (i) Write down $x_c(t)$ in continuous time. **(3 marks)**
> - (ii) Determine the two continuous-time frequencies present in $x_c(t)$ in Hz. **(2 marks)**
> - (iii) Comment on whether both frequencies are below the Nyquist limit. **(2 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** The **angular frequency** $\omega$ of a discrete-time sinusoid $x[n] = A\cos(\omega n + \phi)$ is the rate of phase change **per sample**, measured in **radians per sample** (rad/sample). It is a dimensionless quantity once $T$ is normalized to 1.

**Difference from continuous-time frequency:**

| Aspect | Continuous $\omega_c$ | Discrete $\omega$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Unit | rad/sec | rad/sample |
| Range | $(0, \infty)$ | Unique only in $\left[-\pi, \pi\right]$ |
| Periodicity | Always periodic (any $\omega_c$) | Periodic only if $\omega/2\pi$ is rational |

**Proof of aliasing identity:**

Let $x_1[n] = \cos(\omega_1 n)$ and $x_2[n] = \cos(\omega_2 n)$ with $\omega_2 = \omega_1 + 2\pi k$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_2[n] &= \cos((\omega_1 + 2\pi k) n) \\
&= \cos(\omega_1 n + 2\pi k n) \\
&= \cos(\omega_1 n)\cos(2\pi k n) - \sin(\omega_1 n)\sin(2\pi k n)
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $k, n \in \mathbb{Z}$, the product $kn$ is an integer, so:

$$\cos(2\pi k n) = 1, \quad \sin(2\pi k n) = 0$$

Therefore:

$$x_2[n] = \cos(\omega_1 n) = x_1[n] \quad \blacksquare$$

**(b)(i) Recovering $x_c(t)$:**

Since $x[n] = x_c(nT)$ with $T = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}$ s, we have $n = t/T$, so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_c(t) &= 3\cos\!\left(0.5\pi \cdot \frac{t}{T} + \frac{\pi}{3}\right) + 5\sin\!\left(0.75\pi \cdot \frac{t}{T}\right) \\
&= 3\cos\!\left(\frac{0.5\pi t}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}} + \frac{\pi}{3}\right) + 5\sin\!\left(\frac{0.75\pi t}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}\right) \\
&= 3\cos(1000\pi t + \pi/3) + 5\sin(1500\pi t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b)(ii) Continuous-time frequencies:**

- First component: $\omega_{c1} = 1000\pi$ rad/sec $\Rightarrow f_{c1} = 500$ Hz
- Second component: $\omega_{c2} = 1500\pi$ rad/sec $\Rightarrow f_{c2} = 750$ Hz

**(b)(iii) Nyquist check:**

$$f_s = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 2000 \text{ Hz}, \quad f_N = \frac{f_s}{2} = 1000 \text{ Hz}$$

- $f_{c1} = 500$ Hz $< 1000$ Hz ✅ (no aliasing)
- $f_{c2} = 750$ Hz $< 1000$ Hz ✅ (no aliasing)

Both frequencies are below the Nyquist limit. **No aliasing occurs.** ✅

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert** $\omega$ to $f$ (or vice-versa) — marks lost in unit conversion (1–2 marks).
> 2. **Incorrectly assuming** discrete sinusoids are always periodic — they are periodic **only if $\omega/2\pi$ is rational**.
> 3. **Forgetting the factor $T$** when converting from $x[n]$ to $x_c(t)$: $x_c(t) = x[n]\big\vert_{n = t/T}$, **not** $x_c(t) = x[t]$.
> 4. **Mixing up** the principal range: $\omega \in \left[-\pi, \pi\right]$ vs $f \in \left[-1/2, 1/2\right]$. Using the wrong one in aliasing questions costs 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Not reducing fractions** when finding the fundamental period: $\omega/2\pi = 6/8$ gives period $N = 4$, not $N = 8$. Always simplify.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🎯

### Core Definitions
- **Discrete-time signal** $x[n]$: a sequence indexed by integer $n \in \mathbb{Z}$.
- **Frequency $f$** (cycles/sample): normalized digital frequency, range $\left[-1/2, 1/2\right]$.
- **Angular frequency $\omega$** (rad/sample): range $\left[-\pi, \pi\right]$.

### Must-Know Formulas
1. $x[n] = A\cos(\omega_0 n + \phi)$ — canonical DSP form.
2. $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0 T$ — angular frequency from continuous frequency.
3. $f_s = 1/T$ — sampling rate.
4. $f_N = f_s/2$ and $\omega_N = \pi$ — Nyquist limits.
5. $\omega$ and $\omega + 2\pi k$ are **identical** discrete signals (aliasing identity).
6. $\omega/2\pi$ rational $\Rightarrow$ periodic; $\omega/2\pi$ irrational $\Rightarrow$ aperiodic.

### Key Properties to Remember
- ✅ Discrete frequencies are **periodic with period $2\pi$** in $\omega$ (or $1$ in $f$).
- ✅ Only the **principal domain** $\left[-\pi, \pi\right]$ contains unique representations.
- ✅ Complex exponentials $e^{j\omega n}$ are the **fundamental eigenfunctions** of discrete LTI systems.
- ✅ Sampling converts continuous sinusoids $x_c(t)$ into discrete sinusoids $x[n]$ via $t = nT$.

### Quick Revision Checklist
- ☐ Can I write a discrete sinusoid in three equivalent forms?
- ☐ Can I convert $f$ to $\omega$ and vice-versa, with and without $T$?
- ☐ Can I find the fundamental period from $\omega$?
- ☐ Do I know the Nyquist boundary in both $f$ and $\omega$?
- ☐ Can I prove the $2\pi$-periodicity of $\omega$?
- ☐ Can I check if a discrete sinusoid is periodic or aperiodic?
- ☐ Can I detect aliasing given $f_c$ and $f_s$?

> **Final Examiner Tip:** In KTU 2024 Scheme exams, **always state the principal frequency range** explicitly when solving aliasing problems. A 1-line justification like *"Since $\omega = 0.7\pi$ lies in $\left[-\pi, \pi\right]$, it is the unique representation"* earns you full marks and demonstrates conceptual clarity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Continuous-Time and Discrete-Time Signals: Periodic and Non-Periodic Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT604 - DIGITAL SIGNALS AND PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of Continuous-Time and Discrete-Time Signals: Periodic and Non-Periodic Signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Continuous-Time (CT) and Discrete-Time (DT) Signals — A Foundation

Before classifying signals as **periodic** or **non-periodic**, we must understand the two parent domains in which every signal exists.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> - A **Continuous-Time Signal** $x(t)$ is a function defined over a **continuous range of the independent variable** $t \in \mathbb{R}$ (real numbers). It is defined for *every* instant of time.
> - A **Discrete-Time Signal** $x(n)$ is a function defined only at **discrete (integer) instants** $n \in \mathbb{Z}$. It is defined *only* at specific points in time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The keyword difference:** CT → independent variable is **continuous** ($\mathbb{R}$); DT → independent variable is **discrete** ($\mathbb{Z}$). Both are signals; only the *sampling* of the time axis changes.

### 1.2 The Concept of "Periodicity" — Plain English Analogy

Imagine you are standing on a balcony watching two different events:

- **Event A (Periodic):** A lighthouse beam sweeping the sea. It sweeps past you **every 10 seconds, again, and again, forever**. You can predict the exact moment of the next sweep. This repetition-with-a-fixed-interval is *periodicity*.
- **Event B (Aperiodic / Non-Periodic):** A single thunderclap during a storm. It happens **once, unpredictably**, and never repeats in the same way. This is *aperiodicity*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition — Periodic Signal:**
> A signal is **periodic** if it repeats its values exactly after a fixed time interval, for all time.
> - **CT:** $x(t) = x(t + T)$ for all $t$, where the smallest positive $T$ is the **fundamental period $T_0$**.
> - **DT:** $x(n) = x(n + N)$ for all $n$, where the smallest positive integer $N$ is the **fundamental period $N_0$**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition — Aperiodic (Non-Periodic) Signal:**
> A signal is **aperiodic** if **no finite positive value** of $T$ (or $N$) exists such that $x(t) = x(t+T)$ (or $x(n) = x(n+N)$) for all $t$ (or $n$).
> - Aperiodic signals do **not** repeat; every sample is unique in the infinite timeline.

### 1.3 Standard KTU Examples to Commit to Memory

| Signal Type | Continuous-Time Example | Discrete-Time Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Periodic** | $x(t) = \sin(2\pi t)$ | $x(n) = \cos(\pi n / 4)$ |
| **Aperiodic** | $x(t) = e^{-at} u(t)$, $a>0$ | $x(n) = a^n u(n)$, $0<a<1$ |
| **DC (Special Periodic)** | $x(t) = 5$ (constant) | $x(n) = 7$ (constant) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A DC (constant) signal is periodic with *any* period $T$** (CT) or $N$ (DT). However, by convention, we say a constant signal has **no well-defined fundamental period** because no *smallest positive* period exists. The fundamental period is formally defined as $\infty$ for DC signals in many texts.
</br>
</br>
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Period — The "Why" Behind It

When a signal satisfies $x(t) = x(t+T)$, the value $T$ is called a **period** of the signal. However, infinitely many such $T$ values may exist (e.g., $T, 2T, 3T, \ldots$ all work). The **fundamental period $T_0$** is the *smallest positive* value of $T$ for which the equality holds. It is the "atomic" repetition unit.

> **The "Why":** The fundamental period uniquely characterizes how fast a signal oscillates. A smaller $T_0$ means a faster-repeating (higher-frequency) signal.

### 2.2 Conditions for Periodicity in Discrete-Time

A discrete-time sinusoid $x(n) = A \cos(\omega_0 n + \phi)$ is periodic **if and only if** the normalized digital frequency $\omega_0 / 2\pi$ is a **rational number**.

If $\omega_0 = 2\pi (p/q)$ where $p, q$ are positive integers (in lowest terms), then the fundamental period is $N_0 = q$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A CT sinusoid $x(t) = \cos(\omega_0 t)$ is ALWAYS periodic** (for any real $\omega_0$), but a **DT sinusoid is periodic ONLY IF $\omega_0$ is a rational multiple of $2\pi$**. This is one of the most-tested KTU facts.

### 2.3 Sum of Two Periodic Signals — A Critical Theorem

If $x_1(t)$ has period $T_1$ and $x_2(t)$ has period $T_2$, then $x(t) = x_1(t) + x_2(t)$ is periodic **if and only if** the ratio $T_1 / T_2$ is a **rational number** $p/q$.

The fundamental period of the sum is:

$$T_0 = \text{LCM}(T_1, T_2) = \frac{T_1 \cdot T_2}{\gcd(T_1, T_2)} \cdot m$$

where $m$ is the smallest positive integer making $m \cdot T_1 / T_2$ an integer. In practice, $T_0$ is the **Least Common Multiple (LCM)** of $T_1$ and $T_2$.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU Module 1 question paper.**

| # | Concept | Continuous-Time Formula | Discrete-Time Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Periodicity Definition** | $x(t) = x(t + T_0)$ for all $t$ | $x(n) = x(n + N_0)$ for all $n$ |
| 2 | **Fundamental Period (Sinusoid)** | $T_0 = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_0}$ | $N_0 = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_0} \cdot k$ (smallest integer $k$ s.t. $N_0 \in \mathbb{Z}$) |
| 3 | **Period of Sum** | $T_0 = \text{lcm}(T_1, T_2)$ if $T_1/T_2 \in \mathbb{Q}$ | $N_0 = \text{lcm}(N_1, N_2)$ if $N_1/N_2 \in \mathbb{Q}$ |
| 4 | **Power of Periodic Signal** | $P = \dfrac{1}{T_0} \int_{0}^{T_0} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$ | $P = \dfrac{1}{N_0} \sum_{n=0}^{N_0-1} \vert x(n) \vert^2$ |
| 5 | **Energy of Aperiodic Signal** | $E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt < \infty$ | $E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(n) \vert^2 < \infty$ |
| 6 | **Complex Exponential** | $x(t) = e^{j\omega_0 t}$ has $T_0 = 2\pi / \vert \omega_0 \vert$ | $x(n) = e^{j\omega_0 n}$ periodic iff $\omega_0 / 2\pi \in \mathbb{Q}$ |
| 7 | **Harmonic Frequency** | $\omega_k = k \omega_0$, $k \in \mathbb{Z}$ | $\omega_k = \omega_0 + 2\pi k$ |
| 8 | **Time Scaling** | $x(at) \Rightarrow T' = T_0 / a$ | $x(kn) \Rightarrow N' = N_0 / k$ |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Periodic signals** model AC power lines (50/60 Hz sinusoidal), clock oscillators in microprocessors, and carrier waves in communications.
- **Aperiodic signals** model voice (speech), biomedical signals (ECG, EEG), seismic waves, and transient responses in circuits.
- **The Fourier Series** applies to periodic signals; the **Fourier Transform** applies to aperiodic signals. Knowing the periodicity classification is the gateway to choosing the right transform.
</br>
</br>
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Fundamental Period of a Sum of Two Sinusoids

**Problem:** Find the fundamental period of $x(t) = 3\cos(50\pi t) + 5\cos(60\pi t)$.

**Step 1 — Identify individual frequencies and periods.**

For the first term, $\omega_1 = 50\pi$ rad/s, so:

$$T_1 = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_1} = \frac{2\pi}{50\pi} = \frac{1}{25} \text{ s}$$

For the second term, $\omega_2 = 60\pi$ rad/s, so:

$$T_2 = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_2} = \frac{2\pi}{60\pi} = \frac{1}{30} \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — Check the ratio of periods for rationality.**

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{1/25}{1/30} = \frac{30}{25} = \frac{6}{5}$$

Since $6/5 \in \mathbb{Q}$, the sum **is periodic**.

**Step 3 — Compute the LCM of $T_1$ and $T_2$.**

Write $T_1 = 1/25$ and $T_2 = 1/30$ as fractions. A common period $T$ must be an integer multiple of both:

$$T = m \cdot T_1 = n \cdot T_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad m \cdot \frac{1}{25} = n \cdot \frac{1}{30}$$

$$30m = 25n \quad \Rightarrow \quad 6m = 5n$$

The smallest positive integers satisfying $6m = 5n$ are $m = 5, n = 6$.

**Step 4 — Compute the fundamental period.**

$$T_0 = m \cdot T_1 = 5 \cdot \frac{1}{25} = \frac{1}{5} \text{ s} = 0.2 \text{ s}$$

**Verification:** $n \cdot T_2 = 6 \cdot (1/30) = 1/5$ s ✓

### 3.2 Derivation: Power of a Periodic Square Wave

**Problem:** Find the average power of the CT periodic signal $x(t)$ defined as $x(t) = 1$ for $0 \leq t < 1$ and $x(t) = 0$ for $1 \leq t < 2$, with period $T_0 = 2$ s.

**Step 1 — Recall the periodic power formula.**

$$P = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{0}^{T_0} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $T_0 = 2$ and split the integral at $t = 1$.**

$$P = \frac{1}{2} \left[ \int_{0}^{1} (1)^2 \, dt + \int_{1}^{2} (0)^2 \, dt \right]$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each integral.**

$$P = \frac{1}{2} \left[ (1)(1 - 0) + (0)(2 - 1) \right] = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1 = 0.5 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result.** The signal is "on" 50% of the time, so the average power is $0.5$ W (or $0.5$ in normalized units).

### 3.3 Derivation: Periodicity of $x(n) = \cos(0.5\pi n)$

**Step 1 — Apply the DT periodicity condition.**

$$x(n) = x(n + N) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \cos(0.5\pi n) = \cos(0.5\pi (n + N))$$

**Step 2 — Use the identity $\cos(\theta) = \cos(\theta + 2\pi k)$.**

$$0.5\pi N = 2\pi k \quad \Rightarrow \quad N = 4k$$

**Step 3 — Choose the smallest positive integer $N$.** Setting $k = 1$ gives $N_0 = 4$. The fundamental period is **4 samples**.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Is a Signal Periodic?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this matrix as a KTU exam-time decision flowchart.**

| Question to Ask | If YES | If NO |
|---|---|---|
| Is the signal a constant (DC)? | Periodic, $T_0 = \infty$ (or any $T$) | Continue checking ↓ |
| Is it a CT sinusoid $\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$? | Always periodic, $T_0 = 2\pi / \vert \omega_0 \vert$ | Aperiodic |
| Is it a DT sinusoid $\cos(\omega_0 n + \phi)$? | Is $\omega_0 / 2\pi \in \mathbb{Q}$? $\rightarrow$ Yes $\Rightarrow$ Periodic | Aperiodic |
| Is it a sum $x_1 + x_2$ of periodic signals? | Is $T_1 / T_2 \in \mathbb{Q}$? $\rightarrow$ Yes $\Rightarrow$ Periodic, $T_0 = \text{lcm}(T_1, T_2)$ | Aperiodic |
| Is it a decaying exponential $e^{-at} u(t)$? | Aperiodic | — |
| Is it a finite-duration pulse? | Aperiodic (zero outside its support, never repeats) | — |
</br>
</br>
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Top-Level Classification of Signals

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[Signal x] --> B0[Independent Variable Type]
    B0 --> C1[Continuous Time x of t]
    B0 --> C2[Discrete Time x of n]
    C1 --> D1[Periodic CT]
    C1 --> D2[Aperiodic CT]
    C2 --> D3[Periodic DT]
    C2 --> D4[Aperiodic DT]
    D1 --> E1["x t equals x t plus T0"]
    D1 --> E2[Examples: sin, cos, square wave]
    D2 --> E3[No finite T satisfies x t equals x t plus T]
    D2 --> E4[Examples: decaying exponential, finite pulse]
    D3 --> E5["x n equals x n plus N0"]
    D3 --> E6[Examples: cos of 0.5 pi n, digital clock]
    D4 --> E7[No finite N satisfies x n equals x n plus N]
    D4 --> E8[Examples: 0.5 power n u n, speech samples]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Decision Flow for Testing Periodicity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F0[Start: Given Signal x] --> F1{Is x constant DC?}
    F1 -- Yes --> F2[Result: Periodic T0 arbitrary]
    F1 -- No --> F3{Is x sinusoidal?}
    F3 -- No --> F4{Is x a sum of periodic signals?}
    F3 -- Yes CT --> F5{Continuous Time?}
    F3 -- Yes DT --> F6{Is omega0 over 2pi rational?}
    F4 -- Yes --> F7{Is T1 over T2 rational?}
    F5 -- Yes --> F8[Result: Periodic T0 equals 2 pi over omega0]
    F6 -- Yes --> F9[Result: Periodic N0 equals smallest integer k with k times omega0 divisible by 2 pi]
    F6 -- No --> F10[Result: Aperiodic]
    F7 -- Yes --> F11[Result: Periodic T0 equals lcm of T1 and T2]
    F7 -- No --> F10
    F4 -- No --> F12{Decaying or transient?}
    F12 -- Yes --> F10
    F12 -- No --> F13[Default: Treat as Aperiodic unless proven periodic]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Continuous vs Discrete Sinusoid Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph CTWORLD[Continuous Time Sinusoid]
        CT1["x of t equals sin of omega0 t"] --> CT2[Always Periodic]
        CT2 --> CT3["T0 equals 2 pi over omega0"]
    end
    subgraph DTWORLD[Discrete Time Sinusoid]
        DT1["x of n equals sin of omega0 n"] --> DT2{omega0 over 2pi rational?}
        DT2 -- Yes --> DT3[Periodic with N0 equals denominator]
        DT2 -- No --> DT4[APERIODIC Key KTU trap]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** The rightmost branch (`DT4`) is one of the most-missed concepts. Students often assume DT sinusoids behave like CT ones, but **discretization can destroy periodicity**.
</br>
</br>
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` Define a periodic discrete-time signal. Is $\cos(0.6\pi n)$ periodic? Justify.**

**Model Answer:**
A discrete-time signal $x(n)$ is periodic if there exists a positive integer $N$ such that $x(n) = x(n+N)$ for all $n \in \mathbb{Z}$. The smallest such $N$ is the fundamental period $N_0$.

For $x(n) = \cos(0.6\pi n)$:

$$\frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{0.6\pi}{2\pi} = \frac{0.3}{1} = \frac{3}{10}$$

Since $3/10 \in \mathbb{Q}$, the signal is **periodic** with $N_0 = 10$ samples.

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` Distinguish between periodic and aperiodic signals with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Periodic Signal | Aperiodic Signal |
|---|---|---|
| Repetition | Repeats after fixed interval $T_0$ or $N_0$ | No repetition |
| Energy | Infinite ($E = \infty$) | Finite ($E < \infty$) |
| Power | Finite, non-zero ($P > 0$) | Zero ($P = 0$) |
| Example (CT) | $\sin(2\pi t)$ | $e^{-2t} u(t)$ |
| Example (DT) | $\cos(\pi n / 3)$ | $(0.5)^n u(n)$ |

---

### Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Determine whether the following signals are periodic. If periodic, find the fundamental period.

- (i) $x_1(t) = 5\cos(20\pi t) + 3\sin(30\pi t)$
- (ii) $x_2(n) = \cos(0.3\pi n) + 2\sin(0.4\pi n)$

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compute the average power of the periodic signal $x(t) = 2\cos(100\pi t) + \sin(150\pi t)$. Comment on its energy.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a)(i)** For $x_1(t) = 5\cos(20\pi t) + 3\sin(30\pi t)$:

$$T_a = \frac{2\pi}{20\pi} = \frac{1}{10} \text{ s}, \quad T_b = \frac{2\pi}{30\pi} = \frac{1}{15} \text{ s}$$

$$\frac{T_a}{T_b} = \frac{1/10}{1/15} = \frac{15}{10} = \frac{3}{2} \in \mathbb{Q}$$

Hence periodic. LCM: smallest integers $m, n$ such that $m/10 = n/15 \Rightarrow 15m = 10n \Rightarrow 3m = 2n$, giving $m = 2, n = 3$.

$$T_0 = m \cdot T_a = 2 \cdot \frac{1}{10} = \frac{1}{5} \text{ s} = 0.2 \text{ s}$$

**(a)(ii)** For $x_2(n) = \cos(0.3\pi n) + 2\sin(0.4\pi n)$:

$$\frac{0.3\pi}{2\pi} = \frac{3}{20}, \quad \frac{0.4\pi}{2\pi} = \frac{2}{10} = \frac{1}{5}$$

Period 1: $N_1 = 20$ samples. Period 2: $N_2 = 5$ samples.

$$N_0 = \text{lcm}(20, 5) = 20 \text{ samples}$$

**Signal is periodic with $N_0 = 20$.**

**(b)** Average power of $x(t) = 2\cos(100\pi t) + \sin(150\pi t)$:

$$\omega_1 = 100\pi \Rightarrow T_1 = \frac{1}{50} \text{ s}; \quad \omega_2 = 150\pi \Rightarrow T_2 = \frac{1}{75} \text{ s}$$

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{75}{50} = \frac{3}{2} \in \mathbb{Q} \Rightarrow T_0 = \frac{1}{25} \text{ s}$$

Power of each component (by orthogonality of sinusoids of different frequencies over their common period):

$$P_1 = \frac{2^2}{2} = 2 \text{ W}, \quad P_2 = \frac{1^2}{2} = 0.5 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{\text{total}} = P_1 + P_2 = 2.5 \text{ W}$$

**Energy:** Since $x(t)$ is periodic and non-zero, its energy is:

$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt = \infty$$

A periodic non-zero signal is a **power signal**, not an energy signal.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat mathematical conditions, explain the periodicity of continuous-time and discrete-time sinusoidal signals. Why does a discrete-time sinusoid sometimes fail to be periodic?

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the signal $x(n) = 2 + 3\cos(0.25\pi n + \pi/4) - 4\sin(0.5\pi n)$:

- (i) Test each component for periodicity.
- (ii) Find the fundamental period of $x(n)$ if periodic.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** A CT sinusoid $x(t) = A\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$ is periodic if $x(t) = x(t+T)$ for all $t$, giving $\omega_0 T = 2\pi k$, $k \in \mathbb{Z}$. The fundamental period is:

$$T_0 = \frac{2\pi}{\vert \omega_0 \vert}$$

A DT sinusoid $x(n) = A\cos(\omega_0 n + \phi)$ is periodic if $\omega_0 N = 2\pi k$ for some integer $N$. The smallest such $N$ is the fundamental period, which exists only if $\omega_0 / 2\pi \in \mathbb{Q}$.

**Why DT fails:** In CT, $t$ can be any real number, so $T$ is automatically a real number for any $\omega_0$. In DT, $n$ is restricted to integers, so $N$ must be an integer. If $\omega_0 / 2\pi$ is irrational, no integer $N$ can satisfy the condition, and the signal is **aperiodic**. For example, $x(n) = \cos(n)$ (with $\omega_0 = 1$ rad/sample) is aperiodic because $1/2\pi$ is irrational.

**(b)(i)** Component-wise analysis:

- **DC term $2$:** Constant; periodic with any period (treat $N_0 = 1$).
- **$3\cos(0.25\pi n + \pi/4)$:** $\dfrac{0.25\pi}{2\pi} = \dfrac{1}{8} \Rightarrow N_1 = 8$.
- **$-4\sin(0.5\pi n)$:** $\dfrac{0.5\pi}{2\pi} = \dfrac{1}{4} \Rightarrow N_2 = 4$.

**(b)(ii)** Common period:

$$N_0 = \text{lcm}(1, 8, 4) = 8 \text{ samples}$$

**The signal is periodic with fundamental period $N_0 = 8$.**

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in periodicity problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the rationality check:** Writing $T_0 = 2\pi/\omega_0$ for a DT sinusoid without checking if $\omega_0/2\pi$ is rational. The KTU board deducts **2-3 marks** for this.
> 2. **Confusing $T_0$ with $2\pi/\omega_0$ directly:** In DT, you must find the *smallest integer* $N$, not the real-valued $2\pi/\omega_0$.
> 3. **Treating DC as aperiodic:** A constant signal is periodic (with any period); the convention is to accept it as periodic.
> 4. **Missing the LCM step:** When the ratio of periods is *irrational*, students often compute an LCM anyway. Always check rationality *first*.
> 5. **Wrong power formula for periodic signals:** Use $P = \frac{1}{T_0}\int_0^{T_0}\vert x(t) \vert^2 dt$ (over *one* period), not over $[-\infty, \infty]$.
> 6. **Saying periodic signals have finite energy:** They have *infinite* energy and *finite* power — the exact opposite of aperiodic signals.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Classification of Periodic & Aperiodic Signals**
>
> - **CT periodic condition:** $x(t) = x(t + T_0)$ for all $t$; fundamental period $T_0$ is the smallest positive $T$ that works.
> - **DT periodic condition:** $x(n) = x(n + N_0)$ for all $n$; $N_0$ is the **smallest positive integer** that works.
> - **CT sinusoid** $\cos(\omega_0 t)$ is **always periodic** with $T_0 = 2\pi / \vert \omega_0 \vert$.
> - **DT sinusoid** $\cos(\omega_0 n)$ is periodic **iff** $\omega_0 / 2\pi \in \mathbb{Q}$.
> - **Sum of periodic signals** is periodic **iff** the ratio of their periods is **rational**; the period of the sum is the LCM.
> - **Periodic signal energy** $E = \infty$ (unless signal is identically zero); **Power** $P$ is finite and non-zero.
> - **Aperiodic signal energy** $E$ is finite; **Power** $P = 0$ (zero-mean) — they are **energy signals**.
> - **DC constant signals** are periodic (with $T_0 = $ any value, by convention taken as $\infty$).
> - **Decaying exponentials** $e^{-at} u(t)$, finite-duration pulses, and **DT sinusoids with irrational digital frequency** are classic aperiodic examples.
> - **KTU exam formula to memorize:** $T_0 = 2\pi / \vert \omega_0 \vert$ (CT), $N_0 = $ smallest integer $k$ with $k\omega_0 / 2\pi \in \mathbb{Z}$ (DT).
> - **Pitfall:** Never compute $N_0 = 2\pi / \omega_0$ as a decimal for DT signals — it must be an **integer**.

</br>
</br>
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Even and Odd Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBEVT604 - DIGITAL SIGNALS AND PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Even and Odd Signals # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) In the rigorous mathematical framework of **Signals and Systems**, signals are broadly classified by their symmetry properties with respect to the vertical (amplitude) axis. For any general signal $x(t)$ (continuous-time) or $x[n]$ (discrete-time), two fundamental symmetry classes govern signal behavior: **Even Signals** and **Odd Signals**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Continuous-Time):** > A continuous-time signal $x(t)$ is called an **Even Signal** if and only if it satisfies the identity: > $$x(t) = x(-t) \quad \text{for all values of } t \in \mathbb{R}$$ > A continuous-time signal $x(t)$ is called an **Odd Signal** if and only if: > $$x(t) = -x(-t) \quad \text{for all values of } t \in \mathbb{R}$$ For discrete-time signals, the corresponding conditions are $x[n] = x[-n]$ (even) and $x[n] = -x[-n]$ (odd), where $n \in \mathbb{Z}$. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition Think of an **even signal** like a **mirror reflection against a vertical mirror placed at $t = 0$**. If you stand at $t = +5$ and look at the amplitude, and then move to $t = -5$ and look at the amplitude again, both readings will be **identical**. The signal is perfectly symmetric about the y-axis. A perfect example is $\cos(t)$ — its value at $+2$ is exactly the same as its value at $-2$. An **odd signal** behaves like a **rotational symmetry of 180° about the origin**. The left half is a flipped, upside-down version of the right half. If the right side at $t = +3$ has a value of $+5$, then the left side at $t = -3$ will have a value of $-5$. A perfect example is $\sin(t)$ — at $+\pi/2$ it is $+1$, and at $-\pi/2$ it is $-1$. > [!NOTE] > **Quick Check Rule:** For any odd signal, the value at exactly $t = 0$ (or $n = 0$) MUST be zero, because substituting $t = 0$ into $x(t) = -x(-t)$ yields $x(0) = -x(0)$, which forces $x(0) = 0$. ## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Highlighted) - **Symmetry Axis:** The vertical axis ($t = 0$ or $n = 0$) for even signals, and the origin $(0,0)$ for odd signals. - **Mandatory Origin Value for Odd Signals:** $\mathbf{x(0) = 0}$ is a **strict necessary condition** — if this fails, the signal is NOT odd. - **Domain of Validity:** The symmetry property must hold for **ALL** $t$ (or $n$) in the signal's domain — a single violation disqualifies the signal from that classification. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Structured Logical Breakdown ### Step 1: Even Signal Characterization - An even signal exhibits **y-axis (vertical) symmetry**. - The amplitude profile on the right side of the y-axis is a perfect carbon copy of the left side. - The signal contains only **cosine-type harmonics** in its Fourier series expansion — sine components are mathematically forbidden. ### Step 2: Odd Signal Characterization - An odd signal exhibits **origin symmetry** (180° rotational symmetry). - The amplitude profile on the left side is the **negative mirror image** of the right side. - The Fourier series of a real odd signal contains only **sine harmonics** — cosine and DC (constant) components are forbidden. ### Step 3: Decomposition Principle (MOST HIGH-YIELD FOR KTU) - **Every** real-valued signal $x(t)$ — no matter how complex — can be uniquely decomposed into the **sum of an even part and an odd part**: $$x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$$ - The **even part** is extracted using the averaging filter: $$x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2}$$ - The **odd part** is extracted using the differencing filter: $$x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}$$ ### Step 4: Algebraic Closure Properties - Sum of two even signals → **Even**. - Sum of two odd signals → **Odd**. - Even $\times$ Even → **Even**. - Odd $\times$ Odd → **Even**. - Even $\times$ Odd → **Odd**. - Scaling an even signal by any constant → **Even**. - Scaling an odd signal by any constant → **Odd**. ### Step 5: Energy Decomposition Theorem - The total energy of any real signal equals the **sum of energies of its even and odd parts** (no cross-energy term because the cross-product of an even and odd signal is an odd function that integrates to zero over symmetric limits): $$E_{total} = E_{even} + E_{odd}$$ ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Property | Continuous-Time Formula | Discrete-Time Formula | Engineering Use | |---|----------|------------------------|------------------------|-----------------| | 1 | Even Condition | $x(t) = x(-t)$ | $x[n] = x[-n]$ | Symmetry check, Fourier cosine series | | 2 | Odd Condition | $x(t) = -x(-t)$ | $x[n] = -x[-n]$ | Symmetry check, Fourier sine series | | 3 | Even Part | $x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2}$ | $x_e[n] = \frac{x[n] + x[-n]}{2}$ | Signal decomposition | | 4 | Odd Part | $x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}$ | $x_o[n] = \frac{x[n] - x[-n]}{2}$ | Signal decomposition | | 5 | Odd Origin Value | $x(0) = 0$ | $x[0] = 0$ | Validity check | | 6 | Even Signal Energy | $E_e = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x_e(t) \vert^2 \, dt$ | $E_e = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x_e[n] \vert^2$ | Power/Energy analysis | | 7 | Odd Signal Energy | $E_o = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x_o(t) \vert^2 \, dt$ | $E_o = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x_o[n] \vert^2$ | Power/Energy analysis | | 8 | Total Energy | $E = E_e + E_o$ | $E = E_e + E_o$ | Orthogonality theorem | | 9 | Even Integral | $\int_{-a}^{a} x_e(t) \, dt = 2 \int_{0}^{a} x_e(t) \, dt$ | $\sum_{n=-N}^{N} x_e[n] = 2 \sum_{n=0}^{N} x_e[n]$ | Quick area calculation | | 10 | Odd Integral | $\int_{-a}^{a} x_o(t) \, dt = 0$ | $\sum_{n=-N}^{N} x_o[n] = 0$ | Quick area calculation | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Fourier Series Analysis:** Knowing whether a signal is even or odd allows engineers to **immediately conclude** that either cosine (for even) or sine (for odd) coefficients will be zero — saving massive computation time. - **Filter Design:** Symmetric FIR filters (Linear Phase) are constructed by exploiting even/odd symmetry in impulse response. - **Communication Systems:** Real-valued even signals have **real and even** Fourier transforms; real-valued odd signals have **purely imaginary and odd** Fourier transforms — this duality is the backbone of modulation theory. - **Biomedical Signals (ECG/EEG):** Decomposition into even/odd parts helps isolate symmetric features (like R-wave peaks) from antisymmetric features. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Why Any Signal Decomposes into Even + Odd **Starting Hypothesis:** Let $x(t)$ be **any arbitrary real signal** with no pre-assumed symmetry. We claim that it can be uniquely written as: $$x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$$ where $x_e(t)$ is the even part and $x_o(t)$ is the odd part. **Step 1 — Apply the time-reversal transformation** to both sides. Replace $t$ with $-t$: $$x(-t) = x_e(-t) + x_o(-t)$$ **Step 2 — Apply the definition of even and odd functions:** - Since $x_e$ is even: $x_e(-t) = x_e(t)$ - Since $x_o$ is odd: $x_o(-t) = -x_o(t)$ Substituting these into Step 1: $$x(-t) = x_e(t) - x_o(t)$$ **Step 3 — Form a system of two linear equations:** Equation (A): $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$ Equation (B): $x(-t) = x_e(t) - x_o(t)$ **Step 4 — Add equations (A) and (B) to eliminate $x_o(t)$:** $$ \begin{aligned} x(t) + x(-t) &= \bigl[x_e(t) + x_o(t)\bigr] + \bigl[x_e(t) - x_o(t)\bigr] \\ x(t) + x(-t) &= 2\,x_e(t) + 0 \\ x_e(t) &= \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2} \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Subtract equation (B) from equation (A) to eliminate $x_e(t)$:** $$ \begin{aligned} x(t) - x(-t) &= \bigl[x_e(t) + x_o(t)\bigr] - \bigl[x_e(t) - x_o(t)\bigr] \\ x(t) - x(-t) &= 0 + 2\,x_o(t) \\ x_o(t) &= \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2} \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Model Solution Pattern) **Problem:** Given the discrete-time signal $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ defined for $n = -2, -1, 0, 1, 2$. Find its even and odd decomposition. **Step 1 — Write the signal in indexed form:** $$ \begin{aligned} x[-2] &= 1, \quad x[-1] = 2, \quad x[0] = 3, \quad x[1] = 4, \quad x[2] = 5 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Construct the time-reversed signal $x[-n]$:** $$ \begin{aligned} x[2] &= 5, \quad x[1] = 4, \quad x[0] = 3, \quad x[-1] = 2, \quad x[-2] = 1 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Apply the even part formula $x_e[n] = \frac{x[n] + x[-n]}{2}$:** $$ \begin{aligned} x_e[-2] &= \frac{x[-2] + x[2]}{2} = \frac{1 + 5}{2} = 3 \\ x_e[-1] &= \frac{x[-1] + x[1]}{2} = \frac{2 + 4}{2} = 3 \\ x_e[0] &= \frac{x[0] + x[0]}{2} = \frac{3 + 3}{2} = 3 \\ x_e[1] &= \frac{x[1] + x[-1]}{2} = \frac{4 + 2}{2} = 3 \\ x_e[2] &= \frac{x[2] + x[-2]}{2} = \frac{5 + 1}{2} = 3 \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** $x_e[n] = \{3, 3, 3, 3, 3\}$ — a perfect constant (which is trivially even). ✓ **Step 4 — Apply the odd part formula $x_o[n] = \frac{x[n] - x[-n]}{2}$:** $$ \begin{aligned} x_o[-2] &= \frac{x[-2] - x[2]}{2} = \frac{1 - 5}{2} = -2 \\ x_o[-1] &= \frac{x[-1] - x[1]}{2} = \frac{2 - 4}{2} = -1 \\ x_o[0] &= \frac{x[0] - x[0]}{2} = \frac{3 - 3}{2} = 0 \\ x_o[1] &= \frac{x[1] - x[-1]}{2} = \frac{4 - 2}{2} = 1 \\ x_o[2] &= \frac{x[2] - x[-2]}{2} = \frac{5 - 1}{2} = 2 \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** $x_o[n] = \{-2, -1, 0, 1, 2\}$ — perfectly odd (note $x_o[0] = 0$). ✓ **Step 5 — Verify the reconstruction $x[n] = x_e[n] + x_o[n]$:** $$ \begin{aligned} x[-2] &= 3 + (-2) = 1 \; \checkmark \\ x[-1] &= 3 + (-1) = 2 \; \checkmark \\ x[0] &= 3 + 0 = 3 \; \checkmark \\ x[1] &= 3 + 1 = 4 \; \checkmark \\ x[2] &= 3 + 2 = 5 \; \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ All values match the original signal. **Decomposition verified.** ## 3.3 Analytical Verification — Energy Orthogonality **Theorem:** The even and odd parts of a real signal are orthogonal over symmetric limits. **Proof:** $$ \begin{aligned} \int_{-a}^{a} x_e(t) \cdot x_o(t) \, dt &= \int_{-a}^{a} \left[\frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2}\right] \cdot \left[\frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}\right] dt \\ &= \frac{1}{4} \int_{-a}^{a} \bigl[x(t) + x(-t)\bigr]\bigl[x(t) - x(-t)\bigr] \, dt \\ &= \frac{1}{4} \int_{-a}^{a} \bigl[x^2(t) - x^2(-t)\bigr] \, dt \end{aligned} $$ Now apply the substitution $u = -t$ in the second term. Since $du = -dt$ and the limits flip: $$ \begin{aligned} \int_{-a}^{a} x^2(-t) \, dt &= \int_{a}^{-a} x^2(u) \cdot (-du) = \int_{-a}^{a} x^2(u) \, du \end{aligned} $$ Therefore the two terms are equal, and the difference is **zero**: $$\int_{-a}^{a} x_e(t) \cdot x_o(t) \, dt = 0 \quad \blacksquare$$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Signal Classification Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Input Signal x(t)"] --> B{"Check Symmetry
x(t) vs x(-t)"} B -- "x(t) equals x(-t)
for ALL t" --> C["CLASSIFY AS EVEN
x(t) = x(-t)"] B -- "x(t) equals -x(-t)
for ALL t" --> D["CLASSIFY AS ODD
x(t) = -x(-t)"] B -- "Neither condition
holds" --> E["NEITHER EVEN NOR ODD
Needs Decomposition"] C --> F["Example: cos t,
t squared, constant"] D --> G["Example: sin t,
t cubed, sign t"] E --> H["Compute Even Part
xe = x t + x -t over 2"] E --> I["Compute Odd Part
xo = x t - x -t over 2"] H --> J["Verify xe is even"] I --> K["Verify xo is odd"] J --> L["Reconstruction:
x(t) = xe(t) + xo(t)"] K --> L ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of Even/Odd Decomposition Engine ```mermaid flowchart LR inp["Input Signal
x(t)"] --> d1 inp --> d2 d1["Time-Reversal
Block: x(-t)"] --> s1 d1["Time-Reversal
Block: x(-t)"] --> s2 s1["SUMMER
x(t) + x(-t)"] --> div1 s2["SUBTRACTOR
x(t) - x(-t)"] --> div2 div1["Gain: 1/2
x_e(t)"] --> out1 div2["Gain: 1/2
x_o(t)"] --> out2 out1["Even Part
Output"] out2["Odd Part
Output"] out1 --> sum out2 --> sum sum["Reconstruction
Adder"] --> verify["Verify:
