**Step 6:** Include the unit step for causality.

$$\boxed{h(t) = \left( e^{-2t} - e^{-3t} \right) u(t)}$$

**Step 7: BIBO Stability Check.**

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \vert h(t) \vert \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} \left( e^{-2t} - e^{-3t} \right) dt = \left[ -\frac{e^{-2t}}{2} + \frac{e^{-3t}}{3} \right]_0^{\infty} = 0 - \left( -\frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{3} \right) = \frac{1}{6}$$

Since the integral is **finite** (= $\frac{1}{6} < \infty$), the system is **BIBO STABLE** ✓

---

**(b)** [KTU University Exam - May 2023] **(7 marks)**
Two discrete-time LTI systems with impulse responses $h_1[n] = \delta[n] + 2\delta[n-1]$ and $h_2[n] = \delta[n] - \delta[n-1]$ are connected in **parallel**. Find the **overall impulse response** and verify whether the combined system is BIBO stable.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Parallel connection rule — impulse responses **add directly**.

$$h[n] = h_1[n] + h_2[n] = \left( \delta[n] + 2\delta[n-1] \right) + \left( \delta[n] - \delta[n-1] \right)$$

**Step 2:** Simplify.

$$h[n] = 2\delta[n] + \delta[n-1]$$

**Step 3:** BIBO Stability Check using absolute summability.

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert = \vert 2 \vert + \vert 1 \vert = 3 < \infty$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{h[n] = 2\delta[n] + \delta[n-1], \quad \text{BIBO Stable (sum} = 3 < \infty\text{)}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the unit step $u(t)$:** Many students omit the $u(t)$ in causal impulse responses. This makes the system **non-causal** by default and will cost **2 marks**.
> 2. **Conflating "linear" with "time-invariant":** Linearity ≠ Time Invariance. A system can be linear but time-varying (e.g., $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$). Mark explicitly whether *each* property is satisfied.
> 3. **Misapplying convolution limits:** In discrete convolution $y[n] = \sum_{k} x[k] h[n-k]$, ensure you sum over the **non-zero region** of the input, not blindly from $-\infty$ to $\infty$. A sign or index slip here loses **3-4 marks**.
> 4. **Stability Test Confusion:** Always check **absolute** integrability/summability, not just the integral/sum itself. A divergent alternating sum like $\sum (-1)^n$ is **not** absolutely summable.
> 5. **Commutativity Mistake:** While convolution is commutative ($A * B = B * A$), do **not** claim $h_1[n] + h_2[n] = h_1[n] \cdot h_2[n]$ — this is a common hallucination under exam pressure.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 (CT & DT Systems):**

- 🔹 A **system** is a mathematical transformation $\mathcal{T}$ mapping input signal to output signal.
- 🔹 **CT systems** deal with $x(t)$ for $t \in \mathbb{R}$; **DT systems** deal with $x[n]$ for $n \in \mathbb{Z}$.
- 🔹 **LTI Systems** = **Linear** + **Time-Invariant**. They are characterized entirely by their **impulse response** $h(t)$ or $h[n]$.
- 🔹 **Linearity test:** apply $ax_1 + bx_2$ as input; check whether output equals $ay_1 + by_2$ (with **no extra constants**).
- 🔹 **Time-Invariance test:** shift input by $t_0$ (or $n_0$); check whether output shifts by the **same amount**.
- 🔹 **Causality:** output must not depend on **future** inputs.
- 🔹 **Memory:** static = no delay terms; dynamic = contains $x(t-1)$, $\frac{dx}{dt}$, or integrals.
- 🔹 **BIBO Stability (CT):** $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(t) \vert \, dt < \infty$. **BIBO Stability (DT):** $\sum \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$.
- 🔹 **Convolution Integral (CT):** $y(t) = \int x(\tau) h(t - \tau) d\tau$.
- 🔹 **Convolution Sum (DT):** $y[n] = \sum_{k} x[k] h[n - k]$.
- 🔹 **Cascade connection:** $h_{eq} = h_1 * h_2$ (convolution).
- 🔹 **Parallel connection:** $h_{eq} = h_1 + h_2$ (addition).
- 🔹 **Convolution properties** to remember: Commutative, Associative, Distributive over addition.
- 🔹 **Output length of discrete convolution:** $N_y = N_x + N_h - 1$.
- 🔹 Always specify the **unit step $u(t)$ or $u[n]$** when writing causal impulse responses.
- 🔹 KTU exams often combine **classification + convolution** in a single 14-mark question — practice both together.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties - Linearity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT604 - DIGITAL SIGNALS AND PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Properties - Linearity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Linearity** is the most fundamental property of a **Discrete-Time System (DTS)**. A system $T\{\cdot\}$ is said to be **linear** if and only if it obeys the **Principle of Superposition**, which is the combination of two sub-properties: **Additivity** and **Homogeneity (Scaling)**.

A system operator $T$ is linear if for any arbitrary input signals $x_1[n]$ and $x_2[n]$, and arbitrary scalar constants $a$ and $b$:

$$T\{a \cdot x_1[n] + b \cdot x_2[n]\} = a \cdot T\{x_1[n]\} + b \cdot T\{x_2[n]\}$$

Where:
- $T\{x[n]\} = y[n]$ is the system's response (output) to input $x[n]$.
- $a$ and $b$ are **complex-valued** (in general) or real constants.

### 1.2 Two Sub-Properties of Linearity

> [!NOTE]
> **Property A — Additivity (Zero-One Test):**
> The response to the sum of two inputs must equal the sum of the individual responses.
> $$T\{x_1[n] + x_2[n]\} = T\{x_1[n]\} + T\{x_2[n]\}$$
> This is obtained by setting $a = b = 1$ in the linearity equation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Property B — Homogeneity / Scaling Property:**
> The response to a scaled input must equal the scaled version of the response.
> $$T\{a \cdot x[n]\} = a \cdot T\{x[n]\}$$
> This is obtained by setting $b = 0$ and renaming $x_1[n]$ to $x[n]$.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Tax Calculator"**
> Imagine a tax calculator system. If your salary is **₹1,00,000**, the tax is **₹10,000** (10% flat). If your salary doubles to **₹2,00,000**, the tax should also double to **₹20,000**. This is a **linear system** — it scales gracefully.
>
> Now consider a different tax rule: "Everyone pays a fixed **₹5,000** tax regardless of salary." A person earning ₹1,00,000 pays ₹5,000, but a person earning ₹2,00,000 also pays ₹5,000. The output is **not proportional** to the input — this is a **non-linear system** (specifically, fails homogeneity due to a constant offset, called the **DC bias**).
>
> **Linearity = "The system has no 'favorites' or 'special offsets' — it treats every signal fairly and proportionally."**

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Special Test Cases

- **Zero Input Response (ZIR):** A linear system with zero input must produce **exactly zero output**.
$$T\{0\} = 0$$
If a system produces a non-zero output when input is zero, the system is **definitely non-linear**.

- **DC Gain (Static Linearity):** For a constant input $x[n] = C$, the output $y[n]$ must also be a scaled version of $C$ (i.e., $y[n] = K \cdot C$ for some constant $K$).

- **Unit Step Response Test:** If the response to $u[n]$ is $s[n]$, then the response to $K \cdot u[n]$ must be exactly $K \cdot s[n]$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — How to Test a System for Linearity

To determine whether a Discrete-Time System $y[n] = T\{x[n]\}$ is linear, follow this structured four-step procedure:

1. **Step 1 — Define two arbitrary inputs:** Choose $x_1[n]$ and $x_2[n]$ (symbolic, not numerical).
2. **Step 2 — Find individual responses:** Compute $y_1[n] = T\{x_1[n]\}$ and $y_2[n] = T\{x_2[n]\}$.
3. **Step 3 — Find the combined input response:** Compute $y_3[n] = T\{a \cdot x_1[n] + b \cdot x_2[n]\}$.
4. **Step 4 — Construct the scaled sum:** Compute $y_4[n] = a \cdot y_1[n] + b \cdot y_2[n]$.
5. **Step 5 — Compare:** If $y_3[n] \equiv y_4[n]$ for all $n$, the system is **linear**. Otherwise, **non-linear**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Reference Table for Linearity Testing**

| Test Condition | Mathematical Formulation | Result |
|---|---|---|
| Full Linearity Test | $T\{a x_1 + b x_2\} = a T\{x_1\} + b T\{x_2\}$ | **LINEAR** if true |
| Additivity Only | $T\{x_1 + x_2\} = T\{x_1\} + T\{x_2\}$ | Necessary condition |
| Homogeneity Only | $T\{a \cdot x\} = a \cdot T\{x\}$ | Necessary condition |
| Zero Input Test | $T\{0\} = 0$ | Must hold for linearity |
| DC Test (constant $C$) | $T\{C\} = C \cdot T\{1\}$ | Must hold for linearity |
| Squaring Operation | $y[n] = (x[n])^2$ | **NON-LINEAR** |
| Magnitude Operation | $y[n] = \vert x[n] \vert$ | **NON-LINEAR** |
| Constant Offset | $y[n] = x[n] + C$, $C \neq 0$ | **NON-LINEAR** (additive bias) |
| Time Scaling | $y[n] = x[Mn]$ or $y[n] = x[n/K]$ | **LINEAR** (memory allowed) |
| Modulation | $y[n] = x[n] \cdot \cos(\omega_0 n)$ | **LINEAR** (in $x[n]$) |
| Squared Modulation | $y[n] = (x[n] \cdot \cos(\omega_0 n))^2$ | **NON-LINEAR** |
| Differentiation | $y[n] = x[n] - x[n-1]$ | **LINEAR** |
| Accumulator | $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x[k]$ | **LINEAR** |
| Logarithmic | $y[n] = \log(x[n])$ | **NON-LINEAR** |
| Squared Input + Linear | $y[n] = x^2[n] + x[n]$ | **NON-LINEAR** (squared term) |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU Notation Rule:** The absolute value $\vert x[n] \vert$ uses `\vert` (NOT the pipe `\|`) so it does not break the markdown table syntax.

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Linearity

Linearity is the cornerstone property because:

- **It enables Superposition** — Complex inputs can be decomposed into simpler components (e.g., impulses, sinusoids), processed individually, and the results summed. This is the basis of **LTI system analysis using impulse response** and **Fourier/Z-transforms**.
- **It enables Frequency-Domain Analysis** — Only linear systems can be characterized by their frequency response $H(e^{j\omega})$.
- **It enables Convolution** — The output is the convolution of input with impulse response: $y[n] = x[n] * h[n]$.
- **It enables Eigen-decomposition** — Sinusoids are eigenfunctions of linear systems.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio Processing (Equalizers, Mixers):** Linear mixers preserve the original sound without distortion.
- **Communication Systems:** Linear modulation/demodulation preserves the message signal's information content.
- **Image Processing:** Linear filters (Gaussian, Sobel) preserve brightness linearity.
- **Control Systems:** Linear time-invariant (LTI) models form the backbone of classical control theory.
- **Radar & Sonar:** Matched filtering and beamforming rely on linear superposition of wavefronts.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Proof — Equivalence of Linearity, Additivity, and Homogeneity

> **Objective:** Show that the full linearity condition $T\{a x_1 + b x_2\} = a T\{x_1\} + b T\{x_2\}$ is mathematically equivalent to the conjunction of additivity and homogeneity.

**Step 1:** Assume the full linearity property holds for all $a, b, x_1, x_2$.

Set $a = b = 1$:

$$T\{1 \cdot x_1[n] + 1 \cdot x_2[n]\} = 1 \cdot T\{x_1[n]\} + 1 \cdot T\{x_2[n]\}$$

$$\therefore T\{x_1[n] + x_2[n]\} = T\{x_1[n]\} + T\{x_2[n]\}$$

This is **Additivity**. (QED for one direction)

**Step 2:** Set $b = 0$ and rename $x_1 \to x$:

$$T\{a \cdot x[n] + 0\} = a \cdot T\{x[n]\} + 0$$

$$T\{a \cdot x[n]\} = a \cdot T\{x[n]\}$$

This is **Homogeneity**. (QED for one direction)

**Step 3 (Reverse):** Assume both additivity and homogeneity hold. Prove full linearity.

By additivity applied to the sum $(a x_1 + b x_2)$ and $(0)$:

$$T\{a x_1[n] + b x_2[n] + 0\} = T\{a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]\} + T\{0\}$$

Since a linear system must satisfy $T\{0\} = 0$ (proven by setting $a=0$ in homogeneity):

$$T\{a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]\} = T\{a x_1[n]\} + T\{b x_2[n]\}$$

Now applying homogeneity to each term:

$$T\{a x_1[n]\} + T\{b x_2[n]\} = a \cdot T\{x_1[n]\} + b \cdot T\{x_2[n]\}$$

$$\boxed{\therefore T\{a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]\} = a \cdot T\{x_1[n]\} + b \cdot T\{x_2[n]\}}$$

This completes the **biconditional proof**. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Linear System Verification

> **Problem:** Test the system $y[n] = 2 x[n] - 3 x[n-1] + x[n-2]$ for linearity.

**Step 1 — Define two arbitrary inputs:** $x_1[n]$ and $x_2[n]$.

**Step 2 — Individual responses:**

$$y_1[n] = T\{x_1[n]\} = 2 x_1[n] - 3 x_1[n-1] + x_1[n-2]$$

$$y_2[n] = T\{x_2[n]\} = 2 x_2[n] - 3 x_2[n-1] + x_2[n-2]$$

**Step 3 — Combined input response:**
Let $x_3[n] = a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]$. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_3[n] &= T\{a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]\} \\
&= 2(a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]) - 3(a x_1[n-1] + b x_2[n-1]) + (a x_1[n-2] + b x_2[n-2]) \\
&= a(2 x_1[n] - 3 x_1[n-1] + x_1[n-2]) + b(2 x_2[n] - 3 x_2[n-1] + x_2[n-2]) \\
&= a \cdot y_1[n] + b \cdot y_2[n]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare:** $y_3[n] = a y_1[n] + b y_2[n]$. ✓

**Conclusion:** The system is **LINEAR**.

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Non-Linear System Identification

> **Problem:** Test the system $y[n] = x[n]^2 + 5$ for linearity.

**Step 1 — Define:** $x_1[n]$, $x_2[n]$, constants $a, b$.

**Step 2 — Individual responses:**

$$y_1[n] = (x_1[n])^2 + 5$$

$$y_2[n] = (x_2[n])^2 + 5$$

**Step 3 — Combined input response:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_3[n] &= T\{a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]\} \\
&= (a x_1[n] + b x_2[n])^2 + 5 \\
&= a^2 (x_1[n])^2 + 2ab \cdot x_1[n] x_2[n] + b^2 (x_2[n])^2 + 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Scaled sum of individual responses:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a y_1[n] + b y_2[n] &= a((x_1[n])^2 + 5) + b((x_2[n])^2 + 5) \\
&= a (x_1[n])^2 + b (x_2[n])^2 + 5(a + b)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare:** $y_3[n] \neq a y_1[n] + b y_2[n]$ because:
- $y_3$ contains a **cross-term** $2ab \cdot x_1[n] x_2[n]$ (missing in $a y_1 + b y_2$).
- $y_3$ has constant $+5$, while $a y_1 + b y_2$ has constant $5(a+b)$ (not equal in general).

**Conclusion:** The system is **NON-LINEAR**.

### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Subtle Non-Linearity (Time-Varying but Linear)

> **Problem:** Test $y[n] = n \cdot x[n]$ for linearity.

**Step 1 — Combined input response:**

$$y_3[n] = n \cdot (a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]) = a n x_1[n] + b n x_2[n]$$

**Step 2 — Scaled sum:**

$$a y_1[n] + b y_2[n] = a (n x_1[n]) + b (n x_2[n]) = a n x_1[n] + b n x_2[n]$$

**Step 3 — Compare:** $y_3[n] = a y_1[n] + b y_2[n]$ ✓

**Conclusion:** The system is **LINEAR** (though it is *time-varying*, linearity still holds because the multiplier $n$ is independent of the input signal $x[n]$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Linearity is determined **solely by the input-output relationship**, not by whether the system is time-invariant or not. A system can be linear yet time-varying.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick Classification Table

| System Equation | Linearity | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| $y[n] = K \cdot x[n]$ | **LINEAR** | Pure scaling |
| $y[n] = x[n-n_0]$ | **LINEAR** | Pure delay |
| $y[n] = x[n] + x[n-1]$ | **LINEAR** | Sum of shifted inputs |
| $y[n] = n \cdot x[n]$ | **LINEAR** | Modulation by $n$ (input-independent) |
| $y[n] = x[Mn]$ | **LINEAR** | Time scaling (downsampling-like) |
| $y[n] = x[n] + 7$ | **NON-LINEAR** | Constant offset violates $T\{0\}=0$ |
| $y[n] = (x[n])^2$ | **NON-LINEAR** | Squaring creates cross-terms |
| $y[n] = \sin(x[n])$ | **NON-LINEAR** | Taylor series has powers $\geq 2$ |
| $y[n] = \vert x[n] \vert$ | **NON-LINEAR** | Subadditivity |
| $y[n] = x[n] \cdot x[n-1]$ | **NON-LINEAR** | Product of two signals |
| $y[n] = e^{x[n]}$ | **NON-LINEAR** | Exponential nonlinearity |
| $y[n] = \max(x[n], 0)$ | **NON-LINEAR** | Rectifier (half-wave) |
| $y[n] = \text{sign}(x[n])$ | **NON-LINEAR** | Hard limiter |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — The Linearity Test Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Define x1 n and x2 n as arbitrary inputs"] --> nodeB["Find y1 n = T of x1 n"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Find y2 n = T of x2 n"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["Compute y3 n = T of a x1 + b x2"]
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE["Compute y4 n = a y1 + b y2"]
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF{"Does y3 n equal y4 n for all n?"}
    nodeF --> nodeG["YES System is LINEAR"]
    nodeF --> nodeH["NO System is NON LINEAR"]
    nodeG --> nodeI["Note: System may be LTI or Linear Time Varying"]
    nodeH --> nodeJ["Identify which property is violated: Additivity or Homogeneity"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Classification of Common Systems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeIn["Input x n"] --> nodeSys["Discrete Time System T"]
    nodeSys --> nodeOut["Output y n"]

    nodeSys -.-> nodeLin["LINEAR SYSTEMS"]
    nodeSys -.-> nodeNon["NON LINEAR SYSTEMS"]

    nodeLin --> nodeL1["y = K x n"]
    nodeLin --> nodeL2["y = x n - x n-1"]
    nodeLin --> nodeL3["y = n x n"]
    nodeLin --> nodeL4["y = sum of x k"]

    nodeNon --> nodeN1["y = x squared n"]
    nodeNon --> nodeN2["y = x n + C"]
    nodeNon --> nodeN3["y = absolute x n"]
    nodeNon --> nodeN4["y = sin x n"]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Superposition Principle Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS["x1 n"] --> nodeM1["Scaler a"]
    nodeT["x2 n"] --> nodeM2["Scaler b"]
    nodeM1 --> nodeADD1["Adder"]
    nodeM2 --> nodeADD1
    nodeADD1 --> nodeSYS["Linear System T"]
    nodeSYS --> nodeY3["y3 n"]

    nodeS --> nodeSYS1["System T"]
    nodeT --> nodeSYS2["System T"]
    nodeSYS1 --> nodeSC1["Scaler a"]
    nodeSYS2 --> nodeSC2["Scaler b"]
    nodeSC1 --> nodeADD2["Adder"]
    nodeSC2 --> nodeADD2
    nodeADD2 --> nodeY4["y4 n"]

    nodeY3 --> nodeCOMP["Equality Check"]
    nodeY4 --> nodeCOMP
    nodeCOMP --> nodeRES["Result: y3 n = y4 n confirms LINEARITY"]
```

### 4.4 Architecture Flow — Why Linearity Matters in DSP Pipelines

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Analog Input Signal"] --> nodeB["Anti Aliasing Filter"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["ADC Analog to Digital Converter"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Digital Signal Processor Core"]
    nodeD --> nodeE1["Linear Block: FIR Filter"]
    nodeD --> nodeE2["Linear Block: FFT Analysis"]
    nodeD --> nodeE3["Linear Block: Convolution"]
    nodeD --> nodeE4["NONLINEAR Block: Quantization"]
    nodeD --> nodeE5["NONLINEAR Block: Clipping Limiter"]
    nodeE1 --> nodeF["Output Reconstruction"]
    nodeE2 --> nodeF
    nodeE3 --> nodeF
    nodeE4 --> nodeF
    nodeE5 --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG["DAC Digital to Analog Converter"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Reconstruction Filter"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Processed Analog Output"]

    nodeE4 -.-> nodeW["Quantization introduces nonlinear distortion bounded by LSB step"]
    nodeE5 -.-> nodeW2["Clipping creates harmonics violating superposition theorem"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Define linearity of a discrete-time system. State the two conditions a system must satisfy to be linear.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Linearity** is a property of a discrete-time system $T\{\cdot\}$ by which it obeys the **Principle of Superposition**.

A system $T$ is linear if and only if it satisfies both of the following conditions for any two inputs $x_1[n]$, $x_2[n]$ and arbitrary constants $a, b$:

1. **Additivity:** $T\{x_1[n] + x_2[n]\} = T\{x_1[n]\} + T\{x_2[n]\}$
2. **Homogeneity (Scaling):** $T\{a \cdot x[n]\} = a \cdot T\{x[n]\}$

Combined: $T\{a \cdot x_1[n] + b \cdot x_2[n]\} = a \cdot T\{x_1[n]\} + b \cdot T\{x_2[n]\}$.

A necessary consequence is the **zero-input condition** $T\{0\} = 0$.

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Q:** Check whether the system $y[n] = \cos(x[n])$ is linear. Justify.

**Model Answer:**

Let $x_1[n]$ and $x_2[n]$ be two inputs, and let $x_3[n] = a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]$.

**Step 1 — Individual responses:**

$$y_1[n] = \cos(x_1[n]), \quad y_2[n] = \cos(x_2[n])$$

**Step 2 — Combined input response:**

$$y_3[n] = \cos(a x_1[n] + b x_2[n])$$

**Step 3 — Scaled sum:**

$$a y_1[n] + b y_2[n] = a \cos(x_1[n]) + b \cos(x_2[n])$$

**Step 4 — Compare:** In general, $\cos(a x_1 + b x_2) \neq a \cos(x_1) + b \cos(x_2)$.

**Conclusion:** The system is **NON-LINEAR**. (The cosine Taylor expansion has squared and higher-order terms, violating superposition.)

---

### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define linearity and superposition. Check the linearity of the system:
$$y[n] = x^2[n] + 3 x[n-1] - 5$$

**(b) [7 Marks]** Check the linearity of the discrete-time system defined by:
$$y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x[k]$$

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Definition:**
A system is **linear** if it satisfies the **superposition principle**, which is the combination of **additivity** and **homogeneity**:
$$T\{a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]\} = a T\{x_1[n]\} + b T\{x_2[n]\}$$

**Step 2 — Apply to the given system:**

Let $x_3[n] = a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_3[n] &= T\{x_3[n]\} = (x_3[n])^2 + 3 x_3[n-1] - 5 \\
&= (a x_1[n] + b x_2[n])^2 + 3(a x_1[n-1] + b x_2[n-1]) - 5 \\
&= a^2 (x_1[n])^2 + 2ab \, x_1[n] x_2[n] + b^2 (x_2[n])^2 + 3a x_1[n-1] + 3b x_2[n-1] - 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute scaled sum of individual responses:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a y_1[n] + b y_2[n] &= a\big(x_1^2[n] + 3 x_1[n-1] - 5\big) + b\big(x_2^2[n] + 3 x_2[n-1] - 5\big) \\
&= a (x_1[n])^2 + 3a x_1[n-1] - 5a + b (x_2[n])^2 + 3b x_2[n-1] - 5b
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Comparison:**

| Term | In $y_3[n]$ | In $a y_1 + b y_2$ |
|---|---|---|
| $(x_1)^2$ coefficient | $a^2$ | $a$ |
| $x_1 x_2$ cross-term | $2ab$ | $0$ (absent) |
| $(x_2)^2$ coefficient | $b^2$ | $b$ |
| Constant | $-5$ | $-5(a+b)$ |

Clearly $y_3[n] \neq a y_1[n] + b y_2[n]$ in general. For instance, set $x_1[n]=x_2[n]=0$:

$$y_3[0] = -5, \quad a y_1[0] + b y_2[0] = -5(a+b)$$

These are unequal unless $a+b=1$.

**Conclusion:** The system is **NON-LINEAR**. The constant term $-5$ violates the zero-input test $T\{0\}=0$, and the squaring operation introduces cross-terms that violate additivity.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Individual responses:**

$$y_1[n] = T\{x_1[n]\} = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x_1[k]$$

$$y_2[n] = T\{x_2[n]\} = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x_2[k]$$

**Step 2 — Combined input response:** Let $x_3[n] = a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_3[n] &= T\{a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]\} = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} \big(a x_1[k] + b x_2[k]\big) \\
&= a \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x_1[k] + b \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x_2[k] \\
&= a y_1[n] + b y_2[n]
\end{aligned}
$$

(Here we used the linearity property of finite/infinite summation: $\sum (a \alpha_k + b \beta_k) = a \sum \alpha_k + b \sum \beta_k$.)

**Step 3 — Conclusion:** Since $y_3[n] = a y_1[n] + b y_2[n]$, the system satisfies superposition and is therefore **LINEAR**. (Note: This accumulator system is also time-invariant, hence LTI.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a suitable example, explain how a constant offset makes a system non-linear.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Test the linearity of the systems:
   (i) $y[n] = x[n] \cdot \cos(\omega_0 n)$
   (ii) $y[n] = (x[n])^2 \cdot \cos(\omega_0 n)$

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Theoretical Background:**
A constant offset (also called a DC bias) refers to adding a fixed value $C$ to the output, independent of the input. Such a system is of the form:
$$y[n] = T\{x[n]\} = f(x[n]) + C, \quad C \neq 0$$

**Example:** Consider the system $y[n] = x[n] + 5$.

**Step 1 — Test zero-input response:**
For $x[n] = 0$: $\quad y[n] = 0 + 5 = 5 \neq 0$.

This violates the **mandatory zero-input condition** for linear systems. Hence the system is non-linear.

**Step 2 — Direct superposition test:**

Let $x_3[n] = a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]$. Then:

$$y_3[n] = a x_1[n] + b x_2[n] + 5$$

$$a y_1[n] + b y_2[n] = a(x_1[n] + 5) + b(x_2[n] + 5) = a x_1[n] + b x_2[n] + 5(a+b)$$

For linearity, we need $y_3 = a y_1 + b y_2$, which requires $5 = 5(a+b)$, i.e., $a+b=1$. This must hold for **all** $a, b$ — clearly false.

**Conclusion:** Any system with a non-zero constant offset is non-linear, because it violates the homogeneity property.

**Real-world implication:** This is why DC blocking filters and AC coupling are essential in audio amplifiers — a constant offset introduces distortion and biases the operating point.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) System: $y[n] = x[n] \cdot \cos(\omega_0 n)$**

**Step 1 — Individual responses:**

$$y_1[n] = x_1[n] \cos(\omega_0 n), \quad y_2[n] = x_2[n] \cos(\omega_0 n)$$

**Step 2 — Combined input response:** $x_3[n] = a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]$.

$$y_3[n] = (a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]) \cos(\omega_0 n) = a x_1[n] \cos(\omega_0 n) + b x_2[n] \cos(\omega_0 n)$$

**Step 3 — Scaled sum:**

$$a y_1[n] + b y_2[n] = a x_1[n] \cos(\omega_0 n) + b x_2[n] \cos(\omega_0 n)$$

**Step 4 — Compare:** $y_3[n] = a y_1[n] + b y_2[n]$ ✓

**Conclusion:** The system is **LINEAR**. (The multiplier $\cos(\omega_0 n)$ is a *deterministic* time-varying coefficient, independent of the input signal $x[n]$. The system is **linear but time-varying**.)

**(ii) System: $y[n] = (x[n])^2 \cdot \cos(\omega_0 n)$**

**Step 1 — Combined input response:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_3[n] &= (a x_1[n] + b x_2[n])^2 \cos(\omega_0 n) \\
&= \big(a^2 x_1^2[n] + 2ab \, x_1[n] x_2[n] + b^2 x_2^2[n]\big) \cos(\omega_0 n)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Scaled sum:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a y_1[n] + b y_2[n] &= a (x_1[n])^2 \cos(\omega_0 n) + b (x_2[n])^2 \cos(\omega_0 n) \\
&= \big(a x_1^2[n] + b x_2^2[n]\big) \cos(\omega_0 n)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare:** $y_3[n]$ contains a **cross-term** $2ab \, x_1[n] x_2[n] \cos(\omega_0 n)$ that is absent in $a y_1 + b y_2$.

**Conclusion:** The system is **NON-LINEAR** (the squaring operation is the source of non-linearity; the cosine factor alone does not affect linearity).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to check BOTH additivity and homogeneity:** A common mistake is testing only one of the two sub-properties. The system MUST satisfy both to be linear. (Penalty: 1–2 marks lost.)
>
> 2. **Mistaking time-variation for non-linearity:** A system can be **time-varying but still linear** (e.g., $y[n] = n \cdot x[n]$ or $y[n] = x[n] \cos(\omega_0 n)$). Linearity and time-invariance are **independent** properties. (Penalty: up to 3 marks.)
>
> 3. **Not verifying $T\{0\} = 0$:** This is the fastest first-test for linearity. If a system produces a non-zero output for zero input, it is *immediately* non-linear — no further work needed.
>
> 4. **Misinterpreting "squared modulus":** $y[n] = \vert x[n] \vert^2$ is **LINEAR** (since $\vert x[n] \vert^2 = x[n] \cdot x^*[n]$ is a memoryless quadratic), but $y[n] = (x[n])^2$ is **NON-LINEAR** when the input is real. For real signals, both reduce to $x^2[n]$ and are non-linear. For complex signals, they differ.
>
> 5. **Skipping the cross-term identification:** When squaring or multiplying inputs, always expand $(a x_1 + b x_2)^2$ to expose the cross-term $2ab x_1 x_2$. The presence of this term is a definitive proof of non-linearity.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Linearity Property**

- ✅ **Linearity = Additivity + Homogeneity** (Superposition Principle).
- ✅ **Master equation:** $T\{a x_1 + b x_2\} = a T\{x_1\} + b T\{x_2\}$.
- ✅ **Zero-input rule:** $T\{0\} = 0$ is a *necessary* condition. Failing this → system is non-linear.
- ✅ **Memoryless nonlinearities:** $y = x^2$, $y = |x|$, $y = \sin(x)$, $y = e^x$, $y = \text{sign}(x)$, $y = \log(x)$ — all are non-linear.
- ✅ **Memoryless linearities:** $y = Kx$, $y = x + a$ (a is zero only).
- ✅ **Linear but time-varying:** $y[n] = n x[n]$, $y[n] = x[Mn]$, $y[n] = x[n] \cos(\omega_0 n)$.
- ✅ **LTI systems:** $y[n] = \sum_k h[k] x[n-k]$ (convolution) — always linear.
- ✅ **Constant offset kills linearity:** $y = x + C$ (for $C \neq 0$) is non-linear.
- ✅ **Squaring creates cross-terms:** $(ax_1 + bx_2)^2 \neq a x_1^2 + b x_2^2$ — proof of non-linearity.
- ✅ **Linearity is independent of time-invariance:** A system can be LTI, Linear Time-Varying (LTV), Non-Linear Time-Invariant (squarer with delay), or Non-Linear Time-Varying.
- ✅ **Practical test order:** (1) Check $T\{0\}=0$. (2) Try scaling: $T\{2x\} = 2T\{x\}$? (3) Try sum: $T\{x_1+x_2\}=T\{x_1\}+T\{x_2\}$? Fail any → Non-Linear.
- ✅ **Engineering utilities:** Convolution, Fourier/Z-transform, frequency response $H(e^{j\omega})$, and eigen-decomposition all **require linearity**.
- ✅ **Common KTU trap:** "Is the system $y[n] = x^2[n] - x^2[n-1]$ linear?" Answer: **YES, LINEAR** because it's the difference of two non-linear *operations on independent signal samples*, which happens to be expressible as $f(x[n]) - f(x[n-1])$ where $f$ is non-linear. Wait — actually, the cross-term test still fails. Let me clarify: $T\{a x_1 + b x_2\} = (ax_1+bx_2)^2 - (ax_1[n-1]+bx_2[n-1])^2$, which expands to have cross-terms. So this is **NON-LINEAR**. Always test rigorously!
- ✅ **Mnemonic:** **"LAD"** — **L**inearity, **A**dditivity, **D**ecomposability (scaling).

> **END OF NOTES — Module 2: Properties — Linearity**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Time Invariance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT604 - DIGITAL SIGNALS AND PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Time Invariance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A system is classified as **Time-Invariant (TI)** if a time shift in the input signal produces an identical time shift in the output signal. In other words, the system's behavior, characteristics, and parameters do **not** change with time. The system responds to an input today in exactly the same way it will respond to the same input tomorrow.

Mathematically, a continuous-time system $T[\cdot]$ is time-invariant if and only if for every input signal $x(t)$ and every time shift $t_0 \in \mathbb{R}$:

$$T\{x(t - t_0)\} = y(t - t_0)$$

where $y(t) = T\{x(t)\}$ is the original output response.

For discrete-time systems, the corresponding condition is:

$$T\{x[n - n_0]\} = y[n - n_0]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Verbatim Standard):**
> A system is said to be time-invariant if its input-output relationship does not change with time. Shifting the input signal in time results in an equivalent shift in the output signal, with **no other alteration** in the output waveform shape, amplitude, or phase.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are teaching a small child to clap when you say the word "Clap". 

- **Scenario 1 (Time-Invariant):** If you say "Clap" now, the child claps now. If you say "Clap" after 5 seconds of silence, the child claps exactly 5 seconds later. The *response rule* (clap = clap) never changes, only its timing relative to a global clock changes.

- **Scenario 2 (Time-Variant):** If you say "Clap" now, the child claps loudly. But if you wait 5 seconds and say "Clap" then, the child whispers a clap, or perhaps does not clap at all because the child has gotten bored. Here, the *rule itself* depends on when the input arrives.

A system is time-invariant when **"what you input is what you get out, just delayed by the same amount."**

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Notation | Standard Unit | Significance |
|-----------|----------|---------------|--------------|
| Time shift | $t_0$ (CT) / $n_0$ (DT) | **seconds** (CT) / **samples** (DT) | Magnitude of input delay |
| Arbitrary test input | $x(t)$, $x[n]$ | **Volts (V)** or normalized | Any general signal |
| System response | $T\{\cdot\}$ | Operator | Maps input to output |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 2):**
> Time Invariance is a foundational property tested extensively in **Part A (3 marks)** and is a mandatory sub-part in **Part B (14 marks)** questions on system classification. Mastery of the formal shift test is non-negotiable for scoring above 80% in this module.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Time-Invariance Shift Test — Step-by-Step Logic

To formally verify whether a system is time-invariant or time-variant, students must execute the following structured protocol:

**Step 1 — Compute the Original Response:**
Apply the input $x(t)$ to the system and obtain the output $y(t) = T\{x(t)\}$.

**Step 2 — Apply Time Shift to the Output:**
Delay the output $y(t)$ by $t_0$ to obtain $y(t - t_0)$.

**Step 3 — Compute the Shifted Input Response:**
Replace the independent variable $t$ with $(t - t_0)$ inside the input signal and apply it to the system. This yields $T\{x(t - t_0)\}$.

**Step 4 — Compare the Two Results:**
- If $T\{x(t - t_0)\} = y(t - t_0)$ → **System is Time-Invariant**.
- If $T\{x(t - t_0)\} \neq y(t - t_0)$ → **System is Time-Variant**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works:** The test isolates *when* the input arrives from *what* the system does to the input. If the system's mathematical rule contains an explicit time variable (e.g., $t \cdot x(t)$ or $\cos(t) \cdot x(t)$), then the system behavior shifts with the global clock, breaking invariance.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | System Rule $y(t) = T\{x(t)\}$ | Time-Invariant? | Reason |
|---|--------------------------------|-----------------|--------|
| 1 | $y(t) = x(t)$ | **Yes** | Identity system, no time dependence |
| 2 | $y(t) = x(t - t_0)$ | **Yes** | Pure delay element |
| 3 | $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$ | **No** | Multiplier $t$ is explicit time-dependent |
| 4 | $y(t) = x(2t)$ | **No** | Time scaling alters system's temporal mapping |
| 5 | $y(t) = x(-t)$ | **No** | Time reversal changes direction of flow |
| 6 | $y(t) = \cos(t) \cdot x(t)$ | **No** | Modulation envelope depends on $t$ |
| 7 | $y(t) = x(t) + x(t-1)$ | **Yes** | Linear combination of fixed delays |
| 8 | $y(t) = \dfrac{dx(t)}{dt}$ | **Yes** | Differentiator has constant coefficients |
| 9 | $y[n] = n \cdot x[n]$ | **No** | Explicit index $n$ appears in the rule |
| 10 | $y[n] = x[n] - x[n-1]$ | **Yes** | Standard discrete differentiator |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule of Thumb (KTU Examiner's Favourite):**
> Look for any occurrence of the independent time variable ($t$ or $n$) **outside** the input signal $x(\cdot)$. If you see $t$ multiplying $x(t)$, $t$ added to $x(t)$, or $t$ used as a coefficient — the system is **Time-Variant** with 100% certainty.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Time-invariance is not merely a mathematical abstraction. It has profound practical implications:

- **LTI Systems Foundation:** Time-invariance, combined with linearity, forms the class of **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI)** systems. Almost all communication channels, filters (FIR, IIR), and control systems are modeled as LTI because they allow the use of **convolution**, **Fourier Transform**, and **Z-Transform** analysis.

- **Filter Design:** In FIR and IIR digital filter design (Module 3 & 4 of your syllabus), time-invariance is *assumed by default*. If a filter were time-variant, its frequency response would change unpredictably with time, making it useless for stable signal processing.

- **DSP Hardware Implementation:** Real-time DSP chips (like the TMS320 series) implement time-invariant algorithms. A time-variant system would require the hardware coefficients to be updated every clock cycle — an impractical design constraint.

- **Audio/Image Processing:** Equalizers, noise filters, and image sharpeners are all designed as time-invariant systems. A time-variant equalizer would change its equalization curve while the music is playing — a chaotic listening experience.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Time-Variant System Proof

**Problem:** Determine whether the system described by $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$ is time-invariant.

**Solution using the Four-Step Shift Test:**

**Step 1 — Original Output:**
Apply input $x(t)$ to the system directly. The system rule multiplies the input by $t$:

$$y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$$

**Step 2 — Shift the Output by $t_0$:**
Delay the output $y(t)$ by $t_0$ seconds by substituting $(t - t_0)$ for $t$ in the expression:

$$y(t - t_0) = (t - t_0) \cdot x(t - t_0)$$

**Step 3 — Apply Shifted Input to System:**
Now replace $t$ with $(t - t_0)$ inside the input of the system rule:

$$T\{x(t - t_0)\} = t \cdot x(t - t_0)$$

**Step 4 — Compare the Two Expressions:**

$$\underbrace{(t - t_0) \cdot x(t - t_0)}_{\text{Delayed output}} \quad \neq \quad \underbrace{t \cdot x(t - t_0)}_{\text{Shifted input response}}$$

Since $(t - t_0) \neq t$ for $t_0 \neq 0$, the two expressions are **not equal**.

$$\boxed{\therefore \text{The system } y(t) = t \cdot x(t) \text{ is TIME-VARIANT.}}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Time-Invariant System Proof

**Problem:** Determine whether the system described by $y(t) = x(t - 5)$ is time-invariant.

**Solution using the Four-Step Shift Test:**

**Step 1 — Original Output:**
The system delays the input by 5 seconds:

$$y(t) = x(t - 5)$$

**Step 2 — Shift the Output by $t_0$:**
Substitute $(t - t_0)$ for $t$ in the output expression:

$$y(t - t_0) = x((t - t_0) - 5) = x(t - t_0 - 5)$$

**Step 3 — Apply Shifted Input to System:**
Replace $t$ with $(t - t_0)$ inside the input of the system rule. The system will then delay this already-shifted input by another 5 seconds:

$$T\{x(t - t_0)\} = x((t - t_0) - 5) = x(t - t_0 - 5)$$

**Step 4 — Compare the Two Expressions:**

$$\underbrace{x(t - t_0 - 5)}_{\text{Delayed output}} \quad = \quad \underbrace{x(t - t_0 - 5)}_{\text{Shifted input response}}$$

The two expressions are **identically equal** for all $t$ and all $t_0$.

$$\boxed{\therefore \text{The system } y(t) = x(t - 5) \text{ is TIME-INVARIANT.}}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Discrete-Time System with Modulation

**Problem:** Test the system $y[n] = (-1)^n \cdot x[n]$ for time-invariance.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Original Output:**

$$y[n] = (-1)^n \cdot x[n]$$

**Step 2 — Shift the Output by $n_0$ Samples:**

$$y[n - n_0] = (-1)^{n - n_0} \cdot x[n - n_0]$$

**Step 3 — Apply Shifted Input to System:**
Replace $n$ with $(n - n_0)$ throughout the system rule:

$$T\{x[n - n_0]\} = (-1)^n \cdot x[n - n_0]$$

**Step 4 — Compare:**

$$(-1)^{n - n_0} \cdot x[n - n_0] \quad \neq \quad (-1)^n \cdot x[n - n_0]$$

The factor $(-1)^{-n_0}$ is missing from the right side. Therefore:

$$\boxed{\therefore \text{The system } y[n] = (-1)^n \cdot x[n] \text{ is TIME-VARIANT.}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The multiplier $(-1)^n$ is an explicit function of time $n$, making this a classic **time-varying modulator**. This type of system appears in **communication engineering** as a BPSK modulator, where the carrier sign alternates with each time instant.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Cumulative Sum (Accumulator) System

**Problem:** Test the discrete-time system $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x[k]$ for time-invariance.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Original Output:**

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x[k]$$

**Step 2 — Shift the Output by $n_0$ Samples:**
Replace $n$ with $(n - n_0)$ in the upper limit of summation:

$$y[n - n_0] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n - n_0} x[k]$$

**Step 3 — Apply Shifted Input to System:**
The shifted input is $x[n - n_0]$. Substituting this into the system rule:

$$T\{x[n - n_0]\} = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x[k - n_0]$$

**Step 4 — Perform Variable Substitution:**
Let $m = k - n_0$, which means $k = m + n_0$. When $k = -\infty$, $m = -\infty$. When $k = n$, $m = n - n_0$. The sum becomes:

$$T\{x[n - n_0]\} = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{n - n_0} x[m]$$

**Step 5 — Compare:**

$$\sum_{m=-\infty}^{n - n_0} x[m] \quad = \quad \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n - n_0} x[k]$$

Both sums have identical limits and identical summand structure. They are equal.

$$\boxed{\therefore \text{The cumulative sum system is TIME-INVARIANT.}}$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Quick Reference for Exam Hall

| Observation in System Equation | Classification | Marks Tendency |
|-------------------------------|----------------|----------------|
| $t$ or $n$ appears as a **multiplier/coefficient** of $x$ | Time-Variant | Common 3-mark question |
| $t$ or $n$ appears **added/subtracted inside** $x(\cdot)$ argument | Time-Invariant | Standard delay |
| $t$ or $n$ appears as a **modulating function** (e.g., $\cos(t)$) | Time-Variant | Favourite 7-mark question |
| Coefficients of derivatives/differences are **constants** | Time-Invariant | LTI prerequisite |
| System contains **time scaling** (e.g., $x(2t)$) | Time-Variant | Edge case question |
| System contains **time reversal** (e.g., $x(-t)$) | Time-Variant | Edge case question |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Time-Invariance Test Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start Test: Arbitrary Input x(t)"] --> B["Step 1: Compute y(t) = T{x(t)}"]
    B --> C["Step 2: Delay Output: y(t - t0)"]
    C --> D["Step 3: Compute T{x(t - t0)}"]
    D --> E{"Step 4: Is T{x(t - t0)} == y(t - t0) ?"}
    E -- YES --> F["System is TIME-INVARIANT TI"]
    E -- NO --> G["System is TIME-VARIANT TV"]
    F --> H["End: TI Confirmed"]
    G --> I["End: TV Confirmed"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — LTI System Decomposition Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SYS["Discrete-Time System T"] --> L{"Linear?"}
    L -- YES --> TI{"Time-Invariant?"}
    L -- NO --> NL["Non-Linear System - Cannot use Convolution"]
    TI -- YES --> LTI["LTI System - Analyse via Convolution, Z-Transform, DFT"]
    TI -- NO --> LTV["Linear Time-Variant - No Convolution Kernel"]
    LTI --> APP1["FIR Filter Design Module 3"]
    LTI --> APP2["IIR Filter Design Module 4"]
    LTI --> APP3["FFT Spectral Analysis Module 5"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Conceptual Flow — Time Shift Equivalence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PATH1["Path A: Shift Input First"]
        X1["Input x(t)"] --> S1["Shift by t0: x(t - t0)"] --> SYS1["System T"] --> Y1["Output: T{x(t - t0)}"]
    end
    subgraph PATH2["Path B: Shift Output First"]
        X2["Input x(t)"] --> SYS2["System T"] --> S2["Shift by t0: y(t - t0)"] --> Y2["Output: y(t - t0)"]
    end
    Y1 -- IF EQUAL --> TI["System is TIME-INVARIANT"]
    Y1 -- IF NOT EQUAL --> TV["System is TIME-VARIANT"]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Classification Tree — System Property Identification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT["Given System y(t) = f{x(t)}"] --> Q1{"Does t appear OUTSIDE x argument?"}
    Q1 -- YES --> TV1["Time-Variant TV"]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{"Are coefficients of x terms CONSTANT?"}
    Q2 -- YES --> TI1["Time-Invariant TI"]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3{"Are coefficients functions of t?"}
    Q3 -- YES --> TV2["Time-Variant TV"]
    Q3 -- NO --> TI2["Time-Invariant TI"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define a time-invariant system. Provide one example of a continuous-time time-varying system and justify why it is time-variant.**

**Model Answer:**

A system is **time-invariant** if a time shift in the input signal produces an identical time shift in the output signal, with no other change. Mathematically:

$$T\{x(t - t_0)\} = y(t - t_0)$$

**Example:** The system $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$.

**Justification:** The coefficient $t$ multiplying the input is an explicit function of time. If we shift the input by $t_0$, the system produces $T\{x(t - t_0)\} = t \cdot x(t - t_0)$, but the delayed output is $y(t - t_0) = (t - t_0) \cdot x(t - t_0)$. Since $t \neq (t - t_0)$ for $t_0 \neq 0$, the system is time-variant.

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State the condition for a discrete-time LTI system and write the convolution sum formula.**

**Model Answer:**

A discrete-time **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI)** system is one that satisfies both linearity (superposition) and time-invariance simultaneously. The output of any LTI system is given by the **convolution sum**:

$$y[n] = x[n] * h[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] \cdot h[n - k]$$

where $h[n]$ is the **impulse response** of the system. The existence of a well-defined impulse response is *only* guaranteed under the LTI assumption. Time-invariance is the critical property that allows us to use this elegant convolution-based analysis.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Test whether the continuous-time system $y(t) = x(t) \cdot \cos(\omega_c t)$ is time-invariant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A discrete-time system is defined by $y[n] = n \cdot x[n] + x[n-1]$. Determine if the system is time-invariant. Show all steps of the shift test. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Original Output:**

$$y(t) = x(t) \cdot \cos(\omega_c t)$$

**Step 2 — Delay the Output by $t_0$:**

$$y(t - t_0) = x(t - t_0) \cdot \cos(\omega_c (t - t_0))$$

**Step 3 — Apply Shifted Input to System:**

$$T\{x(t - t_0)\} = x(t - t_0) \cdot \cos(\omega_c t)$$

**Step 4 — Comparison:**

$$x(t - t_0) \cdot \cos(\omega_c (t - t_0)) \quad \neq \quad x(t - t_0) \cdot \cos(\omega_c t)$$

The cosine arguments differ by $\omega_c t_0$, so the two outputs are not equal.

**Conclusion:** The system $y(t) = x(t) \cdot \cos(\omega_c t)$ is **TIME-VARIANT**. It is in fact a sinusoidal modulator used in amplitude modulation (AM) communication systems, where the time-varying nature is intentional and desirable.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Original Output:**

$$y[n] = n \cdot x[n] + x[n-1]$$

**Step 2 — Delay the Output by $n_0$ Samples:**

$$y[n - n_0] = (n - n_0) \cdot x[n - n_0] + x[n - n_0 - 1]$$

**Step 3 — Apply Shifted Input to System:**
Substitute $x[n - n_0]$ into the system rule:

$$T\{x[n - n_0]\} = n \cdot x[n - n_0] + x[n - n_0 - 1]$$

**Step 4 — Comparison:**

$$(n - n_0) \cdot x[n - n_0] + x[n - n_0 - 1] \quad \neq \quad n \cdot x[n - n_0] + x[n - n_0 - 1]$$

The first term differs by $n_0 \cdot x[n - n_0]$.

**Conclusion:** The system $y[n] = n \cdot x[n] + x[n-1]$ is **TIME-VARIANT** because of the $n \cdot x[n]$ term. The $x[n-1]$ term alone would be time-invariant, but the explicit time dependence in the coefficient destroys the overall invariance.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a clear mathematical proof, demonstrate that the system $y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau) \, d\tau$ is time-invariant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Test the discrete-time system $y[n] = x[-n]$ for time-invariance and time-reversal property. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Original Output:**

$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 2 — Delay the Output by $t_0$:**

$$y(t - t_0) = \int_{-\infty}^{t - t_0} x(\tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 3 — Apply Shifted Input to System:**
The shifted input is $x(t - t_0)$. Substituting into the system:

$$T\{x(t - t_0)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau - t_0) \, d\tau$$

**Step 4 — Variable Substitution:**
Let $u = \tau - t_0$, so $du = d\tau$. When $\tau = -\infty$, $u = -\infty$. When $\tau = t$, $u = t - t_0$:

$$T\{x(t - t_0)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{t - t_0} x(u) \, du$$

**Step 5 — Comparison:**

$$\int_{-\infty}^{t - t_0} x(\tau) \, d\tau \quad = \quad \int_{-\infty}^{t - t_0} x(u) \, du$$

Both integrals have identical limits and identical integrands.

**Conclusion:** The integrator $y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau) \, d\tau$ is **TIME-INVARIANT**. This system is the continuous-time analog of the accumulator, a fundamental building block in analog computer design and signal processing.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Original Output:**

$$y[n] = x[-n]$$

**Step 2 — Delay the Output by $n_0$ Samples:**

$$y[n - n_0] = x[-(n - n_0)] = x[-n + n_0]$$

**Step 3 — Apply Shifted Input to System:**

$$T\{x[n - n_0]\} = x[-(n - n_0)] = x[-n + n_0]$$

**Step 4 — Comparison:**

$$x[-n + n_0] \quad = \quad x[-n + n_0]$$

The two expressions are identical.

**Conclusion:** The system $y[n] = x[-n]$ is **TIME-INVARIANT** in the formal sense, but it performs **time-reversal**. The output is a mirror image of the input about the origin. Although the shift test passes, this is a *non-causal* system because the output at time $n$ depends on the future value $x[-n]$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Skipping the algebraic comparison:** Many students compute $T\{x(t - t_0)\}$ and $y(t - t_0)$ but never explicitly state whether they are equal. Always end with a boxed "Equal" or "Not Equal" statement. **(−1 to −2 marks)**
> 2. **Replacing the wrong variable:** In discrete-time, students often shift the output using $y[n + n_0]$ instead of $y[n - n_0]$. The shift convention must be **consistent** between the output delay and the input delay. **(−2 marks)**
> 3. **Forgetting to check for explicit $t$ or $n$:** A common shortcut is to claim "the system is time-variant because the answer came out different." Examiners want the **identification of the offending term** (e.g., "the multiplier $n$ in $n \cdot x[n]$ causes time-variance"). **(−1 mark)**
> 4. **Confusing causality with time-invariance:** A non-causal system can still be time-invariant. Do not equate the two properties. **(−1 mark)**
> 5. **Discrete-time boundary errors:** When dealing with $y[n] = x[2n]$ (down-sampler) or $y[n] = x[n/2]$ (up-sampler), the shift test requires careful handling of integer indices. Examiners often test these edge cases. **(−2 marks)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Time Invariance (KTU Module 2)**

- **Definition:** A system is time-invariant if shifting the input by $t_0$ shifts the output by the same $t_0$, with no other change.

- **Master Equation (CT):** $T\{x(t - t_0)\} = y(t - t_0)$

- **Master Equation (DT):** $T\{x[n - n_0]\} = y[n - n_0]$

- **The 4-Step Test:** (1) Find $y$, (2) Find $y$ delayed, (3) Find $T$ of delayed input, (4) Compare.

- **Golden Rule:** Any explicit occurrence of $t$ or $n$ *outside* the input argument $x(\cdot)$ is a **red flag** for time-variance.

- **LTI Prerequisite:** Time-invariance + Linearity = LTI system. Only LTI systems can be analyzed using convolution, Fourier Transform, Laplace Transform, and Z-Transform.

- **Causality vs Time-Invariance:** These are **independent** properties. A system can be causal but time-variant, or non-causal but time-invariant.

- **Memory vs Time-Invariance:** Also independent. $y[n] = x[n-1]$ is both memoryless (no, it has memory) — wait, it has memory and is time-invariant.

- **Time-Variant Examples to Memorize:** $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$, $y[n] = n \cdot x[n]$, $y(t) = \cos(t) \cdot x(t)$, $y[n] = (-1)^n \cdot x[n]$, $y(t) = x(2t)$, $y(t) = x(-t)$.

- **Time-Invariant Examples to Memorize:** $y(t) = x(t - t_0)$, $y[n] = x[n - n_0]$, $y(t) = \dfrac{dx(t)}{dt}$, $y[n] = x[n] - x[n-1]$, $y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau) d\tau$, $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x[k]$.

- **Engineering Relevance:** All practical digital filters (FIR, IIR), communication channels (in steady state), and control systems are modeled as **LTI**. Time-invariance is the cornerstone assumption of modern DSP.

- **Common Trap:** $y[n] = x[n] \cdot \cos(n)$ is **time-variant** because of the $n$ in the cosine. But $y[n] = x[n] \cdot \cos(\omega_0 n)$ is also time-variant for the same reason. Memorize this trap.

- **Bonus Tip:** When the system equation is given in block-diagram form (e.g., with multipliers, delays, adders), look at the multiplier coefficients. If they are constants → time-invariant. If they depend on $n$ or $t$ → time-variant.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Causality
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT604 - DIGITAL SIGNALS AND PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Causality

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Causal System:** A discrete-time system is said to be **causal** (or **non-anticipatory**) if its output $y(n)$ at any instant $n = n_0$ depends *only* on the present input $x(n_0)$ and past inputs $x(n)$ for $n < n_0$. It must be **completely independent of all future inputs** $x(n)$ for $n > n_0$.

Mathematically, a system described by the operator $\mathcal{T}\{\cdot\}$ is causal if and only if for every input $x(n)$:

$$y(n) = \mathcal{T}\{x(n)\} \quad \Rightarrow \quad y(n) \text{ depends only on } x(k) \text{ for } k \leq n$$

For an **LTI (Linear Time-Invariant)** system characterized by its impulse response $h(n)$, the system is causal **if and only if**:

$$\boxed{h(n) = 0 \quad \text{for all} \quad n < 0}$$

Equivalently, in the **z-domain**, a causal LTI system has a system function $H(z)$ whose **Region of Convergence (ROC)** is the **exterior of a circle** — i.e., of the form $\vert z \vert > r_{\max}$, where $r_{\max}$ is the radius of the outermost pole.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Echo of Yesterday's Voice":**
> Imagine a person standing at the edge of a cliff. He can only hear the **past echoes** of his own voice bouncing off the valley walls. He **cannot** hear a sound that has not yet been made. The cliff acts as a **causal system** — its response at any time $t$ is determined solely by the history of sounds up to that moment. In contrast, a system that could respond to *tomorrow's* sound today would be like a fortune-teller's crystal ball — that's an **anticausal** (non-causal) system, which is physically unrealizable in real time.

**Another simple analogy — Newspaper vs. Stock Market Feed:**
- A **causal** system is like a *newspaper* — today's edition reports yesterday's closing prices. The output today is based purely on past events.
- A **non-causal** system is like a *time-traveler's blog* — it reports prices that haven't happened yet. Useful only for **offline processing** (e.g., recorded audio/image processing).

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!TIP]
> - **Memoryless systems** are **always causal** because $y(n)$ depends only on the present input $x(n)$.
> - **Causality** is a property of the **system**, **not** of the **signal**.
> - The output of a causal system at $n=0$ for an input applied at $n=0$ is the **initial condition** $y(0) = \mathcal{T}\{x(0)\}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Causality for LTI Systems

For an LTI system, the output is the **linear convolution** of the input with the impulse response:

$$y(n) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} h(k)\, x(n-k)$$

**Step-by-step logical interpretation:**

- **Step 1:** Substitute $n$ with the current time index. The summation index $k$ ranges over the **entire history** of $h(k)$.
- **Step 2:** For a particular output sample $y(n_0)$, the term $x(n_0 - k)$ represents the input samples weighted by $h(k)$.
- **Step 3:** When $k < 0$, the impulse response $h(k)$ contributes weights. For a **causal** system, we require these weights to be **zero**, so that only $k \geq 0$ (i.e., past and present inputs) contribute.
- **Step 4:** Therefore, the convolution sum reduces to a **one-sided** (right-sided) sum for causal systems:

$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{+\infty} h(k)\, x(n-k)$$

- **Step 5:** In the z-domain, taking the z-transform of this one-sided impulse response yields a rational function whose ROC extends **outward to infinity** from the outermost pole.

---

### 2.2 Causal LTI Systems — Pole-Zero Geometry

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Fundamental Rule:** A causal LTI system is **stable** (BIBO stable) if and only if **all poles lie strictly inside the unit circle** $\vert z \vert < 1$. This is the famous **causality + stability ⇒ poles inside unit circle** condition.

**Pole locations summarized:**

| Pole Location | Implication on Causality | Implication on Stability |
|---|---|---|
| All poles inside $\vert z \vert < 1$ | Causal (ROC: $\vert z \vert > r_{\max}$ with $r_{\max} < 1$) | **Stable** |
| Some pole ON $\vert z \vert = 1$ | Causal | **Marginally stable / Unstable** |
| Pole outside $\vert z \vert = 1$ | Causal | **Unstable** |
| Pole at $z=0$ (origin) | Causal, FIR-type | Stable if no other issues |

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Property / Formula | Mathematical Expression | ROC Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Causality condition (time domain)** | $h(n) = 0,\ \forall\, n < 0$ | Right-sided impulse response |
| 2 | **Causality condition (z-domain)** | ROC is exterior of a circle | $\vert z \vert > r_{\max}$ |
| 3 | **Convolution sum for causal system** | $y(n) = \displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} h(k)\,x(n-k)$ | One-sided sum |
| 4 | **Stability condition for causal LTI** | $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \vert h(n) \vert < \infty$ | All poles inside unit circle |
| 5 | **Anticausal condition** | $h(n) = 0,\ \forall\, n > 0$ | ROC: $\vert z \vert < r_{\min}$ |
| 6 | **Two-sided (non-causal) signal** | $h(n) \neq 0$ for both $n<0$ and $n>0$ | ROC: ring-shaped, $r_1 < \vert z \vert < r_2$ |
| 7 | **Memoryless ⇒ Causal** | $y(n) = K\,x(n)$ | Always causal |
| 8 | **Ideal delay ⇒ Causal** | $y(n) = x(n - n_0),\ n_0 \geq 0$ | $H(z) = z^{-n_0}$ |
| 9 | **Ideal advance ⇒ Non-causal** | $y(n) = x(n + n_0),\ n_0 > 0$ | $H(z) = z^{+n_0}$ |
| 10 | **Accumulator (causal)** | $y(n) = \displaystyle\sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x(k)$ | $H(z) = \dfrac{1}{1-z^{-1}}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Note on notation:** All absolute value bars inside table cells are written using `\vert` so the markdown table structure remains intact.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Real-time DSP systems** (audio amplifiers, control systems, telecommunications) **must be causal** because they cannot wait for future samples.
> - **Offline processing** (image processing, seismic analysis, ECG analysis from recorded data) **can be non-causal** since the entire signal is pre-recorded.
> - The **causality constraint** drives the choice of IIR vs. FIR filter topologies in embedded system design.
> - In **biomedical signal processing** (e.g., hearing aids), causality is critical — the patient must hear the sound *as it occurs*.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Causality from the Convolution Sum

We begin with the **general convolution sum** relating the output of an LTI system to its input and impulse response:

$$y(n) = x(n) * h(n) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} h(k)\, x(n-k)$$

**Step 1 — Split the infinite sum into two parts** (negative and non-negative indices of $k$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(n) &= \sum_{k=-\infty}^{-1} h(k)\, x(n-k) \;\;+\;\; \sum_{k=0}^{+\infty} h(k)\, x(n-k)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Interpret the first sum (the "future-input" term):**
For $k = -1, -2, -3, \ldots$, the input argument is $n - k = n + 1, n + 2, n + 3, \ldots$, which corresponds to **future** input values $x(n+1), x(n+2), x(n+3), \ldots$.

**Step 3 — Interpret the second sum (the "present/past-input" term):**
For $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$, the input argument is $n - k = n, n-1, n-2, \ldots$, which corresponds to **present and past** input values $x(n), x(n-1), x(n-2), \ldots$.

**Step 4 — Apply the causality requirement:**
For the system to be causal, the coefficient of every future input sample must be **exactly zero**. The future-input samples are weighted by $h(k)$ for $k < 0$. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h(k) = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad k < 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back into the convolution sum:**
With $h(k) = 0$ for $k < 0$, the first sum vanishes identically, and we obtain the **causal convolution formula**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{+\infty} h(k)\, x(n-k)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** A discrete-time LTI system is causal **if and only if** $h(n) = 0$ for $n < 0$. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Verifying Causality of Common Systems

| # | System | Difference Equation / Relation | $h(n)$ Expression | $h(n) = 0$ for $n<0$? | **Causal?** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Identity (Wire) | $y(n) = x(n)$ | $h(n) = \delta(n)$ | ✓ | **Yes** |
| 2 | Pure Delay | $y(n) = x(n - 3)$ | $h(n) = \delta(n-3)$ | ✓ | **Yes** |
| 3 | Pure Advance | $y(n) = x(n + 2)$ | $h(n) = \delta(n+2)$ | ✗ (nonzero at $n=-2$) | **No (Anticausal)** |
| 4 | Moving Average | $y(n) = \frac{1}{3}[x(n)+x(n-1)+x(n-2)]$ | $h(n) = \frac{1}{3}[\delta(n)+\delta(n-1)+\delta(n-2)]$ | ✓ | **Yes** |
| 5 | Accumulator | $y(n) = y(n-1) + x(n)$ | $h(n) = u(n)$ | ✓ | **Yes** |
| 6 | Backward Difference | $y(n) = x(n) - x(n+1)$ | $h(n) = \delta(n) - \delta(n+1)$ | ✗ | **No (Anticausal)** |
| 7 | Centered Average | $y(n) = \frac{1}{3}[x(n-1)+x(n)+x(n+1)]$ | $h(n) = \frac{1}{3}[\delta(n-1)+\delta(n)+\delta(n+1)]$ | ✗ | **No (Non-causal)** |
| 8 | Squarer (Memoryless) | $y(n) = x^2(n)$ | — (not LTI) | N/A | **Yes** (depends only on $x(n)$) |

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — Determining Causality from Pole-Zero Plot

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A causal LTI system has system function $H(z) = \dfrac{(z - 0.5)}{(z - 0.8)(z - 1.2)}$. Determine if it is stable.

**Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1 — Identify the poles:**
The poles are at $z = 0.8$ and $z = 1.2$.

**Step 2 — Check causality (already given as causal):**
Since the system is causal, the ROC must be $\vert z \vert > 1.2$ (exterior of the outermost pole).

**Step 3 — Check stability:**
A causal LTI system is stable if and only if **all poles lie inside the unit circle**, i.e., $\vert p_k \vert < 1$ for all poles $p_k$.

- Pole 1: $\vert 0.8 \vert = 0.8 < 1$ ✓ (inside unit circle)
- Pole 2: $\vert 1.2 \vert = 1.2 > 1$ ✗ (outside unit circle)

**Step 4 — Conclude:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Since pole } z = 1.2 \text{ lies outside the unit circle, the system is \textbf{causal but UNSTABLE}.}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Invertibility & Causality

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Show that the system $y(n) = n \cdot x(n)$ is linear, memoryless, and causal.

**Step 1 — Linearity:** For inputs $x_1, x_2$ and scalars $a, b$:
$\mathcal{T}\{a x_1 + b x_2\} = n(a x_1(n) + b x_2(n)) = a \cdot n x_1(n) + b \cdot n x_2(n) = a y_1(n) + b y_2(n)$ ✓

**Step 2 — Memoryless:** $y(n)$ at instant $n$ depends only on $x(n)$ at the **same** instant $n$ — no memory of past or future samples. ✓

**Step 3 — Causal:** $y(n) = n x(n)$ uses **only the present input** $x(n)$. There is no dependence on $x(n+1), x(n+2), \ldots$ ✓

**Conclusion:** The system is **linear, memoryless, and causal**. $\blacksquare$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Causal vs. Non-Causal LTI System

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Input Signal x(n)] --> B{System Type}
    B -- Causal --> C[Convolution y_c n = sum k from 0 to inf of h k times x n minus k]
    B -- Non Causal --> D[Convolution y_nc n = sum k from minus inf to plus inf of h k times x n minus k]
    C --> E[Output y_c n depends only on present and past inputs]
    D --> F[Output y_nc n depends on past present AND future inputs]
    E --> G[ROC exterior of circle in z domain]
    E --> H[Poles inside unit circle for stability]
    F --> I[ROC ring shaped in z domain]
    F --> J[Common in offline image processing]
    H --> K[Real time implementation possible]
    G --> K
    I --> L[Real time implementation NOT possible]
    J --> L

    classDef causalStyle fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef noncausalStyle fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decisionStyle fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#007bff,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef resultStyle fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class A causalStyle
    class B decisionStyle
    class C,E,G,H,K causalStyle
    class D,F,I,J,L noncausalStyle
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The green (left) path represents a **causal LTI system**: convolution starts at $k=0$, ROC is exterior of a circle, and stable systems have all poles inside the unit circle.
- The red (right) path represents a **non-causal LTI system**: convolution includes negative $k$ values, ROC is annular (ring-shaped), and such systems can only be implemented offline.

---

### 4.2 Decision Flowchart — "Is this LTI System Causal?"

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Given System H z or h n] --> B{Is impulse response nonzero for n less than 0}
    B -- No --> C[System is CAUSAL]
    B -- Yes --> D{Does h n vanish for n greater than 0}
    D -- No --> E[System is NON CAUSAL two sided]
    D -- Yes --> F[System is ANTICAUSAL]
    C --> G{Do all poles lie strictly inside unit circle}
    G -- Yes --> H[System is CAUSAL and STABLE]
    G -- No --> I[System is CAUSAL but UNSTABLE]
    H --> J[Suitable for real time DSP applications]
    I --> K[Unsuitable without stabilization]
    F --> L[ROC is interior of a circle]
    E --> M[ROC is ring shaped]

    classDef successStyle fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef warningStyle fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dangerStyle fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef neutralStyle fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#6c757d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class A neutralStyle
    class B,D,G neutralStyle
    class C,H,J successStyle
    class E,M dangerStyle
    class F,L dangerStyle
    class I,K warningStyle
```

---

### 4.3 Time-Domain Visualization of Causal vs. Non-Causal Impulse Responses

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[h n for causal system nonzero only for n greater than or equal to 0] --> B[Right sided impulse response]
    C[h n for anticausal system nonzero only for n less than 0] --> D[Left sided impulse response]
    E[h n for non causal system nonzero for both n less than 0 and n greater than 0] --> F[Two sided impulse response]

    classDef typeAStyle fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#007bff,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef typeBStyle fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef typeCStyle fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class A,B typeAStyle
    class C,D typeBStyle
    class E,F typeCStyle
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**  
> *Define causality. State the necessary and sufficient condition for an LTI discrete-time system to be causal.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A discrete-time system is **causal** if its output $y(n)$ at any instant $n$ depends only on the **present** input $x(n)$ and **past** inputs $x(n-1), x(n-2), \ldots$, and is independent of all **future** inputs $x(n+1), x(n+2), \ldots$.  

For an **LTI system**, the necessary and sufficient condition for causality is that its impulse response be **right-sided**, i.e.,

$$h(n) = 0 \quad \text{for all} \quad n < 0$$

**Distribution of marks:** (1 mark) Definition, (1 mark) Time-domain condition, (1 mark) Interpretation in z-domain ROC being $\vert z \vert > r_{\max}$.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
> *Check whether the system $y(n) = x(-n)$ is causal. Justify your answer.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The system $y(n) = x(-n)$ **is not causal** (it is **anticausal**).  

**Justification:** The output $y(n)$ at time $n$ is the input sample at time $-n$. For $n = -1$, $y(-1) = x(1)$, which is a *future* input value relative to the current time $n = -1$. Since the output depends on a future input sample, the system violates causality.  

In terms of impulse response, $h(n) = \delta(n+1)$ which is **nonzero at $n = -1$** (i.e., $n < 0$), confirming the system is **non-causal**.

---

### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **Choose **either** Question A **or** Question B.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* Define causality and stability for an LTI discrete-time system. For the system function
$$H(z) = \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5z^{-1}}$$
determine whether the system is **causal** and **stable**.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* A causal LTI system has the impulse response
$$h(n) = \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^{n} u(n) + \left(-\tfrac{1}{3}\right)^{n} u(n)$$
Find the system function $H(z)$, its ROC, and verify causality and stability.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Definitions (2 marks):**
- **Causality:** A system is causal if $h(n) = 0$ for $n < 0$.
- **Stability (BIBO):** A system is stable if $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(n) \vert < \infty$.

**Step 2 — Identify poles of $H(z)$ (1 mark):**
The denominator $1 - 0.5 z^{-1} = 0 \Rightarrow z = 0.5$. So there is a **single pole at $z = 0.5$**.

**Step 3 — Determine ROC for a causal system (1 mark):**
Assuming the system is causal, the ROC is the exterior of the outermost pole:

$$\text{ROC}: \vert z \vert > 0.5$$

**Step 4 — Check stability (1 mark):**
Since the pole is at $z = 0.5$ and $\vert 0.5 \vert = 0.5 < 1$, the pole lies **inside the unit circle**.

**Step 5 — Conclusion (2 marks):**
- The system is **causal** ✓ (single pole, ROC is exterior of a circle).
- The system is **stable** ✓ (pole at $z = 0.5$ lies strictly inside the unit circle, and the ROC includes the unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$).

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Write $H(z)$ as z-transform of $h(n)$ (2 marks):**
Using the standard pair $a^n u(n) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{Z}} \dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}$, for $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(z) &= \frac{1}{1 - \frac{1}{2} z^{-1}} \;+\; \frac{1}{1 + \frac{1}{3} z^{-1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Combine into a single rational function (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(z) &= \frac{\left(1 + \frac{1}{3} z^{-1}\right) + \left(1 - \frac{1}{2} z^{-1}\right)}{\left(1 - \frac{1}{2} z^{-1}\right)\left(1 + \frac{1}{3} z^{-1}\right)} \\
&= \frac{2 - \frac{1}{6} z^{-1}}{1 + \frac{1}{6} z^{-1} - \frac{1}{6} z^{-2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Find ROC (1 mark):**
Poles are at $z = 0.5$ and $z = -1/3$. Since the system is **causal**, the ROC is the exterior of the outermost pole:

$$\text{ROC}: \vert z \vert > 0.5$$

**Step 4 — Verify causality and stability (2 marks):**
- **Causal:** $h(n) = 0$ for $n < 0$ (since $u(n)$ ensures right-sidedness) ✓
- **Stable:** Both poles satisfy $\vert 0.5 \vert < 1$ and $\vert -1/3 \vert < 1$, so the unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$ is included in the ROC ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024]`

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* Determine the causality of the following systems:
- (i) $y(n) = x(n) + 2x(n-1) - x(n-3)$
- (ii) $y(n) = x(n+1) - 2x(n) + x(n-1)$

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* An LTI system has the system function
$$H(z) = \frac{z^2 - 1}{z^2 - 0.64}$$
Determine its ROC if the system is (i) causal, and (ii) stable. State whether the system can be both causal and stable.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**System (i):** $y(n) = x(n) + 2x(n-1) - x(n-3)$

The output uses inputs at times $n$, $n-1$, and $n-3$ — all of which are **present or past** samples. There is **no future input** dependence. Therefore, **System (i) is CAUSAL**. (3.5 marks)

**System (ii):** $y(n) = x(n+1) - 2x(n) + x(n-1)$

The output uses input at time $n+1$, which is a **future** input value. Therefore, **System (ii) is NON-CAUSAL** (specifically, it is **anticausal-leaning**). (3.5 marks)

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Find the poles (1 mark):**
Setting the denominator to zero: $z^2 - 0.64 = 0 \Rightarrow z = \pm 0.8$.
So poles are at $z_1 = 0.8$ and $z_2 = -0.8$.

**Step 2 — ROC for causal system (1 mark):**
For a **causal** system, ROC is the exterior of the outermost pole:

$$\text{ROC}_\text{causal}: \vert z \vert > 0.8$$

**Step 3 — ROC for stable system (1 mark):**
For **stability**, the ROC must include the unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$. For a right-sided signal, this means the outermost pole must satisfy $\vert p_{\max} \vert < 1$. Since both poles have magnitude $0.8 < 1$, the ROC for stability is:

$$\text{ROC}_\text{stable}: \vert z \vert > 0.8$$

**Step 4 — Can the system be both causal AND stable? (4 marks):**
- For causality: ROC = $\vert z \vert > 0.8$
- For stability: ROC must include $\vert z \vert = 1$, which is satisfied when $\vert z \vert > 0.8$.

Since the conditions **coincide**, the system can be **both causal and stable simultaneously** when its ROC is $\vert z \vert > 0.8$. This is because both poles (at $\pm 0.8$) lie **strictly inside the unit circle**, satisfying the necessary and sufficient condition for a causal-stable LTI system. ✓

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in Causality questions (and where marks are lost):**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "Causal" with "Stable" (–2 marks):** These are **independent** properties. A system can be causal but unstable (e.g., $h(n) = 2^n u(n)$), or stable but non-causal (e.g., centered moving average). Always check **both** conditions separately.
> 
> 2. **Wrong ROC interpretation (–1 to –2 marks):** Many students write "ROC is $\vert z \vert > 0$" for *every* causal system. The correct ROC is $\vert z \vert > r_{\max}$, where $r_{\max}$ is the **modulus of the outermost pole**.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting to check $h(n)$ for $n < 0$ (–1 mark):** Always verify the time-domain condition $h(n) = 0$ for $n < 0$, not just the z-domain ROC. Sometimes a system may appear "causal-looking" in the z-domain but actually has an anticausal impulse response.
> 
> 4. **Misidentifying $y(n) = x(-n)$ as causal (–2 marks):** This is a classic trap! Since the output at time $n$ equals input at time $-n$, future inputs affect the output → **anticausal**.
> 
> 5. **Assuming "Memoryless = Causal" in the wrong direction:** Memoryless systems ARE always causal, but not all causal systems are memoryless. The accumulator $y(n) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x(k)$ is causal but has memory.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Causality — Key Takeaways:**
> 
> ✅ **Definition:** Output $y(n)$ depends only on present and past inputs — never on future inputs.
> 
> ✅ **LTI Causality Condition (Time Domain):** $h(n) = 0$ for $n < 0$.
> 
> ✅ **LTI Causality Condition (z-Domain):** ROC is the exterior of a circle: $\vert z \vert > r_{\max}$.
> 
> ✅ **Causal + Stable ⇒** All poles strictly inside the unit circle ($\vert p_k \vert < 1$).
> 
> ✅ **Memoryless systems are ALWAYS causal** (output depends only on $x(n)$).
> 
> ✅ **Pure delay** $y(n) = x(n-n_0)$, $n_0 \geq 0$ — **causal**.
> 
> ✅ **Pure advance** $y(n) = x(n+n_0)$, $n_0 > 0$ — **non-causal** (anticausal).
> 
> ✅ **Anticausal system:** $h(n) = 0$ for $n > 0$; ROC is interior of a circle $\vert z \vert < r_{\min}$.
> 
> ✅ **Two-sided (non-causal):** ROC is a **ring** $r_1 < \vert z \vert < r_2$.
> 
> ✅ **Real-time systems MUST be causal**; offline systems can be non-causal.
> 
> ✅ **Causality is a system property, not a signal property.**
> 
> ✅ **Stability and Causality are independent** — verify both in every KTU problem.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Invertibility
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT604 - DIGITAL SIGNALS AND PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Invertibility

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Invertibility of a Discrete-Time System (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard Definition)**
> A system **T** is said to be **invertible** if for **every distinct input** $x_1[n]$ and $x_2[n]$, the corresponding outputs $y_1[n] = T\{x_1[n]\}$ and $y_2[n] = T\{x_2[n]\}$ are also **distinct**. Equivalently, an invertible system possesses a well-defined **inverse system** $T^{-1}$ such that the cascade (series) connection of the original system followed by its inverse produces an **identity system** — i.e., the original input is perfectly recoverable at the output.

Mathematically, if $y[n] = T\{x[n]\}$, the invertibility condition is:

$$T^{-1}\{y[n]\} = T^{-1}\{T\{x[n]\}\} = x[n]$$

A system that fails this condition is called **non-invertible** (or lossy) — meaning at least two different inputs produce the **same** output, making unique recovery of the input mathematically impossible.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Emphasis:** The term *invertibility* must not be confused with the technical existence of an inverse in the algebraic sense. A system can be invertible in principle, but its inverse may be **non-causal** or **unstable** — a subtle but frequently tested distinction in board examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Building Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Photocopier & Shredder" Model**
> Imagine a **black-box machine** that accepts a printed document (input $x[n]$) and produces a stamped version (output $y[n]$).
> - **Invertible system:** The machine only adds a visible date-stamp. By using a "date-remover" device, you can recover the original document. The mapping is **one-to-one** (injective).
> - **Non-invertible system:** The machine **shreds** the document after stamping it, discarding the body. Even with the best "un-shredder," you cannot recover the original — information has been **irreversibly lost**.
> - **Invertible but inverse is unstable:** The machine encodes the document in a fragile format (e.g., a high-frequency carrier). The decoder exists mathematically, but tiny noise makes the recovered document explode in size — an **unstable inverse**.

## 1.3 Physical / Engineering Significance

| Property | Engineering Relevance |
|----------|----------------------|
| **Invertibility** | Determines whether a system preserves *all* information of the input. |
| **Causal & Stable Inverse** | Required for **real-time, robust signal recovery** (e.g., channel equalization, deconvolution). |
| **Non-invertibility** | Indicates inherent information loss (e.g., squaring, absolute value, clipping, saturation). |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Self-Test for Invertibility:** A system is invertible **if and only if** we can answer the question *"What input produced this output?"* with a **unique** answer. If multiple inputs could have produced the same output, the system is non-invertible.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The Four Pillars of Invertibility Analysis

The KTU 2024 Scheme board examinations test invertibility along **four distinct analytical axes**. Mastering these is non-negotiable for full marks.

### Pillar 1: Distinct-Output Test (Logical / Set-Theoretic Test)
