- A system $T$ is invertible if the mapping $x[n] \to y[n]$ is **one-to-one (injective)**.
- If $x_1[n] \neq x_2[n]$ but $T\{x_1[n]\} = T\{x_2[n]\}$ for some pair, the system is **non-invertible**.

### Pillar 2: Inverse-System Construction Test
- Find an explicit operator $T^{-1}$ such that $T^{-1}(T(x[n])) = x[n]$.
- If such an operator can be written as a valid LTI system (even if non-causal), the system is invertible.

### Pillar 3: LTI Transfer-Function Test
For an LTI system with rational transfer function $H(z)$:

$$H_{\text{inv}}(z) = \frac{1}{H(z)} = \frac{N(z)}{D(z)}$$

- The **zeros of $H(z)$ become the poles of $H_{\text{inv}}(z)$**.
- The **poles of $H(z)$ become the zeros of $H_{\text{inv}}(z)$**.
- **Invertibility condition:** $H(z) \neq 0$ for all $z$ on the region of convergence of $H(z)$ — i.e., $H(z)$ has **no zeros** in the ROC.

### Pillar 4: Causal & Stable Invertibility
- The system is invertible in the **strongest engineering sense** (causal + stable inverse) **iff** all **zeros** of $H(z)$ lie **strictly inside the unit circle** $|z| < 1$.
- This is the celebrated **Minimum-Phase Condition** in DSP.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A common trap question asks *"Is $y[n] = x[n] + x[n-1]$ invertible?"* The answer is **YES** (it is invertible), but its inverse $H_{\text{inv}}(z) = \frac{1}{1+z^{-1}}$ has a pole at $z = -1$ on the unit circle — hence the inverse is **marginally stable / not stable**, not a causal stable LTI system. Many students incorrectly mark it as non-invertible.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Mathematical Expression | Key Condition / Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---------|------------------------|----------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Invertibility definition | $T^{-1}\{T\{x[n]\}\} = x[n]$ | One-to-one mapping | All reversible DSP operations |
| 2 | Cascade identity | $H(z) \cdot H_{\text{inv}}(z) = 1$ | Frequency domain identity | System design & equalization |
| 3 | Inverse transfer function | $H_{\text{inv}}(z) = \dfrac{1}{H(z)}$ | Must be proper / well-defined | Deconvolution, inverse filtering |
| 4 | Zeros ↔ Poles swap | Zeros of $H$ → Poles of $H^{-1}$ | Pole-zero symmetry | Stability analysis |
| 5 | Causal Stable Inverse | All zeros of $H(z)$ inside $\vert z \vert < 1$ | Unit circle reference | Minimum-phase systems |
| 6 | Delay element inverse | $y[n] = x[n-N] \Rightarrow x[n] = y[n+N]$ | Shift is always invertible | Buffering, synchronization |
| 7 | Accumulator inverse | $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x[k] \Rightarrow x[n] = y[n] - y[n-1]$ | Difference is inverse of sum | Integration / differentiation |
| 8 | Modulo inverse | $y[n] = (x[n] \mod M)$ | Non-invertible for $M > 1$ | Overflow, wrap-around effects |
| 9 | Squaring inverse | $y[n] = x^2[n] \Rightarrow x[n] = \pm\sqrt{y[n]}$ | Two solutions → non-invertible | Power measurement, envelope |
| 10 | Identity system | $H(z) = 1$, self-inverse | Self-invertible | Reference / benchmark |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Note:** In the table above, $\vert z \vert < 1$ uses the typeset form for absolute value to prevent markdown table syntax breakage. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in tables.

## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Channel Equalization in Communication:** A communication channel distorts the transmitted signal via $H_c(z)$. The receiver applies $H_{\text{eq}}(z) = 1/H_c(z)$ to recover the original — a textbook application of invertibility.
- **Image Deblurring:** A blurred image is the convolution of the sharp image with a point-spread function (PSF). Deconvolution ($H^{-1}(z)$) is invertible blur removal.
- **Audio Reverberation Removal:** Inverse filtering cancels room impulse response — possible only if the room response is invertible (no non-minimum-phase zeros).
- **Control Systems:** An LTI plant is *minimum-phase* iff its inverse is stable; this determines whether stable feedback control is achievable.
- **Biomedical Signal Processing:** ECG denoising, EEG artifact removal — invertibility ensures no diagnostic information is destroyed.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Invertibility of the First-Difference System

**Problem Setup:** Determine whether the system described by $y[n] = x[n] - x[n-1]$ is invertible, and if so, derive its inverse.

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation

Starting with the system equation:

$$y[n] = x[n] - x[n-1]$$

Rearrange to isolate $x[n]$ on one side:

$$x[n] = y[n] + x[n-1]$$

Now we can express the *running* form by iterating the recursion:

$$x[n] = y[n] + y[n-1] + x[n-2]$$

Continuing this expansion one more step to make the pattern explicit:

$$x[n] = y[n] + y[n-1] + y[n-2] + x[n-3]$$

After $k$ such iterations, the general pattern emerges as:

$$x[n] = \sum_{m=0}^{k-1} y[n-m] + x[n-k]$$

In the limit as $k \to \infty$, assuming the input is *causal* ($x[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$), the boundary term $x[n-k] \to 0$, giving:

$$x[n] = \sum_{m=0}^{\infty} y[n-m]$$

This is precisely the **discrete-time accumulator** — the *causal* inverse of the first-difference system.

### Z-Domain Verification

Take the $z$-transform of the system equation. Recall the time-shift property: $x[n-1] \leftrightarrow z^{-1} X(z)$.

$$Y(z) = X(z) - z^{-1} X(z) = X(z)\,(1 - z^{-1})$$

Therefore, the transfer function is:

$$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = 1 - z^{-1}$$

The inverse transfer function is computed by direct algebraic inversion:

$$H_{\text{inv}}(z) = \frac{1}{H(z)} = \frac{1}{1 - z^{-1}} = \frac{z}{z - 1}$$

**Pole-Zero Analysis:**
- $H(z) = 1 - z^{-1}$ has a **zero at $z = 1$** and a pole at $z = 0$.
- $H_{\text{inv}}(z) = \frac{z}{z-1}$ has a **pole at $z = 1$** and a zero at $z = 0$.
- The pole of $H_{\text{inv}}$ lies **on the unit circle** — the inverse is **marginally stable** (a pure accumulator).

### Conclusion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The first-difference system $y[n] = x[n] - x[n-1]$ is **invertible** (it is one-to-one), and its inverse is the **discrete accumulator** $x[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} y[k]$. However, this inverse is **not stable** in the strict BIBO sense — a small DC offset in $y[n]$ causes $x[n]$ to grow without bound.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Invertibility of $y[n] = nx[n]$

**Problem Setup:** Test the invertibility of the time-varying system $y[n] = n\,x[n]$.

### Analytical Approach

The system is **invertible at every sample where $n \neq 0$**, because for $n \neq 0$:

$$x[n] = \frac{y[n]}{n}$$

However, at $n = 0$:

$$y[0] = 0 \cdot x[0] = 0$$

regardless of the value of $x[0]$. Therefore, $x[0]$ cannot be uniquely recovered from $y[0] = 0$ — the system is **non-invertible** at $n = 0$.

### Formal Conclusion

> [!NOTE]
> The system $y[n] = n\,x[n]$ is **NOT globally invertible** because the coefficient $n = 0$ at the origin destroys all information about $x[0]$. A common KTU question variation asks: *"Is $y[n] = (n+1)x[n]$ invertible?"* — here, the coefficient $(n+1)$ is **never zero**, so the system is **globally invertible** with inverse $x[n] = \dfrac{y[n]}{n+1}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Invertibility of the Squaring System

**Problem Setup:** Test $y[n] = x^2[n]$.

### Analytical Approach

If $x_1[n] = +a$ and $x_2[n] = -a$ (with $a \neq 0$), then:

$$y_1[n] = (+a)^2 = a^2 \quad \text{and} \quad y_2[n] = (-a)^2 = a^2$$

Thus $y_1[n] = y_2[n]$ but $x_1[n] \neq x_2[n]$. The mapping is **not one-to-one** (it is two-to-one for every non-zero output), and the **sign of the input is irreversibly lost**.

### Conclusion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The squaring system $y[n] = x^2[n]$ is **strictly non-invertible**. The inverse $x[n] = \pm\sqrt{y[n]}$ returns **two** valid solutions for every positive $y[n]$, and the original sign cannot be recovered from the output alone.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — KTU-Boards Quick-Reference for Common Systems

| System Equation $y[n] = $ | Invertible? | Inverse System | Caveat / KTU Note |
|---------------------------|-------------|----------------|--------------------|
| $x[n]$ | ✅ Yes (Identity) | $x[n] = y[n]$ | Self-inverse; canonical reference |
| $x[n] - x[n-1]$ | ✅ Yes | $x[n] = \sum y[k]$ (accumulator) | Inverse is not stable |
| $x[n] + x[n-1]$ | ✅ Yes | $x[n] = y[n] - y[n-1] + y[n-2] - \dots$ | Inverse is recursive, needs initial condition |
| $x[n-N]$ (pure delay) | ✅ Yes | $x[n] = y[n+N]$ (advance) | Inverse is non-causal |
| $\sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x[k]$ (accumulator) | ✅ Yes | $x[n] = y[n] - y[n-1]$ (difference) | Inverse is causal & stable |
| $n \cdot x[n]$ | ❌ No | — | Loses info at $n = 0$ |
| $(n+1) \cdot x[n]$ | ✅ Yes | $x[n] = y[n]/(n+1)$ | Never-zero coefficient |
| $x^2[n]$ | ❌ No | — | Sign information lost |
| $\vert x[n] \vert$ | ❌ No | — | Sign information lost |
| $e^{x[n]}$ | ❌ No | — | Cannot recover negative inputs |
| $0$ (zero system) | ❌ No | — | All inputs map to same output |
| $\cos(x[n])$ | ❌ No | — | Periodic ambiguity |
| $x[n] \mod M$ | ❌ No (for $M>1$) | — | Modular wrap-around loses info |
| $a \cdot x[n]$, $a \neq 0$ | ✅ Yes | $x[n] = y[n]/a$ | Scalar gain is invertible |
| $\max\{x[n], x[n-1]\}$ | ❌ No | — | Ordering information lost |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Invertibility Cascade

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **canonical invertibility architecture** used universally in DSP textbooks and KTU reference material.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeX["INPUT x of n"] --> nodeA["SYSTEM T : Forward LTI Block"]
    nodeA --> nodeY["OUTPUT y of n = T applied to x of n"]
    nodeY --> nodeB["INVERSE SYSTEM T INVERSE : Recovery Block"]
    nodeB --> nodeZ["RECOVERED INPUT x hat of n equals x of n"]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **forward path** (top) maps the input through system $T$, producing $y[n]$.
- The **inverse path** (bottom) maps the output back through $T^{-1}$, recovering $\hat{x}[n]$.
- **Perfect invertibility** means $\hat{x}[n] \equiv x[n]$ for *all* valid inputs.

---

## 4.2 Decision Tree — KTU-Boards Invertibility Test Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["START: System Equation y of n equals T applied to x of n"] --> nodeQ1{"Q1: Are distinct inputs guaranteed to produce distinct outputs?"}
    nodeQ1 -- "YES" --> nodeQ2["Mark system as INVERTIBLE in principle"]
    nodeQ1 -- "NO" --> nodeNoInv["Mark system as NON INVERTIBLE : information is lost"]
    nodeQ2 --> nodeQ3{"Q2: Can a valid LTI inverse system be constructed?"}
    nodeQ3 -- "YES, causal and stable" --> nodeFullInv["STRONG INVERTIBILITY : all zeros of H of z inside unit circle"]
    nodeQ3 -- "YES, but non causal or unstable" --> nodeWeakInv["WEAK INVERTIBILITY : inverse exists but is non causal or unstable"]
    nodeQ3 -- "NO" --> nodeNoInv
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Strategy Tip:** When you face an invertibility problem in the exam, always walk through these two questions in this order. The board answer key rewards students who **distinguish "invertible" from "invertible with stable inverse."**

---

## 4.3 Conceptual State Diagram — Information Flow Under Invertibility

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA1["Input Space X : all possible input signals x of n"] -->|"Forward T"| nodeB1["Output Space Y : all possible output signals y of n"]
    nodeB1 -->|"Inverse T INVERSE"| nodeA1
    nodeSub1["Distinct inputs x1 of n, x2 of n with x1 not equal to x2"] -.->|"Mapped by T"| nodeSub2["Distinct outputs y1 of n, y2 of n with y1 not equal to y2"]
    nodeSub1 -.->|"Mapped by T INVERSE"| nodeB1
    nodeB1 -.->|"Recovered uniquely"| nodeSub1
```

---

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks Total)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]
**"Define invertibility of a discrete-time system. State any two conditions under which a system is non-invertible."** *(3 Marks)*

### Model Answer (3 Marks — Step-by-Step)

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
A discrete-time system $T$ is said to be **invertible** if distinct inputs always produce distinct outputs. Equivalently, there exists an inverse system $T^{-1}$ such that:

$$T^{-1}\{T\{x[n]\}\} = x[n] \quad \text{for all } x[n]$$

The cascade of $T$ followed by $T^{-1}$ yields the **identity system**.

**Two Conditions for Non-Invertibility (1.5 Marks — 0.75 each):**
1. **Information loss at specific samples:** e.g., $y[n] = n \cdot x[n]$ — at $n=0$, the output is always $y[0]=0$ regardless of $x[0]$, making unique recovery impossible.
2. **Sign/phase ambiguity:** e.g., $y[n] = x^2[n]$ or $y[n] = \vert x[n] \vert$ — the sign of the input is lost, so two distinct inputs ($\pm a$) map to the same output ($a^2$).

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Retest]
**"Is the system $y[n] = x[n] + x[n-1]$ invertible? Justify your answer using the transfer function."** *(3 Marks)*

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Claim (0.5 Marks):** Yes, the system **is invertible**, but the inverse is **not stable**.

**Transfer Function (1 Mark):** Taking the $z$-transform:

$$Y(z) = X(z) + z^{-1} X(z) \implies H(z) = 1 + z^{-1} = \frac{z+1}{z}$$

**Zero Location (0.5 Mark):** $H(z)$ has a zero at $z = -1$ — this lies **on** the unit circle ($\vert -1 \vert = 1$).

**Inverse Transfer Function (0.5 Mark):**

$$H_{\text{inv}}(z) = \frac{1}{H(z)} = \frac{1}{1+z^{-1}} = \frac{z}{z+1}$$

**Conclusion (0.5 Mark):** The inverse has a pole at $z = -1$ (on the unit circle), so it is **marginally stable / not strictly stable**. The system is invertible in principle, but does not possess a stable causal LTI inverse. *(Note: KTU answer key accepts "invertible, inverse not stable" as full marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]

**(a)** Define system invertibility. Test the invertibility of the following systems and find the inverse where it exists: *(7 Marks)*
- (i) $y[n] = x[n] - x[n-1]$
- (ii) $y[n] = \cos(\pi n / 4) \cdot x[n]$

**(b)** For the LTI system with transfer function $H(z) = \dfrac{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.9 z^{-1}}$, determine whether the system is invertible. If yes, find $H_{\text{inv}}(z)$ and state the conditions under which the inverse is **both causal and stable**. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

### Part (a) — Invertibility Tests (7 Marks)

**(i) System: $y[n] = x[n] - x[n-1]$** *(3.5 Marks)*

**Step 1 — Transfer Function (1 Mark):** Applying the $z$-transform:

$$Y(z) = X(z)(1 - z^{-1}) \implies H(z) = 1 - z^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Pole-Zero Pattern (0.5 Mark):** Zero at $z = 1$ (on unit circle), pole at $z = 0$ (inside unit circle).

**Step 3 — Inverse Transfer Function (1 Mark):**

$$H_{\text{inv}}(z) = \frac{1}{1 - z^{-1}} = \frac{z}{z - 1}$$

**Step 4 — Inverse System in Time Domain (1 Mark):** The pole at $z = 1$ corresponds to the recursive difference equation:

$$x[n] = y[n] + x[n-1] \implies x[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} y[k]$$

This is the **accumulator** (discrete integrator).

**Conclusion:** ✅ The system is **invertible**, and its inverse is the **accumulator**. However, since the zero of $H(z)$ lies on the unit circle, the inverse is **not strictly BIBO stable** (it produces a pole on the unit circle).

---

**(ii) System: $y[n] = \cos(\pi n / 4) \cdot x[n]$** *(3.5 Marks)*

**Step 1 — Examine the coefficient:** The factor $\cos(\pi n / 4)$ takes the following values for integer $n$:

$$n=0:\ 1, \quad n=1:\ \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}, \quad n=2:\ 0, \quad n=3:\ -\tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}, \quad n=4:\ -1, \ldots$$

**Step 2 — Identify zero-coefficient sample (1 Mark):** At $n = 2$, $\cos(\pi/2) = 0$, so:

$$y[2] = 0 \cdot x[2] = 0 \quad \text{regardless of } x[2]$$

**Step 3 — Loss of information (1 Mark):** The input $x[2]$ cannot be recovered from $y[2]$ — infinitely many values of $x[2]$ all map to $y[2] = 0$.

**Conclusion (1.5 Marks):** ❌ The system is **non-invertible** because the multiplicative coefficient $\cos(\pi n / 4)$ is zero at $n = 2$ (and periodically thereafter), erasing the input at those samples.

---

### Part (b) — Inverse Transfer Function of LTI System (7 Marks)

**Given:** $H(z) = \dfrac{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.9 z^{-1}}$

**Step 1 — Identify Pole and Zero (1 Mark):**
- Zero of $H(z)$: $1 - 0.5 z^{-1} = 0 \implies z = 0.5$ → **inside** the unit circle ($\vert 0.5 \vert < 1$).
- Pole of $H(z)$: $1 - 0.9 z^{-1} = 0 \implies z = 0.9$ → **inside** the unit circle ($\vert 0.9 \vert < 1$).

**Step 2 — Construct the Inverse (1 Mark):**

$$H_{\text{inv}}(z) = \frac{1}{H(z)} = \frac{1 - 0.9 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}$$

**Step 3 — Pole-Zero Swap Visualization (1 Mark):**
- Poles of $H_{\text{inv}}$ (zeros of $H$): $z = 0.5$ (inside unit circle).
- Zeros of $H_{\text{inv}}$ (poles of $H$): $z = 0.9$ (inside unit circle).

**Step 4 — Causal + Stable Inverse Conditions (2 Marks):**
For the inverse to be **causal and stable**, all its poles must lie **strictly inside the unit circle** $\vert z \vert < 1$. The pole of $H_{\text{inv}}$ at $z = 0.5$ satisfies $\vert 0.5 \vert < 1$. ✅

**Step 5 — Causality check (1 Mark):** The ROC of $H_{\text{inv}}$ for a right-sided (causal) sequence is $\vert z \vert > 0.5$, which contains the unit circle → causal & stable inverse exists.

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** ✅ The system is **invertible with a causal and stable inverse**, because the **single zero** of $H(z)$ lies strictly inside the unit circle. This is a **minimum-phase** system.

---

### Question B [Alternative Choice — KTU University Exam Model Paper, Dec 2023 Retest]

**(a)** Explain the concept of invertibility of discrete-time systems. For each of the following systems, determine whether the system is invertible. If yes, find the inverse. *(7 Marks)*
- (i) $y[n] = nx[n-1]$
- (ii) $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x[k]$ (accumulator)
- (iii) $y[n] = x[2n]$ (downsampler)

**(b)** An LTI system is described by the difference equation $y[n] - 0.8 y[n-1] = x[n] + 0.6 x[n-1]$. Find $H(z)$, determine if the system is invertible, find $H_{\text{inv}}(z)$, and check whether the inverse is causal and stable. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

### Part (a) — Conceptual + Three-System Analysis (7 Marks)

**Conceptual Explanation (1 Mark):** A system is invertible if every distinct input produces a distinct output, equivalently if an inverse system $T^{-1}$ exists such that $T^{-1}(T(x[n])) = x[n]$.

**(i) System: $y[n] = n \cdot x[n-1]$** *(2 Marks)*

At sample $n = 0$: $y[0] = 0 \cdot x[-1] = 0$, so $x[-1]$ cannot be recovered. ❌ **Non-invertible** due to the multiplicative zero coefficient at $n=0$.

**(ii) System: $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x[k]$** *(2 Marks)*

Applying the first-difference (backward difference):

$$y[n] - y[n-1] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x[k] - \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n-1} x[k] = x[n]$$

Therefore $x[n] = y[n] - y[n-1]$ — the inverse is the **first-difference operator**, which is **causal and stable**. ✅ **Invertible** (strong invertibility).

**(iii) System: $y[n] = x[2n]$ (downsampler by 2)** *(2 Marks)*

The downsampler discards the odd-indexed samples of $x[n]$. For example, $x[0]$ and $x[2]$ both contribute to $y[0] = x[0]$ and $y[1] = x[2]$ respectively, but the *odd-indexed* samples $x[1], x[3], x[5], \ldots$ are never transmitted to the output. They are **irretrievably lost**. ❌ **Non-invertible**.

---

### Part (b) — LTI System Invertibility (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Take the $z$-transform (1 Mark):**

$$Y(z) - 0.8 z^{-1} Y(z) = X(z) + 0.6 z^{-1} X(z)$$

$$Y(z)(1 - 0.8 z^{-1}) = X(z)(1 + 0.6 z^{-1})$$

**Step 2 — Transfer Function (1 Mark):**

$$H(z) = \frac{1 + 0.6 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.8 z^{-1}}$$

**Step 3 — Identify poles and zeros (1 Mark):**
- Zero: $1 + 0.6 z^{-1} = 0 \implies z = -0.6$, magnitude $\vert -0.6 \vert = 0.6 < 1$ → **inside** unit circle.
- Pole: $1 - 0.8 z^{-1} = 0 \implies z = 0.8$, magnitude $\vert 0.8 \vert = 0.8 < 1$ → **inside** unit circle.

**Step 4 — Inverse Transfer Function (1 Mark):**

$$H_{\text{inv}}(z) = \frac{1 - 0.8 z^{-1}}{1 + 0.6 z^{-1}}$$

**Step 5 — Inverse system difference equation (1 Mark):** Cross-multiplying $H_{\text{inv}}(z) = X(z)/Y(z)$:

$$X(z)(1 + 0.6 z^{-1}) = Y(z)(1 - 0.8 z^{-1})$$

$$x[n] + 0.6\, x[n-1] = y[n] - 0.8\, y[n-1]$$

$$x[n] = -0.6\, x[n-1] + y[n] - 0.8\, y[n-1]$$

**Step 6 — Causal & Stable? (1 Mark):** The pole of $H_{\text{inv}}$ is at $z = -0.6$ (inside unit circle), and the ROC for a causal sequence is $\vert z \vert > 0.6$, which contains the unit circle. ✅ **The inverse is both causal and stable.**

**Conclusion (0.5 Mark):** The system is **strongly invertible** (minimum-phase) with a causal, stable LTI inverse given by the recursive equation above.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks**
> 1. **Conflating "invertible" with "invertible with stable inverse":** Many students write *"the system $y[n] = x[n] - x[n-1]$ is non-invertible"* because the inverse accumulator is not stable. The correct KTU-accepted answer is *"invertible, but inverse not stable."* This distinction alone is worth **1–2 marks** on a 7-mark question.
> 2. **Ignoring the zero-location test:** When asked about LTI invertibility, students often forget to **explicitly state** that the zero(s) of $H(z)$ determine the poles of $H^{-1}(z)$, and the **location of zeros relative to the unit circle** determines stability. Always draw the pole-zero plot.
> 3. **Treating time-varying systems like LTI systems:** For $y[n] = n\,x[n]$, students incorrectly invoke $H(z) = z\frac{d}{dz}$ tricks or write a transfer function. **Time-varying systems do not have a transfer function** in the conventional LTI sense. Use the *direct output equation* to test.
> 4. **Forgetting the "downsampler is non-invertible" rule:** The system $y[n] = x[2n]$ is a favorite KTU trick question. Students often answer "invertible with $x[n] = y[n/2]$" — but this recovers only *even* samples, leaving odd samples lost. It is **non-invertible**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print Before Exam)**

- [ ] **Invertibility** ⇔ one-to-one mapping from input to output space.
- [ ] **Formal test:** Find $T^{-1}$ such that $T^{-1}(T(x[n])) = x[n]$ for all $x[n]$.
- [ ] **LTI test:** $H_{\text{inv}}(z) = 1/H(z)$ exists iff $H(z) \neq 0$ on its ROC.
- [ ] **Strong invertibility (causal + stable inverse)** ⇔ **all zeros of $H(z)$ lie strictly inside the unit circle** $\vert z \vert < 1$ → *minimum-phase* system.
- [ ] **Weak invertibility:** Inverse exists but is non-causal, unstable, or both.
- [ ] **Pole-zero swap rule:** Zeros of $H$ become poles of $H^{-1}$, and vice versa.
- [ ] **Common invertible systems:** Pure delay $x[n-N]$ (non-causal inverse), accumulator (difference inverse), scalar gain $a \neq 0$, time-shift $x[n-n_0]$.
- [ ] **Common non-invertible systems:** $y[n] = 0$, $y[n] = x^2[n]$, $y[n] = \vert x[n] \vert$, $y[n] = e^{x[n]}$, $y[n] = n \cdot x[n]$ (loses $x[0]$), $y[n] = x[2n]$ (downsampler), $y[n] = x[n] \bmod M$.
- [ ] **First-difference is invertible; its inverse is the accumulator** — but the inverse is **not strictly stable** (pole at $z=1$ on unit circle).
- [ ] **$y[n] = x[n] + x[n-1]$ is invertible** (zero at $z=-1$, inverse marginally stable) — students frequently mark this *non-invertible*. It is **invertible, weakly**.
- [ ] **Time-varying systems** must be tested **point-by-point**; the $z$-transform method applies only to **LTI** systems.
- [ ] **Cascade identity:** $H(z) \cdot H_{\text{inv}}(z) = 1$ — a direct verification test for any candidate inverse.
- [ ] **KTU-Favorite Trap:** "Squaring" and "absolute value" lose the **sign** of the input → non-invertible.
- [ ] **KTU-Favorite Trap:** "Downsampling" and "modulo-$M$" operations are **irreversible** in general.
- [ ] **Engineering slogan to remember:** *"A system is invertible iff it preserves information; it has a stable inverse iff that information can be recovered causally in real time."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT604 - DIGITAL SIGNALS AND PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Discrete-Time Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) Systems**, **Stability** refers to the **Bounded-Input Bounded-Output (BIBO) stability** criterion. A discrete-time LTI system is said to be **BIBO stable** if and only if every bounded input sequence $x[n]$ produces a bounded output sequence $y[n]$.

Mathematically, the system is BIBO stable if and only if there exists a finite constant $M$ such that:

$$\vert y[n] \vert \leq M < \infty \quad \text{for all } n$$

whenever the input is bounded, i.e., $\vert x[n] \vert \leq K < \infty$ for all $n$.

For an LTI system, this condition is equivalent to requiring that the **impulse response $h[n]$ is absolutely summable**:

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Stability of discrete-time systems is assessed via (1) impulse response summability and (2) pole-location criterion in the Z-plane. Both are **mandatory** for ESE and internal assessments.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **leaking bucket** with a small hole at the bottom. You pour water (input) into the bucket, and the water level (output) rises. The system is **stable** because no matter how much water you pour (bounded input), the bucket never overflows catastrophically — the water leaks out at a rate faster than you can pour.

Now imagine the same bucket with the hole **plugged** (no leakage). If you keep pouring water (input), the level keeps rising until it overflows. This represents an **unstable system** — the output grows without bound even though the input was bounded.

In DSP terms:
- The **bucket** = the LTI system with impulse response $h[n]$
- The **water input** = bounded input $x[n]$
- The **water level** = output $y[n]$
- The **leak rate** = how fast the impulse response $h[n]$ decays to zero

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Takeaway:** A system is *stable* when its internal "memory" (impulse response) doesn't accumulate forever. The output must settle down and not "run away" to infinity.

### 1.3 Physical / Engineering Significance

In real-world engineering, instability can lead to:
- **Oscillator overflow** in digital filters (saturation at $\pm 1$ in fixed-point arithmetic)
- **Catastrophic feedback** in control systems (motors spinning out of control)
- **Numerical overflow** in DSP processors

Hence, ensuring stability is the **first design step** in any digital filter or discrete-time control system.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conditions for BIBO Stability — The Two Equivalent Criteria

**Criterion 1: Time-Domain (Impulse Response Criterion)**
- An LTI system is BIBO stable **if and only if** its impulse response $h[n]$ is **absolutely summable**:

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$$

- **Why this works:** The output is the convolution $y[n] = x[n] * h[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] \, h[n-k]$. If both $x[k]$ and the sum of $h[\cdot]$ are bounded, the convolution result is finite.

**Criterion 2: Z-Domain (Pole-Location Criterion)**
- An LTI system is BIBO stable **if and only if** the **Region of Convergence (ROC)** of its system function $H(z)$ includes the **unit circle** $\vert z \vert = 1$.

- Equivalently, **all poles of $H(z)$ must lie strictly inside the unit circle** in the Z-plane (i.e., $\vert p_k \vert < 1$ for every pole $p_k$).

> [!TIP]
> **Why the unit circle?** When we evaluate $H(z)$ on the unit circle ($z = e^{j\omega}$), we obtain the **Frequency Response** $H(e^{j\omega})$ — i.e., the DTFT of $h[n]$. For the DTFT to exist, the impulse response must be absolutely summable, which is exactly the BIBO condition.

### 2.2 Special Cases: Causal and Anti-Causal Systems

For a **causal LTI system** (right-sided impulse response, $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$):
- ROC is of the form $\vert z \vert > r_{\max}$, where $r_{\max}$ is the radius of the outermost pole.
- **BIBO Stable Causal System:** ROC includes unit circle $\Rightarrow$ $r_{\max} < 1$ $\Rightarrow$ all poles **inside** the unit circle.

For an **anti-causal LTI system** (left-sided impulse response, $h[n] = 0$ for $n > 0$):
- ROC is of the form $\vert z \vert < r_{\min}$, where $r_{\min}$ is the radius of the innermost pole.
- **BIBO Stable Anti-Causal System:** ROC includes unit circle $\Rightarrow$ $r_{\min} > 1$ $\Rightarrow$ all poles **outside** the unit circle.

### 2.3 Pole Location and Time-Domain Behavior

The position of poles directly determines how the impulse response behaves:

| Pole Location | Impulse Response Behavior | Stability |
|---------------|---------------------------|-----------|
| $\vert p_k \vert < 1$ (inside unit circle) | Decays exponentially as $n \to \infty$ | **Stable** |
| $\vert p_k \vert = 1$ (on unit circle, simple) | Oscillates with constant amplitude (e.g., sinusoid) | **Marginally Stable** (not BIBO) |
| $\vert p_k \vert = 1$ (on unit circle, repeated) | Amplitude grows as $n^k$ | **Unstable** |
| $\vert p_k \vert > 1$ (outside unit circle) | Grows exponentially as $n \to \infty$ | **Unstable** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** A simple pole on the unit circle gives a sustained oscillation (marginally stable). For BIBO stability, the unit circle must lie in the ROC — so **no pole can lie on the unit circle**.

### 2.4 Stability of LTI Systems Described by Difference Equations

For a system described by a Linear Constant-Coefficient Difference Equation (LCCDE):

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, y[n-k] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x[n-k]$$

The system function is:

$$H(z) = \frac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, z^{-k}}{\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, z^{-k}} = \frac{B(z)}{A(z)}$$

The poles are the roots of the **characteristic polynomial** $A(z) = 0$. The system is stable **iff all N poles lie strictly inside the unit circle**.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Notes |
|---|---------|---------------------|-------|
| 1 | BIBO Stability (Time Domain) | $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$ | Absolute summability |
| 2 | BIBO Stability (Z-Domain) | ROC of $H(z)$ includes $\vert z \vert = 1$ | Equivalent to criterion 1 |
| 3 | Pole-Location Criterion | $\vert p_k \vert < 1 \quad \forall k$ | All poles strictly inside unit circle |
| 4 | Causal Stable ROC | $\vert z \vert > r_{\max}$ with $r_{\max} < 1$ | Causal + BIBO stable |
| 5 | Anti-Causal Stable ROC | $\vert z \vert < r_{\min}$ with $r_{\min} > 1$ | Anti-causal + BIBO stable |
| 6 | Marginal Stability | $\vert p_k \vert = 1$, simple pole | Sustained oscillation |
| 7 | Marginal Stability Check | $h[n]$ bounded but not absolutely summable | Not BIBO stable |
| 8 | Output Bound | $\vert y[n] \vert \leq K \sum_{k} \vert h[k] \vert$ | BIBO input-output relation |
| 9 | System Function | $H(z) = \frac{B(z)}{A(z)} = \frac{\sum b_k z^{-k}}{\sum a_k z^{-k}}$ | Poles = roots of $A(z)$ |
| 10 | Unstable System | $\exists \, p_k : \vert p_k \vert \geq 1$ | Any pole on/outside unit circle |

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **Digital Filter Design:** IIR filter coefficients are chosen so all poles lie inside the unit circle, ensuring stability.
- **Audio Processing:** Unstable filters produce distortion and "exploding" audio — stability is non-negotiable.
- **Biomedical Signal Processing:** ECG/EEG filters must be stable; instability could produce life-threatening artifacts.
- **Control Systems:** Discrete-time controllers (e.g., in digital PID) require pole placement inside the unit circle for closed-loop stability.
- **Communication Receivers:** Adaptive filters in echo cancellers need guaranteed stability to avoid divergence.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: BIBO Stability $\iff$ Absolute Summability of $h[n]$

**Goal:** Prove that an LTI system is BIBO stable **if and only if** $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$.

**Step 1: Setup the output via convolution.**
For an LTI system with impulse response $h[n]$ and input $x[n]$, the output is:

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] \, h[n-k]$$

**Step 2: Bound the input.**
Assume bounded input, so $\vert x[k] \vert \leq K$ for all $k$, where $K$ is a finite positive constant.

**Step 3: Apply the triangle inequality.**
Taking absolute value on both sides of the convolution sum:

$$\vert y[n] \vert = \left\vert \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] \, h[n-k] \right\vert$$

$$\vert y[n] \vert \leq \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[k] \vert \cdot \vert h[n-k] \vert$$

**Step 4: Substitute the input bound.**
Using $\vert x[k] \vert \leq K$:

$$\vert y[n] \vert \leq K \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n-k] \vert$$

**Step 5: Substitute $m = n - k$.**
This re-indexes the sum (since summing over all $k$ is equivalent to summing over all $m$):

$$\vert y[n] \vert \leq K \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[m] \vert$$

**Step 6: Define the absolute sum.**
Let $S_h = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[m] \vert$. If $S_h < \infty$ (finite), then:

$$\vert y[n] \vert \leq K \cdot S_h < \infty$$

Hence $y[n]$ is bounded for all $n$. $\blacksquare$

**Step 7: Necessity (converse direction).**
If $\sum \vert h[n] \vert = \infty$, choose a specific bounded input:

$$x[n] = \frac{\text{sgn}(h[-n])}{A}$$

for some $A > 0$. Then $y[0] = \sum_k x[k] h[-k] = \frac{1}{A} \sum_k \vert h[k] \vert = \infty$, which is unbounded. This violates BIBO stability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** BIBO stability $\iff$ absolute summability of $h[n]$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Pole-Location Equivalence to ROC Containing Unit Circle

**Step 1: Express $h[n]$ as a sum of exponentials.**
For a causal LTI system with simple poles $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_N$, the partial fraction expansion of $H(z)$ gives:

$$h[n] = \sum_{k=1}^{N} A_k \, (p_k)^n \, u[n]$$

**Step 2: Examine absolute summability.**
For $h[n]$ to be absolutely summable, we require:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \left\vert \sum_{k=1}^{N} A_k \, (p_k)^n \right\vert < \infty$$

**Step 3: Sufficient condition.**
If $\vert p_k \vert < 1$ for all $k$, then each term $\vert A_k \vert \cdot \vert p_k \vert^n$ is a decaying geometric sequence:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \vert A_k \vert \cdot \vert p_k \vert^n = \frac{\vert A_k \vert}{1 - \vert p_k \vert} < \infty$$

Summing over all $k$ gives a finite total. Hence the system is BIBO stable.

**Step 4: Necessary condition.**
If any pole $\vert p_k \vert \geq 1$, the corresponding term does not decay. Even a single pole with $\vert p_k \vert > 1$ causes $h[n]$ to grow without bound. If $\vert p_k \vert = 1$ (simple pole), the term oscillates with constant amplitude and the sum is not absolutely summable. Hence the system is unstable.

**Step 5: Link to ROC.**
The ROC of a causal system is the exterior of the outermost pole: $\vert z \vert > \max_k \vert p_k \vert$. For the ROC to include the unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$, we need:

$$1 > \max_k \vert p_k \vert \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \vert p_k \vert < 1 \quad \forall k$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This proves the equivalence: **All poles inside the unit circle $\iff$ ROC includes the unit circle $\iff$ BIBO stable.**

---

### 3.3 Analytical Example: Stability Check of a Given System

**Problem:** Determine the BIBO stability of the system:

$$H(z) = \frac{1}{1 - 1.5 z^{-1} + 0.5 z^{-2}}$$

**Step 1: Convert to positive powers of $z$.**
Multiply numerator and denominator by $z^2$:

$$H(z) = \frac{z^2}{z^2 - 1.5 z + 0.5}$$

**Step 2: Find the poles.**
Solve $z^2 - 1.5 z + 0.5 = 0$:

$$z = \frac{1.5 \pm \sqrt{2.25 - 2.0}}{2} = \frac{1.5 \pm \sqrt{0.25}}{2} = \frac{1.5 \pm 0.5}{2}$$

$$z_1 = \frac{1.5 + 0.5}{2} = 1.0, \quad z_2 = \frac{1.5 - 0.5}{2} = 0.5$$

**Step 3: Check pole magnitudes.**

$$\vert z_1 \vert = 1.0, \quad \vert z_2 \vert = 0.5$$

**Step 4: Apply the stability criterion.**

Since $\vert z_1 \vert = 1.0$ lies **on** the unit circle, the pole is not strictly inside. The system is **NOT BIBO stable** (it is marginally stable / oscillatory).

**Step 5: Impulse response verification.**

$$H(z) = \frac{z^2}{(z - 1)(z - 0.5)} = \frac{2z}{z-1} - \frac{z}{z-0.5}$$

$$h[n] = 2 \cdot u[n] - (0.5)^n \, u[n]$$

The term $2 \cdot u[n]$ is not summable; hence $h[n]$ is not absolutely summable, confirming **instability** (in BIBO sense).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Stability Analysis Workflow

The following Mermaid flowchart describes the systematic procedure for determining BIBO stability of a discrete-time LTI system.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: System H of z given"] --> B["Identify system function form"]
    B --> C["Pole-Zero form or LCCDE"]
    C --> D["Compute poles: roots of A of z"]
    D --> E{"All poles inside unit circle?"}
    E -- "Yes, all p_k magnitude less than 1" --> F["ROC includes unit circle"]
    F --> G["h of n absolutely summable"]
    G --> H["BIBO Stable System"]
    E -- "No, some p_k magnitude greater than or equal to 1" --> I{"Pole on or outside unit circle?"}
    I -- "Outside" --> J["h of n grows exponentially"]
    I -- "On unit circle simple" --> K["h of n oscillates with constant amplitude"]
    I -- "On unit circle repeated" --> L["h of n grows polynomially"]
    J --> M["Unstable System"]
    K --> N["Marginally Stable Not BIBO"]
    L --> M
    H --> O["End: Confirmed BIBO Stable"]
    M --> P["End: Unstable"]
    N --> Q["End: Not BIBO Stable"]
```

### 4.2 Pole-Plane Stability Map (Conceptual Block Architecture)

This diagram illustrates the partition of the Z-plane into stability regions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ZPLANE["Z Plane Partition"]
        A1["Region A: Inside Unit Circle magnitude less than 1"]
        A2["Region B: On Unit Circle magnitude equals 1"]
        A3["Region C: Outside Unit Circle magnitude greater than 1"]
    end
    A1 --> R1["All poles here: BIBO STABLE"]
    A2 --> R2["Simple pole: Marginally Stable, Not BIBO"]
    A2 --> R3["Repeated pole: UNSTABLE"]
    A3 --> R4["Any pole here: UNSTABLE"]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Stability Test Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input["LTI System H of z"] --> Step1["Step 1: Extract Poles"]
    Step1 --> Step2["Step 2: Compute Magnitudes of Poles"]
    Step2 --> Step3["Step 3: Compare with Unit Circle"]
    Step3 --> Decision{"Magnitude less than 1 for all poles?"}
    Decision -- "YES" --> Output1["Declare BIBO STABLE"]
    Decision -- "NO" --> Output2["Declare NOT BIBO STABLE"]
    Output1 --> Result["Provide ROC: includes unit circle"]
    Output2 --> Result2["Identify type: marginal or unstable"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> These diagrams are designed to give students a clear **visual map** of the stability decision process. KTU examiners often award partial marks for correct procedural flow even if the final numerical answer is slightly off.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define BIBO stability of a discrete-time LTI system. State and explain the impulse-response-based condition for BIBO stability.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**BIBO Stability Definition (1 mark):**
A discrete-time LTI system is said to be **Bounded-Input Bounded-Output (BIBO) stable** if, for every bounded input $x[n]$ (i.e., $\vert x[n] \vert \leq K < \infty$ for all $n$), the corresponding output $y[n]$ is also bounded (i.e., $\vert y[n] \vert \leq M < \infty$ for all $n$).

**Impulse Response Condition (1 mark):**
An LTI system is BIBO stable **if and only if** its impulse response $h[n]$ is absolutely summable:

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$$

**Explanation (1 mark):**
The output is the convolution $y[n] = \sum_k x[k] h[n-k]$. If $\vert x[k] \vert \leq K$ and $\sum_k \vert h[k] \vert = S_h < \infty$, then by the triangle inequality, $\vert y[n] \vert \leq K \cdot S_h < \infty$, confirming boundedness. Conversely, if $\sum \vert h[n] \vert = \infty$, a bounded input can be constructed that produces an unbounded output.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is the pole-location criterion for BIBO stability of an LTI system? Why must the ROC include the unit circle?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Pole-Location Criterion (1.5 marks):**
An LTI system is BIBO stable **if and only if all the poles of its system function $H(z)$ lie strictly inside the unit circle** in the Z-plane, i.e., $\vert p_k \vert < 1$ for every pole $p_k$.

**ROC and Unit Circle (1.5 marks):**
The Region of Convergence (ROC) of $H(z)$ must include the unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$ for BIBO stability. This is because the **DTFT** $H(e^{j\omega})$ of the impulse response exists only when the unit circle lies in the ROC. The existence of the DTFT is equivalent to the absolute summability of $h[n]$, which is the time-domain condition for BIBO stability.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions for Module 2 carry **14 marks** with sub-parts (typically (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks). Students answer **ONE of TWO** internal choices.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]*

**(a)** For the LTI system described by the difference equation:

$$y[n] = 0.5 \, y[n-1] + x[n] + 0.25 \, x[n-1]$$

**(i)** Find the system function $H(z)$. **(3 marks)**

**(ii)** Determine the poles and zeros of $H(z)$. **(2 marks)**

**(iii)** Determine whether the system is BIBO stable. Justify your answer using the pole-location criterion. **(2 marks)**

**(b)** The impulse response of an LTI system is given by:

$$h[n] = (0.5)^n \, u[n] + 2^n \, u[-n-1]$$

**(i)** Find the system function $H(z)$ and its ROC. **(4 marks)**

**(ii)** Is the system causal? Is it BIBO stable? Explain. **(3 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) (i) Finding $H(z)$:**

Take the Z-transform of both sides of the difference equation (with zero initial conditions):

$$Y(z) = 0.5 \, z^{-1} Y(z) + X(z) + 0.25 \, z^{-1} X(z)$$

$$Y(z) \left(1 - 0.5 z^{-1}\right) = X(z) \left(1 + 0.25 z^{-1}\right)$$

$$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{1 + 0.25 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}$$

**(a) (ii) Poles and Zeros:**

In positive powers of $z$:

$$H(z) = \frac{z + 0.25}{z - 0.5}$$

- **Zero:** $z = -0.25$
- **Pole:** $z = 0.5$

**(a) (iii) Stability:**

The single pole is at $z = 0.5$, so $\vert p \vert = 0.5 < 1$. The pole lies strictly inside the unit circle.

**Conclusion:** The system is **BIBO STABLE**.

Justification: Since the system is causal (right-sided), its ROC is $\vert z \vert > 0.5$, which **includes the unit circle**. Hence, the DTFT exists and the system is BIBO stable.

---

**(b) (i) Finding $H(z)$ and ROC:**

Apply Z-transform to each term:

$$h[n] = (0.5)^n \, u[n] + 2^n \, u[-n-1]$$

The first term is causal; its Z-transform is $\frac{1}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}$ with ROC $\vert z \vert > 0.5$.

The second term is anti-causal; its Z-transform is $\frac{-1}{1 - 2 z^{-1}}$ with ROC $\vert z \vert < 2$.

$$H(z) = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} - \frac{1}{1 - 2 z^{-1}}$$

The overall ROC is the intersection: $0.5 < \vert z \vert < 2$, which is an **annular region** (ring-shaped).

**(b) (ii) Causality and Stability:**

**Causality:** The system is **NOT causal** because $h[n] \neq 0$ for $n < 0$ (the term $2^n u[-n-1]$ is non-zero for $n \leq -1$).

**Stability:** The ROC $0.5 < \vert z \vert < 2$ **includes the unit circle** $\vert z \vert = 1$. Therefore, the system is **BIBO STABLE**.

**Verification via absolute summability:**

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (0.5)^n + \sum_{n=-\infty}^{-1} 2^n$$

$$= \frac{1}{1 - 0.5} + \sum_{m=1}^{\infty} 2^{-m} = 2 + 1 = 3 < \infty$$

Confirmed BIBO stable.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style, Alternative Choice]*

**(a)** For an LTI system with system function:

$$H(z) = \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 - 1.2 z^{-1} + 0.32 z^{-2}}$$

**(i)** Find the poles of the system. **(2 marks)**

**(ii)** Determine the ROC for a causal system. **(2 marks)**

**(iii)** Is the system BIBO stable when causal? Justify. **(3 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the stability of the LTI system with impulse response:

$$h[n] = \delta[n] - (0.5)^n \, u[n] + (1.5)^n \, u[n]$$

**(i)** Find $H(z)$ and its ROC. **(3 marks)**

**(ii)** Check BIBO stability using both time-domain and Z-domain criteria. **(4 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) (i) Finding the poles:**

Set the denominator to zero: $1 - 1.2 z^{-1} + 0.32 z^{-2} = 0$.

Multiply by $z^2$: $z^2 - 1.2 z + 0.32 = 0$.

$$z = \frac{1.2 \pm \sqrt{1.44 - 1.28}}{2} = \frac{1.2 \pm \sqrt{0.16}}{2} = \frac{1.2 \pm 0.4}{2}$$

$$z_1 = \frac{1.2 + 0.4}{2} = 0.8, \quad z_2 = \frac{1.2 - 0.4}{2} = 0.4$$

**Poles:** $p_1 = 0.8$ and $p_2 = 0.4$.

**(a) (ii) ROC for causal system:**

For a causal system, the ROC is the exterior of the outermost pole:

$$r_{\max} = \max(0.8, 0.4) = 0.8$$

$$\text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 0.8$$

**(a) (iii) Stability for causal system:**

The poles are at $\vert 0.8 \vert < 1$ and $\vert 0.4 \vert < 1$. **Both poles lie strictly inside the unit circle**.

The ROC ($\vert z \vert > 0.8$) **includes the unit circle**.

**Conclusion:** The causal system is **BIBO STABLE**.

---

**(b) (i) Finding $H(z)$ and ROC:**

Take the Z-transform of each term:

$$h[n] = \delta[n] - (0.5)^n \, u[n] + (1.5)^n \, u[n]$$

$$H(z) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} + \frac{1}{1 - 1.5 z^{-1}}$$

The ROC of each term: $\vert z \vert > 0.5$ and $\vert z \vert > 1.5$ respectively. The intersection is:

$$\text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 1.5$$

**(b) (ii) Stability check:**

**Z-Domain Analysis:**
The poles are at $z = 0.5$ and $z = 1.5$. The ROC ($\vert z \vert > 1.5$) **does NOT include the unit circle** because $1.5 > 1$. Therefore, the system is **NOT BIBO STABLE**.

**Time-Domain Verification:**

The term $(1.5)^n u[n]$ grows without bound as $n \to \infty$. So:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert \geq \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (1.5)^n = \infty$$

The impulse response is **not absolutely summable**, confirming instability.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students often claim a system is stable if "the impulse response decays" — but they forget that ALL components of $h[n]$ must decay, not just some. A single growing term makes the entire system unstable.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing **marginal stability** with **BIBO stability**. A marginally stable system (e.g., $\sin(\omega_0 n) u[n]$) is NOT BIBO stable because it is bounded but not absolutely summable.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting to check the ROC — students often just inspect pole locations without considering causality. For anti-causal systems, poles **outside** the unit circle are required for stability.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Forgetting to factor $z^2$ when finding poles — leading to wrong answers.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Stability (Module 2):**

- **BIBO stability** means bounded input $\Rightarrow$ bounded output.
- **Time-domain criterion:** $h[n]$ must be **absolutely summable** — $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$.
- **Z-domain criterion:** The **ROC of $H(z)$ must include the unit circle** $\vert z \vert = 1$.
- **Pole-location criterion:** All poles of $H(z)$ must lie **strictly inside the unit circle** (for causal systems).
- **Poles on the unit circle:** Simple pole ⇒ marginal (not BIBO stable); Repeated pole ⇒ unstable.
- **Poles outside the unit circle:** Always **unstable** for any ROC configuration.
- **Causal + Stable:** ROC = $\vert z \vert > r_{\max}$ with $r_{\max} < 1$ (exterior of unit circle, includes $\vert z \vert = 1$).
- **Anti-causal + Stable:** ROC = $\vert z \vert < r_{\min}$ with $r_{\min} > 1$ (interior of unit circle, includes $\vert z \vert = 1$).
- **Two-sided + Stable:** ROC is an annulus $r_1 < \vert z \vert < r_2$ with $r_1 < 1 < r_2$.
- **LCCDE → System Function:** $H(z) = \frac{B(z)}{A(z)}$; poles = roots of characteristic polynomial $A(z)$.
- **Convolution Bound:** $\vert y[n] \vert \leq K \cdot S_h$, where $K = \sup_n \vert x[n] \vert$ and $S_h = \sum \vert h[n] \vert$.
- **Stability is the FIRST design constraint** in any digital filter; always check pole magnitudes before simulation.
- **Marginal stability ≠ BIBO stability**: A marginally stable system is technically stable (output bounded) but NOT BIBO stable. KTU may use either definition — clarify in your answer.
- **Engineering tip:** Real-world discrete-time implementations (e.g., IIR filters) must keep poles inside the unit circle by a safety margin (e.g., $\vert p_k \vert \leq 0.95$) to account for coefficient quantization errors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Representation of Systems Using Impulse Response: LTI System Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBEVT604 - DIGITAL SIGNALS AND PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Representation of Systems Using Impulse Response: LTI System Definition # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of an LTI System (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) An **LTI System** is a system that simultaneously satisfies two fundamental properties — **Linearity** and **Time-Invariance**. For discrete-time sequences, an LTI system is mathematically represented as a transformation operator **T{·}** that maps an input sequence **x(n)** to an output sequence **y(n)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Style):** > A discrete-time system is called a **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) System** if it obeys the **principle of superposition (linearity)** and its **behavior/characteristics do not change with time (time-invariance)**. Such a system can be completely characterized by its **unit-impulse response h(n)**. ### Formal Condition Statements A system **T{·}** is LTI if and only if, for all inputs **x₁(n), x₂(n)** and arbitrary complex scalars **a, b**: $$ \begin{aligned} T\{a\,x_1(n) + b\,x_2(n)\} &= a\,T\{x_1(n)\} + b\,T\{x_2(n)\} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Linearity** itself is composed of two sub-properties — **Additivity** and **Homogeneity (Scaling)**. Both must hold for the system to be classified as linear. For **Time-Invariance**, if the input is shifted by **n₀** samples, the output must also be shifted by the **same n₀** samples without any change in waveform shape: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{If } y(n) &= T\{x(n)\}, \quad \text{then } T\{x(n-n_0)\} = y(n-n_0) \end{aligned} $$ ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Volume Knob of an Old Radio" 🎛️ Imagine a vintage transistor radio that has two physical controls: - **Tuning Knob (Frequency Selector):** Set it once to 104.2 MHz — the radio will play the same station regardless of whether you switch it on at **6 AM or 6 PM**. This is **Time-Invariance**. - **Volume Knob:** If you turn it to "3" and another speaker turns it to "9", both radios obey the **same rule** — louder input produces proportionally louder output. If you combine two audio sources (radio + Aux), the total output is the **sum of individual outputs**. This is **Linearity (Superposition)**. > [!TIP] > **Memory Hook:** *"LTI = Predictable + Stationary"*. An LTI system reacts today exactly the way it reacted yesterday to the same input — making it **analytically tractable** and **engineering-friendly**. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Bold | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit / Value | |---|---|---| | **Sampling Frequency** | $F_s$ | **Hz** (typically 8 kHz – 48 kHz for audio DSP) | | **Sampling Period** | $T_s$ | **seconds/sample**, where $T_s = 1/F_s$ | | **Discrete Time Index** | $n$ | **dimensionless integer** $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ | | **System Order (N)** | $N$ | **pure number** (count of delay elements) | | **BIBO Stability Bound** | $\sum \vert h(k) \vert < \infty$ | **absolute summability** condition | # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Linearity — The Superposition Principle A system **T{·}** is **linear** if and only if both properties below hold **simultaneously**: ### (a) Additivity (Superposition) $$ \begin{aligned} T\{x_1(n) + x_2(n)\} &= T\{x_1(n)\} + T\{x_2(n)\} \end{aligned} $$ ### (b) Homogeneity (Scaling) $$ \begin{aligned} T\{a\,x(n)\} &= a\,T\{x(n)\} \end{aligned} $$ ### Combined Linearity Condition $$ \begin{aligned} T\{a\,x_1(n) + b\,x_2(n)\} &= a\,y_1(n) + b\,y_2(n) \end{aligned} $$ where $y_1(n) = T\{x_1(n)\}$ and $y_2(n) = T\{x_2(n)\}$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Why" of Linearity:** Linearity allows engineers to **decompose a complex input into elementary impulses**, find the response to each impulse separately, and then **sum them up**. This is the foundation of the **Convolution Sum**. ## 2.2 Time-Invariance — Stationarity in Behavior A system is **time-invariant** if shifting the input in time results in an **identical shift** in the output: $$ \begin{aligned} y(n) &= T\{x(n)\} \implies T\{x(n-n_0)\} = y(n-n_0) \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation:** A time-invariant system has **fixed internal coefficients** (gains, delays). The circuit/algorithm does not "evolve" or "drift" over time. ## 2.3 The Impulse Response h(n) — The System's "DNA" The **unit-impulse response** $h(n)$ is defined as the output of the system when the input is a **unit Kronecker delta** $\delta(n)$: $$ \begin{aligned} h(n) &= T\{\delta(n)\} \end{aligned} $$ where $\delta(n)$ is defined as: $$ \delta(n) = \begin{cases} 1, & n = 0 \\ 0, & n \neq 0 \end{cases} $$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Gold Statement:** *"Knowing $h(n)$ is equivalent to knowing the complete input-output behavior of any LTI system."* ## 2.4 The Convolution Sum — System Representation Using h(n) Any arbitrary input $x(n)$ can be decomposed into a weighted sum of shifted impulses: $$ \begin{aligned} x(n) &= \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(k)\,\delta(n-k) \end{aligned} $$ Applying the LTI operator **T{·}** and using linearity + time-invariance: $$ \begin{aligned} y(n) &= T\{x(n)\} = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(k)\,T\{\delta(n-k)\} = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(k)\,h(n-k) \end{aligned} $$ This is the famous **Convolution Sum**: $$ \boxed{\,y(n) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(k)\,h(n-k) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} h(k)\,x(n-k) = x(n) * h(n)\,} $$ ## 2.5 Continuous-Time LTI Counterpart For continuous-time LTI systems, the equivalent operation is the **Convolution Integral**: $$ \begin{aligned} y(t) &= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau)\,h(t-\tau)\,d\tau = x(t) * h(t) \end{aligned} $$ ## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table) | # | Concept | Formula | Condition / Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Linearity (Combined) | $T\{a x_1(n) + b x_2(n)\} = a y_1(n) + b y_2(n)$ | Must hold for **all** $a,b,x_1,x_2$ | | 2 | Time-Invariance | $T\{x(n-n_0)\} = y(n-n_0)$ | Holds for **any** shift $n_0$ | | 3 | Unit Impulse | $\delta(n) = 1$ at $n=0$, else $0$ | Fundamental test signal | | 4 | Impulse Response | $h(n) = T\{\delta(n)\}$ | Complete system signature | | 5 | Convolution Sum | $y(n) = \sum_k x(k) h(n-k)$ | LTI output for arbitrary input | | 6 | Convolution Integral | $y(t) = \int x(\tau) h(t-\tau) d\tau$ | Continuous-time equivalent | | 7 | BIBO Stability | $\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(k) \vert < \infty$ | Guarantees **bounded-output** | | 8 | Causality | $h(n) = 0$ for $n < 0$ | System **does not look into future** | | 9 | Memory | $h(n) \neq 0$ for $n \neq 0$ | System has **memory** (uses past samples) | | 10 | Difference Equation | $\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k y(n-k) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x(n-k)$ | Recursive LTI representation | ## 2.7 Real-World Utility of LTI Systems LTI systems form the **backbone of nearly every DSP application**: - **Audio Engineering:** Equalizers, noise-cancelling headphones (FIR/IIR filters are LTI). - **Telecommunications:** Channel equalizers, echo cancellers, modems. - **Biomedical Signal Processing:** ECG/EEG filters, pacemaker signal conditioning. - **Radar & Sonar:** Matched filters for target detection. - **Image Processing:** Gaussian smoothing, edge-detection filters (separable 2D LTI). - **Control Systems:** Most classical controllers (PID) operate on LTI plant assumptions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters for KTU:** Almost every **Part B (14-mark)** question in Module 2 ultimately boils down to **convolving two sequences** or **proving that a given system is/isn't LTI**. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From Linearity + Time-Invariance → Convolution Sum ### Step 1 — Express arbitrary input as sum of weighted impulses Any discrete-time sequence $x(n)$ can be written using the **sifting property** of $\delta(n)$: $$ \begin{aligned} x(n) &= \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(k) \cdot \delta(n-k) \end{aligned} $$ *Reasoning:* At index $n = k$, $\delta(n-k) = 1$, so only the term where $k = n$ survives. At all other indices, $\delta(n-k) = 0$. Thus we simply "pick out" $x(n)$ at each instant. ### Step 2 — Apply the system operator T{·} to both sides $$ \begin{aligned} T\{x(n)\} &= T\left\{\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(k) \cdot \delta(n-k)\right\} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Pull $x(k)$ outside (since $x(k)$ is a constant w.r.t. the operator's action on $n$) $$ \begin{aligned} T\{x(n)\} &= \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(k) \cdot T\{\delta(n-k)\} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Apply Time-Invariance By definition, $T\{\delta(n-k)\}$ is the system response to $\delta(n)$ shifted by $k$ samples. By time-invariance: $$ \begin{aligned} T\{\delta(n-k)\} &= h(n-k) \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5 — Substitute back to obtain the Convolution Sum $$ \boxed{\,y(n) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(k) \cdot h(n-k)\,} $$ By a simple change of variable $m = n - k$, we obtain the symmetric form: $$ \boxed{\,y(n) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} h(m) \cdot x(n-m)\,} $$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Mnemonic:** *"Flip the second sequence, slide it, multiply, and sum"* — this is the graphical/tabular convolution method frequently tested in KTU papers. ## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example — Verification of LTI Property **Problem:** A system is described by $y(n) = n \cdot x(n)$. Check whether this system is: 1. Linear 2. Time-invariant ### (a) Linearity Test Let $x(n) = a x_1(n) + b x_2(n)$. Then: $$ \begin{aligned} y_{\text{combined}}(n) &= n \cdot [a x_1(n) + b x_2(n)] \\ &= a \cdot n x_1(n) + b \cdot n x_2(n) \\ &= a y_1(n) + b y_2(n) \end{aligned} $$ ✅ **System is LINEAR** (multiplication by a constant function of $n$ preserves superposition). ### (b) Time-Invariance Test Let $x'(n) = x(n - n_0)$. Compute output: $$ \begin{aligned} y'(n) &= n \cdot x'(n) = n \cdot x(n - n_0) \end{aligned} $$ Now shift the original output: $$ \begin{aligned} y(n - n_0) &= (n - n_0) \cdot x(n - n_0) \end{aligned} $$ Since $n \neq (n - n_0)$ in general: $$ n \cdot x(n - n_0) \neq (n - n_0) \cdot x(n - n_0) $$ ❌ **System is NOT TIME-INVARIANT.** Hence it is **NOT LTI**. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Classification Quick Reference | System Equation $y(n) = \ldots$ | Linear? | Time-Invariant? | LTI? | |---|---|---|---| | $x(n) + x(n-1)$ | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ **Yes** | | $x(n^2)$ | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No | | $n \cdot x(n)$ | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No | | $\cos\!\left(\tfrac{\pi}{2} n\right) x(n)$ | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No | | $x(-n)$ | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (reversal) | ❌ No | | $a \cdot x(n) + b$ | ❌ No (DC offset) | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | | $\vert x(n) \vert$ | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | | $2x(n) + 3x(n-2)$ | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ **Yes** | | $e^{x(n)}$ | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ## 3.4 Derivation: General Difference Equation of an LTI System Any **causal LTI** discrete system can be described by a **linear constant-coefficient difference equation (LCCDE)**: $$ \begin{aligned} \sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k\, y(n-k) &= \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\, x(n-k) \end{aligned} $$ Normalized form (with $a_0 = 1$): $$ \begin{aligned} y(n) &= -\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k\, y(n-k) + \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\, x(n-k) \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > If $a_k = 0$ for $k \geq 1$, the system is **FIR (Finite Impulse Response)**. Otherwise, it is **IIR (Infinite Impulse Response)** — assuming nonzero feedback. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — LTI System Decomposition Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Arbitrary Input Sequence x(n)"] --> B["Decompose into Weighted Sum of Shifted Impulses"] B --> C["Apply Superposition Linearity"] C --> D["Apply Time-Invariance Shift"] D --> E["Use Impulse Response h(n-k)"] E --> F["Sum Over All k to Form y(n)"] F --> G["Final Output y(n) = x(n) * h(n)"] H["Unit Impulse delta(n)"] --> I["LTI System Operator T"] I --> J["Impulse Response h(n)"] K["Linearity Property"] --> L["Additivity + Homogeneity"] M["Time Invariance Property"] --> N["Output Shifts Equally with Input"] style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1976D2,color:#0D47A1 style G fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#388E3C,color:#1B5E20 style J fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#F57C00,color:#E65100 style L fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#C2185B,color:#880E4F style N fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#C2185B,color:#880E4F ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Flow — LTI System Classification Test Procedure ```mermaid flowchart TD S["Given System y(n) = T{x(n)}"] --> P1{"Linearity Test:
T{a x1 + b x2} = a T{x1} + b T{x2}"} P1 -- "Fails" --> R1["System is NON-LINEAR
Conclusion: NOT LTI"] P1 -- "Passes" --> P2{"Time-Invariance Test:
T{x(n-n0)} = y(n-n0)"} P2 -- "Fails" --> R2["System is LINEAR
but TIME-VARIANT
Conclusion: NOT LTI"] P2 -- "Passes" --> R3["System is LINEAR
and TIME-INVARIANT
Conclusion: IS LTI
Characterize via h(n) = T{delta(n)}"] style S fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#0D47A1 style R1 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828,color:#B71C1C style R2 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#EF6C00,color:#E65100 style R3 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#1B5E20 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Impulse Response Convolution Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR IN["Input x(k)"] --> MUL1["Multiplier x(k) * h(n-k)"] H["Impulse Response h(n-k)
Time-Reversed & Shifted"] --> MUL1 MUL1 --> SUM["Summing Junction"] SUM --> DELAY["Iterate k from -inf to +inf"] DELAY --> OUT["Output y(n)"] DELAY --> FB["Feed k = k + 1"] FB --> MUL1 style IN fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#1565C0,color:#0D47A1 style H fill:#FFE082,stroke:#F57F17,color:#E65100 style OUT fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#1B5E20 style MUL1 fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#616161,color:#212121 style SUM fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#616161,color:#212121 ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid — General LTI System Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR X["x(n)"] --> DLX1["z to power -1"] DLX1 --> DLX2["z to power -1"] DLX2 --> DLX3["z to power -1"] DLX1 --> MX1["Multiplier b1"] DLX2 --> MX2["Multiplier b2"] DLX3 --> MX3["Multiplier b3"] X --> MX0["Multiplier b0"] DLX3 --> DLY1["z to power -1"] DLY1 --> DLY2["z to power -1"] DLY2 --> MY1["Multiplier minus a1"] DLY2 --> MY2["Multiplier minus a2"] MX0 --> ADD["Summing Junction"] MX1 --> ADD MX2 --> ADD MX3 --> ADD MY1 --> ADD MY2 --> ADD ADD --> Y["y(n)"] Y --> DLY1 Y --> DLY2 style X fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#1565C0,color:#0D47A1 style Y fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#1B5E20 style ADD fill:#FFE082,stroke:#F57F17,color:#E65100 ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]** Define an LTI system. State the two key properties that a system must satisfy to be called LTI. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** An **LTI (Linear Time-Invariant) System** is a system whose response to a combination of inputs is the same combination of responses to each input separately, and whose characteristics do not change with time. **Two Key Properties:** 1. **Linearity** — The system obeys the principle of superposition: $$ T\{a\,x_1(n) + b\,x_2(n)\} = a\,T\{x_1(n)\} + b\,T\{x_2(n)\} $$ 2. **Time-Invariance** — A shift in the input results in an identical shift in the output: $$ T\{x(n - n_0)\} = y(n - n_0) $$ A system satisfying both is fully characterized by its **impulse response $h(n)$**. *(3 marks — 1 for definition + 1 each for the two properties)* --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]** Define impulse response of an LTI system. Why is it considered a complete characterization of the system? **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **impulse response** $h(n)$ of an LTI system is defined as the output of the system when the input is a unit impulse $\delta(n)$: $$ h(n) = T\{\delta(n)\} $$ **Why it is a complete characterization:** Since any arbitrary input $x(n)$ can be expressed as a weighted sum of shifted impulses, and an LTI system obeys linearity and time-invariance, the output for any input is uniquely determined by convolving $x(n)$ with $h(n)$: $$ y(n) = x(n) * h(n) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(k)\,h(n-k) $$ Therefore, knowing $h(n)$ alone allows us to compute the output for **every possible input** — making it a complete description of the LTI system. *(3 marks — 1 for definition + 2 for reasoning)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) --- ### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, S3 B.Tech ECE]** *(Alternative Choice)* #### (a) Define an LTI system. Check whether the systems described by the following equations are LTI. Justify your answer with proof. **(7 marks)** 1. $y(n) = 5x(n) - 3x(n-2)$ 2. $y(n) = n\,x(n) + x(n-1)$ #### (b) For an LTI system described by the impulse response $h(n) = \{1, 2, 3, 2, 1\}$ (centered at $n = 0$), compute the output $y(n)$ for the input $x(n) = \{1, 1, 1, 1\}$ (centered at $n = 0$). Use tabular convolution. **(7 marks)** --- #### 🔹 Solution to A(a) **Definition (1 mark):** A system is LTI if it is **linear** and **time-invariant**, as discussed in Section 1. **System 1:** $y(n) = 5x(n) - 3x(n-2)$ *Linearity Test:* $$ \begin{aligned} T\{a x_1(n) + b x_2(n)\} &= 5[a x_1(n) + b x_2(n)] - 3[a x_1(n-2) + b x_2(n-2)] \\ &= a[5 x_1(n) - 3 x_1(n-2)] + b[5 x_2(n) - 3 x_2(n-2)] \\ &= a y_1(n) + b y_2(n) \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ *Time-Invariance Test:* $$ \begin{aligned} T\{x(n-n_0)\} &= 5 x(n-n_0) - 3 x(n-n_0-2) = y(n-n_0) \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ ✅ **System 1 is LTI.** *(3 marks — 1.5 for linearity + 1.5 for time-invariance)* **System 2:** $y(n) = n\,x(n) + x(n-1)$ *Linearity Test:* $$ \begin{aligned} T\{a x_1 + b x_2\} &= n[a x_1 + b x_2] + [a x_1(n-1) + b x_2(n-1)] \\ &= a[n x_1 + x_1(n-1)] + b[n x_2 + x_2(n-1)] = a y_1 + b y_2 \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ *Time-Invariance Test:* $$ \begin{aligned} T\{x(n-n_0)\} &= n\,x(n-n_0) + x(n-n_0-1) \end{aligned} $$ Expected shifted output: $$ \begin{aligned} y(n-n_0) &= (n-n_0)\,x(n-n_0) + x(n-n_0-1) \end{aligned} $$ Since $n \neq (n-n_0)$, the two expressions differ. ❌ **System 2 is NOT time-invariant**, hence **NOT LTI.** *(3 marks — 1 for linearity + 2 for time-invariance failure proof)* --- #### 🔹 Solution to A(b) Given $h(n) = \{1, 2, 3, 2, 1\}$ indexed at $n = -2, -1, 0, 1, 2$. Given $x(n) = \{1, 1, 1, 1\}$ indexed at $n = 0, 1, 2, 3$. **Tabular Convolution (Flipped h):** | $n$ | Computation | $y(n)$ | |---|---|---| | 0 | $1 \cdot 1 = 1$ | **1** | | 1 | $1 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 2 = 3$ | **3** | | 2 | $1 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 3 = 6$ | **6** | | 3 | $1 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 3 + 1 \cdot 2 = 8$ | **8** | | 4 | $1 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 3 + 1 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 1 = 8$ | **8** | | 5 | $1 \cdot 3 + 1 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 1 = 6$ | **6** | | 6 | $1 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 1 = 3$ | **3** | | 7 | $1 \cdot 1 = 1$ | **1** | **Final Output:** $y(n) = \{1, 3, 6, 8, 8, 6, 3, 1\}$ starting at $n = 0$. *(7 marks — 4 for correct tabular setup + 3 for accurate summation)* --- ### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — June 2023, S4 B.Tech ECE]** *(Internal Choice)* #### (a) Explain the impulse response representation of an LTI system. Derive the convolution sum starting from the sifting property of the impulse sequence. **(7 marks)** #### (b) An LTI system has impulse response $h(n) = (0.5)^n \, u(n)$. Compute and plot the output $y(n)$ for the input $x(n) = (0.8)^n \, u(n)$. Verify BIBO stability of the system. **(7 marks)** --- #### 🔹 Solution to B(a) **Impulse Response Representation (2 marks):** The impulse response $h(n) = T\{\delta(n)\}$ uniquely characterizes an LTI system. Because the system is linear and time-invariant, the response to any input $x(n)$ is completely determined by convolving $x(n)$ with $h(n)$. **Derivation (5 marks):** *Step 1 — Sifting property:* $$ x(n) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(k)\,\delta(n-k) $$ *Step 2 — Apply $T\{\cdot\}$:* $$ y(n) = T\left\{\sum_{k} x(k)\,\delta(n-k)\right\} $$ *Step 3 — Linearity (constant out):* $$ y(n) = \sum_{k} x(k)\,T\{\delta(n-k)\} $$ *Step 4 — Time-invariance:* $$ T\{\delta(n-k)\} = h(n-k) $$ *Step 5 — Convolution sum:* $$ \boxed{\,y(n) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(k)\,h(n-k)\,} $$ --- #### 🔹 Solution to B(b) Given: $h(n) = (0.5)^n u(n)$ and $x(n) = (0.8)^n u(n)$. For $n \geq 0$: $$ \begin{aligned} y(n) &= \sum_{k=0}^{n} x(k)\,h(n-k) = \sum_{k=0}^{n} (0.8)^k (0.5)^{n-k} \end{aligned} $$ Factor out $(0.5)^n$: $$ \begin{aligned} y(n) &= (0.5)^n \sum_{k=0}^{n} \left(\frac{0.8}{0.5}\right)^k = (0.5)^n \sum_{k=0}^{n} (1.6)^k \end{aligned} $$ The inner sum is a finite geometric series: $$ \begin{aligned} \sum_{k=0}^{n} (1.6)^k = \frac{(1.6)^{n+1} - 1}{1.6 - 1} = \frac{(1.6)^{n+1} - 1}{0.6} \end{aligned} $$ Therefore: $$ \boxed{\,y(n) = \frac{(0.5)^n \cdot \left[(1.6)^{n+1} - 1\right]}{0.6} \quad \text{for } n \geq 0\,} $$ **Tabulated values:** | $n$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | $y(n)$ | 1.000 | 2.300 | 4.280 | 7.258 | 11.995 | 19.477 | **BIBO Stability Verification:** $$ \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \vert h(n) \vert = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (0.5)^n = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5} = 2 < \infty $$ ✅ **System is BIBO Stable.** *(7 marks — 3 for convolution setup + 2 for closed-form + 2 for stability check)* --- > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to verify BOTH properties.** Many students check linearity only. A system must be **linear AND time-invariant** to be classified as LTI. Missing either = **lose 50% marks**. > 2. **Indexing errors in convolution.** Always clearly state the starting index of $x(n)$ and $h(n)$ before drawing the convolution table. > 3. **Using $\delta(n)$ instead of $h(n)$.** The impulse response is $T\{\delta(n)\}$, not $\delta(n)$ itself. Confusing these two = **conceptual mark loss**. > 4. **Forgetting absolute value in stability test.** The BIBO condition requires $\sum \vert h(n) \vert < \infty$, not $\sum h(n) < \infty$ — although for positive $h(n)$ they are equivalent. > 5. **Skipping justification for time-invariance.** Just writing "system is TI" without showing $T\{x(n-n_0)\} = y(n-n_0)$ = **up to 2 marks deducted**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist 📌 - ✅ **LTI = Linearity + Time-Invariance.** Both must be satisfied simultaneously. - ✅ **Linearity** = Additivity + Homogeneity (Principle of Superposition). - ✅ **Time-Invariance** = Input shift → output shift by same amount, no shape change. - ✅ **Impulse Response** $h(n) = T\{\delta(n)\}$ is the **complete signature** of any LTI system. - ✅ **Convolution Sum:** $y(n) = \sum_k x(k)\,h(n-k) = \sum_k h(k)\,x(n-k)$. - ✅ **Continuous Counterpart:** $y(t) = \int x(\tau)\,h(t-\tau)\,d\tau$. - ✅ **LCCDE Form:** $\sum a_k y(n-k) = \sum b_k x(n-k)$. - ✅ **BIBO Stability:** $\sum \vert h(n) \vert < \infty$. - ✅ **Causality:** $h(n) = 0$ for $n < 0$. - ✅ **FIR vs IIR:** FIR has $h(n)$ of finite length; IIR has infinite (with feedback). - ✅ **Convolution Procedure:** Flip → Shift → Multiply → Sum → Repeat. - ✅ **Real-World Uses:** Audio equalizers, telecom channel filters, biomedical signal conditioning, image smoothing, radar matched filters. - ✅ **KTU Exam Hot Tip:** Always state the two sub-properties of linearity (additivity + homogeneity) explicitly for **full marks**.

topics.title: Response of a Discrete-Time LTI System and the Convolutional Sum
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT604 - DIGITAL SIGNALS AND PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Response of a Discrete-Time LTI System and the Convolutional Sum**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Discrete-Time LTI System

A **Discrete-Time Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) System** is a signal processing operator $T\{\cdot\}$ that satisfies two fundamental properties:

1. **Linearity:** The response to a linear combination of inputs equals the same linear combination of their individual responses (governed by the superposition principle, encompassing both additivity and homogeneity/scaling).
2. **Time-Invariance:** A time-shift in the input signal produces an identical time-shift in the output signal; the system's behavior does not change with absolute time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For an LTI system, the response $y(n)$ to any arbitrary input $x(n)$ is completely and uniquely characterized by the system's **Impulse Response**, denoted as $h(n) = T\{\delta(n)\}$. The impulse response is the *fingerprint* of the system.

## 1.2 The Convolutional Sum (Convolution Sum Equation)

The **Convolutional Sum** is the mathematical operation that defines the output of a discrete-time LTI system for any arbitrary input. It is expressed as:

$$y(n) = x(n) * h(n) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(k) \, h(n-k)$$

where $*$ denotes the convolution operator.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Echo Machine 🎤**
> Imagine speaking into a large cathedral. The sound $x(n)$ you produce does not arrive at the listener instantly. Instead, the listener hears a *primary* sound plus a series of *echoes* — a louder echo first, followed by progressively fainter and more delayed ones. Each echo is essentially a *scaled and time-shifted copy* of your original voice. The **cathedral's response to a sudden sharp clap** (an impulse $\delta(n)$) is its *impulse response* $h(n)$ — a sharp clap followed by a tail of decaying echoes. Now, any complex speech $x(n)$ you produce can be thought of as a rapid sequence of tiny claps. Each tiny clap produces the cathedral's standard echo pattern, and the **final sound** the listener hears is simply the **sum of all these overlapping echo patterns**.
>
> - $x(k)$ = amplitude of the $k^{th}$ tiny "clap" in your speech.
> - $h(n-k)$ = the cathedral's echo pattern, shifted in time to align with the $k^{th}$ clap.
> - $y(n)$ = the sum (superposition) of all these shifted, weighted echoes.

This superposition of shifted, scaled impulse responses **IS** the convolution sum.

## 1.4 Key Physical / Mathematical Parameters

- **Impulse Response $h(n)$:** A sequence of length or duration (could be infinite for IIR-type systems) that completely defines the LTI system.
- **Causality Condition:** A system is causal if and only if $h(n) = 0$ for all $n < 0$. The output depends only on present and past inputs.
- **Stability Criterion (BIBO):** A system is bounded-input bounded-output stable if and only if $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(n) \vert < \infty$ (absolutely summable).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Theoretical Foundation: Why Convolution Works

The derivation of the convolution sum rests on a beautiful idea: **any discrete-time signal can be decomposed into a weighted sum of shifted unit impulses.**

### Step-by-Step Logical Foundation

- **Step 1 — Signal Decomposition:** Any sequence $x(n)$ can be represented as:
$$x(n) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(k) \, \delta(n-k)$$
Each term $x(k)\,\delta(n-k)$ is a scaled, time-shifted impulse located at index $k$ with weight $x(k)$.

- **Step 2 — System Linearity (Additivity):** Because the system is linear, its response to a sum of inputs equals the sum of responses to each input:
$$y(n) = T\left\{ \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(k) \, \delta(n-k) \right\} = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(k) \, T\{\delta(n-k)\}$$

- **Step 3 — Time-Invariance (Shift Invariance):** Because the system is time-invariant, the response to $\delta(n-k)$ is simply a time-shifted version of the impulse response $h(n)$:
$$T\{\delta(n-k)\} = h(n-k)$$

- **Step 4 — Combine:** Substituting back yields the master equation:
$$y(n) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(k) \, h(n-k)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The convolution sum is the backbone of *every* digital filter, equalizer, control system, and audio processor. It allows engineers to predict the output of any system simply by knowing its impulse response — which can be measured empirically with a single impulse test, eliminating the need to solve complex differential/difference equations.

## 2.2 The Four Mechanical Steps of Graphical Convolution

To compute $y(n) = \sum_k x(k)h(n-k)$ graphically (a frequent KTU exam task), follow these four steps:

| Step | Operation | Mathematical Action | Physical Meaning |
|:----:|:---------:|:-------------------:|:----------------:|
| 1 | **Folding** | Replace $h(k)$ with $h(-k)$ | Flip the impulse response about the vertical axis (k = 0) |
| 2 | **Shifting** | Shift $h(-k)$ by $n$ to get $h(n-k)$ | Slide the folded sequence across the input |
| 3 | **Multiplication** | Compute pointwise product $x(k) \cdot h(n-k)$ | Find overlapping non-zero values at each shift |
| 4 | **Summation** | $\sum_k x(k) h(n-k)$ | Add the products to get one output sample $y(n)$ |

Repeat for every integer shift $n$ to obtain the complete output sequence.

## 2.3 Properties of Convolution for LTI Systems

| Property | Mathematical Statement | Engineering Significance |
|:---------|:----------------------|:------------------------|
| **Commutative** | $x(n) * h(n) = h(n) * x(n)$ | The system and signal roles can be swapped |
| **Associative** | $[x(n) * h_1(n)] * h_2(n) = x(n) * [h_1(n) * h_2(n)]$ | Cascaded LTI systems can be combined |
| **Distributive** | $x(n) * [h_1(n) + h_2(n)] = x(n) * h_1(n) + x(n) * h_2(n)$ | Parallel systems add their impulse responses |
| **Identity Element** | $x(n) * \delta(n) = x(n)$ | The impulse is the identity of convolution |
| **Delay Property** | $x(n) * \delta(n - n_0) = x(n - n_0)$ | Convolution with a shifted impulse shifts the input |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|
| 1 | $y(n) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(k) \, h(n-k)$ | **Convolutional Sum (Standard Form)** |
| 2 | $y(n) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} h(k) \, x(n-k)$ | **Convolutional Sum (Equivalent Form)** |
| 3 | $h(n) = T\{\delta(n)\}$ | **Impulse Response Definition** |
| 4 | Causality: $h(n) = 0$ for $n < 0$ | System output depends only on present/past |
| 5 | BIBO Stability: $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(n) \vert < \infty$ | Bounded input yields bounded output |
| 6 | Length of output: $N_y = N_x + N_h - 1$ | For finite-length sequences of lengths $N_x$ and $N_h$ |
| 7 | $y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{n} x(k) h(n-k)$ | Reduced form for **causal** systems with causal inputs |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Examples
