## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define an anti-aliasing filter. Why is it placed before the sampler in a digital signal processing system?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An anti-aliasing filter is a **continuous-time low-pass analog filter** placed before the analog-to-digital converter. Its function is to **bandlimit the input signal** by removing all frequency components above the Nyquist frequency $f_s/2$, thereby preventing the phenomenon of *aliasing* in which high-frequency components fold back into the baseband and corrupt the sampled data.

The filter is placed *before* the sampler because once sampling occurs, high-frequency information that violates the Nyquist criterion cannot be recovered by any subsequent digital operation. Since real-world signals are never truly bandlimited, the AAF is a **mandatory pre-processing stage** to enforce the condition $f_m \leq f_s/2$.

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark — definition; 1 mark — Nyquist context; 1 mark — justification of placement.)*

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the Nyquist sampling theorem. What happens if a signal containing a 9 kHz component is sampled at 6 kHz?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Nyquist Sampling Theorem:** A bandlimited signal with maximum frequency $f_m$ can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency $f_s \geq 2 f_m$.

For the given case: $f_0 = 9\,\text{kHz}$, $f_s = 6\,\text{kHz}$.

Step 1: Nyquist frequency is $f_s/2 = 3\,\text{kHz}$.

Step 2: Since $9\,\text{kHz} > 3\,\text{kHz}$, the sampling theorem is violated.

Step 3: Aliased frequency is $f_a = \vert 9 - 1 \cdot 6 \vert = 3\,\text{kHz}$.

The $9\,\text{kHz}$ tone will appear as a $3\,\text{kHz}$ tone in the digital domain — this is **aliasing**.

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark — theorem; 1 mark — Nyquist calculation; 1 mark — aliasing result.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the phenomenon of aliasing in digital signal processing with suitable mathematical expressions. Show that the sampled spectrum is a periodic replication of the baseband spectrum. (7 marks)

**(b)** An analog signal $x(t)$ has frequency components up to $f_m = 8\,\text{kHz}$. It is sampled at $f_s = 24\,\text{kHz}$. Design a Butterworth anti-aliasing filter with passband ripple $A_p = 1\,\text{dB}$ at $f_p = 7\,\text{kHz}$ and stopband attenuation $A_s = 40\,\text{dB}$ at the Nyquist frequency. Determine the minimum filter order. (7 marks)

---

##### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Define sampling operation.** Sampling a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ with sampling period $T_s = 1/f_s$ produces:

$$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t), \quad p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - n T_s)$$

**Step 2 — Fourier series of impulse train.** The periodic impulse train has Fourier series:

$$p(t) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j 2 \pi k f_s t}$$

**Step 3 — Multiplication in time becomes convolution in frequency.** Applying the Fourier transform:

$$X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.** The sampled spectrum $X_s(f)$ is the original spectrum $X(f)$ replicated periodically every $f_s$ Hz and scaled by $1/T_s$.

**Step 5 — Aliasing condition.** If the support of $X(f)$ exceeds $f_s/2$, the replicas overlap, and the resulting spectrum is a **sum** of overlapping copies — this overlap is aliasing. The high-frequency portion of $X(f)$ folds into the baseband, producing a frequency $f_a = \vert f_0 - k f_s \vert$ that is lower than $f_0$.

**Step 6 — Mathematical demonstration with two tones.** Consider $x(t) = \cos(2 \pi f_0 t)$ sampled at $f_s$. The discrete-time sequence is:

$$x(n) = \cos(2 \pi f_0 n / f_s) = \cos\!\left(2 \pi \frac{f_0 - k f_s}{f_s} n\right) = \cos(2 \pi f_a n / f_s)$$

for any integer $k$, demonstrating that frequencies $f_0$ and $f_0 - k f_s$ are indistinguishable after sampling.

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks — derivation; 2 marks — periodicity result; 2 marks — aliasing condition; 1 mark — tone example.)*

##### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters.**

$$f_p = 7\,\text{kHz}, \quad f_s = 24\,\text{kHz}, \quad f_s/2 = 12\,\text{kHz}, \quad A_p = 1\,\text{dB}, \quad A_s = 40\,\text{dB}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Butterworth order formula.**

$$N = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{10^{A_s/10} - 1}{10^{A_p/10} - 1}\right)}{\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{f_s/2}{f_p}\right)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator.**

$$10^{A_s/10} = 10^{4} = 10000, \quad 10^{A_p/10} = 10^{0.1} = 1.2589$$

$$\text{Numerator} = \frac{10000 - 1}{1.2589 - 1} = \frac{9999}{0.2589} \approx 38621$$

$$\log_{10}(38621) \approx 4.587$$

**Step 4 — Compute denominator.**

$$\frac{f_s/2}{f_p} = \frac{12}{7} \approx 1.714$$

$$\log_{10}(1.714) \approx 0.234$$

**Step 5 — Compute $N$.**

$$N = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{4.587}{0.234} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 19.61 = 9.80$$

**Step 6 — Round up to integer.**

$$\boxed{N = 10}$$

A 10th-order Butterworth filter is required.

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks — formula; 2 marks — numerator/denominator; 2 marks — final N; 1 mark — rounding logic.)*

---

#### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the role of the anti-aliasing filter in a DSP system. Compare ideal and practical anti-aliasing filter characteristics. (7 marks)

**(b)** A signal has components at 100 Hz, 500 Hz, 1.2 kHz, 2.5 kHz, 4 kHz, and 7 kHz. It is sampled at $f_s = 5\,\text{kHz}$. Determine the Nyquist frequency and identify the frequencies that will be aliased. Compute the apparent frequencies. (7 marks)

---

##### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Block diagram description.**

The DSP acquisition chain is:

$$x_a(t) \rightarrow \boxed{\text{AAF } H_{AA}(f)} \rightarrow \boxed{\text{Sampler}} \rightarrow x(n) \rightarrow \text{DSP}$$

**Step 2 — Role of AAF.** The AAF is a low-pass analog filter that bandlimits the input to frequencies below $f_s/2$ to prevent aliasing.

**Step 3 — Ideal characteristics.**

$$H_{\text{ideal}}(f) = \begin{cases} 1, & \vert f \vert \leq f_s/2 \\ 0, & \vert f \vert > f_s/2 \end{cases}$$

Features: brick-wall response, zero transition band, infinite stopband attenuation. **Physically unrealizable** (non-causal).

**Step 4 — Practical characteristics.** Defined by three regions:
- Passband: $\vert f \vert \leq f_p$ with ripple $\delta_p$ (typically $< 1\,\text{dB}$).
- Transition band: $f_p < \vert f \vert < f_s/2$ with finite roll-off.
- Stopband: $\vert f \vert \geq f_s/2$ with attenuation $\geq A_s$ (typically $40$–$80\,\text{dB}$).

**Step 5 — Comparison table.**

| **Property** | **Ideal** | **Practical** |
|:-------------|:----------|:--------------|
| Transition band | Zero | Finite |
| Attenuation | Infinite | Finite ($A_s$) |
| Realizability | No (non-causal) | Yes (causal) |
| Phase response | Zero | Nonlinear (or linear for Bessel) |

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks — block diagram; 2 marks — ideal description; 2 marks — practical description; 1 mark — comparison.)*

##### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Compute Nyquist frequency.**

$$f_N = f_s / 2 = 2500\,\text{Hz} = 2.5\,\text{kHz}$$

**Step 2 — Identify aliased components.** Components with $f > 2.5\,\text{kHz}$: **4 kHz and 7 kHz** will be aliased.

**Step 3 — Compute aliased frequency for 4 kHz.**

$$k = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{4000}{5000}\right) = 1$$

$$f_a = \vert 4000 - 1 \cdot 5000 \vert = 1000\,\text{Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Compute aliased frequency for 7 kHz.**

$$k = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{7000}{5000}\right) = 1$$

$$f_a = \vert 7000 - 1 \cdot 5000 \vert = 2000\,\text{Hz}$$

**Step 5 — Final mapping.**

| **Original $f_0$ (Hz)** | **Aliased $f_a$ (Hz)** | **Status** |
|:-----------------------:|:----------------------:|:-----------|
| 100 | 100 | Clean |
| 500 | 500 | Clean |
| 1200 | 1200 | Clean |
| 2500 | 2500 | At Nyquist edge |
| 4000 | 1000 | **Aliased** |
| 7000 | 2000 | **Aliased** |

**Step 6 — Conclusion.** The 4 kHz tone appears as 1 kHz, and the 7 kHz tone appears as 2 kHz. An anti-aliasing filter with cutoff $\leq 2.5\,\text{kHz}$ must precede sampling to prevent this.

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark — Nyquist calc; 2 marks — alias identification; 2 marks — frequency computation; 1 mark — final tabulation; 1 mark — AAF recommendation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> Students most commonly lose marks in the following ways:
> 1. **Placing the AAF after the sampler** — AAF must be in the *analog* domain. Marks deducted: 2.
> 2. **Forgetting the absolute value** in $f_a = \vert f_0 - k f_s \vert$. Marks deducted: 1.
> 3. **Wrong choice of $k$** — must be the integer that places $f_a$ in $[-f_s/2, f_s/2]$. Marks deducted: 1.
> 4. **Confusing Nyquist rate with Nyquist frequency** — $2 f_m$ vs. $f_s/2$. Marks deducted: 1.
> 5. **Omitting units** in filter specifications. Marks deducted: 0.5.
> 6. **Not rounding up** filter order to next integer. Marks deducted: 1.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Anti-Aliasing Filters**

- **Definition:** An anti-aliasing filter is a *continuous-time*, *analog*, *low-pass* filter placed *before* the ADC/sampler.
- **Purpose:** Bandlimits the input signal to $f_m \leq f_s/2$ to prevent spectral folding and irreversible data corruption.
- **Nyquist Frequency:** $f_N = f_s/2$ — the highest representable frequency without aliasing.
- **Nyquist Rate:** $f_{\text{Nyquist}} = 2 f_m$ — the minimum sampling rate for a bandlimited signal.
- **Aliasing Formula:** $f_a = \vert f_0 - k f_s \vert$ with $k$ chosen so that $\vert f_a \vert \leq f_s/2$.
- **Sampled Spectrum:** $X_s(f) = \dfrac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)$ — periodic replicas every $f_s$ Hz.
- **Ideal AAF:** Brick-wall response with infinite attenuation in the stopband; *physically unrealizable* (non-causal).
- **Practical AAF:** Has passband, transition band, and stopband; realizable with Butterworth, Chebyshev, Bessel, or Elliptic designs.
- **Butterworth Order Formula:** $N = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{10^{A_s/10} - 1}{10^{A_p/10} - 1}\right)}{\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{f_s/2}{f_p}\right)}$ — always round *up*.
- **Irreversibility:** Aliased content **cannot be recovered** by digital post-processing; prevention is the only cure.
- **Placement Rule:** AAF must be in the *analog domain*, *before* the sampler — never digital, never after sampling.
- **Common Examples:** Audio CDs (44.1 kHz with 20 kHz AAF), ECG systems (Butterworth with $f_c \approx 40\,\text{Hz}$ to $150\,\text{Hz}$), digital cameras (optical low-pass filter to prevent Moiré).
- **Filter Selection Heuristic:** Bessel for phase-critical (biomedical), Butterworth for general purpose, Chebyshev/Elliptic for steepest roll-off, Elliptic for sharpest AAF with relaxed phase.
- **Key Mistake to Avoid:** *Confusing reconstruction filter (after DAC) with anti-aliasing filter (before ADC)* — they are different physical filters in the system.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Discrete-Time Fourier Transforms - Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT604 - DIGITAL SIGNALS AND PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Discrete-Time Fourier Transforms - Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of the DTFT and Its Property Framework

The **Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT)** of a discrete-time signal $x[n]$ is a continuous, periodic function of the normalized angular frequency $\omega$ (in radians per sample), formally defined as:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] e^{-j\omega n}$$

The corresponding **Inverse DTFT (IDTFT)** is given by:

$$x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\omega}) e^{j\omega n} d\omega$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3)**
> A thorough understanding of DTFT properties is *non-negotiable* for Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions. The university frequently tests whether students can apply properties (e.g., duality, convolution, Parseval's) to **simplify computations** rather than blindly applying the summation definition.

**DTFT Properties** are mathematical identities that describe how the spectrum $X(e^{j\omega})$ is modified when specific operations (shifting, reversal, convolution, modulation, etc.) are performed in either the time domain or the frequency domain. Each property has a **dual partner**: a property of the DTFT and its corresponding property of the inverse DTFT.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Audio Equalizer" Intuition

Imagine a digital audio file (a discrete-time signal) being played through a graphic equalizer (the DTFT acts as this equalizer in the frequency domain). The equalizer sliders correspond to $X(e^{j\omega})$ at different frequencies $\omega$.

- **Time Shifting in DTFT** → equivalent to delaying a track by a few milliseconds. The *energy* of the song is unchanged, but its *phase* shifts because the waveform's start point moves. Similarly, $x[n-n_0] \leftrightarrow e^{-j\omega n_0} X(e^{j\omega})$.
- **Frequency Shifting (Modulation)** → like changing the pitch or the carrier frequency of a radio station: $e^{j\omega_0 n} x[n] \leftrightarrow X(e^{j(\omega-\omega_0)})$.
- **Convolution in Time** → equivalent to a cascade of two audio filters: the output spectrum is the *product* of individual spectra (multiplication is easier to compute than convolution).
- **Parseval's Relation** → like an energy audit: total energy computed in the time domain equals the total energy computed by integrating the squared magnitude spectrum (energy is conserved — it just changes form).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Study DTFT Properties?**
> 1. They allow **analytical simplification** of complex spectral computations.
> 2. They reveal **symmetries** (real, imaginary, even, odd) in signals.
> 3. They are essential for **system analysis** in Z-domain and LTI systems.
> 4. They are heavily tested in **Part A 3-mark questions** of KTU ESE.

## 1.3 Existence Condition of the DTFT

The DTFT $X(e^{j\omega})$ exists (converges absolutely) if and only if $x[n]$ is **absolutely summable**:

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert < \infty$$

Equivalently, if $x[n]$ is **square-summable** (finite energy), the DTFT exists in the mean-square sense:

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2 < \infty$$

This corresponds to the ROC of the Z-transform including the unit circle: $\vert z \vert = 1$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Master Catalog of DTFT Properties

The following properties form the **canonical KTU reference set** for ESE Module 3 questions. Each property has a forward transform pair and (where applicable) a dual partner.

### 2.1.1 Linearity (Superposition Principle)

If $x_1[n] \leftrightarrow X_1(e^{j\omega})$ and $x_2[n] \leftrightarrow X_2(e^{j\omega})$, then for any constants $a$ and $b$:

$$a\, x_1[n] + b\, x_2[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; a\, X_1(e^{j\omega}) + b\, X_2(e^{j\omega})$$

**Why it works:** The DTFT summation operator $\sum_{n}$ is linear — the exponential $e^{-j\omega n}$ is just a complex weight, and linear combination of weights is preserved.

### 2.1.2 Time Shifting

$$x[n-n_0] \;\longleftrightarrow\; e^{-j\omega n_0} X(e^{j\omega})$$

- Pure **time delay** in the discrete domain corresponds to multiplication by a complex exponential $e^{-j\omega n_0}$ in the frequency domain.
- The **magnitude spectrum** $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ remains unchanged; only the **phase** rotates linearly with $\omega$.

### 2.1.3 Frequency Shifting (Modulation)

$$e^{j\omega_0 n} x[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; X(e^{j(\omega - \omega_0)})$$

- Multiplying the signal by $e^{j\omega_0 n}$ **shifts the spectrum** to the right by $\omega_0$.
- This is the dual of time shifting: shift in one domain ↔ modulation by complex exponential in the other.

### 2.1.4 Time Reversal (Folding)

$$x[-n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; X(e^{-j\omega})$$

- For **real signals**, $X(e^{-j\omega}) = X^*(e^{j\omega})$, so time reversal equals complex conjugation in frequency.

### 2.1.5 Conjugation

$$x^*[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; X^*(e^{-j\omega})$$

- Combined with time reversal: $x^*[-n] \leftrightarrow X^*(e^{j\omega})$.

### 2.1.6 Time Expansion (Decimation by Integer M)

$$x_{(k)}[n] = \begin{cases} x[n/k], & n = \text{multiple of } k \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \;\longleftrightarrow\; X(e^{j\omega k})$$

### 2.1.7 Convolution Theorem (Most Important for KTU)

$$x[n] * y[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; X(e^{j\omega}) Y(e^{j\omega})$$

**Dual (Multiplication/Multiplication/Modulation):**

$$x[n] \, y[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\theta}) Y(e^{j(\omega-\theta)}) d\theta$$

This is the **periodic convolution** of the two spectra, taken over one period $2\pi$.

### 2.1.8 Differentiation in Frequency

$$n\, x[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; j \frac{d X(e^{j\omega})}{d\omega}$$

### 2.1.9 Differencing in Time

$$x[n] - x[n-1] \;\longleftrightarrow\; (1 - e^{-j\omega}) X(e^{j\omega})$$

### 2.1.10 Accumulation

$$\sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x[k] \;\longleftrightarrow\; \frac{1}{1 - e^{-j\omega}} X(e^{j\omega}) + \pi X(e^{j0}) \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(\omega - 2\pi k)$$

### 2.1.11 Conjugate Symmetry for Real Signals

If $x[n]$ is **real**, then:
- $X(e^{-j\omega}) = X^*(e^{j\omega})$ (conjugate symmetric spectrum).
- **Real part** of $X$: even function of $\omega$.
- **Imaginary part** of $X$: odd function of $\omega$.
- **Magnitude** $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert$: even.
- **Phase** $\angle X(e^{j\omega})$: odd.

### 2.1.12 Symmetry Properties — Complete Summary

| Property of $x[n]$ | DTFT $X(e^{j\omega})$ |
|---|---|
| Real | Conjugate symmetric: $X(e^{-j\omega}) = X^*(e^{j\omega})$ |
| Real and Even | Real and Even |
| Real and Odd | Imaginary and Odd |
| Imaginary | Conjugate anti-symmetric: $X(e^{-j\omega}) = -X^*(e^{j\omega})$ |

### 2.1.13 Parseval's Relation (Energy Conservation)

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 d\omega$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical interpretation:** The total energy of the signal can be computed either by summing squared samples in time or by integrating the energy spectral density $\frac{\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2}{2\pi}$ in frequency. This is the KTU favourite 3-mark short question.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **complete property catalog** you must memorize. Note the use of `\vert` instead of `|` to prevent markdown table breakage.

| # | Property | Time Domain $x[n]$ | Frequency Domain $X(e^{j\omega})$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Linearity | $a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]$ | $a X_1(e^{j\omega}) + b X_2(e^{j\omega})$ |
| 2 | Time Shift | $x[n - n_0]$ | $e^{-j\omega n_0} X(e^{j\omega})$ |
| 3 | Frequency Shift | $e^{j\omega_0 n} x[n]$ | $X(e^{j(\omega - \omega_0)})$ |
| 4 | Time Reversal | $x[-n]$ | $X(e^{-j\omega})$ |
| 5 | Conjugation | $x^*[n]$ | $X^*(e^{-j\omega})$ |
| 6 | Time Expansion | $x_{(k)}[n]$ | $X(e^{j\omega k})$ |
| 7 | Convolution | $x[n] * y[n]$ | $X(e^{j\omega}) Y(e^{j\omega})$ |
| 8 | Multiplication | $x[n] y[n]$ | $\frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\theta}) Y(e^{j(\omega - \theta)}) d\theta$ |
| 9 | Freq. Differentiation | $n x[n]$ | $j \frac{dX(e^{j\omega})}{d\omega}$ |
| 10 | Time Differencing | $x[n] - x[n-1]$ | $(1 - e^{-j\omega}) X(e^{j\omega})$ |
| 11 | Accumulation | $\sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x[k]$ | $\frac{X(e^{j\omega})}{1 - e^{-j\omega}} + \pi X(e^{j0}) \sum_{k} \delta(\omega - 2\pi k)$ |
| 12 | Conjugate Symmetry (real $x$) | $\text{Re}\{x[n]\}$ even, $\text{Im}\{x[n]\}$ odd | $X(e^{j\omega}) = X^*(e^{-j\omega})$ |
| 13 | Parseval's Theorem | $\sum_n \vert x[n] \vert^2$ | $\frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 d\omega$ |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Property Used |
|---|---|
| Radar/Doppler signal delay estimation | Time shifting + phase linearity |
| OFDM modulation/demodulation | Frequency shifting (modulation theorem) |
| Echo/Audio filtering (FIR convolution) | Convolution theorem |
| Power spectral density measurement | Parseval's theorem |
| Speech codec design (e.g., MP3) | Multiplication ↔ periodic convolution |
| Hilbert transformer design | Differencing + conjugation |
| Communication channel equalization | Deconvolution via spectral division |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Marks are awarded for *complete* derivations. Skipping even one algebraic step in Part B leads to a 1–2 mark cut. The following derivations are written in exam-ready form.

## 3.1 Derivation of the Time Shifting Property

**Statement:** $x[n - n_0] \;\longleftrightarrow\; e^{-j\omega n_0} X(e^{j\omega})$

**Proof:**

Let $y[n] = x[n - n_0]$. By the definition of the DTFT:

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} y[n] e^{-j\omega n}$$

Substituting $y[n] = x[n - n_0]$:

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n - n_0] e^{-j\omega n}$$

Apply the change of variable $m = n - n_0 \;\Rightarrow\; n = m + n_0$ and $d m = d n$:

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m] e^{-j\omega (m + n_0)}$$

Split the exponential:

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m] e^{-j\omega m} e^{-j\omega n_0}$$

Factor out the constant $e^{-j\omega n_0}$ (independent of $m$):

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega n_0} \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m] e^{-j\omega m}$$

The summation is the DTFT of $x[n]$:

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega n_0} X(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega n_0} X(e^{j\omega}) \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Frequency Shifting Property

**Statement:** $e^{j\omega_0 n} x[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; X(e^{j(\omega - \omega_0)})$

**Proof:**

Let $y[n] = e^{j\omega_0 n} x[n]$. The DTFT of $y[n]$ is:

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j\omega_0 n} x[n] e^{-j\omega n}$$

Combine the exponentials:

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] e^{-j(\omega - \omega_0) n}$$

This summation is exactly the DTFT of $x[n]$ evaluated at $\omega - \omega_0$:

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = X(e^{j(\omega - \omega_0)}) \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Convolution Theorem

**Statement:** $x[n] * y[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; X(e^{j\omega}) Y(e^{j\omega})$

**Proof:**

By definition, the convolution of two sequences is:

$$z[n] = x[n] * y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] y[n - k]$$

The DTFT of $z[n]$ is:

$$Z(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} z[n] e^{-j\omega n} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \left( \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] y[n-k] \right) e^{-j\omega n}$$

Assuming absolute convergence, swap the order of summation:

$$Z(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] \left( \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} y[n-k] e^{-j\omega n} \right)$$

Apply the time-shifting property to the inner sum: $y[n - k] \leftrightarrow e^{-j\omega k} Y(e^{j\omega})$:

$$Z(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] e^{-j\omega k} Y(e^{j\omega})$$

Factor $Y(e^{j\omega})$ outside the sum:

$$Z(e^{j\omega}) = Y(e^{j\omega}) \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] e^{-j\omega k} = Y(e^{j\omega}) X(e^{j\omega}) \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.4 Derivation of the Multiplication Theorem (Periodic Convolution)

**Statement:** $x[n] y[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\theta}) Y(e^{j(\omega - \theta)}) d\theta$

**Proof:**

Let $w[n] = x[n] y[n]$. Then:

$$W(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] y[n] e^{-j\omega n}$$

Express $x[n]$ via the IDTFT:

$$x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\theta}) e^{j\theta n} d\theta$$

Substitute:

$$W(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \left( \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\theta}) e^{j\theta n} d\theta \right) y[n] e^{-j\omega n}$$

Swap sum and integral:

$$W(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\theta}) \left( \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} y[n] e^{-j(\omega - \theta) n} \right) d\theta$$

The inner sum is the DTFT of $y[n]$ at $\omega - \theta$:

$$W(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\theta}) Y(e^{j(\omega - \theta)}) d\theta \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.5 Derivation of the Frequency Differentiation Property

**Statement:** $n x[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; j \frac{dX(e^{j\omega})}{d\omega}$

**Proof:**

Differentiate $X(e^{j\omega})$ with respect to $\omega$:

$$\frac{dX(e^{j\omega})}{d\omega} = \frac{d}{d\omega} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] e^{-j\omega n}$$

Assuming uniform convergence, interchange derivative and sum:

$$\frac{dX(e^{j\omega})}{d\omega} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \frac{d}{d\omega} e^{-j\omega n} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] (-jn) e^{-j\omega n}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{dX(e^{j\omega})}{d\omega} = -j \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} n x[n] e^{-j\omega n} = -j \cdot \text{DTFT}\{n x[n]\}$$

Rearranging:

$$\text{DTFT}\{n x[n]\} = j \frac{dX(e^{j\omega})}{d\omega} \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.6 Derivation of Parseval's Relation

**Statement:** $\sum_n \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 d\omega$

**Proof:**

Start with the right-hand side integral:

$$E = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\omega}) X^*(e^{j\omega}) d\omega$$

Substitute the IDTFT expression for $X^*(e^{j\omega})$ using the conjugate property $X^*(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_n x^*[n] e^{j\omega n}$:

$$X^*(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x^*[m] e^{j\omega m}$$

So:

$$E = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\omega}) \left( \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x^*[m] e^{j\omega m} \right) d\omega$$

Swap the integral and sum:

$$E = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x^*[m] \left( \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\omega}) e^{j\omega m} d\omega \right)$$

The bracketed integral is the IDTFT, which yields $x[m]$:

$$E = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x^*[m] \cdot x[m] = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[m] \vert^2 \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.7 Worked Example: Applying Properties to a Specific Signal

**Problem:** Compute the DTFT of $x[n] = a^{\vert n \vert}$ for $\vert a \vert < 1$ using the **time-reversal** and **conjugation** properties combined with the geometric series.

**Solution:**

For $n \geq 0$, the signal is $x[n] = a^n$. Its DTFT is the known geometric series:

$$X_+(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{1 - a e^{-j\omega}}$$

For $n < 0$, the signal is $x[n] = a^{-n}$. Let $n = -m$ where $m > 0$:

$$x[-m] = a^m, \quad m > 0$$

This is a time-reversed version of the one-sided exponential. By the **time-reversal property**:

$$\text{DTFT}\{x[-m]\} = X_+(e^{-j\omega}) = \frac{1}{1 - a e^{j\omega}}$$

Since $x[n]$ is the sum of these two one-sided parts:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{1 - a e^{-j\omega}} + \frac{1}{1 - a e^{j\omega}} - 1$$

(the $-1$ corrects for the $n=0$ double-counting). Combining:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{(1 - a e^{j\omega}) + (1 - a e^{-j\omega}) - (1 - a e^{-j\omega})(1 - a e^{j\omega})}{(1 - a e^{-j\omega})(1 - a e^{j\omega})}$$

Expanding the numerator:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1 - a^2}{1 - 2a \cos\omega + a^2} \quad \blacksquare$$

This is a real, even, non-negative function — a classic example used in KTU ESE 14-mark problems.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Functional Map: DTFT Property Architecture

The following diagram illustrates the **complete dual-domain architecture** of DTFT properties, showing how time-domain operations map to frequency-domain transformations and vice versa.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[DTFT Properties Framework] --> B[Linearity]
    A --> C[Shift Properties]
    A --> D[Symmetry Properties]
    A --> E[Convolution Multiplication]
    A --> F[Differentiation Properties]
    A --> G[Energy Properties]

    B --> B1[Time Domain: ax1 plus bx2]
    B --> B2[Freq Domain: aX1 plus bX2]

    C --> C1[Time Shift: x of n minus n0]
    C --> C2[Freq Shift: e power jw0n times x of n]
    C --> C3[Time Shift Map: Multiplication by e power minus jw n0]
    C --> C4[Freq Shift Map: Spectrum shift by w0]

    D --> D1[Time Reversal: x of minus n]
    D --> D2[Conjugation: x star of n]
    D --> D3[Map: X of e power minus jw]
    D --> D4[Map: X star of e power minus jw]

    E --> E1[Time Conv: x convolve y]
    E --> E2[Time Multiply: x times y]
    E --> E3[Map: Product of X and Y]
    E --> E4[Map: Periodic Convolution over 2 pi]

    F --> F1[Freq Differ: n times x of n]
    F --> F2[Time Differ: x of n minus x of n minus 1]
    F --> F3[Map: j dX by dw]
    F --> F4[Map: 1 minus e power minus jw times X]

    G --> G1[Time Energy: sum of mod x square]
    G --> G2[Freq Energy Integral: 1 by 2 pi integral of mod X square]
    G --> G3[Parseval Theorem: Equate Both]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Convolution Theorem Workflow

This flow diagram represents the **operational pipeline** when applying the convolution theorem in an LTI system.

```mermaid
graph LR
    sig1[Input Signal x of n] --> dtft1[Compute DTFT X of e power jw]
    sig2[Impulse Response h of n] --> dtft2[Compute DTFT H of e power jw]
    dtft1 --> mult[Spectral Multiplication]
    dtft2 --> mult
    mult --> outdtft[Output Spectrum Y of e power jw]
    outdtft --> idtft[Apply Inverse DTFT]
    idtft --> outsig[Output Signal y of n equals x convolve h]

    note1[Time Domain Convolution] -.-> conv[Direct Convolution Sum]
    sig1 --> conv
    sig2 --> conv
    conv --> outsig
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Decision Tree: Choosing the Right Property for a Problem

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Given Problem Type] --> q1{Is there a shift in n or w?}
    q1 -- Yes, in n --> ts[Use Time Shifting Property]
    q1 -- Yes, in w --> fs[Use Frequency Shifting Property]
    q1 -- No --> q2{Is there convolution?}
    q2 -- Yes, in time --> cv[Use Convolution Theorem: Product in Frequency]
    q2 -- Yes, in frequency --> mc[Use Multiplication Theorem: Periodic Convolution in Time]
    q2 -- No --> q3{Is the signal real or complex?}
    q3 -- Real --> sym[Apply Conjugate Symmetry]
    q3 -- Complex --> conj[Apply Conjugation and Time Reversal]
    q3 -- Neither --> q4{Is there multiplication by n?}
    q4 -- Yes --> fd[Use Frequency Differentiation]
    q4 -- No --> q5{Is energy to be computed?}
    q5 -- Yes --> pv[Use Parseval Theorem]
    q5 -- No --> lin[Use Linearity]
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Schematic: The "Dual Domain Mirror"

| Time Domain Operation | Mirror in Frequency Domain |
|---|---|
| Linear combination | Linear combination |
| Time shift $n_0$ | Phase multiplication $e^{-j\omega n_0}$ |
| Modulation $e^{j\omega_0 n}$ | Frequency shift $\omega_0$ |
| Time reversal $n \to -n$ | Spectrum reflection $\omega \to -\omega$ |
| Convolution $x * y$ | Spectral product $X \cdot Y$ |
| Product $x \cdot y$ | Periodic convolution |
| Differencing $\Delta x$ | Spectral filter $(1 - e^{-j\omega})$ |
| Multiplication by $n$ | Differentiation $j \frac{d}{d\omega}$ |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**State and prove the time-shifting property of the Discrete-Time Fourier Transform.**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** If $x[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; X(e^{j\omega})$, then the time-shifted signal $x[n - n_0]$ has DTFT:

$$x[n - n_0] \;\longleftrightarrow\; e^{-j\omega n_0} X(e^{j\omega})$$

**Proof:**

Let $y[n] = x[n - n_0]$. The DTFT of $y[n]$ is:

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n - n_0] e^{-j\omega n}$$

Let $m = n - n_0$, so $n = m + n_0$:

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m] e^{-j\omega(m + n_0)} = e^{-j\omega n_0} \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m] e^{-j\omega m} = e^{-j\omega n_0} X(e^{j\omega})$$

**Significance:** The magnitude spectrum is preserved; only a linear phase term is introduced. $\blacksquare$

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**State Parseval's theorem for DTFT and mention its significance.**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** For a finite-energy signal $x[n]$ with DTFT $X(e^{j\omega})$:

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 d\omega$$

**Significance:**

1. **Energy conservation:** Total energy computed in the time domain equals the energy computed by integrating the energy spectral density in the frequency domain.
2. **PSD interpretation:** $\frac{\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2}{2\pi}$ is the energy spectral density (ESD) of the signal.
3. **Practical use:** It enables engineers to compute signal energy from spectrum analyzer data without reconstructing the time-domain signal.
4. **Filter design utility:** Used in calculating energy in passband/stopband regions of filters. $\blacksquare$

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - **Pitfall 1:** Students often forget to apply the **periodic convolution** with the factor $\frac{1}{2\pi}$ when using the multiplication theorem. Marks deducted: up to 3.
> - **Pitfall 2:** When proving conjugate symmetry for real signals, students skip the algebraic manipulation $X(e^{-j\omega}) = \sum_n x[n] e^{j\omega n} = \left(\sum_n x[n] e^{-j\omega n}\right)^* = X^*(e^{j\omega})$. Marks deducted: 2.
> - **Pitfall 3:** Confusing the **time shifting** with **frequency shifting** properties. The exponential in time ($e^{j\omega_0 n}$) causes a *shift* in frequency, while a shift in time ($n - n_0$) causes an *exponential* factor in frequency.
> - **Pitfall 4:** Failing to mention **absolute convergence** assumptions when interchanging sums and integrals in convolution/multiplication proofs.

---

### Question A [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — (14 Marks)

**(a)** State and prove the **convolution theorem** for DTFT. Also, mention the dual property. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compute the DTFT of the signal $x[n] = (0.5)^{n+2} u[n+2]$ using the **time-shifting property**. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**Statement:** If $x[n] \leftrightarrow X(e^{j\omega})$ and $y[n] \leftrightarrow Y(e^{j\omega})$, then:

$$x[n] * y[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; X(e^{j\omega}) Y(e^{j\omega})$$

**Proof:** Given in Section 3.3 above (write the full steps as shown).

**Dual Property (Multiplication Theorem):**

$$x[n] y[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\theta}) Y(e^{j(\omega - \theta)}) d\theta \quad \text{(Periodic Convolution)}$$

This duality is a manifestation of the **duality principle**: convolution in time ↔ multiplication in frequency, and multiplication in time ↔ periodic convolution in frequency.

**Part (b):**

Given $x[n] = (0.5)^{n+2} u[n+2]$.

We can rewrite this as:

$$x[n] = (0.5)^{n+2} u[n+2] = (0.5)^2 \cdot (0.5)^n \cdot u[n+2] = 0.25 \cdot (0.5)^n u[n+2]$$

Recognize that $(0.5)^n u[n] \leftrightarrow \frac{1}{1 - 0.5 e^{-j\omega}}$ (standard right-sided exponential DTFT).

Now, $u[n+2]$ is the unit step shifted *left* by 2 units (i.e., advanced by 2). The signal $(0.5)^n u[n+2]$ can be expressed as the sum of three parts:

$$(0.5)^n u[n+2] = (0.5)^n u[n] + (0.5)^{n+1} u[n+1] + (0.5)^{n+2} u[n+2] \cdot \delta_{\text{add}}$$

Alternatively, observe the cleanest path: Use the **time-shifting property** by recognizing the signal is a *left-shifted* version of $a^n u[n]$ where the shifting index is $n_0 = -2$:

$$x[n] = (0.5)^{(n+2)} u[n+2] = y[n+2] \quad \text{where} \quad y[n] = (0.5)^n u[n]$$

So $x[n] = y[n+2]$, i.e., a **time advance** of 2 units (since $n_0 = -2$ in $y[n - n_0] = y[n - (-2)] = y[n+2]$).

Apply the time-shifting property $y[n - n_0] \leftrightarrow e^{-j\omega n_0} Y(e^{j\omega})$ with $n_0 = -2$:

$$x[n] = y[n+2] = y[n - (-2)] \;\longleftrightarrow\; e^{-j\omega(-2)} Y(e^{j\omega}) = e^{j2\omega} Y(e^{j\omega})$$

Since $Y(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5 e^{-j\omega}}$:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{e^{j2\omega}}{1 - 0.5 e^{-j\omega}}$$

This is the required DTFT. $\blacksquare$

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — (14 Marks)

**(a)** State and prove the **frequency differentiation** property of DTFT. Hence, find the DTFT of $x[n] = n a^n u[n]$ for $\vert a \vert < 1$. (7 marks)

**(b)** State and prove **Parseval's relation** for DTFT. Verify the relation for $x[n] = \delta[n]$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**Statement:** If $x[n] \leftrightarrow X(e^{j\omega})$, then:

$$n x[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; j \frac{dX(e^{j\omega})}{d\omega}$$

**Proof:** As given in Section 3.5 above.

**Application:** Given $x[n] = n a^n u[n]$, we have the base signal $a^n u[n]$ with DTFT:

$$X_0(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{1 - a e^{-j\omega}}$$

Therefore:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = j \frac{d}{d\omega} \left( \frac{1}{1 - a e^{-j\omega}} \right)$$

Differentiate using the chain rule. Let $u = 1 - a e^{-j\omega}$. Then $\frac{du}{d\omega} = -a(-j) e^{-j\omega} = j a e^{-j\omega}$:

$$\frac{d X_0}{d\omega} = -\frac{1}{u^2} \cdot \frac{du}{d\omega} = -\frac{j a e^{-j\omega}}{(1 - a e^{-j\omega})^2}$$

Therefore:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = j \cdot \left( -\frac{j a e^{-j\omega}}{(1 - a e^{-j\omega})^2} \right) = \frac{a e^{-j\omega}}{(1 - a e^{-j\omega})^2} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Part (b):**

**Statement:** As given in Section 3.6 above.

**Proof:** As given in Section 3.6.

**Verification for $x[n] = \delta[n]$:**

LHS (Time Domain Energy):

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert \delta[n] \vert^2 = \vert \delta[0] \vert^2 = 1^2 = 1$$

RHS (Frequency Domain Energy):

The DTFT of $\delta[n]$ is the constant $X(e^{j\omega}) = 1$ for all $\omega$. Therefore:

$$\frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \vert 1 \vert^2 d\omega = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} 1 \, d\omega = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot 2\pi = 1$$

LHS = RHS = 1. Hence Parseval's relation is verified. $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU ESE Module 3**

### Core Identities (Memorize)

- **Convolution Theorem:** $x[n] * y[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; X(e^{j\omega}) Y(e^{j\omega})$
- **Multiplication Theorem:** $x[n] y[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\theta}) Y(e^{j(\omega - \theta)}) d\theta$
- **Time Shift:** $x[n - n_0] \;\longleftrightarrow\; e^{-j\omega n_0} X(e^{j\omega})$
- **Frequency Shift:** $e^{j\omega_0 n} x[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; X(e^{j(\omega - \omega_0)})$
- **Freq. Differentiation:** $n x[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; j \frac{d X(e^{j\omega})}{d\omega}$
- **Time Differencing:** $x[n] - x[n-1] \;\longleftrightarrow\; (1 - e^{-j\omega}) X(e^{j\omega})$
- **Time Reversal:** $x[-n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; X(e^{-j\omega})$
- **Parseval's Theorem:** $\sum_n \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 d\omega$

### Symmetry Rules (For Real Signals)

- Spectrum is **conjugate symmetric:** $X(e^{j\omega}) = X^*(e^{-j\omega})$
- $\text{Re}\{X(e^{j\omega})\}$ is **even** in $\omega$
- $\text{Im}\{X(e^{j\omega})\}$ is **odd** in $\omega$
- $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ is **even** in $\omega$
- $\angle X(e^{j\omega})$ is **odd** in $\omega$

### Key Pitfalls to Avoid

1. **Do not confuse** $X(e^{j\omega})$ with $X(\Omega)$ (DTFT vs. CTFT) — the DTFT is **always periodic** with period $2\pi$.
2. The factor $\frac{1}{2\pi}$ in the **multiplication theorem** (periodic convolution) is often forgotten.
3. **Time reversal** flips the spectrum: $\omega \to -\omega$, which is equivalent to complex conjugation for *real* signals.
4. For **discrete convolution** $x[n] * y[n]$, the result length is $N_1 + N_2 - 1$ (for finite sequences).
5. Always check **absolute summability** before interchanging sums and integrals.

### Frequently Tested Topics (High-Weightage)

- **Convolution theorem proof** (Module 3 favourite — almost every KTU paper has it)
- **Parseval's theorem statement + verification** (3-mark frequent)
- **DTFT of $n a^n u[n]$ via differentiation property** (commonly asked in Part B)
- **Symmetry-based magnitude/phase plot analysis** (graphical problems)
- **Time-shifting combined with known transforms** (e.g., rectangular pulse shifts)

> **Final Tip:** When applying multiple properties in sequence, **list them step-by-step** with labels (e.g., "Step 1: Time Shift", "Step 2: Linearity"). Examiners award method marks generously when the logical chain is clearly shown.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Discrete Fourier Transform - Properties - DFT as a Linear Transformation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT604 - DIGITAL SIGNALS AND PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Discrete Fourier Transform - Properties - DFT as a Linear Transformation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)

The **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** is a fundamental mathematical transform in digital signal processing that converts a finite-length discrete-time sequence $x[n]$ of length $N$ into a finite-length discrete-frequency sequence $X[k]$ of the same length $N$. Formally, the $N$-point DFT is defined as:

$$X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \cdot e^{-j\frac{2\pi}{N}kn} = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \cdot W_N^{kn}, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \dots, N-1$$

where $W_N = e^{-j\frac{2\pi}{N}}$ is the **complex twiddle factor** (also called the *Nth root of unity*), and $j = \sqrt{-1}$ is the imaginary unit.

The corresponding **Inverse DFT (IDFT)** reconstructs the time-domain sequence as:

$$x[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] \cdot W_N^{-kn} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] \cdot e^{j\frac{2\pi}{N}kn}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \dots, N-1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The DFT is the cornerstone of *Module 3*. Examiners frequently test (i) the formulation of $W_N$, (ii) matrix representation, and (iii) the proof that DFT is a **linear transformation**.

### 1.2 What is a Linear Transformation?

In linear algebra, a **linear transformation** $T: \mathbb{C}^N \to \mathbb{C}^N$ is a mapping between two vector spaces that preserves the two fundamental operations of vector addition and scalar multiplication. Mathematically, $T$ is linear if and only if, for any vectors $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{C}^N$ and any scalars $a, b \in \mathbb{C}$:

$$T\{a\mathbf{x} + b\mathbf{y}\} = a \cdot T\{\mathbf{x}\} + b \cdot T\{\mathbf{y}\}$$

Every linear transformation in a finite-dimensional vector space can be represented as multiplication by a **square matrix** $\mathbf{W}$: $T(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{W}\mathbf{x}$.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Prism of Signals**
>
> Imagine you are standing in a room illuminated only by white light. Although the light looks uniform, it is actually a *mixture* of every color of the visible spectrum. When you pass this white light through a **glass prism**, the prism mathematically *decomposes* the incoming beam into its constituent color components (red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo, violet). Each color emerges at a specific angle, weighted by its strength in the original beam.
>
> The **DFT is the prism of digital signals**:
> - The **white light** = the time-domain signal $x[n]$ (an opaque mixture of unknown frequency components).
> - The **prism** = the DFT matrix $\mathbf{W}$ (a fixed mathematical operator).
> - The **separated rainbow** = the frequency-domain sequence $X[k]$ (each $k$-th sample tells you *how much* of frequency $k$ is present).
>
> Just as a prism is a *linear optical device* (it obeys superposition: red + blue light produces a rainbow whose components are the sum of the individual rainbows), the DFT is a **linear mathematical transformation** that obeys the superposition principle.

### 1.4 DFT as a Linear Transformation: The Core Idea

Because the DFT summation involves only a weighted linear combination of the input samples $x[n]$ (no products, no non-linear operations like squaring, logarithm, or rectification), it is a **linear** operation. Every output $X[k]$ is a linear function of every input $x[n]$, which means the entire DFT can be compactly written as a single **matrix-vector product**:

$$\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{W}_N \cdot \mathbf{x}$$

where $\mathbf{W}_N$ is the $N \times N$ **DFT matrix** whose $(k,n)$-th entry is $W_N^{kn}$. The $k$-th row of $\mathbf{W}_N$ is the "filter" or "basis vector" that extracts the $k$-th frequency component from $x[n]$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Viewing the DFT as a linear transformation unlocks the entire toolbox of linear algebra — matrix inversion, eigenvalues, unitary matrices, orthogonality, and singular value decomposition — to analyze and implement the transform efficiently (e.g., the **Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)** is essentially a clever factorization of $\mathbf{W}_N$).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Twiddle Factor $W_N$ — The Atom of the DFT

The **twiddle factor** is the fundamental complex exponential used in every DFT/IDFT operation:

$$W_N = e^{-j\frac{2\pi}{N}} = \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi}{N}\right) - j\sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi}{N}\right)$$

This is one of the $N$-th roots of unity on the **unit circle** of the complex plane. Its key algebraic properties are:

- $(W_N)^{N} = 1$
- $(W_N^{k})^* = W_N^{-k} = W_N^{N-k}$
- $W_N^{k+N} = W_N^{k}$ (**periodicity**)
- $\vert W_N^{k} \vert = 1$ for all $k$ (lies on the unit circle)

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The DFT operation can be decomposed into the following structured logic steps:

1. **Sample the input**: Collect $N$ time-domain samples $x[0], x[1], \dots, x[N-1]$ into a column vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{C}^{N \times 1}$.
2. **Construct the basis matrix**: Build the $N \times N$ matrix $\mathbf{W}_N$ with entry $(W_N)_{k,n} = W_N^{kn}$ (row $k$, column $n$).
3. **Perform matrix-vector multiplication**: Compute $\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{W}_N \mathbf{x}$ to obtain all $N$ frequency-domain samples simultaneously.
4. **Verify linearity check**: $\mathbf{W}_N (a\mathbf{x} + b\mathbf{y}) = a\mathbf{W}_N\mathbf{x} + b\mathbf{W}_N\mathbf{y}$ holds by distributivity of matrix multiplication.

### 2.3 Why the DFT is a Linear Transformation — The "Why" Behind It

- **The forward DFT** is a finite linear combination: each $X[k]$ is a *sum* of terms $x[n] \cdot W_N^{kn}$. Multiplication by the complex constant $W_N^{kn}$ is a linear scaling operation, and summing linear operations is linear.
- **The inverse DFT** has the same structure: $x[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_k X[k] W_N^{-kn}$ — again a linear combination, with a global scalar $1/N$.
- **Composition**: If $\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{W}_N \mathbf{x}$, then $\mathbf{x} = \frac{1}{N}\mathbf{W}_N^* \mathbf{X}$. The DFT matrix is therefore **invertible**, and its inverse is $\mathbf{W}_N^{-1} = \frac{1}{N}\mathbf{W}_N^*$, where $^*$ denotes the **conjugate transpose** (Hermitian adjoint).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Role of DFT as Linear Transformation |
|---|---|
| **Audio Signal Processing** | Equalizers, MP3 compression, noise cancellation |
| **Telecommunications (OFDM)** | Modulator/demodulator in 4G/5G systems uses IDFT/DFT |
| **Medical Imaging (MRI, CT)** | Fourier-based reconstruction algorithms |
| **Image Processing** | JPEG compression, edge detection, filtering |
| **Vibration Analysis** | Identifying fault frequencies in rotating machinery |
| **Radar & Sonar** | Matched filtering, target range-Doppler mapping |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a **rapid-revision cheat sheet** for the DFT. All absolute value bars have been replaced with `\vert` to preserve markdown table syntax.

| # | Formula / Property | Mathematical Expression | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Forward DFT | $X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] W_N^{kn}$ | $k = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$ |
| 2 | Inverse DFT | $x[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] W_N^{-kn}$ | $n = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$ |
| 3 | Twiddle Factor | $W_N = e^{-j 2\pi / N}$ | $\vert W_N \vert = 1$ |
| 4 | General Twiddle | $W_N^{kn} = e^{-j 2\pi kn / N}$ | $(k,n)$ indexed |
| 5 | Periodicity in $k, n$ | $W_N^{(k+N)n} = W_N^{k(n+N)} = W_N^{kn}$ | Periodic with period $N$ |
| 6 | Conjugate Property | $(W_N^{kn})^* = W_N^{-kn} = W_N^{N-kn}$ | $^*$ = complex conjugate |
| 7 | DFT Matrix Entry | $(\mathbf{W}_N)_{k,n} = W_N^{kn}$ | $k$-th row, $n$-th column |
| 8 | Matrix Form of DFT | $\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{W}_N \mathbf{x}$ | $N \times N$ matrix times $N \times 1$ vector |
| 9 | IDFT via Hermitian | $\mathbf{W}_N^{-1} = \frac{1}{N}\mathbf{W}_N^{H}$ | $\mathbf{W}_N^{H} = (\mathbf{W}_N)^*$ |
| 10 | Linearity | $\text{DFT}\{a x[n] + b y[n]\} = a X[k] + b Y[k]$ | $a, b$ = arbitrary complex scalars |
| 11 | Periodicity of $X[k]$ | $X[k+N] = X[k]$ | $X[k]$ is implicitly periodic |
| 12 | Periodicity of $x[n]$ | $x[n+N] = x[n]$ | $x[n]$ treated as periodic of period $N$ |
| 13 | Unitarity Check | $\mathbf{W}_N \mathbf{W}_N^{H} = N \mathbf{I}_N$ | $\mathbf{I}_N$ is identity matrix |
| 14 | Energy (Parseval's) | $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert X[k] \vert^2$ | Energy conservation |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Writing DFT as a Matrix-Vector Product

We begin with the definition of the $N$-point DFT:

$$X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] W_N^{kn}$$

**Step 1 — Expand the summation for each value of $k$:**

For $k=0$: $\quad X[0] = x[0] W_N^{0\cdot 0} + x[1] W_N^{0\cdot 1} + x[2] W_N^{0\cdot 2} + \dots + x[N-1] W_N^{0\cdot(N-1)}$

For $k=1$: $\quad X[1] = x[0] W_N^{1\cdot 0} + x[1] W_N^{1\cdot 1} + x[2] W_N^{1\cdot 2} + \dots + x[N-1] W_N^{1\cdot(N-1)}$

... and so on until $k = N-1$.

**Step 2 — Recognize the pattern.** Since $W_N^{0} = 1$, all entries simplify, and the system of equations takes the matrix form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} X[0] \\ X[1] \\ X[2] \\ \vdots \\ X[N-1] \end{bmatrix}
&=
\begin{bmatrix}
W_N^{0\cdot 0} & W_N^{0\cdot 1} & W_N^{0\cdot 2} & \cdots & W_N^{0\cdot(N-1)} \\
W_N^{1\cdot 0} & W_N^{1\cdot 1} & W_N^{1\cdot 2} & \cdots & W_N^{1\cdot(N-1)} \\
W_N^{2\cdot 0} & W_N^{2\cdot 1} & W_N^{2\cdot 2} & \cdots & W_N^{2\cdot(N-1)} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
W_N^{(N-1)\cdot 0} & W_N^{(N-1)\cdot 1} & W_N^{(N-1)\cdot 2} & \cdots & W_N^{(N-1)(N-1)}
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} x[0] \\ x[1] \\ x[2] \\ \vdots \\ x[N-1] \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compact notation.** Defining the column vector $\mathbf{X} = [X[0], X[1], \dots, X[N-1]]^T$, the column vector $\mathbf{x} = [x[0], x[1], \dots, x[N-1]]^T$, and the matrix $\mathbf{W}_N$ with entry $(k,n) = W_N^{kn}$, we obtain:

$$\boxed{\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{W}_N \, \mathbf{x}}$$

This is the **canonical matrix representation of the DFT** — a *single line* that captures the entire transform.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Explicit 4-Point DFT Matrix

For $N = 4$, the twiddle factor is:

$$W_4 = e^{-j\frac{2\pi}{4}} = e^{-j\frac{\pi}{2}} = -j$$

**Step 1 — Pre-compute the powers of $W_4$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_4^0 &= 1 \\
W_4^1 &= -j \\
W_4^2 &= (-j)^2 = -1 \\
W_4^3 &= (-j)^3 = j \\
W_4^4 &= 1 \quad (\text{back to } W_4^0) \\
W_4^5 &= -j \\
W_4^6 &= -1 \\
W_4^7 &= j
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Form the 4×4 DFT matrix** $\mathbf{W}_4$ where entry $(k,n) = W_4^{k \cdot n}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{W}_4 =
\begin{bmatrix}
W_4^0 & W_4^0 & W_4^0 & W_4^0 \\
W_4^0 & W_4^1 & W_4^2 & W_4^3 \\
W_4^0 & W_4^2 & W_4^4 & W_4^6 \\
W_4^0 & W_4^3 & W_4^6 & W_4^9
\end{bmatrix}
&=
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\
1 & -j & -1 & j \\
1 & -1 & 1 & -1 \\
1 & j & -1 & -j
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify symmetry.** Note that row 3 of $\mathbf{W}_4$ is the *conjugate* of row 1: $[1, -1, 1, -1]^* = [1, -1, 1, -1]$ (real, so equal). Row 4 is the conjugate of row 2: $[1, j, -1, -j]$ is the conjugate of $[1, -j, -1, j]$. This **conjugate symmetry** is a hallmark of the DFT matrix when the input is real-valued.

### 3.3 Formal Proof: Linearity of the DFT

**Theorem:** The DFT is a linear transformation. If $X_1[k] = \text{DFT}\{x_1[n]\}$ and $X_2[k] = \text{DFT}\{x_2[n]\}$, then for arbitrary complex constants $a$ and $b$:

$$\text{DFT}\{a \, x_1[n] + b \, x_2[n]\} = a \, X_1[k] + b \, X_2[k]$$

**Proof:**

**Step 1 — Apply the DFT definition to the linear combination $a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DFT}\{a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]\} &= \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \big(a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]\big) W_N^{kn}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Distribute the summation (linearity of the sum operator):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= a \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x_1[n] W_N^{kn} + b \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x_2[n] W_N^{kn}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Recognize each sum as a DFT:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= a \cdot X_1[k] + b \cdot X_2[k] \qquad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

**Alternative proof using the matrix form** (KTU favourite!):

Let $\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2 \in \mathbb{C}^{N\times 1}$ and scalars $a, b \in \mathbb{C}$. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{W}_N (a \mathbf{x}_1 + b \mathbf{x}_2) &= a (\mathbf{W}_N \mathbf{x}_1) + b (\mathbf{W}_N \mathbf{x}_2) \quad \text{(distributivity of matrix mult.)} \\
&= a \mathbf{X}_1 + b \mathbf{X}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

This satisfies the definition of linearity exactly. $\blacksquare$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — 4-Point DFT of a Real Sequence

**Problem:** Compute the 4-point DFT of $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ using the matrix method.

**Step 1 — Write the input as a column vector:**

$$\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 3 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by the 4×4 DFT matrix** (from §3.2):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{W}_4 \mathbf{x} &=
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\
1 & -j & -1 & j \\
1 & -1 & 1 & -1 \\
1 & j & -1 & -j
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 3 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute each row separately:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X[0] &= (1)(1) + (1)(2) + (1)(3) + (1)(4) = 1+2+3+4 = 10 \\
X[1] &= (1)(1) + (-j)(2) + (-1)(3) + (j)(4) = 1 - 2j - 3 + 4j = -2 + 2j \\
X[2] &= (1)(1) + (-1)(2) + (1)(3) + (-1)(4) = 1 - 2 + 3 - 4 = -2 \\
X[3] &= (1)(1) + (j)(2) + (-1)(3) + (-j)(4) = 1 + 2j - 3 - 4j = -2 - 2j
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final result:**

$$\boxed{\mathbf{X} = [10, \; -2+2j, \; -2, \; -2-2j]^T}$$

**Verification using IDFT matrix** (recover $x$ from $X$):

The IDFT matrix is $\mathbf{W}_4^{-1} = \frac{1}{4} \mathbf{W}_4^*$. Let's verify the first sample:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x[0] &= \frac{1}{4}\big[X[0] \cdot 1 + X[1] \cdot 1 + X[2] \cdot 1 + X[3] \cdot 1\big] \\
&= \frac{1}{4}[10 + (-2+2j) + (-2) + (-2-2j)] \\
&= \frac{1}{4}[10 - 2 - 2 - 2 + j(2 - 2)] = \frac{4}{4} = 1 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

This confirms the **unitarity** (up to the $1/N$ factor) of the DFT matrix.

### 3.5 Periodicity Property — Derivation

**Theorem:** The DFT $X[k]$ is periodic in $k$ with period $N$:

$$X[k+N] = X[k]$$

**Proof:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X[k+N] &= \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] W_N^{(k+N)n} = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] W_N^{kn} W_N^{Nn}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $W_N^{Nn} = (e^{-j2\pi/N})^{Nn} = e^{-j2\pi n} = 1^n = 1$ for all integer $n$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X[k+N] &= \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] W_N^{kn} \cdot 1 = X[k] \qquad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: DFT as a Linear Transformation Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the architecture of the DFT viewed as a linear transformation $\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{W}_N \mathbf{x}$. Each output $X[k]$ is computed as a weighted sum of all input samples $x[n]$, with weights $W_N^{kn}$ supplied by the corresponding row of the DFT matrix.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Vector x of size N] --> B[DFT Matrix W_N of size NxN]
    B --> C[Matrix Vector Multiplication]
    C --> D[Output Vector X of size N]
    E[Twiddle Factor W_N] --> B
    F[Row k of W_N] --> G[Weighted Sum for Xk]
    A --> G
    G --> D
```

### 4.2 Detailed Computational Flow — N=4 Case

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph inputN[Input Layer N samples]
        x0[x0]
        x1[x1]
        x2[x2]
        x3[x3]
    end

    subgraph matrixN[DFT Matrix W4 of order 4]
        r0[Row 0 contains 1 1 1 1]
        r1[Row 1 contains 1 negative j negative 1 j]
        r2[Row 2 contains 1 negative 1 1 negative 1]
        r3[Row 3 contains 1 j negative 1 negative j]
    end

    subgraph outputN[Output Layer N spectral samples]
        X0val[X0 DC component]
        X1val[X1 fundamental frequency]
        X2val[X2 second harmonic]
        X3val[X3 third harmonic]
    end

    x0 --> r0
    x1 --> r0
    x2 --> r0
    x3 --> r0
    r0 --> X0val

    x0 --> r1
    x1 --> r1
    x2 --> r1
    x3 --> r1
    r1 --> X1val

    x0 --> r2
    x1 --> r2
    x2 --> r2
    x3 --> r2
    r2 --> X2val

    x0 --> r3
    x1 --> r3
    x2 --> r3
    x3 --> r3
    r3 --> X3val
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Flow: Signal Decomposition Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Time Domain Signal xt] --> B[DFT Linear Transformation]
    B --> C[Frequency Domain Spectrum Xk]
    C --> D[Analysis and Filtering]
    D --> E[Inverse DFT IDFT]
    E --> F[Reconstructed Time Signal xt hat]

    G[Linearity Property] -.-> B
    H[Periodicity Property] -.-> B
    I[Matrix Multiplication] -.-> B
```

### 4.4 Properties Inheritance Map — DFT as Linear Transformation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[DFT Definition] --> B[Linear Transformation]
    A --> C[Periodic in k with period N]
    A --> D[Periodic in n with period N]
    A --> E[Conjugate Symmetry for real x]
    A --> F[Circular Convolution Theorem]
    A --> G[Parsevals Energy Theorem]

    B --> H[Matrix Vector Product]
    B --> I[Superposition Holds]
    B --> J[Invertible via W inverse]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**State and prove the linearity property of the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Statement:** The DFT is a linear transformation. If $X_1[k] = \text{DFT}\{x_1[n]\}$ and $X_2[k] = \text{DFT}\{x_2[n]\}$, then for any constants $a, b$:
> $$\text{DFT}\{a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]\} = a X_1[k] + b X_2[k]$$
>
> **Proof:**
> $$\begin{aligned} \text{DFT}\{a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]\} &= \sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\big(a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]\big)W_N^{kn} \\ &= a\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}x_1[n]W_N^{kn} + b\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}x_2[n]W_N^{kn} \\ &= a X_1[k] + b X_2[k] \end{aligned}$$
>
> This confirms that the DFT is a linear operator. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Define the twiddle factor $W_N$. State any two of its important properties.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition:** The twiddle factor is the complex exponential $W_N = e^{-j\frac{2\pi}{N}}$ used as the kernel of the DFT.
>
> **Properties (any two):**
> 1. **Unit modulus:** $\vert W_N^{k} \vert = 1$ for all integer $k$ (lies on the unit circle).
> 2. **Periodicity:** $W_N^{k+N} = W_N^{k}$ and $W_N^{N} = 1$.
> 3. **Conjugate symmetry:** $(W_N^{k})^* = W_N^{-k} = W_N^{N-k}$.
> 4. **Orthogonality:** $\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} W_N^{k(m-n)} = N \cdot \delta[m-n]$. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified]**

**(a) Express the $N$-point DFT as a linear transformation. Derive the $4 \times 4$ DFT matrix $\mathbf{W}_4$ and hence compute the DFT of $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Matrix form of DFT (2 marks):**

The $N$-point DFT equations $X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]W_N^{kn}$ for $k=0,1,\dots,N-1$ can be written compactly as:

$$\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{W}_N \mathbf{x}, \quad \text{where } (\mathbf{W}_N)_{k,n} = W_N^{kn}$$

**Step 2 — Twiddle factor for $N=4$ (1 mark):**

$$W_4 = e^{-j\frac{2\pi}{4}} = e^{-j\frac{\pi}{2}} = -j$$

Powers: $W_4^0 = 1, \; W_4^1 = -j, \; W_4^2 = -1, \; W_4^3 = j, \; W_4^4 = 1, \; W_4^6 = -1, \; W_4^9 = -j$.

**Step 3 — Construct $\mathbf{W}_4$ (2 marks):**

$$\mathbf{W}_4 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -j & -1 & j \\ 1 & -1 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & j & -1 & -j \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Compute DFT (2 marks):**

$$\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{W}_4 \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 3 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1+2+3+4 \\ 1-2j-3+4j \\ 1-2+3-4 \\ 1+2j-3-4j \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 10 \\ -2+2j \\ -2 \\ -2-2j \end{bmatrix}$$

---

**(b) Prove the periodicity property of the DFT in both time and frequency indices. Comment on why this property is essential for circular convolution.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Periodicity in $k$ (3 marks):**

$$X[k+N] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] W_N^{(k+N)n} = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] W_N^{kn} \cdot W_N^{Nn} = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] W_N^{kn} \cdot 1 = X[k]$$

Hence $X[k]$ is periodic with period $N$.

**Step 2 — Periodicity in $n$ (2 marks):**

By the duality of the transform (or by direct substitution into the IDFT), $x[n]$ is also treated as periodic with period $N$. This is because the IDFT sum has the same kernel $W_N^{-kn}$ and exhibits the same periodicity.

**Step 3 — Connection to circular convolution (2 marks):**

Multiplication in the frequency domain corresponds to **circular convolution** in the time domain. Because $X[k]$ and $x[n]$ are *both* treated as $N$-periodic, the convolution index "wraps around" modulo $N$ — this is the defining feature of **circular convolution**:

$$x_1[n] \circledast x_2[n] = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x_1[m] \, x_2[(n-m) \bmod N]$$

Without periodicity, the convolution would be linear (with growing length $2N-1$), not circular.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]**

**(a) Define the twiddle factor. Derive the Hermitian property $\mathbf{W}_N^{-1} = \frac{1}{N}\mathbf{W}_N^H$ of the DFT matrix.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Twiddle factor definition (1 mark):**

$$W_N = e^{-j\frac{2\pi}{N}}$$

**Step 2 — Set up the IDFT via the matrix (2 marks):**

From the IDFT formula:
$$x[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] W_N^{-kn}$$

In matrix form: $\mathbf{x} = \frac{1}{N} \mathbf{M} \mathbf{X}$ where $M_{n,k} = W_N^{-kn}$.

**Step 3 — Show $\mathbf{M} = \mathbf{W}_N^H$ (3 marks):**

The Hermitian (conjugate transpose) of $\mathbf{W}_N$ is defined by $(\mathbf{W}_N^H)_{n,k} = (\mathbf{W}_N)_{k,n}^* = (W_N^{kn})^* = W_N^{-kn}$. This matches $M_{n,k}$ exactly, so $\mathbf{M} = \mathbf{W}_N^H$.

**Step 4 — Conclude (1 mark):**

$$\mathbf{x} = \frac{1}{N} \mathbf{W}_N^H \mathbf{X} \implies \mathbf{W}_N^{-1} = \frac{1}{N} \mathbf{W}_N^H \quad \blacksquare$$

This means $\mathbf{W}_N \mathbf{W}_N^H = N \mathbf{I}_N$, i.e., $\mathbf{W}_N$ is a **unitary matrix up to a scalar** $1/\sqrt{N}$.

---

**(b) For the sequence $x[n] = \{1, 1, 1, 1\}$, compute the 4-point DFT using the matrix form. Comment on the result using the linearity property of the DFT.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Form input vector and use $\mathbf{W}_4$ (2 marks):**

$$\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{W}_4 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -j & -1 & j \\ 1 & -1 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & j & -1 & -j \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\mathbf{X}$ (2 marks):**

$$X[0] = 1+1+1+1 = 4, \quad X[1] = 1-1+1-1 = 0, \quad X[2] = 1-1+1-1 = 0, \quad X[3] = 1+1-1-1 = 0$$

So $\mathbf{X} = [4, 0, 0, 0]^T$.

**Step 3 — Use linearity for verification (2 marks):**

We can write $x[n] = \delta[n] + \delta[n-1] + \delta[n-2] + \delta[n-3]$. By linearity:
$$X[k] = \text{DFT}\{\delta[n]\} + \text{DFT}\{\delta[n-1]\} + \text{DFT}\{\delta[n-2]\} + \text{DFT}\{\delta[n-3]\} = 1 + W_N^k + W_N^{2k} + W_N^{3k}$$

For $k=0$: $X[0] = 1+1+1+1 = 4$. For $k \neq 0$: the sum is a geometric series $\frac{1-W_N^{4k}}{1-W_N^k} = 0$ since $W_N^{4k} = 1$. This matches the matrix result. ✓

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation (1 mark):**

A constant DC sequence has all its energy concentrated in $X[0]$ (the DC bin) and zero energy at all other frequencies — as expected for a constant signal.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Twiddle factor sign error:** Students often write $W_N = e^{+j2\pi/N}$ (positive exponent) instead of the standard convention $W_N = e^{-j2\pi/N}$ used in the *forward* DFT. The sign convention is *inconsistent* across textbooks — always check whether the question specifies "DFT" (negative exponent in forward) or "DTFT-like" notation.
>
> 2. **Missing the $1/N$ factor in IDFT:** A classic 1-mark loss. The IDFT matrix is $\frac{1}{N}\mathbf{W}_N^H$, *not* just $\mathbf{W}_N^H$.
>
> 3. **Exponent arithmetic in matrix entries:** For $\mathbf{W}_4$, entry $(2,3)$ is $W_4^{2 \cdot 3} = W_4^6 = W_4^2 = -1$ (using periodicity), not $W_4^6$ "left as is." Examiners want you to *reduce* the exponent modulo $N$ to a value in $\{0, 1, \dots, N-1\}$.
>
> 4. **Confusing linear and circular convolution:** The DFT-based convolution is *circular*, not linear. Students who write the linear convolution formula under a DFT question lose 2–3 marks.
>
> 5. **Forgetting that $X[0]$ is the DC component:** $X[0] = \sum_n x[n]$ is the *sum* of all samples (a real number for real input). Many students miss this elegant interpretation.
>
> 6. **Skipping the linearity proof:** For a 7-mark question, you must show *all three lines* of the proof (definition, distribute, recognize). Skipping intermediate steps loses 2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — DFT as a Linear Transformation**
>
> - [ ] The DFT is defined by $X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] W_N^{kn}$ where $W_N = e^{-j2\pi/N}$.
> - [ ] The IDFT is $x[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] W_N^{-kn}$.
> - [ ] The DFT can be written as a **matrix-vector product** $\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{W}_N \mathbf{x}$ with $(\mathbf{W}_N)_{k,n} = W_N^{kn}$.
> - [ ] The DFT matrix $\mathbf{W}_N$ is **square** ($N \times N$) and **always invertible**.
> - [ ] The inverse DFT matrix is $\mathbf{W}_N^{-1} = \frac{1}{N}\mathbf{W}_N^H$ (Hermitian / conjugate transpose).
> - [ ] The DFT matrix is **unitary up to a scalar** $1/\sqrt{N}$, i.e., $\mathbf{W}_N \mathbf{W}_N^H = N \mathbf{I}_N$.
> - [ ] **Linearity** is the most fundamental property: $\text{DFT}\{a x_1 + b x_2\} = a X_1 + b X_2$.
> - [ ] **Periodicity** holds in both $k$ and $n$ with period $N$: $X[k+N] = X[k]$ and $x[n+N] = x[n]$.
> - [ ] For $N=4$: $W_4 = -j$, $\mathbf{W}_4 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -j & -1 & j \\ 1 & -1 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & j & -1 & -j \end{bmatrix}$.
> - [ ] **Parseval's theorem** (energy conservation): $\sum_n \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum_k \vert X[k] \vert^2$.
> - [ ] $X[0]$ = DC value = $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]$.
> - [ ] For real $x[n]$: $X[N-k] = X^*[k]$ (conjugate symmetry).
> - [ ] DFT multiplication in frequency = **circular convolution** in time (not linear!).
> - [ ] The **FFT** is essentially a clever sparse factorization of $\mathbf{W}_N$ reducing complexity from $O(N^2)$ to $O(N \log_2 N)$.
> - [ ] The DFT is a *linear operator* on the vector space $\mathbb{C}^N$ — it commutes with scalar multiplication, addition, and matrix composition.
> - [ ] For KTU exams: always reduce twiddle exponents modulo $N$; always include the $1/N$ factor in IDFT; always verify linearity with the *two-condition* test (additivity + homogeneity).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Inverse Discrete Fourier Transform (IDFT)** is the mathematical operation that recovers the original discrete-time signal $x(n)$ from its frequency-domain representation $X(k)$, which was previously obtained using the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT).

For an $N$-point sequence, the IDFT is formally defined as:

$$x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) \, e^{\,j 2\pi k n / N}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$$

Using the twiddle factor notation $W_N = e^{-j2\pi/N}$, the IDFT can be compactly written as:

$$x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) \, W_N^{-kn}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PBEVT604 - Module 3):**
> The IDFT is the **exact dual** of the DFT. The factor of $\frac{1}{N}$ and the sign reversal in the exponent are the two key differences. Together, DFT and IDFFT form a **lossless transform pair** — no information is lost when moving between the time and frequency domains.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of the IDFT as a **musical synthesizer working in reverse**:
- The **DFT** is like breaking a song into a list of individual notes (frequencies) and their loudness (amplitudes). It is the **analyzer** — it tells you *what is in* the signal.
- The **IDFT** is like a **synthesizer** or a chef mixing ingredients — given a list of notes and their amplitudes, it **reconstructs the original song** by adding up all those frequency components (sine and cosine waves) at the right time instants.

A simpler analogy: imagine a smoothie. The DFT identifies the ingredients (banana, milk, sugar). The IDFFT recombines them back into the smoothie. The factor $\frac{1}{N}$ is like the "dilution factor" — since we are reconstructing one signal from $N$ frequency components, each component must contribute $\frac{1}{N}$ of its energy to the final result.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Twiddle Factor:** $W_N = e^{-j 2\pi / N}$ — a complex $N$-th root of unity.
- **Normalization Constant:** $\frac{1}{N}$ — ensures energy conservation between domains (Parseval's theorem).
- **Orthogonality Condition:** $\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} W_N^{k(m-n)} = N \, \delta(m-n)$ — this is the mathematical engine that makes the inversion exact.
- **Periodicity:** $x(n+N) = x(n)$ — IDFT output is always **periodic with period $N$** (even if original was not).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:**
> The IDFT guarantees a **perfect reconstruction** of the time-domain sequence. This is why DFT/IDFT pairs are the foundation of **OFDM (used in 4G/5G, Wi-Fi)**, **JPEG image compression**, and **MP3 audio coding**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How IDFT Works

The IDFT executes the following structured logic:

- **Step 1 — Receive Frequency Samples:** Take the $N$ complex values $X(k)$ for $k = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$ as input.
- **Step 2 — Weight Each Frequency Bin:** Multiply each $X(k)$ by the complex exponential $W_N^{-kn} = e^{j 2\pi kn / N}$ for the current time index $n$.
- **Step 3 — Accumulate the Sum:** Sum all $N$ weighted frequency components together.
- **Step 4 — Normalize:** Divide the resulting sum by $N$ to obtain $x(n)$.
- **Step 5 — Repeat for All $n$:** Steps 2–4 are repeated for $n = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$ to build the entire reconstructed sequence.

> **Why the sign reversal in the exponent?**
> The DFT used $W_N^{+kn}$ (negative imaginary part). To undo it, the IDFT must use $W_N^{-kn}$ (positive imaginary part), which is the **complex conjugate** of the forward exponent. This sign reversal is the rotational direction needed to "unwind" the frequency decomposition.

> **Why divide by $N$?**
> During the forward DFT, the energy from one time sample $x(n)$ gets distributed across all $N$ frequency bins. When we sum $N$ frequency contributions to recreate a single time sample, we have effectively **counted each contribution $N$ times**, so we must scale down by $\frac{1}{N}$ to recover the original amplitude.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Expression** | **Remarks** |
|---|---|---|
| IDFT Definition | $x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) \, e^{j 2\pi k n / N}$ | Primary equation; $n = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$ |
| IDFT in Twiddle Form | $x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) \, W_N^{-kn}$ | Where $W_N = e^{-j 2\pi / N}$ |
| Twiddle Factor | $W_N = e^{-j 2\pi / N} = \cos(2\pi / N) - j \sin(2\pi / N)$ | Complex $N$-th root of unity |
| DFT–IDFT Relation | $x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \left[ \text{DFT}\{X^*(k)\} \right]^*$ | IDFT computed using FFT algorithm |
| Matrix Form | $\mathbf{x} = \frac{1}{N} \mathbf{W_N^*} \, \mathbf{X}$ | $\mathbf{W_N}$ is the $N \times N$ DFT matrix |
| Periodicity | $x(n + N) = x(n)$ | IDFT output is periodic |
| Linearity | $\text{IDFT}\{aX_1(k) + bX_2(k)\} = a \, x_1(n) + b \, x_2(n)$ | For any scalars $a, b$ |
| Time Reversal | $\text{IDFT}\{X((-k))_N\} = x((-n))_N$ | Circular time reversal |
| Conjugate Symmetry | $\text{IDFT}\{X^*(k)\} = x^*((-n))_N$ | Real ↔ Conjugate symmetric |
| Circular Convolution Theorem | $\text{IDFT}\{X_1(k) \cdot X_2(k)\} = x_1(n) \circledast x_2(n)$ | Multiplication in freq = circular conv in time |
| Parseval's Theorem | $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x(n) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert X(k) \vert^2$ | Energy conservation between domains |
| Orthogonality Identity | $\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} W_N^{k(m-n)} = N \, \delta(m-n)$ | Mathematical basis of inversion |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Memorize the **matrix form** and the **DFT-via-IDFT trick** — these are frequent 7-mark questions in Module 3.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Application Domain** | **Role of IDFT** |
|---|---|
| **OFDM (4G/5G, Wi-Fi)** | Reconstructs time-domain OFDM symbols from modulated subcarriers at the receiver. |
| **Audio Coding (MP3, AAC)** | Converts MDCT frequency coefficients back into audible audio samples. |
| **Image Processing (JPEG)** | Inverse 2-D DCT (a real-valued cousin of IDFT) reconstructs pixel blocks. |
| **Spectral Analysis** | Verifies DFT computations by performing IDFT and comparing with original. |
| **Filter Design via Frequency Sampling** | Filters designed as $H(k)$ are mapped back to $h(n)$ via IDFT. |
| **Vibration Analysis in Mechanical Systems** | Reconstructs time-domain vibration signal from frequency spectra for fault diagnosis. |
