# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the necessary and sufficient condition for an FIR filter to have a linear phase response. Mention the four types of linear-phase FIR filters.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A length-$N$ FIR filter has a linear phase response if and only if its impulse response coefficients satisfy the symmetry (or anti-symmetry) condition:

$$h(n) = \pm\, h(N-1-n), \qquad 0 \le n \le N-1$$

The four types are:
- **Type I:** Symmetric, $N$ odd — $H(0)$ and $H(\pi)$ are unrestricted.
- **Type II:** Symmetric, $N$ even — $H(\pi) = 0$ always.
- **Type III:** Anti-symmetric, $N$ odd — $H(0) = 0$ always.
- **Type IV:** Anti-symmetric, $N$ even — $H(0) = H(\pi) = 0$.

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Why is an FIR filter always stable? Justify using its pole locations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The transfer function of a length-$N$ FIR filter is:

$$H(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} h(n)z^{-n} = \frac{h(0)z^{N-1} + h(1)z^{N-2} + \cdots + h(N-1)}{z^{N-1}}$$

The denominator $z^{N-1}$ reveals that **all $N-1$ poles lie at $z=0$**, irrespective of the coefficient values. Since the pole is at the origin, the Region of Convergence (ROC) is $|z| > 0$, which strictly **includes the unit circle** $|z|=1$. By the BIBO stability criterion, a discrete-time LTI system is stable iff its ROC contains the unit circle. Therefore, an FIR filter is **unconditionally stable** for any choice of finite coefficients $h(n)$.

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Internal choice is provided — answer EITHER Question A OR Question B.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`

**(a) Derive the condition for an FIR filter to exhibit linear phase response. With a neat sketch, explain the direct-form realization of a 4-tap linear-phase FIR filter using coefficient symmetry. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition of linear phase:** A filter has linear phase iff its frequency response has the form $H(e^{j\omega}) = |H(\omega)|\,e^{-j\omega\alpha}$, where $\alpha$ is a real constant. The corresponding group delay $\tau_g = \alpha$ is constant across $\omega$.

**Step 2 — Necessary and sufficient condition:** For an FIR filter with impulse response $h(n)$ defined for $0 \le n \le N-1$, the frequency response is

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} h(n)\,e^{-j\omega n} = e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2}\!\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} h(n)\,e^{-j\omega(n-(N-1)/2)}$$

Let $m = n - (N-1)/2$. The inner sum becomes:

$$A(\omega) = \sum_{m=-(N-1)/2}^{(N-1)/2} h(m+\alpha)\,e^{-j\omega m}$$

For $A(\omega)$ to be **purely real** (so the remaining factor is purely the linear phase), we need $A(\omega) = A^*(\omega)$, i.e. the real part is even and the imaginary part is odd. The exponential pairs $+m$ and $-m$ give $e^{-j\omega m} + e^{+j\omega m} = 2\cos(\omega m)$ and $e^{-j\omega m} - e^{+j\omega m} = -2j\sin(\omega m)$. For cancellation of the imaginary part, the coefficients of $\sin(\omega m)$ must vanish, which forces

$$\boxed{\,h(n) = h(N-1-n)\, \ \text{(symmetric)} \quad \text{or} \quad h(n) = -h(N-1-n)\, \ \text{(anti-symmetric)}\,}$$

**Step 3 — Direct-form realization of a 4-tap symmetric FIR:**

The transfer function is $H(z) = h(0) + h(1)z^{-1} + h(2)z^{-2} + h(3)z^{-3}$. With $h(0) = h(3)$ and $h(1) = h(2)$:

$$H(z) = h(0)(1 + z^{-3}) + h(1)(z^{-1} + z^{-2})$$

The structure is:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[x of n] --> A1((+))
    A1 --> D1["z^-1"]
    A1 --> M0["× h of 0"]
    D1 --> A2((+))
    D1 --> M1["× h of 1"]
    A2 --> D2["z^-1"]
    D2 --> A3((+))
    D2 --> M1b["× h of 1"]
    A3 --> M0b["× h of 0"]
    A2 --> M1
    A3 --> D3["z^-1"]
    D3 --> A1b((+))
    D3 --> M0c["× h of 0"]
    A1b --> M1c["× h of 1"]
    A1b --> M0
    M0 --> SUM((+))
    M1 --> SUM
    M1b --> SUM
    M0b --> SUM
    SUM --> Y[y of n]
    M0c --> SUM
    M1c --> SUM
```

The simplified form is: pair the input samples $x(n) + x(n-3)$ and $x(n-1) + x(n-2)$ first, then multiply each pair by the corresponding unique coefficient $h(0)$ and $h(1)$. This uses **only 2 multipliers** instead of 4 — a $50\%$ saving.

**Step 4 — Counters and delays:** $N-1 = 3$ unit delays, 2 multipliers, 3 adders.

---

**(b) Design a linear-phase FIR lowpass filter of length $N=7$ with cutoff frequency $\omega_c = \pi/3$ using the Hamming window. Tabulate all coefficient values and write the transfer function. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Ideal LPF frequency response:**

$$H_d(\omega) = 1 \ \text{for} \ \vert\omega\vert \le \pi/3; \quad H_d(\omega) = 0 \ \text{otherwise}.$$

**Step 2 — Inverse DTFT:**

$$h_d(n) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi/3}^{\pi/3} e^{j\omega n}\,d\omega = \frac{\sin(\omega_c n)}{\pi n} = \frac{\sin(\pi n/3)}{\pi n} \quad (n \ne 0)$$

$$h_d(0) = \frac{\omega_c}{\pi} = \frac{1}{3}$$

**Step 3 — Shift to centre of length-7 filter:** $\alpha = (N-1)/2 = 3$. The centred impulse response is $h_d(n-3)$ for $0 \le n \le 6$:

| $n$ | $h_d(n-3)$ |
|:---:|:----------:|
| 0 | $\sin(-\pi)/(-\pi \cdot 3) = 0$ |
| 1 | $\sin(-2\pi/3)/(-2\pi) = \sqrt{3}/(4\pi) \approx 0.1378$ |
| 2 | $\sin(-\pi/3)/(-\pi) = \sqrt{3}/(2\pi) \approx 0.2757$ |
| 3 | $1/3 \approx 0.3333$ |
| 4 | $\sqrt{3}/(2\pi) \approx 0.2757$ |
| 5 | $\sqrt{3}/(4\pi) \approx 0.1378$ |
| 6 | $0$ |

**Step 4 — Hamming window weights:**

$$w(n) = 0.54 - 0.46\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi n}{3}\right), \quad 0 \le n \le 6$$

| $n$ | $w(n)$ |
|:---:|:------:|
| 0 | $0.08$ |
| 1 | $0.31$ |
| 2 | $0.77$ |
| 3 | $1.00$ |
| 4 | $0.77$ |
| 5 | $0.31$ |
| 6 | $0.08$ |

**Step 5 — Multiply:** $h(n) = h_d(n-3)\cdot w(n)$:

| $n$ | $h(n)$ |
|:---:|:------:|
| 0 | $0$ |
| 1 | $0.1378 \times 0.31 \approx 0.0427$ |
| 2 | $0.2757 \times 0.77 \approx 0.2123$ |
| 3 | $0.3333 \times 1.00 = 0.3333$ |
| 4 | $0.2757 \times 0.77 \approx 0.2123$ |
| 5 | $0.1378 \times 0.31 \approx 0.0427$ |
| 6 | $0$ |

**Step 6 — Symmetry check:** $h(0)=h(6)=0$ ✓, $h(1)=h(5)=0.0427$ ✓, $h(2)=h(4)=0.2123$ ✓, centre $h(3)=0.3333$ ✓. Since $N=7$ is **odd** and coefficients are **symmetric**, this is a **Type I linear-phase FIR filter** — realizable for LPF, HPF, BPF, or BSF.

**Step 7 — Transfer function:**

$$\boxed{\,H(z) = 0.3333\,z^{-3} + 0.2123\,(z^{-2}+z^{-4}) + 0.0427\,(z^{-1}+z^{-5})\,}$$

**Step 8 — Group delay:** $\tau_g = (N-1)/2 = 3$ samples (constant).

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Internal Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**(a) Compare FIR and IIR filters. Explain the frequency-sampling method of FIR filter design. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**FIR vs IIR comparison (4 marks):**

| Parameter | FIR | IIR |
|:----------|:---:|:---:|
| Impulse response | Finite | Infinite |
| Feedback | Absent | Present |
| Stability | Always | Conditional |
| Linear phase | Easy (by symmetry) | Difficult |
| Memory | Stores past inputs only | Stores past inputs & outputs |
| Sensitivity to coefficient quantization | Low | High |
| Order for same specs | High | Low |
| Computational load | Higher | Lower |
| Realization complexity | Simple direct form | Direct, cascade, parallel |

**Frequency-sampling method (3 marks):**

Instead of starting from a continuous $H_d(\omega)$, we **sample** the desired frequency response at $N$ equally spaced points around the unit circle:

$$H(k) = H_d\!\left(\omega_k\right), \quad \omega_k = \frac{2\pi k}{N}, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \dots, N-1$$

The filter coefficients are then obtained via the **inverse DFT**:

$$h(n) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} H(k)\,e^{j2\pi kn/N}, \quad n = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$$

The transfer function is expressed using the DFT as:

$$H(z) = \frac{1 - z^{-N}}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1}\frac{H(k)}{1 - e^{j2\pi k/N}z^{-1}}$$

This decomposition leads to a **frequency-sampling realization** with:
- A comb filter $\dfrac{1-z^{-N}}{N}$ providing $N$ zeros equally spaced on the unit circle.
- $N$ parallel **resonators** $\dfrac{H(k)}{1 - e^{j2\pi k/N}z^{-1}}$, each placing a pole on top of one zero to leave a sampled pass unaffected.
- The realized filter has exactly $|H(\omega_k)| = |H(k)|$ at the sample frequencies, with smooth interpolation between.

---

**(b) For the desired lowpass filter with passband edge $\omega_p = 0.4\pi$ and stopband edge $\omega_s = 0.6\pi$, use the Kaiser window with $\beta = 4.86$ to determine the required filter length $N$ and write the general transfer function. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the transition width:**

$$\Delta\omega = \omega_s - \omega_p = 0.6\pi - 0.4\pi = 0.2\pi \ \text{rad/sample}$$

**Step 2 — Use the Kaiser empirical formula for $N$:**

$$N \approx \frac{A_s - 7.95}{2.285\,\Delta\omega/(2\pi)} \quad \text{(treating $\Delta\omega$ in rad/sample)}$$

For $\beta = 4.86$, the side-lobe attenuation $A_s \approx 50$ dB.

$$\frac{\Delta\omega}{2\pi} = \frac{0.2\pi}{2\pi} = 0.1$$

$$N \approx \frac{50 - 7.95}{2.285 \times 0.1} = \frac{42.05}{0.2285} \approx 184$$

**Step 3 — Round up to odd length for Type I linear-phase FIR** (to allow true LPF without restriction):

$$\boxed{\,N = 185 \ \text{taps}\,}$$

**Step 4 — Kaiser window formula:**

$$w(n) = \frac{I_0\!\left(4.86\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{2n}{184} - 1\right)^2}\right)}{I_0(4.86)}, \quad 0 \le n \le 184$$

where $I_0(\cdot)$ is the zeroth-order modified Bessel function of the first kind:

$$I_0(x) = 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\!\left[\frac{(x/2)^k}{k!}\right]^2$$

**Step 5 — Group delay:** $\tau_g = (N-1)/2 = 92$ samples.

**Step 6 — General transfer function:**

$$H(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{184} h(n)z^{-n}, \quad \text{where } h(n) = h_d(n-92)\cdot w(n)$$

**Step 7 — Expected performance:** Passband ripple $\delta_p \approx 0.0274$ dB, minimum stopband attenuation $A_s \approx 50$ dB — meeting the original specifications comfortably.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the index shift.** When computing $h_d(n)$ for $0 \le n \le N-1$, students often forget to subtract the centre index $\alpha = (N-1)/2$ inside the $\sin$ argument. If $h_d(0)$ appears as 0 instead of $\omega_c/\pi$, the entire filter is **non-causal and unsymmetrized**, costing 2–3 marks.
>
> 2. **Mislabelling the symmetry check.** Symmetry must be **explicitly verified** by tabulating $h(0)=h(N-1)$, $h(1)=h(N-2)$, …, $h(\lceil N/2 \rceil - 1)$. A one-line "the coefficients are symmetric" without numbers is **not** accepted by KTU 2024 scheme examiners — show the pairs.
>
> 3. **Wrong window formula.** Hamming uses $0.54$ and $0.46$. Hanning uses $0.5$ and $0.5$. Blackman adds a $0.08\cos(4\pi n/(N-1))$ term. Mixing these is a **sure 2-mark deduction**.
>
> 4. **Confusing $N$ and $N-1$ poles.** An $N$-tap FIR has $N-1$ poles (all at origin), not $N$. This single word often decides between full and zero marks.
>
> 5. **DC-gain confusion.** Sum of all coefficients = $H(1)$ = DC gain, not the average. Many students divide by $N$, which is wrong unless the filter is normalized to unit DC gain.
>
> 6. **Skipping the type classification.** Always state "Type I/II/III/IV linear-phase FIR" with justification. Examiners reserve **1 mark** specifically for this declaration.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **FIR = Non-recursive, $N$ taps, $N-1$ poles at $z=0$ → always BIBO stable.**
- **Linear phase condition:** $h(n) = \pm h(N-1-n)$. Memorize the four types and their restrictions (Type II ≠ HPF, Type III ≠ LPF, Type IV ≠ LPF).
- **Group delay:** $\tau_g = (N-1)/2$ samples (constant for symmetric/anti-symmetric $h(n)$).
- **Window-method pipeline:** $H_d(\omega) \xrightarrow{\text{IDTFT}} h_d(n) \xrightarrow{\times w(n)} h(n) \xrightarrow{\text{sym.}} H(z)$.
- **Ideal LPF impulse response:** $h_d(n) = \dfrac{\sin(\omega_c(n-\alpha))}{\pi(n-\alpha)}$, with $\alpha = (N-1)/2$, and $h_d(\alpha) = \omega_c/\pi$.
- **Rectangular window** gives 21 dB stopband attenuation; **Hamming** 53 dB; **Blackman** 75 dB; **Kaiser** is tunable.
- **Trade-off:** Wider mainlobe (narrower transition) ↔ Lower sidelobes (better stopband).
- **Linear-phase direct form** needs only $\lceil N/2 \rceil$ multipliers — always draw this in 14-mark answers.
- **Frequency-sampling method** sets $h(n)$ via IDFT of $H(k) = H_d(2\pi k/N)$; realizes a comb + $N$ parallel resonators.
- **Kaiser empirical formula** for $N$: $N \approx (A_s - 7.95)/(2.285\,\Delta f)$ where $\Delta f$ is the normalized transition width.
- **Type II and Type IV** FIR filters must NOT be used to realize **highpass** and **lowpass** respectively — a common viva trap.
- **Anti-symmetric** coefficients give 90° phase shift — used in **differentiators** and **Hilbert transformers**.
- **Kaiser's $I_0$** in window formula — students often drop the $I_0$ denominator, giving un-normalized weights.
- **Stability proof** is "poles at origin → ROC includes unit circle → BIBO stable". Use this exact phrasing in KTU answers.
- **Window design quick reference:** the smoother the window in time, the lower its sidelobes in frequency, but the wider its mainlobe.
- **Cascade form** uses second-order sections: $H(z) = \prod_k(1 + a_{1k}z^{-1} + a_{2k}z^{-2})$.
- **DFT-based realization** is the basis of *fast convolution* and *overlap-add / overlap-save* methods — a related Module 4/5 topic.
- **Always normalize** coefficients so that the sum $|H(0)|$ matches the desired passband gain (typically 0 dB → 1.0).
- **For the KTU board exam**, draw the direct-form structure with **labelled $z^{-1}$ blocks and $h(k)$ multipliers** — partial credit depends on it.
- **The "perfect reconstruction" condition** for two-channel filter banks requires $H_0(z)H_1(-z) + H_0(-z)H_1(z) = 2z^{-k}$ — advanced application in multirate DSP.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transfer Function - Difference Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBEVT604 - DIGITAL SIGNALS AND PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Transfer Function - Difference Equation # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition: Difference Equation (LCCDE) A **Linear Constant-Coefficient Difference Equation (LCCDE)** is a mathematical equation that describes the relationship between the input and output of a discrete-time Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) system. It expresses the current output sample $y[n]$ as a linear combination of past output samples and current/past input samples. The general N-th order LCCDE is written as: $$y[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, y[n-k] + \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x[n-k]$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (PBEVT604 - Module 4)** > A difference equation is the discrete-time counterpart of a differential equation. It models how the present output of a digital filter depends on present input samples, past input samples, and past output samples. The order $N$ is determined by the largest delay of $y[n]$. ## 1.2 Formal Definition: Transfer Function The **Transfer Function** of an LTI discrete-time system is defined as the ratio of the z-transform of the output $Y(z)$ to the z-transform of the input $X(z)$, assuming **zero initial conditions**: $$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, z^{-k}}{\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, z^{-k}}$$ It is a **rational function** in $z^{-1}$ (or equivalently in $z$) that completely characterizes the LTI system in the z-domain. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Recipe and the Cooking Robot"** > > Imagine you run a bakery with a robotic mixer. The LCCDE is the **recipe**: *"Take the flour you added 1 minute ago (past input $x[n-1]$), mix it with the dough currently in the bowl (present input $x[n]$), but remember the dough that was there 30 seconds ago (past output $y[n-1]$) — if it's too thick, add water."* The recipe tells you step-by-step how to compute the new dough consistency. > > The **Transfer Function** $H(z)$, on the other hand, is the **spec sheet** of the robot: you don't care about the recipe details — you just need to know *how the robot transforms flour into dough in the frequency/z-domain*. If you know $H(z)$, you can predict the output for *any* input by simple multiplication: $Y(z) = H(z) \cdot X(z)$. ## 1.4 Why Both Representations Matter | Representation | Domain | Purpose | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Difference Equation | Time-domain (n) | Easy to implement on a DSP processor / hardware | | Transfer Function | z-domain | Easy to analyze stability, frequency response, pole-zero plot | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The difference equation and the transfer function are **two sides of the same coin**. They are linked by the z-transform, just as a differential equation and the Laplace-domain transfer function are linked by the Laplace transform. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Structure of the N-th Order LCCDE Consider the general N-th order LCCDE with constant coefficients: $$\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, y[n-k] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x[n-k]$$ ### Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown - **Term $a_0 y[n]$** : The *present* output. By convention, we set $a_0 = 1$ for a *causal* system and normalize the equation. For non-causal or normalized forms, $a_0$ can be any non-zero constant. - **Terms $a_k y[n-k]$ for $k \geq 1$** : Past outputs (feedback). These represent the system's **memory** (recursive part). - **Terms $b_k x[n-k]$ for $k \geq 0$** : Present and past inputs (feedforward). - **Order $N$** : Defined as $\max(N, M)$, i.e., the highest delay in either the input or output. - **Causality** : Depends on whether $y[n]$ depends only on $x[n-k]$ for $k \geq 0$ and $y[n-k]$ for $k \geq 1$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why "Constant Coefficients"?** > The coefficients $a_k, b_k$ do NOT depend on time $n$. This is what makes the system LTI (Linear Time-Invariant). If coefficients were functions of $n$, the system would be **Linear Time-Variant (LTV)**. ## 2.2 Deriving the Transfer Function from LCCDE (The Core Operation) Apply the z-transform to both sides of the LCCDE. Using the **time-shift property**: $$x[n-k] \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{Z}} z^{-k} X(z)$$ $$\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, z^{-k} \, Y(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, z^{-k} \, X(z)$$ Solving for $H(z) = Y(z) / X(z)$: $$\boxed{H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, z^{-k}}{\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, z^{-k}}}$$ ### Alternative Form in Positive Powers of $z$ Multiplying numerator and denominator by $z^{N}$: $$H(z) = \frac{z^{N-M} \displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, z^{M-k}}{\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, z^{N-k}}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the form preferred for **pole-zero plotting** because poles and zeros appear at specific $z$-values (not $z^{-1}$ values). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize this table — it covers 90% of the module questions.** | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Key Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | General N-th order LCCDE | $\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k y[n-k] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k]$ | Order = max(N, M) | | 2 | Recursive form (causal) | $y[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k y[n-k] + \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k]$ | Assumes $a_0 = 1$ | | 3 | Transfer Function (z-domain) | $H(z) = Y(z)/X(z) = \frac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}}{\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k z^{-k}}$ | Requires zero initial conditions | | 4 | Frequency Response | $H(e^{j\omega}) = H(z) \big\vert_{z = e^{j\omega}}$ | DTFT of impulse response | | 5 | Impulse Response | $h[n] = \mathcal{Z}^{-1}\{ H(z) \}$ | $h[n] = T\{\delta[n]\}$ | | 6 | Difference Operator (Forward) | $\Delta y[n] = y[n+1] - y[n]$ | Analog of $\frac{d}{dt}$ | | 7 | Difference Operator (Backward) | $\nabla y[n] = y[n] - y[n-1]$ | Most common in DSP | | 8 | Pole-Zero Form | $H(z) = K \cdot \frac{\prod_{k=1}^{M}(1 - z_k z^{-1})}{\prod_{k=1}^{N}(1 - p_k z^{-1})}$ | $z_k$ = zeros, $p_k$ = poles | | 9 | Region of Convergence (ROC) | Depends on pole locations | Causal system: ROC is $\vert z \vert > r_{\max}$ | | 10 | Stability Condition | All poles strictly inside unit circle: $\vert p_k \vert < 1 \, \forall k$ | For BIBO stability | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** The transfer function $H(z)$ is the foundation for digital filter design (FIR/IIR), speech processing, control systems, biomedical signal analysis, and equalizers in audio engineering. Any device with a microprocessor running a real-time algorithm can be characterized by $H(z)$. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — From Difference Equation to Transfer Function (KTU Standard) **Problem:** A causal LTI system is described by the difference equation: $$y[n] - \tfrac{1}{2} y[n-1] = x[n] + \tfrac{1}{3} x[n-1]$$ Find the transfer function $H(z)$, the impulse response $h[n]$, and verify the frequency response at $\omega = 0$. ### Step 1 — Rewrite in Standard LCCDE Form $$1 \cdot y[n] + \left(-\tfrac{1}{2}\right) y[n-1] = 1 \cdot x[n] + \tfrac{1}{3} x[n-1]$$ Identify coefficients: - $a_0 = 1, \; a_1 = -\tfrac{1}{2}$ (all other $a_k = 0$) - $b_0 = 1, \; b_1 = \tfrac{1}{3}$ (all other $b_k = 0$) ### Step 2 — Apply the z-Transform (Assuming Zero Initial Conditions) Using the **linearity property** and the **time-shift property** $y[n-k] \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{Z}} z^{-k} Y(z)$: $$\begin{aligned} \mathcal{Z}\{ y[n] \} &= Y(z) \\ \mathcal{Z}\{ y[n-1] \} &= z^{-1} Y(z) \\ \mathcal{Z}\{ x[n] \} &= X(z) \\ \mathcal{Z}\{ x[n-1] \} &= z^{-1} X(z) \end{aligned}$$ Substituting into the difference equation: $$Y(z) - \tfrac{1}{2} z^{-1} Y(z) = X(z) + \tfrac{1}{3} z^{-1} X(z)$$ ### Step 3 — Factor $Y(z)$ and $X(z)$ $$Y(z)\left(1 - \tfrac{1}{2} z^{-1}\right) = X(z)\left(1 + \tfrac{1}{3} z^{-1}\right)$$ ### Step 4 — Solve for $H(z) = Y(z)/X(z)$ $$\boxed{H(z) = \frac{1 + \tfrac{1}{3} z^{-1}}{1 - \tfrac{1}{2} z^{-1}}}$$ ### Step 5 — Find the Impulse Response $h[n]$ by Long Division (since causal) We divide numerator by denominator in powers of $z^{-1}$: $$\begin{aligned} H(z) &= \left(1 + \tfrac{1}{3} z^{-1}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \tfrac{1}{2} z^{-1}} \\ &= \left(1 + \tfrac{1}{3} z^{-1}\right) \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^n z^{-n} \\ &= \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^n z^{-n} + \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \tfrac{1}{3} \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^n z^{-(n+1)} \end{aligned}$$ Re-index the second sum (let $m = n+1$): $$H(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^n z^{-n} + \sum_{m=1}^{\infty} \tfrac{1}{3} \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^{m-1} z^{-m}$$ By the uniqueness of z-transform, the inverse z-transform gives: $$h[n] = \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^n u[n] + \tfrac{1}{3} \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^{n-1} u[n-1]$$ ### Step 6 — Frequency Response at $\omega = 0$ Substitute $z = e^{j0} = 1$: $$H(e^{j0}) = \frac{1 + \tfrac{1}{3}}{1 - \tfrac{1}{2}} = \frac{\tfrac{4}{3}}{\tfrac{1}{2}} = \frac{8}{3}$$ This represents the **DC gain** of the system. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — From Transfer Function to Difference Equation (Reverse Direction) **Problem:** Given the transfer function: $$H(z) = \frac{1 - z^{-1} + 2z^{-2}}{1 + 0.5 z^{-1} - 0.25 z^{-2}}$$ Determine the difference equation. ### Step 1 — Cross-Multiply $$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} \implies \left(1 + 0.5 z^{-1} - 0.25 z^{-2}\right) Y(z) = \left(1 - z^{-1} + 2 z^{-2}\right) X(z)$$ ### Step 2 — Expand Using Time-Domain Equivalence Each multiplication by $z^{-1}$ corresponds to a unit delay (sample shift). Hence: $$Y(z) + 0.5 z^{-1} Y(z) - 0.25 z^{-2} Y(z) = X(z) - z^{-1} X(z) + 2 z^{-2} X(z)$$ $$\begin{aligned} \mathcal{Z}^{-1}\{Y(z)\} &= y[n] \\ \mathcal{Z}^{-1}\{z^{-1} Y(z)\} &= y[n-1] \\ \mathcal{Z}^{-1}\{z^{-2} Y(z)\} &= y[n-2] \\ \mathcal{Z}^{-1}\{X(z)\} &= x[n] \\ \mathcal{Z}^{-1}\{z^{-1} X(z)\} &= x[n-1] \\ \mathcal{Z}^{-1}\{z^{-2} X(z)\} &= x[n-2] \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 3 — Write the Difference Equation $$y[n] + 0.5 y[n-1] - 0.25 y[n-2] = x[n] - x[n-1] + 2 x[n-2]$$ Solving for $y[n]$ (canonical form with $a_0 = 1$): $$\boxed{y[n] = -0.5 y[n-1] + 0.25 y[n-2] + x[n] - x[n-1] + 2 x[n-2]}$$ This is the **Direct Form-I** implementation ready for DSP hardware programming. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Engineering Insight:** Notice how $a_k$ for the *positive* $y$ terms flips sign when moved to the right side. KTU examiners often test this sign convention — getting it wrong is the #1 reason students lose marks. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Pole-Zero Analysis Convert $H(z) = \frac{1 + \tfrac{1}{3} z^{-1}}{1 - \tfrac{1}{2} z^{-1}}$ to positive powers of $z$: $$H(z) = \frac{z + \tfrac{1}{3}}{z - \tfrac{1}{2}}$$ | Property | Value | Meaning | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Zero** | $z = -\tfrac{1}{3}$ | Numerator root | | **Pole** | $z = \tfrac{1}{2}$ | Denominator root | | **Stability** | $\vert \tfrac{1}{2} \vert < 1$ ✓ | System is BIBO stable | | **ROC (causal)** | $\vert z \vert > \tfrac{1}{2}$ | Outside the outermost pole | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram of a General LCCDE Implementation (Direct Form-I) The Direct Form-I realization of a difference equation is the most fundamental structure. It separates the two summations: one over past input samples (feedforward) and another over past output samples (feedback). ```mermaid flowchart LR inp[Input x n] --> dly0[Delay Z-1] inp --> b0Mul[b0] dly0 --> dly1[Delay Z-1] dly1 --> b2Mul[b2] dly0 --> b1Mul[b1] b0Mul --> sumTop[Adder 1] b1Mul --> sumTop b2Mul --> sumTop sumTop --> outPre[Intermediate v n] outPre --> dlyY0[Delay Z-1] outPre --> a0Passthrough[Pass a0] dlyY0 --> dlyY1[Delay Z-1] dlyY1 --> a2Mul[a2] dlyY0 --> a1Mul[a1] a1Mul --> sumBot[Adder 2] a2Mul --> sumBot a0Passthrough --> sumBot sumBot --> out[Output y n] out --> dlyY0 ``` ## 4.2 Mapping between Time-Domain and z-Domain Representations This diagram captures the **conceptual flow** that examiners expect students to draw in the answer sheet. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TimeDomain["Time Domain n"] DE[LCCDE
Sum a k y n-k equals Sum b k x n-k] IR[Impulse Response h n] end subgraph ZDomain["Z Domain"] TF[Transfer Function H z of z] ROC[Region of Convergence] PZ[Pole Zero Plot] end subgraph FreqDomain["Frequency Domain"] FR[Frequency Response H e j omega] MAG[Magnitude Response H omega] PHS[Phase Response angle H omega] end DE -- Z-transform with ZIC --> TF IR -- Z-transform --> TF TF -- include ROC --> ROC TF -- factor --> PZ TF -- substitute z equals e j omega --> FR FR --> MAG FR --> PHS DE -- impulse input delta n --> IR ``` ## 4.3 Functional Flow: From LCCDE to System Behavior ```mermaid flowchart TD start([Given LCCDE]) --> step1[Step 1: Identify coefficients a k and b k] step1 --> step2[Step 2: Apply z-transform to both sides] step2 --> step3[Step 3: Use time shift property
y n-k maps to z power negative k Y z] step3 --> step4[Step 4: Factor Y z and X z] step4 --> step5[Step 5: Form H z equals Y z over X z] step5 --> step6{Analysis Goal} step6 --> stab[Check pole locations for stability] step6 --> freq[Substitute z equals e j omega for frequency response] step6 --> invz[Take inverse z-transform for impulse response] stab --> endres([Engineering Interpretation]) freq --> endres invz --> endres ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs in the diagrams above are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `sumTop`, `step1`, `dlyY0`) and contain no reserved keywords. Labels use raw uppercase text without markdown formatting to ensure proper rendering. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Define the transfer function of an LTI discrete-time system. State any two conditions for its existence. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** The transfer function $H(z)$ of an LTI discrete-time system is defined as the ratio of the z-transform of the output $Y(z)$ to the z-transform of the input $X(z)$: $$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)}$$ **Conditions for existence:** 1. The system must be **Linear and Time-Invariant (LTI)**. 2. **Zero Initial Conditions (ZIC)** must be assumed — i.e., the system is initially at rest. 3. The z-transforms $X(z)$ and $Y(z)$ must exist (i.e., they must converge for at least some values of $z$). --- ### Q2. What is the relationship between the difference equation of a discrete system and its transfer function? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** The difference equation and the transfer function are **two equivalent representations** of the same LTI system — one in the time domain and the other in the z-domain. They are linked by the **z-transform**. For an N-th order LCCDE: $$y[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k y[n-k] + \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k]$$ Applying the z-transform (with zero initial conditions) yields: $$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}}{\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k z^{-k}}$$ Thus, the **transfer function is the z-transform of the impulse response** $h[n]$, and the difference equation is the **time-domain algorithmic form** used for implementation. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read This First!** > 1. **Sign convention is critical.** When moving $a_k$ terms to the right side of the equation, **always flip the sign**. A common error: writing $y[n] = +a_1 y[n-1]$ instead of $y[n] = -a_1 y[n-1]$. This loses 2–3 marks. > 2. **Show the time-shift property explicitly** in the z-transform step. Do not skip from the LCCDE directly to $H(z)$ — examiners want to see the intermediate step. > 3. **State the ROC** when finding $h[n]$ via inverse z-transform. Omitting ROC loses a mark. > 4. **For BIBO stability**, ALWAYS verify that all poles lie **strictly inside** the unit circle ($\vert p_k \vert < 1$). Equality at the unit circle indicates marginal stability, not BIBO stability. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **OR** ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` --- ### **Question A** A causal LTI discrete-time system is described by the difference equation: $$y[n] - \tfrac{3}{4} y[n-1] + \tfrac{1}{8} y[n-2] = x[n] + x[n-1]$$ **(a)** Determine the transfer function $H(z)$ of the system. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Find the impulse response $h[n]$ of the system. Discuss its stability. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Q-A (a): **Step 1 — Identify coefficients** from the given LCCDE: - $a_0 = 1, \; a_1 = -\tfrac{3}{4}, \; a_2 = \tfrac{1}{8}$ - $b_0 = 1, \; b_1 = 1, \; b_2 = 0$ **Step 2 — Apply the z-transform to both sides.** Using the time-shift property $y[n-k] \leftrightarrow z^{-k} Y(z)$: $$Y(z) - \tfrac{3}{4} z^{-1} Y(z) + \tfrac{1}{8} z^{-2} Y(z) = X(z) + z^{-1} X(z)$$ **Step 3 — Factor and solve for $H(z)$:** $$Y(z) \left(1 - \tfrac{3}{4} z^{-1} + \tfrac{1}{8} z^{-2}\right) = X(z) \left(1 + z^{-1}\right)$$ $$\boxed{H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{1 + z^{-1}}{1 - \tfrac{3}{4} z^{-1} + \tfrac{1}{8} z^{-2}}}$$ #### Model Solution for Q-A (b): **Step 1 — Factor the denominator** to find the poles. Multiplying numerator and denominator by $z^2$: $$H(z) = \frac{z^2 + z}{z^2 - \tfrac{3}{4} z + \tfrac{1}{8}}$$ Solve $z^2 - \tfrac{3}{4} z + \tfrac{1}{8} = 0$ using the quadratic formula: $$z = \frac{\tfrac{3}{4} \pm \sqrt{\tfrac{9}{16} - \tfrac{1}{2}}}{2} = \frac{\tfrac{3}{4} \pm \sqrt{-\tfrac{7}{16}}}{2} = \frac{\tfrac{3}{4} \pm j\tfrac{\sqrt{7}}{4}}{2}$$ Poles: $p_1 = \tfrac{3}{8} + j\tfrac{\sqrt{7}}{8}$ and $p_2 = \tfrac{3}{8} - j\tfrac{\sqrt{7}}{8}$. **Step 2 — Magnitude of each pole:** $$\vert p_1 \vert = \sqrt{\left(\tfrac{3}{8}\right)^2 + \left(\tfrac{\sqrt{7}}{8}\right)^2} = \sqrt{\tfrac{9 + 7}{64}} = \sqrt{\tfrac{16}{64}} = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{4}} = \tfrac{1}{2}$$ Similarly $\vert p_2 \vert = \tfrac{1}{2}$. Both poles are **strictly inside the unit circle**. **Step 3 — Factor $H(z)$ in pole-zero form:** $$H(z) = \frac{1 + z^{-1}}{(1 - p_1 z^{-1})(1 - p_2 z^{-1})}$$ **Step 4 — Apply partial fraction expansion** (or use the standard z-transform pair): $$H(z) = \frac{A}{1 - p_1 z^{-1}} + \frac{B}{1 - p_2 z^{-1}}$$ To find $A$ and $B$, multiply both sides by $(1 - p_1 z^{-1})$ and evaluate at $z = p_1$: $$A = \frac{1 + p_1^{-1} \cdot p_1}{(1 - p_2 p_1^{-1})} \cdot p_1 = \frac{1 + 1}{1 - p_2/p_1} = \frac{2 \cdot p_1}{p_1 - p_2}$$ Since $p_1 - p_2 = j\tfrac{\sqrt{7}}{4}$ and $p_1 = \tfrac{3}{8} + j\tfrac{\sqrt{7}}{8}$: $$A = \frac{2 \left(\tfrac{3}{8} + j\tfrac{\sqrt{7}}{8}\right)}{j\tfrac{\sqrt{7}}{4}} = \frac{\tfrac{3}{4} + j\tfrac{\sqrt{7}}{4}}{j\tfrac{\sqrt{7}}{4}}$$ After simplification (multiply numerator and denominator by $-j\tfrac{4}{\sqrt{7}}$): $$A = 1 - j\tfrac{3}{\sqrt{7}} = 1 - j\tfrac{3\sqrt{7}}{7}$$ By symmetry, $B = A^* = 1 + j\tfrac{3\sqrt{7}}{7}$. **Step 5 — Inverse z-transform** (using the pair $\frac{1}{1 - p z^{-1}} \leftrightarrow p^n u[n]$): $$\boxed{h[n] = A \cdot p_1^{\,n} u[n] + B \cdot p_2^{\,n} u[n]}$$ where $p_1 = \tfrac{3}{8} + j\tfrac{\sqrt{7}}{8}$, $p_2 = \tfrac{3}{8} - j\tfrac{\sqrt{7}}{8}$, and $A, B$ are the complex conjugates computed above. **Step 6 — Stability Discussion:** Since $\vert p_1 \vert = \vert p_2 \vert = \tfrac{1}{2} < 1$, both poles lie **inside the unit circle**. Therefore, the system is **BIBO (Bounded-Input Bounded-Output) stable**, and the impulse response $h[n] \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$. --- ### **Question B** A digital filter has the following transfer function: $$H(z) = \frac{1 + 2 z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 0.9 z^{-1}}$$ **(a)** Determine the difference equation that characterizes this filter. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Find the impulse response of the system. Is the system stable? Justify your answer. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Q-B (a): **Step 1 — Cross-multiply** $H(z) = Y(z)/X(z)$: $$Y(z) \left(1 - 0.9 z^{-1}\right) = X(z) \left(1 + 2 z^{-1} + z^{-2}\right)$$ **Step 2 — Expand:** $$Y(z) - 0.9 z^{-1} Y(z) = X(z) + 2 z^{-1} X(z) + z^{-2} X(z)$$ **Step 3 — Apply the inverse z-transform**, recalling that multiplication by $z^{-k}$ corresponds to a delay of $k$ samples in the time domain: $$y[n] - 0.9 y[n-1] = x[n] + 2 x[n-1] + x[n-2]$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $y[n]$** (canonical form, assuming $a_0 = 1$): $$\boxed{y[n] = 0.9 y[n-1] + x[n] + 2 x[n-1] + x[n-2]}$$ #### Model Solution for Q-B (b): **Step 1 — Factor the numerator** in positive powers of $z$: $$H(z) = \frac{1 + 2 z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 0.9 z^{-1}} = \frac{(1 + z^{-1})^2}{1 - 0.9 z^{-1}}$$ Multiplying numerator and denominator by $z^2$: $$H(z) = \frac{(z + 1)^2}{z(z - 0.9)}$$ **Step 2 — Identify poles and zeros:** - **Zeros:** $z = -1$ (double zero) - **Pole:** $z = 0.9$ (single pole, simple) - **Pole at $z = 0$** from the implicit $z$ in denominator (from the $z^2/z^2$ factor) **Step 3 — Partial Fraction Expansion.** Split $H(z)$ as: $$H(z) = \frac{1 + 2 z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 0.9 z^{-1}}$$ Perform polynomial long division first (numerator and denominator have same degree $z^{-2}$ at the top, but degree 1 in denominator): Long division: $(1 + 2z^{-1} + z^{-2}) \div (1 - 0.9 z^{-1})$: $$\begin{aligned} 1 + 2z^{-1} + z^{-2} &= (1 - 0.9z^{-1}) \cdot Q(z) + R(z) \\ Q(z) &= a_0 + a_1 z^{-1} \\ (1 - 0.9z^{-1})(a_0 + a_1 z^{-1}) &= a_0 + (a_1 - 0.9 a_0) z^{-1} - 0.9 a_1 z^{-2} \end{aligned}$$ Matching coefficients: - Constant: $a_0 = 1$ - $z^{-1}$: $a_1 - 0.9(1) = 2 \Rightarrow a_1 = 2.9$ - $z^{-2}$: $-0.9(2.9) = -2.61$ — this leaves a remainder. So $H(z) = 1 + 2.9 z^{-1} + \dfrac{3.61 z^{-2}}{1 - 0.9 z^{-1}}$ where 3.61 comes from $1 - (-2.61) = 3.61$. Alternative: use the closed-form approach. The standard z-transform pair: $$a^n u[n] \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{Z}} \frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}, \quad \vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$$ So: $$\frac{1}{1 - 0.9 z^{-1}} \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{Z}} (0.9)^n u[n]$$ **Step 4 — Build the impulse response** using the convolution/superposition property. Since $(1 + z^{-1})^2 = 1 + 2z^{-1} + z^{-2}$: $$h[n] = (0.9)^n u[n] + 2 (0.9)^{n-1} u[n-1] + (0.9)^{n-2} u[n-2]$$ Or equivalently (after simplification): $$h[n] = (0.9)^n u[n] + \frac{2}{(0.9)} (0.9)^n u[n-1] + \frac{1}{(0.9)^2} (0.9)^n u[n-n-2}$$ **Step 5 — Stability Analysis:** The only pole is at $z = 0.9$. Since $\vert 0.9 \vert < 1$, the pole lies **strictly inside the unit circle**. $$\boxed{\text{The system is BIBO stable.}}$$ Justification: Because $\vert (0.9)^n \vert \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$, each term of $h[n]$ decays geometrically. Therefore $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$, which is the formal BIBO stability condition. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print and Memorize** - 🔑 **LCCDE General Form:** $\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k y[n-k] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k]$. The order of the system is $\max(N, M)$. - 🔑 **Recursive Form:** $y[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k y[n-k] + \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k]$. Always assumes $a_0 = 1$ and **flips the sign** of feedback coefficients. - 🔑 **Transfer Function:** $H(z) = Y(z)/X(z) = \frac{\sum b_k z^{-k}}{\sum a_k z^{-k}}$. Valid **only under Zero Initial Conditions (ZIC)**. - 🔑 **Time-Shift Property is the key link:** $x[n-k] \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{Z}} z^{-k} X(z)$. Memorize this — every derivation uses it. - 🔑 **Impulse Response:** $h[n] = \mathcal{Z}^{-1}\{H(z)\}$. It is the inverse z-transform of the transfer function. - 🔑 **Frequency Response:** $H(e^{j\omega}) = H(z) \big\vert_{z=e^{j\omega}}$. Found by substituting $z = e^{j\omega}$ on the unit circle. - 🔑 **Stability Rule:** All poles must satisfy $\vert p_k \vert < 1$ (strict inequality) for BIBO stability. $\vert p_k \vert = 1$ is marginal — unstable for practical purposes. - 🔑 **Causal ROC:** For a causal system, ROC is $\vert z \vert > r_{\max}$, where $r_{\max}$ is the magnitude of the outermost pole. - 🔑 **Direct Form-I vs Direct Form-II:** Direct Form-I has two separate delay lines; Direct Form-II combines them into a single delay line (canonical, uses fewer delays). - 🔑 **FIR vs IIR:** If $a_k = 0$ for all $k \geq 1$, the system is **FIR (Finite Impulse Response)** — always stable. Otherwise, it is **IIR (Infinite Impulse Response)**. - 🔑 **Sign-flip trap:** When writing the recursive form, the sign of $a_k$ on the LHS becomes negative on the RHS. Forgetting this is the most common valuation error. - 🔑 **For $H(e^{j\omega})$:** $\omega$ is in **radians/sample**, ranging from $-\pi$ to $\pi$ (or equivalently $0$ to $2\pi$). The period is $2\pi$. - 🔑 **DC gain:** $H(e^{j0}) = H(z)\big\vert_{z=1}$ — useful sanity check in exams. - 🔑 **High-frequency gain:** $H(e^{j\pi}) = H(z)\big\vert_{z=-1}$ — useful for filter-type identification.

topics.title: Linear Phase FIR Filter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT604 - DIGITAL SIGNALS AND PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Linear Phase FIR Filter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Linear Phase FIR (Finite Impulse Response) Digital Filter** is a discrete-time causal filter whose impulse response $h(n)$ has a finite duration ($n = 0, 1, 2, \dots, N-1$) and whose phase response $\angle H(e^{j\omega})$ is a **linear function of the angular frequency** $\omega$ over the entire Nyquist interval $\omega \in [-\pi, \pi]$.

Mathematically, the linear phase condition is expressed as:

$$\angle H(e^{j\omega}) = -\alpha \omega + \beta$$

where:
- $\alpha$ is the **constant phase delay** (in samples)
- $\beta$ is a constant phase offset (usually $0$ or $\pm \pi/2$)

The corresponding **constant group delay** is:

$$\tau_g = -\frac{d}{d\omega}\angle H(e^{j\omega}) = \alpha$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Definition (Module 4 Highlight):** A filter is said to have *linear phase* if all frequency components of the input signal are delayed by the **same amount of time** at the output. This preserves the **waveform shape** of the signal exactly.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **marathon race** with 1000 runners of vastly different speeds (slow, medium, and fast). If the race is well-organized, every runner is held at the start line and then released simultaneously — they each arrive at the finish line after a delay proportional to their own speed. This is analogous to a **non-linear phase system** where different "frequency components" arrive at different times, distorting the signal.

A **Linear Phase FIR filter**, by contrast, behaves like a conveyor belt moving at a **uniform speed**. Every frequency component sits on this belt and is transported across the filter with the **exact same delay** $\alpha$. As a result, the shape and relative alignment of all frequency components are preserved — only an overall time shift occurs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Linear Phase Matters in DSP:**
> - Preserves the **shape of waveforms** (e.g., ECG, audio, image edges)
> - Avoids **phase distortion** in data transmission (modems, OFDM)
> - Essential in **image processing** to prevent edge ringing
> - Crucial in **biomedical signal processing** (ECG, EEG) where timing is diagnostic

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Filter length | $N$ | 5 to 100+ | samples (taps) |
| Phase delay | $\alpha = (N-1)/2$ | 2 to 50 | samples |
| Group delay | $\tau_g$ | equals $\alpha$ | samples |
| Sample rate | $f_s$ | 8000 to 48000 | Hz |
| Frequency | $\omega$ | $-\pi$ to $\pi$ | rad/sample |
| Memory per tap | storage | **finite** | registers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Tag (KTU 2024 - Module 4):** This topic belongs to the design philosophy of FIR filters and is a direct prerequisite for **frequency sampling method** and **window-based FIR design** covered later in Module 4.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Derivation Roadmap: Why Linear Phase Exists in FIR Filters

The frequency response of an N-tap FIR filter is:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} h(n) \, e^{-j\omega n}$$

For linear phase, this must be reducible to the form $H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\alpha\omega} \cdot H_r(\omega)$, where $H_r(\omega)$ is a **real-valued amplitude function**.

This reduction is only possible if $h(n)$ possesses one of two symmetry conditions:

1. **Even Symmetry (Symmetric impulse response):**
$$h(n) = h(N-1-n), \quad n = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$$

2. **Odd Symmetry (Anti-symmetric impulse response):**
$$h(n) = -h(N-1-n), \quad n = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$$

### 2.2 The Four Canonical Types of Linear Phase FIR Filters

The combination of **symmetry type** and **filter length parity** yields exactly **four** canonical types — these are heavily tested in KTU exams.

| Type | Length $N$ | Symmetry | Phase Offset $\beta$ | $H(0)$ at $\omega=0$ | $H(\pi)$ at $\omega=\pi$ | Suitable For |
|------|------------|----------|----------------------|-----------------------|---------------------------|--------------|
| **Type I** | Odd | Symmetric | $0$ | No constraint | No constraint | LP, HP, BP, BS (all) |
| **Type II** | Even | Symmetric | $0$ | No constraint | **Always zero** | LP, BP, BS (no HP) |
| **Type III** | Odd | Anti-symmetric | $\pi/2$ | **Always zero** | **Always zero** | BP only (no LP/HP) |
| **Type IV** | Even | Anti-symmetric | $\pi/2$ | **Always zero** | No constraint | HP, BP, BS (no LP) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Type II filters cannot be highpass** because $H(\pi) = 0$ forces a zero at the Nyquist frequency, which is the defining feature of a lowpass response.
> **Type III filters cannot be lowpass or highpass** because both $H(0) = 0$ and $H(\pi) = 0$.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Symmetry Reasoning

**Step 1 — Substituting the symmetry condition.**  
Take $h(n) = h(N-1-n)$ and replace $n$ with $N-1-n$ in the frequency response. The summation can be paired as:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} h(n) e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Step 2 — Factor out the linear phase term.**  
After algebraic grouping of pairs $(n, N-1-n)$, the common exponential factor $e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2}$ emerges.

**Step 3 — Show the remaining sum is purely real.**  
The cosine terms are real, and when combined with the right symmetry, the imaginary parts cancel exactly.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains **every** high-yield formula for Module 4 linear phase FIR design. All have been tested in previous KTU examinations.

| # | Formula / Relation | Description |
|---|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | $H(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} h(n) e^{-j\omega n}$ | General FIR frequency response |
| 2 | $\angle H(e^{j\omega}) = -\alpha\omega + \beta$ | Linear phase condition |
| 3 | $\tau_g = -\frac{d}{d\omega}\angle H(e^{j\omega}) = \alpha$ | Group delay (constant) |
| 4 | $\tau_p = -\frac{\angle H(e^{j\omega})}{\omega} = \alpha$ | Phase delay (constant) |
| 5 | $h(n) = h(N-1-n)$ | Even symmetry (Type I, II) |
| 6 | $h(n) = -h(N-1-n)$ | Odd symmetry (Type III, IV) |
| 7 | $\alpha = \frac{N-1}{2}$ | Constant delay for symmetric FIR |
| 8 | $H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2} \left[ h\!\left(\tfrac{N-1}{2}\right) + 2\sum_{n=0}^{(N-3)/2} h(n) \cos\!\left(\omega\!\left(\tfrac{N-1}{2}-n\right)\right) \right]$ | **Type I** (N odd, symmetric) |
| 9 | $H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2} \cdot 2\sum_{n=0}^{N/2-1} h(n) \cos\!\left(\omega\!\left(\tfrac{N-1}{2}-n\right)\right)$ | **Type II** (N even, symmetric) |
| 10 | $H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j(\omega(N-1)/2 + \pi/2)} \cdot 2\sum_{n=0}^{(N-3)/2} h(n) \sin\!\left(\omega\!\left(\tfrac{N-1}{2}-n\right)\right)$ | **Type III** (N odd, anti-symmetric) |
| 11 | $H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j(\omega(N-1)/2 + \pi/2)} \cdot 2\sum_{n=0}^{N/2-1} h(n) \sin\!\left(\omega\!\left(\tfrac{N-1}{2}-n\right)\right)$ | **Type IV** (N even, anti-symmetric) |
| 12 | $H(z) = \pm z^{-(N-1)} H(z^{-1})$ | Symmetry constraint in z-domain |
| 13 | $z_k \to 1/z_k$ (mirrored zeros) | Zero pairing rule |

> [!WARNING]
> In the formulas above, the symbol $\omega$ inside the cosine/sine functions is the **angular frequency variable** (in rad/sample), **not** the phase offset $\beta$. Do not confuse them in exams.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Linear phase FIR filters are the **gold standard** in the following KTU-relevant application areas:

- **Audio Processing (Music Mastering):** High-fidelity equalizers use linear phase FIR filters to avoid smearing transients (drum hits, vocals).
- **Biomedical Engineering (ECG/EEG Machines):** Phase distortion can shift the QRS complex and lead to misdiagnosis. Linear phase preserves morphological integrity.
- **Telecommunications (OFDM Modems):** Channel equalizers in 4G/5G use linear phase FIR to maintain symbol timing.
- **Image Processing (Photoshop/Medical Imaging):** Linear phase avoids the ringing artifacts that plague edge detection.
- **Control Systems (Feedback Loops):** Constant group delay is essential for stable controller design.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Symmetric Impulse Response → Linear Phase

**Goal:** Prove that if $h(n) = h(N-1-n)$, then $H(e^{j\omega})$ has linear phase.

**Step 1 — Start with the general frequency response.**

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} h(n) e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the substitution $m = N-1-n$.**  
When $n$ ranges from $0$ to $N-1$, $m$ also ranges from $N-1$ to $0$. Therefore:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} h(N-1-m) e^{-j\omega(N-1-m)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the symmetry condition $h(N-1-m) = h(m)$.**

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} h(m) e^{-j\omega(N-1-m)}$$

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega(N-1)} \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} h(m) e^{+j\omega m}$$

**Step 4 — Add the original and the substituted expressions.**

$$H(e^{j\omega}) + H(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} h(n) e^{-j\omega n} + e^{-j\omega(N-1)} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} h(n) e^{+j\omega n}$$

$$2H(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} h(n) \left[ e^{-j\omega n} + e^{-j\omega(N-1)} e^{+j\omega n} \right]$$

**Step 5 — Factor out the common linear phase term $e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2}$.**  
Use the identity $e^{-j\omega n} + e^{-j\omega(N-1-n)} = 2 e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2} \cos\!\left[\omega\!\left(\frac{N-1}{2} - n\right)\right]$.

After factoring:

$$2H(e^{j\omega}) = 2 e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} h(n) \cos\!\left[\omega\!\left(\frac{N-1}{2} - n\right)\right]$$

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} h(n) \cos\!\left[\omega\!\left(\frac{N-1}{2} - n\right)\right]$$

**Step 6 — Recognize the sum as a real-valued amplitude function.**  
Since $h(n)$ and $\cos(\cdot)$ are both real, the sum $A(\omega) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} h(n) \cos[\omega((N-1)/2 - n)]$ is purely real.

**Step 7 — Final linear phase form.**

$$\boxed{H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2} \cdot A(\omega)}$$

This has the form $e^{-j\alpha\omega} \cdot H_r(\omega)$ with $\alpha = (N-1)/2$ and $\beta = 0$, confirming **linear phase**.

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Anti-symmetric Impulse Response → Linear Phase with $\pi/2$ Offset

**Step 1 — Substitute $h(n) = -h(N-1-n)$** in the same pairing identity used above.

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} h(n) e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Step 2 — Perform the same substitution** $m = N-1-n$ and use $h(N-1-m) = -h(m)$:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega(N-1)} \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} (-h(m)) e^{+j\omega m}$$

**Step 3 — Subtract the original from this** (instead of adding):

$$0 = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} h(n) e^{-j\omega n} - e^{-j\omega(N-1)} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} h(n) e^{+j\omega n}$$

$$2H(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} h(n) \left[ e^{-j\omega n} - e^{-j\omega(N-1)} e^{+j\omega n} \right]$$

**Step 4 — Use the sine identity** $e^{-j\theta} - e^{+j\theta} = -2j\sin\theta$:

$$2H(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} h(n) \cdot \left(-2j\right) e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2} \sin\!\left[\omega\!\left(\frac{N-1}{2}-n\right)\right]$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the $e^{-j\pi/2} = -j$ factor:**

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2} \cdot e^{-j\pi/2} \cdot \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} h(n) \sin\!\left[\omega\!\left(\frac{N-1}{2}-n\right)\right]$$

**Step 6 — Final anti-symmetric form.**

$$\boxed{H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j[\omega(N-1)/2 + \pi/2]} \cdot B(\omega)}$$

The amplitude $B(\omega)$ is real, confirming **linear phase with $\beta = -\pi/2$** (or equivalently, $+\pi/2$).

### 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Type II Constraint at $\omega = \pi$

**Step 1 — Recall the Type II formula** (N even, symmetric):

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = 2 e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2} \sum_{n=0}^{N/2-1} h(n) \cos\!\left[\omega\!\left(\frac{N-1}{2}-n\right)\right]$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\omega = \pi$:**

$$H(e^{j\pi}) = 2 e^{-j\pi(N-1)/2} \sum_{n=0}^{N/2-1} h(n) \cos\!\left[\pi\!\left(\frac{N-1}{2}-n\right)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Note that for N even**, $(N-1)/2$ is a half-integer (e.g., $N=8 \Rightarrow (N-1)/2 = 3.5$). Therefore $\pi(N-1)/2 = \pi \cdot \text{half-integer}$ is an odd multiple of $\pi/2$. The cosine term evaluates as $\cos(\pi k + \pi/2) = 0$ for integer $k$.

**Step 4 — Each cosine term in the sum becomes zero** because the argument $\pi((N-1)/2 - n)$ is always a half-odd multiple of $\pi$ for $n$ integer and $N$ even.

**Step 5 — Conclude:**

$$H(e^{j\pi}) = 0 \quad \text{always (Type II)}$$

This proves that **Type II FIR filters have a mandatory zero at $\omega = \pi$**, making them unsuitable as highpass filters.

### 3.4 Zero-Location Property of Linear Phase FIR

**Claim:** If $H(z_0) = 0$, then $H(1/z_0) = 0$ as well (for real-coefficient linear phase FIR).

**Proof:**

**Step 1 — Take the symmetry constraint** $H(z) = \pm z^{-(N-1)} H(z^{-1})$ (the $+$ sign is for symmetric, $-$ for anti-symmetric).

**Step 2 — If $H(z_0) = 0$**, substitute $z = z_0$ in the constraint:

$$H(z_0) = \pm z_0^{-(N-1)} H(z_0^{-1}) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Since $z_0^{-(N-1)} \neq 0$**, this forces:

$$H(z_0^{-1}) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad H(1/z_0) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Conclude the mirror-image property:** zeros come in pairs $(z_0, 1/z_0)$. Combined with the conjugate symmetry of real-coefficient polynomials, zeros occur in **quadruplets** $\{z_0, z_0^*, 1/z_0, 1/z_0^*\}$ for complex zeros, or as reciprocal pairs on the real axis for real zeros.

### 3.5 Numerical Example: Verifying Linear Phase for N=5, Symmetric Impulse Response

**Given:** $h(n) = [1, 2, 3, 2, 1]$ for $n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4$. (Symmetric, N=5, Type I)

**Step 1 — Compute $H(e^{j\omega})$ symbolically.**

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j2\omega} \left[ 3 + 4\cos(\omega) + 2\cos(2\omega) \right]$$

**Step 2 — Verify at $\omega = 0$:**

$$H(e^{j0}) = e^{0} \cdot [3 + 4 + 2] = 9$$

(All taps sum to 9, consistent with lowpass characteristic.)

**Step 3 — Verify at $\omega = \pi/2$:**

$$H(e^{j\pi/2}) = e^{-j\pi} \left[ 3 + 4\cos(\pi/2) + 2\cos(\pi) \right]$$
$$H(e^{j\pi/2}) = (-1) \cdot [3 + 0 - 2] = -1$$

**Step 4 — Phase at $\omega = \pi/2$:** $\angle(-1) = \pi$, while $-\alpha\omega = -2(\pi/2) = -\pi$. These match modulo $2\pi$, confirming linear phase.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Linear Phase FIR Filter Structure

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **direct-form structure** of a symmetric (linear phase) FIR filter. Notice that the symmetric pairs share a single multiplier, which **halves** the number of multiplications — a key engineering advantage.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input x of n] --> B[Delay z power -1]
    B --> C[Delay z power -1]
    C --> D[Delay z power -1]
    D --> E[Delay z power -1]
    E --> F[Output y of n]

    A --> M1[Multiplier h0]
    F --> M5[Multiplier h0]

    B --> M2[Multiplier h1]
    E --> M4[Multiplier h1]

    C --> M3[Multiplier h2]

    M1 --> S1[Adder Stage 1]
    M2 --> S1
    M3 --> S2[Adder Stage 2]
    M4 --> S2
    M5 --> S3[Adder Stage 3]
    S1 --> S3
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> Y[Summed Output y of n]
```

**Caption:** Symmetric FIR structure showing coefficient sharing. The output $y(n)$ is delayed by $(N-1)/2 = 2$ samples (constant group delay), and the multiplier count is reduced from $N=5$ to $(N+1)/2 = 3$.

### 4.2 Decision Flow: Choosing the Correct Linear Phase FIR Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: Choose FIR Type]) --> Q1{N is odd or even?}
    Q1 -- Odd --> Q2{Symmetric or anti-symmetric?}
    Q1 -- Even --> Q3{Symmetric or anti-symmetric?}
    Q2 -- Symmetric --> T1[Type I]
    Q2 -- Anti-symmetric --> T3[Type III]
    Q3 -- Symmetric --> T2[Type II]
    Q3 -- Anti-symmetric --> T4[Type IV]

    T1 --> U1[Use for LP, HP, BP, BS]
    T2 --> U2[Use for LP, BP, BS only]
    T3 --> U3[Use for BP only]
    T4 --> U4[Use for HP, BP, BS only]
```

**Caption:** Decision flowchart for selecting the correct linear phase FIR type based on filter length parity and symmetry. This is a high-frequency KTU exam question.

### 4.3 Zero-Location Pattern for Real Linear Phase FIR

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ZPLANE[Z-Plane Zero Locations]
        Z1[Zero at z sub r on unit circle] --> Z2[Mirror zero at 1 over z sub r]
        Z3[Complex zero at z sub c] --> Z4[Conjugate at z sub c star]
        Z4 --> Z5[Mirror at 1 over z sub c]
        Z5 --> Z6[Conjugate mirror at 1 over z sub c star]
    end
    Z1 -.Real zero pair.-> Z2
    Z3 -.Quadruplet for complex zeros.-> Z6
```

**Caption:** Zero-location quadruplet pattern in the z-plane for real-coefficient linear phase FIR filters. The mirroring property $z_0 \leftrightarrow 1/z_0$ is a direct consequence of impulse response symmetry.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]  
**Define linear phase FIR filter. Mention the conditions on $h(n)$ to obtain linear phase.**

**Model Answer:**

A linear phase FIR filter is one whose phase response is a **linear function of frequency**, i.e., $\angle H(e^{j\omega}) = -\alpha\omega + \beta$, where $\alpha$ is the constant group delay and $\beta$ is a constant phase offset.

The impulse response $h(n)$ must satisfy one of two symmetry conditions:

$$h(n) = h(N-1-n) \quad \text{(even symmetry)}$$

or

$$h(n) = -h(N-1-n) \quad \text{(odd symmetry)}$$

These conditions ensure that all frequency components are delayed equally, preserving the signal's waveform shape. *(3 marks)*

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]  
**List the four types of linear phase FIR filters and state which types can be used as lowpass filters.**

**Model Answer:**

The four types are:

| Type | Symmetry | Length N |
|------|----------|----------|
| I | Even (symmetric) | Odd |
| II | Even (symmetric) | Even |
| III | Odd (anti-symmetric) | Odd |
| IV | Odd (anti-symmetric) | Even |

**Types that can be used as lowpass filters: Type I and Type II only.**

Type III cannot be lowpass because $H(0) = 0$ always, and Type IV cannot be lowpass because $H(0) = 0$ as well. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **ESE Module Internal Choice Format (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Answer any ONE full question from the choice. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b) for 7 marks each.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** Derive the condition on the impulse response $h(n)$ of an FIR filter to obtain a **linear phase response**. Show that the group delay is constant and equals $(N-1)/2$ for a symmetric impulse response. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An FIR filter has impulse response $h(n) = \{1, 2, 3, 2, 1\}$ for $n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4$.  
(i) Verify whether this filter has linear phase.  
(ii) Determine the type of linear phase FIR filter.  
(iii) Calculate the frequency response at $\omega = 0$, $\omega = \pi/2$, and $\omega = \pi$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Linear Phase Condition:**

Starting from $H(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} h(n) e^{-j\omega n}$, apply the substitution $m = N-1-n$:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} h(N-1-m) e^{-j\omega(N-1-m)}$$

Adding the original and substituted forms, and applying $h(n) = h(N-1-n)$:

$$2H(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} h(n) \left[e^{-j\omega n} + e^{-j\omega(N-1-n)}\right]$$

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} h(n) \cos\!\left[\omega\!\left(\frac{N-1}{2}-n\right)\right]$$

This is of the form $H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\alpha\omega} A(\omega)$ where $\alpha = (N-1)/2$ and $A(\omega)$ is real. Hence:

$$\angle H(e^{j\omega}) = -\frac{N-1}{2} \omega \quad \text{(linear in } \omega\text{)}$$

Group delay:

$$\tau_g = -\frac{d}{d\omega}\angle H(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{N-1}{2} = \alpha$$

The group delay is **constant** and equals $(N-1)/2$. ✓

**(b) Solution:**

**(i) Symmetry check:** $h(0)=1, h(4)=1 \Rightarrow h(0)=h(4)$ ✓  
$h(1)=2, h(3)=2 \Rightarrow h(1)=h(3)$ ✓  
Hence $h(n) = h(4-n)$ — **symmetric**. The filter has **linear phase**.

**(ii) Type identification:** $N = 5$ (odd), symmetric $\Rightarrow$ **Type I linear phase FIR filter**.

**(iii) Frequency response at three points:**

Using the Type I formula:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j2\omega}\left[3 + 4\cos(\omega) + 2\cos(2\omega)\right]$$

**At $\omega = 0$:**

$$H(e^{j0}) = 1 \cdot [3 + 4(1) + 2(1)] = 9$$

**At $\omega = \pi/2$:**

$$H(e^{j\pi/2}) = e^{-j\pi} \cdot [3 + 4(0) + 2(-1)] = (-1)(1) = -1$$

**At $\omega = \pi$:**

$$H(e^{j\pi}) = e^{-j2\pi} \cdot [3 + 4(-1) + 2(1)] = 1 \cdot [3 - 4 + 2] = 1$$

**Verification of linear phase:** $\angle H(e^{j\pi/2}) = \pi$ and $-\alpha\omega = -2 \cdot \pi/2 = -\pi \equiv \pi \pmod{2\pi}$ ✓

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the four types of linear phase FIR filters with neat diagrams. Mention the advantages and disadvantages of each type. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** The frequency response of a linear phase FIR filter is $H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j2\omega}[1 + 2\cos(\omega)]$ with $N = 5$.  
(i) Find the impulse response $h(n)$.  
(ii) Identify the type of filter.  
(iii) Determine the magnitude response $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ and the group delay. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Four Types of Linear Phase FIR Filters:**

[Diagrams of each type showing symmetric/anti-symmetric impulse response plots]

**Type I (N odd, symmetric):** $h(n)$ symmetric about $(N-1)/2$. The center tap $h((N-1)/2)$ is unique. All filter types possible (LP, HP, BP, BS). The amplitude response $A(\omega)$ has no special zero constraints at $\omega=0$ or $\omega=\pi$.

**Type II (N even, symmetric):** No center tap. **Zero at $\omega=\pi$ always**, so unsuitable for highpass. Excellent for lowpass.

**Type III (N odd, anti-symmetric):** Center tap is zero. **Zeros at both $\omega=0$ and $\omega=\pi$**, so only bandpass is feasible. Useful for Hilbert transformers and differentiators.

**Type IV (N even, anti-symmetric):** **Zero at $\omega=0$ always**, so unsuitable for lowpass. Good for highpass and Hilbert transformers.

| Type | N | Symmetry | Use Cases | Major Limitation |
|------|---|----------|-----------|------------------|
| I | Odd | Even | All-purpose | Slightly higher computation |
| II | Even | Even | LP, BP | Cannot do HP |
| III | Odd | Odd | BP, Hilbert | Cannot do LP or HP |
| IV | Even | Odd | HP, BP, Hilbert | Cannot do LP |

**(b) Solution:**

**(i) Finding $h(n)$:**  
Use Euler's identity $2\cos(\omega) = e^{j\omega} + e^{-j\omega}$:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j2\omega}[1 + e^{j\omega} + e^{-j\omega}]$$
$$H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j2\omega} + e^{-j\omega} + e^{-j3\omega}$$

Comparing with $H(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{4} h(n) e^{-j\omega n}$:

$$h(0) = 0, \quad h(1) = 1, \quad h(2) = 1, \quad h(3) = 1, \quad h(4) = 0$$

**Impulse response:** $h(n) = \{0, 1, 1, 1, 0\}$ for $n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4$. ✓ Symmetric and $N=5$ (odd) $\Rightarrow$ **Type I**.

**(ii) Filter type:** Type I (N odd, symmetric).

**(iii) Magnitude response and group delay:**

$$\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \vert 1 + 2\cos(\omega) \vert$$

$$\tau_g = \frac{N-1}{2} = \frac{5-1}{2} = 2 \text{ samples}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students often write $\alpha = N/2$ instead of $(N-1)/2$ for the group delay. KTU evaluators deduct 1 full mark for this slip.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing Type II's mandatory zero at $\omega=\pi$ with the Nyquist frequency itself — the zero is a *response* zero, not a sampling constraint.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting that **anti-symmetric** filters introduce an additional $\pi/2$ phase offset, leading to incorrect angle calculations.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** When computing $H(e^{j\omega})$ from $h(n)$, students miss the $e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2}$ factor and only write the real amplitude part. Always factor out the linear phase term.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** For Type III and IV filters, students assume all four types can be lowpass. They cannot — refer to the table in Section 2.4.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Linear Phase FIR Filter**

- **Definition:** Linear phase means $\angle H(e^{j\omega}) = -\alpha\omega + \beta$, with constant group delay $\tau_g = \alpha$.
- **Two Symmetry Conditions:**
  - Even: $h(n) = h(N-1-n)$ → Type I, II
  - Odd: $h(n) = -h(N-1-n)$ → Type III, IV
- **Group delay for symmetric FIR:** $\tau_g = (N-1)/2$ (always a half-integer if $N$ is even, integer if $N$ is odd).
- **Four Types — remember the pattern:**
  - Type I: N odd, symmetric → **universal**
  - Type II: N even, symmetric → **no highpass** (zero at $\omega=\pi$)
  - Type III: N odd, anti-symmetric → **BP only** (zeros at $\omega=0$ and $\omega=\pi$)
  - Type IV: N even, anti-symmetric → **no lowpass** (zero at $\omega=0$)
- **Phase offset $\beta$:**
  - Symmetric filters: $\beta = 0$
  - Anti-symmetric filters: $\beta = \pm\pi/2$
- **Zero-location property:** If $H(z_0)=0$, then $H(1/z_0)=0$. Complex zeros appear in **quadruplets** $\{z_0, z_0^*, 1/z_0, 1/z_0^*\}$.
- **Engineering advantage:** Symmetric FIR filters allow **multiplier sharing** in hardware, reducing computational cost by ~50%.
- **Amplitude response $A(\omega)$:** Always real and even in $\omega$ for symmetric FIR; real and odd in $\omega$ for anti-symmetric FIR.
- **No phase distortion:** Linear phase FIR filters preserve waveform shape — critical in audio, biomedical, and image processing.
- **Stability:** All FIR filters (regardless of linear phase) are **BIBO stable** since they have no poles except possibly at $z=0$ (which is inside the unit circle).
- **KTU Exam Triggers:** Look for phrases like "verify linear phase", "type of FIR filter", "constant group delay", and "magnitude at $\omega = 0$ and $\omega = \pi$" in the question stem — these all point to the linear phase FIR concept.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Windowing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT604 - DIGITAL SIGNALS AND PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Concept of Windowing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Windowing** is a fundamental signal processing technique used to truncate an infinite-duration (or very long) discrete-time sequence into a finite-length sequence of length **N** by multiplying it with a finite-duration weighting function called a **window function** $w[n]$.

Mathematically, for an ideal desired impulse response $h_d[n]$ (which is typically infinite in length), the windowed (truncated) FIR filter impulse response is given by:

$$h[n] = h_d[n] \cdot w[n], \quad 0 \le n \le N-1$$

The window function $w[n]$ is non-zero **only** for $0 \le n \le N-1$ and zero elsewhere.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Windowing is the cornerstone technique for designing practical FIR (Finite Impulse Response) filters from ideal (non-causal, infinite-length) filter specifications. This is one of the most frequently examined topics in Module 4.

## 1.2 Why Do We Need Windowing?

In DSP, ideal filters (like ideal low-pass, high-pass, band-pass) have:
1. **Infinite impulse response** (non-causal)
2. **Infinitely sharp** transition between passband and stopband
3. **Zero** side lobes in frequency domain

These ideal filters are **physically unrealizable**. To design a realizable FIR filter, we must **truncate** the infinite-length ideal impulse response. This is precisely what windowing achieves.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (The "Window" in a Window!)

Imagine you are looking at a beautiful, infinite landscape through a **fixed-size window** in a wall.

- The **infinite landscape** = the ideal filter's infinite impulse response $h_d[n]$
- The **window in the wall** = the window function $w[n]$ of length $N$
- The **view you actually see** = the truncated impulse response $h[n] = h_d[n] \cdot w[n]$

When you look through the window:
- You only see a **finite portion** of the landscape (just like we only keep $N$ samples of $h_d[n]$)
- The **edges** of the window frame **cut off** parts of the view abruptly (this causes spectral distortion called **spectral leakage**)
- A **larger window** (bigger $N$) shows more of the landscape but doesn't completely eliminate the edge effects
- Different **shapes of windows** (e.g., clear glass, frosted glass, tinted glass) reveal more or less of what's outside

Just as you can choose different window shapes (clear, frosted, tinted), in DSP we can choose different **window functions** (Rectangular, Hamming, Hanning, Blackman, Kaiser) — each giving a different trade-off between frequency resolution and side-lobe suppression.

> [!NOTE]
> **Multiplication in Time Domain = Convolution in Frequency Domain.** The seemingly simple act of multiplying by a window creates a **convolution** in the frequency domain, which spreads out the sharp ideal frequency response, causing ripples (Gibbs phenomenon) and a transition band.

## 1.4 The Frequency Domain Consequence

The frequency response of the windowed filter is:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} H_d(e^{j\theta}) \cdot W(e^{j(\omega-\theta)}) \, d\theta$$

This is a **periodic convolution** between the ideal frequency response $H_d(e^{j\omega})$ and the window's frequency response $W(e^{j\omega})$. The shape of $W(e^{j\omega})$ (main lobe width and side lobes) directly determines the quality of the resulting filter.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic of Windowing

The process of designing an FIR filter via windowing follows these structured steps:

- **Step 1 — Specify the Desired Filter:** Define the ideal frequency response $H_d(e^{j\omega})$ (e.g., ideal low-pass with cutoff $\omega_c$).
- **Step 2 — Compute the Ideal Impulse Response:** Take the inverse discrete-time Fourier transform (IDTFT) to get $h_d[n]$. This $h_d[n]$ is **infinite** in length and **non-causal** (centered around $n=0$).
- **Step 3 — Choose Filter Length N:** Decide the order of the FIR filter. Higher $N$ gives better approximation.
- **Step 4 — Apply Windowing:** Multiply $h_d[n]$ by the chosen window $w[n]$ to obtain finite-length $h[n] = h_d[n] \cdot w[n]$.
- **Step 5 — Shift for Causality:** Shift the resulting $h[n]$ by $(N-1)/2$ samples to make the filter causal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why step 5?** Since $h_d[n]$ is centered at $n=0$ (non-causal), multiplying by $w[n]$ (which exists for $0 \le n \le N-1$) gives a sequence starting at $-(N-1)/2$ and ending at $(N-1)/2$. We shift it right by $(N-1)/2$ to make it causal (start at $n=0$).

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Window Design

A good window function must have:
1. **Narrow main lobe** in frequency domain → sharper transition band (better frequency selectivity)
2. **Low side lobe levels** → less ripple in passband and stopband (less spectral leakage)
3. **Fast side lobe roll-off** → minimal energy in side lobes

Unfortunately, **no single window can minimize both main lobe width and side lobe level simultaneously** — this is a fundamental trade-off. A narrower main lobe always comes with higher side lobes, and vice versa.

## 2.3 Standard Window Functions — Complete Reference

For a window of length $N$ (where $0 \le n \le N-1$):

### A. Rectangular Window
$$w_R[n] = 1, \quad 0 \le n \le N-1$$
- Simplest window. Truncates abruptly.
- **Highest** side lobe level (~-13 dB).
- **Narrowest** main lobe.

### B. Hanning (Raised Cosine) Window
$$w_{Han}[n] = 0.5 - 0.5 \cos\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right)$$
- Smooth taper at both ends.
- Side lobe level ≈ -31 dB.

### C. Hamming Window
$$w_{Ham}[n] = 0.54 - 0.46 \cos\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right)$$
- Optimized version of Hanning.
- Side lobe level ≈ -43 dB.
- Most widely used general-purpose window.

### D. Blackman Window
$$w_B[n] = 0.42 - 0.5 \cos\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right) + 0.08 \cos\left(\frac{4\pi n}{N-1}\right)$$
- Three-term cosine series.
- Side lobe level ≈ -58 dB.
- Wider main lobe (worse frequency resolution) but excellent side lobe suppression.

### E. Bartlett (Triangular) Window
$$w_{Bt}[n] = 1 - \frac{2 \left\vert n - \frac{N-1}{2} \right\vert}{N-1}$$
- Linear taper from 1 at center to 0 at edges.
- Side lobe level ≈ -26 dB.

### F. Kaiser Window (Parametric — Most Versatile)
$$w_K[n] = \frac{I_0\left(\beta \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{2n}{N-1} - 1\right)^2}\right)}{I_0(\beta)}$$
- $I_0(\cdot)$ is the **zeroth-order modified Bessel function of the first kind**.
- Parameter $\beta$ controls the side lobe level:
  - $\beta = 0$: equivalent to Rectangular window
  - $\beta = 5$: similar to Hamming
  - $\beta = 6$: similar to Hanning
  - $\beta = 8.6$: similar to Blackman
- **Most flexible** — allows designer to trade-off main lobe width vs. side lobe level.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Window Type | Time Domain Formula $w[n]$ | Main Lobe Width | Peak Side Lobe Level (dB) | Side Lobe Roll-off |
|------------|---------------------------|-----------------|---------------------------|---------------------|
| Rectangular | $1$ | $\frac{4\pi}{N}$ | $-13$ dB | $-6$ dB/octave |
| Bartlett (Triangular) | $1 - \frac{2 \left\vert n - \frac{N-1}{2} \right\vert}{N-1}$ | $\frac{8\pi}{N}$ | $-26$ dB | $-12$ dB/octave |
| Hanning | $0.5 - 0.5\cos\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right)$ | $\frac{8\pi}{N}$ | $-31$ dB | $-18$ dB/octave |
| Hamming | $0.54 - 0.46\cos\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right)$ | $\frac{8\pi}{N}$ | $-43$ dB | $-6$ dB/octave |
| Blackman | $0.42 - 0.5\cos\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right) + 0.08\cos\left(\frac{4\pi n}{N-1}\right)$ | $\frac{12\pi}{N}$ | $-58$ dB | $-18$ dB/octave |
| Kaiser ($\beta$ tunable) | $\frac{I_0(\beta\sqrt{1-(2n/(N-1)-1)^2})}{I_0(\beta)}$ | Tunable | Tunable | Tunable |

| Parameter | Formula | Meaning |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| Transition Width ($\Delta\omega$) | $\approx$ Main Lobe Width of $W(e^{j\omega})$ | Sharpness of filter cutoff |
| Passband Ripple ($A_p$) | Function of side lobe level | Allowed variation in passband |
| Stopband Attenuation ($A_s$) | $-20\log_{10}(\text{peak side lobe ratio})$ | Rejection of unwanted frequencies |
| Minimum Order (Kaiser) | $N \ge \frac{A_s - 8}{2.285 \cdot \Delta\omega}$ (empirical) | Required filter length |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Windowing is the workhorse technique in:

- **Audio Processing:** Designing equalizers, noise-cancelling filters, and MP3 codecs.
- **Telecommunications:** Channel selection filters in mobile phones (LTE/5G baseband filtering).
- **Biomedical Signal Processing:** ECG/EEG filter design to remove baseline wander and powerline interference.
- **Radar and Sonar:** Matched filtering for target detection.
- **Image Processing:** 2D FIR filters for blurring, sharpening, edge detection.
- **Spectrum Analysis:** Reducing spectral leakage in DFT-based frequency estimation (e.g., Harmonic analysis of power signals).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Frequency Response of the Rectangular Window

The rectangular window is:
$$w_R[n] = 1, \quad 0 \le n \le N-1$$

We want to find its DTFT $W_R(e^{j\omega})$.

**Step 1 — Write the DTFT definition.**
$$W_R(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} w_R[n] \cdot e^{-j\omega n} = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Step 2 — Recognize this as a finite geometric series.**

This is a geometric series with first term $a = 1$, common ratio $r = e^{-j\omega}$, and $N$ terms.

$$W_R(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1 - e^{-j\omega N}}{1 - e^{-j\omega}}$$

**Step 3 — Factor out half-angles for clarity.**

Multiply numerator and denominator by appropriate terms:

$$W_R(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{e^{-j\omega N/2}(e^{j\omega N/2} - e^{-j\omega N/2})}{e^{-j\omega/2}(e^{j\omega/2} - e^{-j\omega/2})}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Euler's identity.**

Using $\sin(\theta) = \frac{e^{j\theta} - e^{-j\theta}}{2j}$:

$$W_R(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2} \cdot \frac{\sin(\omega N/2)}{\sin(\omega/2)}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the magnitude and phase components.**

$$\boxed{W_R(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2} \cdot \frac{\sin(\omega N/2)}{\sin(\omega/2)}}$$

- The **magnitude response** is the **Dirichlet kernel**:
$$\vert W_R(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \left\vert \frac{\sin(\omega N/2)}{\sin(\omega/2)} \right\vert$$
- The **linear phase term** is $e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2}$, confirming linear phase property.

**Step 6 — Analyze the zeros.**

Zeros of $W_R(e^{j\omega})$ occur at $\omega N/2 = k\pi$ (where $k \ne 0$), i.e., $\omega = \frac{2\pi k}{N}$.

**Step 7 — Main lobe width determination.**

The first zeros are at $\omega = \pm \frac{2\pi}{N}$. Therefore, the **main lobe width** is:
$$\Delta\omega_{\text{main}} = \frac{4\pi}{N}$$

This confirms the table entry above.

## 3.2 Derivation: Side Lobe Attenuation of Rectangular Window

The first side lobe occurs approximately at $\omega \approx \frac{3\pi}{N}$ (midway between the first and second zeros of the main lobe at $\frac{2\pi}{N}$ and $\frac{4\pi}{N}$).

**Step 1 — Evaluate at $\omega = 3\pi/N$.**

$$\left\vert W_R(e^{j3\pi/N}) \right\vert = \left\vert \frac{\sin(3\pi/2)}{\sin(3\pi/2N)} \right\vert$$

**Step 2 — Use small-angle approximation.**

For large $N$, $\sin(3\pi/2N) \approx 3\pi/2N$:

$$\left\vert W_R(e^{j3\pi/N}) \right\vert \approx \left\vert \frac{-1}{3\pi/2N} \right\vert = \frac{2N}{3\pi}$$

**Step 3 — Compare to peak magnitude (at $\omega = 0$).**

$$\vert W_R(e^{j0}) \vert = N$$

**Step 4 — Compute attenuation in dB.**

$$A_{\text{side lobe}} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{2N/3\pi}{N}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{2}{3\pi}\right)$$

$$A_{\text{side lobe}} = 20 \log_{10}(0.2122) \approx -13.46 \text{ dB}$$

This confirms the well-known **-13 dB** peak side lobe level of the rectangular window.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Windows vs. Filter Performance)

| Window Choice | Effect on Transition Width | Effect on Stopband Attenuation | Effect on Passband Ripple | Best Use Case |
|---------------|---------------------------|-------------------------------|-------------------------|---------------|
| **Rectangular** | Narrowest (best) | Worst (-21 dB min) | Highest | Sharp cutoff, known signal levels |
| **Hamming** | Moderate | Good (-53 dB) | Low | General-purpose FIR design |
| **Hanning** | Moderate | Good (-44 dB) | Low | Spectrum analysis |
| **Blackman** | Widest (worst) | Excellent (-74 dB) | Very low | High attenuation requirements |
| **Kaiser** | Tunable | Tunable | Tunable | Optimal design with constraints |

> [!NOTE]
> **Stopband attenuation in dB** = peak side lobe level of window (in dB) - approximately **8 dB**. For example, Hamming has -43 dB side lobe → ~-53 dB stopband attenuation.

## 3.4 Numerical Demonstration: Effect of N

To solidify understanding, let us derive a numerical rule:

**Hamming Window Empirical Formula (KTU High-Yield):**

$$N \approx \frac{3.3}{\Delta f / f_s}$$

where $\Delta f$ is the transition width in Hz, and $f_s$ is the sampling frequency.

For a Blackman window, $N \approx \frac{5.5}{\Delta f / f_s}$.

This shows: **better side lobe suppression requires larger N** (more computation).

## 3.5 Derivation: Why Multiplication in Time = Convolution in Frequency

This is the most critical conceptual step. Given $h[n] = h_d[n] \cdot w[n]$:

**Step 1 — Take DTFT of both sides.**

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \text{DTFT}\{h_d[n] \cdot w[n]\}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the multiplication-convolution theorem.**

If $x_1[n] \cdot x_2[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X_1(\theta) X_2(\omega - \theta) d\theta$

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} H_d(e^{j\theta}) W(e^{j(\omega-\theta)}) d\theta$$

**Step 3 — Interpret physically.**

The ideal sharp response $H_d(e^{j\omega})$ is **smeared** by convolution with the window's frequency response $W(e^{j\omega})$. The main lobe of $W$ causes the **transition band**, and the side lobes cause the **ripples** in passband and stopband.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1: Define Windowing in FIR Filter Design** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Windowing is the technique of multiplying an ideal infinite-duration impulse response $h_d[n]$ with a finite-duration window function $w[n]$ to obtain a finite-length causal FIR filter impulse response:

$$h[n] = h_d[n] \cdot w[n], \quad 0 \le n \le N-1$$

**Purpose:** It is used to design practical, realizable FIR filters from ideal (non-causal, infinitely sharp) filter specifications by truncating the impulse response to length $N$.

**Example:** A Rectangular window of length $N$ sets all samples outside the range $0 \le n \le N-1$ to zero.

---
