<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**State the properties of a Butterworth filter. Why is it called "maximally flat"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Butterworth filter is an **all-pole** analog filter whose magnitude-squared response is given by:

$$
\vert H(j\Omega)\vert^2 \;=\; \frac{1}{1+\left(\dfrac{\Omega}{\Omega_c}\right)^{2N}}
$$

**Properties:**
1. **Maximally flat magnitude response** at $\Omega = 0$ — the first $2N-1$ derivatives of $\vert H(j\Omega)\vert^2$ with respect to $\Omega$ vanish at the origin, ensuring the flattest possible passband.
2. **Monotonic response** in both passband and stopband — there is **no ripple** in either region.
3. **–3 dB cutoff** at $\Omega = \Omega_c$ for all orders $N$.
4. **All-pole design** — the transfer function has no finite zeros (only zeros at infinity).
5. **Pole locations** are uniformly distributed on a circle of radius $\Omega_c$ in the $s$-plane.

It is called "maximally flat" because no other polynomial of degree $2N$ in $\Omega^2$ can have a flatter magnitude response near $\Omega=0$ — Butterworth's choice of $B_N(\Omega^2) = (\Omega/\Omega_c)^{2N}$ uniquely maximizes the number of zero derivatives at the origin.

*(Distribution: Definition 1 m, 5 properties 1 m, "maximally flat" reasoning 1 m)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Write the general expression for the magnitude-squared response of an Nth-order Butterworth lowpass filter. Define the term "order" of a filter.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The magnitude-squared response of an $N$-th order Butterworth LPF is:

$$
\vert H(j\Omega)\vert^2 \;=\; \frac{1}{1+\left(\dfrac{\Omega}{\Omega_c}\right)^{2N}}
$$

where $\Omega_c$ is the 3-dB cutoff frequency (rad/s) and $N$ is the filter order.

**Definition of "Order":** The order $N$ of a filter is the **number of poles** in its transfer function $H(s)$ and also equals the **degree of the denominator polynomial**. It governs:
- The **roll-off rate** of the magnitude response (–20N dB/decade beyond $\Omega_c$).
- The **sharpness** of the transition from passband to stopband.
- The **complexity** of the analog circuit (more components for higher $N$).

For example, $N=1$ gives –20 dB/decade slope; $N=5$ gives –100 dB/decade slope.

*(Distribution: Magnitude formula 1 m, order definition 1 m, example/slope explanation 1 m)*

---

## PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** In Butterworth design problems, students **most commonly lose marks** at these three points:
> 1. **Forgetting to round up $N$** — the formula gives a real number; always take $\lceil N \rceil$.
> 2. **Using the wrong cutoff formula** — passband form gives a *smaller* $\Omega_c$ than stopband form. Using the wrong one fails the unused specification.
> 3. **Including Right-Half-Plane (RHP) poles** — only LHP poles are kept. Including RHP poles makes the filter unstable, and the examiner deducts 2-3 marks immediately.
> 4. **Sign error in pole angle formula** — the standard form is $s_k = \Omega_c e^{j(2k+N-1)\pi/(2N)}$, NOT $e^{j(2k-1)\pi/(2N)}$ (the latter is the unshifted version). Verify which form your textbook uses and **stay consistent**.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the order $N$ of a Butterworth lowpass filter in terms of the passband and stopband specifications. State clearly the meaning of each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a Butterworth lowpass filter to satisfy:
- $\Omega_p = 0.2\pi$ rad/sample (digital), pre-warped analog $\Omega_p = 0.2\pi \times (2/T) = 2\pi/T$ for $T=1$, so use $\Omega_p = 0.628$ rad/s (assume $T=1$ s)
- $\alpha_p = 1$ dB, $\Omega_s = 0.3\pi$ rad/sample, $\alpha_s = 15$ dB

Find the order $N$, the cutoff $\Omega_c$, and the locations of the LHP poles. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)**

Starting from the Butterworth magnitude-squared function evaluated at $\Omega = \Omega_p$:

$$
1+\left(\frac{\Omega_p}{\Omega_c}\right)^{2N} = 10^{\alpha_p/10}
$$

Solving for $\Omega_c^{2N}$:

$$
\Omega_c^{2N} \;=\; \frac{\Omega_p^{\,2N}}{10^{\alpha_p/10}-1}
$$

Similarly, at the stopband edge $\Omega = \Omega_s$:

$$
1+\left(\frac{\Omega_s}{\Omega_c}\right)^{2N} = 10^{\alpha_s/10}
$$

Solving for $\Omega_c^{2N}$:

$$
\Omega_c^{2N} \;=\; \frac{\Omega_s^{\,2N}}{10^{\alpha_s/10}-1}
$$

Equating the two expressions for $\Omega_c^{2N}$:

$$
\frac{\Omega_p^{\,2N}}{10^{\alpha_p/10}-1} \;=\; \frac{\Omega_s^{\,2N}}{10^{\alpha_s/10}-1}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\left(\frac{\Omega_s}{\Omega_p}\right)^{2N} \;=\; \frac{10^{\alpha_s/10}-1}{10^{\alpha_p/10}-1}
$$

Taking $\log_{10}$ of both sides:

$$
2N\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\Omega_s}{\Omega_p}\right) \;=\; \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10^{\alpha_s/10}-1}{10^{\alpha_p/10}-1}\right)
$$

Solving for $N$:

$$
\boxed{\;N \;\geq\; \frac{\log_{10}\!\left[\,10^{\alpha_s/10}-1\,\right] \;-\; \log_{10}\!\left[\,10^{\alpha_p/10}-1\,\right]}{2\,\log_{10}\!\left(\Omega_s/\Omega_p\right)}\;}
$$

**Meaning of terms:**
- $\Omega_p$ = passband edge frequency (rad/s)
- $\Omega_s$ = stopband edge frequency (rad/s), with $\Omega_s > \Omega_p$
- $\alpha_p$ = maximum passband attenuation (dB)
- $\alpha_s$ = minimum stopband attenuation (dB)
- $N$ = filter order, must be rounded up to the next integer

*(Distribution: Magnitude setup 2 m, equating expressions 2 m, algebraic manipulation 2 m, definition of terms 1 m)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Design (7 marks)**

Using the specifications: $\Omega_p = 0.2\pi \approx 0.6283$ rad/s, $\Omega_s = 0.3\pi \approx 0.9425$ rad/s, $\alpha_p = 1$ dB, $\alpha_s = 15$ dB.

**Step 1 — Compute $N$**

$$
N \;\geq\; \frac{\log_{10}\!\left[\,10^{1.5}-1\,\right] - \log_{10}\!\left[\,10^{0.1}-1\,\right]}{2\,\log_{10}\!\left(0.9425/0.6283\right)}
$$

Compute each piece:

$$
10^{1.5}-1 = 30.623 \;\Rightarrow\; \log_{10} = 1.4859
$$
$$
10^{0.1}-1 = 0.2589 \;\Rightarrow\; \log_{10} = -0.5870
$$
$$
0.9425/0.6283 = 1.500 \;\Rightarrow\; \log_{10} = 0.1761
$$

Substitute:

$$
N \;\geq\; \frac{1.4859 - (-0.5870)}{2 \times 0.1761} \;=\; \frac{2.0729}{0.3522} \;\approx\; 5.886
$$

Round up: $N = 6$.

**Step 2 — Compute $\Omega_c$** (passband form)

$$
\Omega_c \;=\; \frac{0.6283}{(0.2589)^{1/12}} \;=\; \frac{0.6283}{0.8806} \;\approx\; 0.7135 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Locate the LHP Poles for $N=6$**

Pole angles:

$$
\theta_k \;=\; \frac{(2k+N-1)\pi}{2N} \;=\; \frac{(2k+5)\pi}{12}, \quad k=1,\ldots,6
$$

Compute each angle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_1 &= 7\pi/12 = 105.0° \;\;\text{(LHP — keep)}\\
\theta_2 &= 9\pi/12 = 135.0° \;\;\text{(LHP — keep)}\\
\theta_3 &= 11\pi/12 = 165.0° \;\;\text{(LHP — keep)}\\
\theta_4 &= 13\pi/12 = 195.0° \;\;\text{(RHP — discard)}\\
\theta_5 &= 15\pi/12 = 225.0° \;\;\text{(RHP — discard)}\\
\theta_6 &= 17\pi/12 = 255.0° \;\;\text{(RHP — discard)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Pole locations $s_k = 0.7135\,e^{j\theta_k}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_1 &= 0.7135\,e^{j105°} = -0.1847 + j0.6891 \\
s_2 &= 0.7135\,e^{j135°} = -0.5046 + j0.5046 \\
s_3 &= 0.7135\,e^{j165°} = -0.6891 + j0.1847
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Transfer Function**

$$
H(s) \;=\; \frac{0.7135^6}{(s-s_1)(s-s_2)(s-s_3)} \;=\; \frac{0.1321}{(s-s_1)(s-s_2)(s-s_3)}
$$

*(Distribution: Order calculation 2 m, $\Omega_c$ calculation 1 m, pole angle derivation 2 m, LHP selection and final $H(s)$ 2 m)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024] — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain with suitable diagrams how the poles of a Butterworth filter are distributed on the $s$-plane. Show that for an $N$-th order filter, the poles are uniformly spaced on a circle of radius $\Omega_c$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Butterworth lowpass filter is required with specifications: $\Omega_p = 1000$ rad/s, $\alpha_p = 3$ dB, $\Omega_s = 4000$ rad/s, $\alpha_s = 30$ dB. Determine the order $N$ and $\Omega_c$. Write the transfer function $H(s)$ in pole-zero form. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Pole Distribution (7 marks)**

Starting from the denominator polynomial of $\vert H(j\Omega)\vert^2$:

$$
1+\left(\frac{s}{j\Omega_c}\right)^{2N} = 0
$$

Let $w = s/(j\Omega_c)$. The equation becomes $w^{2N} = -1$. The $2N$ complex roots of $-1$ are $w = e^{j\pi(2k-1)/(2N)}$ for $k = 1, 2, \ldots, 2N$.

Converting back to $s$ (using $j = e^{j\pi/2}$):

$$
s_k \;=\; j\Omega_c\,w_k \;=\; \Omega_c\,e^{j\pi/2}\,\cdot\,e^{j\pi(2k-1)/(2N)} \;=\; \Omega_c\,e^{j\pi(2k+N-1)/(2N)}
$$

**Geometric properties of the pole set:**

1. **Magnitude:** $\vert s_k\vert = \Omega_c\,e^{0} = \Omega_c$ for all $k$ → all poles lie on a circle of radius $\Omega_c$.

2. **Angular spacing:** successive poles differ by angle $\pi/N$ → uniform spacing.

3. **Symmetry:** the set is symmetric about both the real and imaginary axes (because the polynomial has real coefficients).

4. **Pole diagram** (for $N=4$):

```
                       jΩ
                        |
                    ✗   |   ✗
                  (LHP) | (LHP)
                        |
        ✗———————————————|———————————————✗
       (LHP)            |            (LHP)
                        |
                    ✗   |   ✗
                  (RHP) | (RHP)
                        |
                       σ
```

For stability, only the LHP poles are retained. For $N=4$, this gives 4 poles in the left half.

*(Distribution: Equation setup 2 m, derivation of pole formula 2 m, uniform spacing proof 1 m, diagram 1 m, LHP selection 1 m)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Design (7 marks)**

Given: $\Omega_p = 1000$ rad/s, $\alpha_p = 3$ dB, $\Omega_s = 4000$ rad/s, $\alpha_s = 30$ dB.

**Step 1 — Order $N$**

$$
N \;\geq\; \frac{\log_{10}(10^3-1) - \log_{10}(10^{0.3}-1)}{2\,\log_{10}(4)}
$$

$$
\log_{10}(999) = 2.9996
$$
$$
10^{0.3} - 1 = 0.9953 \;\Rightarrow\; \log_{10} = -0.0020
$$
$$
\log_{10}(4) = 0.6021
$$

$$
N \;\geq\; \frac{2.9996 - (-0.0020)}{2 \times 0.6021} \;=\; \frac{3.0016}{1.2041} \;\approx\; 2.493
$$

Round up: $N = 3$.

**Step 2 — $\Omega_c$** (using passband form, $\alpha_p = 3$ dB)

$$
\Omega_c \;=\; \frac{1000}{(0.9953)^{1/6}} \;=\; \frac{1000}{0.9992} \;\approx\; 1000.8 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Poles for $N=3$**

Pole angles:

$$
\theta_k \;=\; \frac{(2k+2)\pi}{6} \;=\; \frac{(k+1)\pi}{3}, \quad k=1,2,3
$$

$$
\theta_1 = 2\pi/3 = 120°, \quad \theta_2 = \pi = 180°, \quad \theta_3 = 4\pi/3 = 240°
$$

Pole locations ($s_k = 1000.8\,e^{j\theta_k}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_1 &= 1000.8\,e^{j120°} = -500.4 + j866.8 \\
s_2 &= 1000.8\,e^{j180°} = -1000.8 + j0 \\
s_3 &= 1000.8\,e^{j240°} = -500.4 - j866.8
\end{aligned}
$$

All three have negative real parts → all are LHP → all are retained.

**Step 4 — Transfer Function**

$$
H(s) \;=\; \frac{1000.8^3}{(s-s_1)(s-s_2)(s-s_3)} \;=\; \frac{1.0024 \times 10^9}{(s-s_1)(s-s_2)(s-s_3)}
$$

Expanded form (using the real-coefficient form):

$$
H(s) \;=\; \frac{1.0024 \times 10^9}{(s+1000.8)(s^2 + 1000.8\,s + 1.0024 \times 10^6)}
$$

*(Distribution: Order calculation 2 m, $\Omega_c$ 1 m, pole angles 1 m, pole values 1 m, final $H(s)$ in two equivalent forms 2 m)*

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] Butterworth filter magnitude: $\vert H(j\Omega)\vert^2 = 1/[1+(\Omega/\Omega_c)^{2N}]$.
- [x] "Maximally flat" means the first $2N-1$ derivatives vanish at $\Omega=0$.
- [x] Butterworth is an **all-pole** filter — no finite zeros.
- [x] Roll-off rate beyond $\Omega_c$ is **–20N dB/decade**.
- [x] Pole radius = $\Omega_c$; poles lie on a circle in the $s$-plane.
- [x] Angular spacing between adjacent poles = $\pi/N$.
- [x] For stability, retain only the **$N$ LHP poles**.
- [x] Order formula uses **$\log_{10}$ of attenuation ratios**; always **round up** to integer.
- [x] Two equivalent $\Omega_c$ formulas — passband form is *preferred* in KTU problems.
- [x] Normalized Butterworth polynomials for $N=2,3,4$ are memorised to save time in the exam.
- [x] When asked to *design*, always show all **5 steps** in order.
- [x] Final answer is the **transfer function $H(s)$** in pole-zero or polynomial form.
- [x] Common pitfall: forgetting to round up $N$ or selecting RHP poles.
- [x] Engineering intuition: Butterworth is the **default choice** for ripple-free passband applications.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Strategy:** For a 14-mark Part B question, structure your answer as: (1) State formula for $N$, (2) Compute $N$ numerically, (3) Compute $\Omega_c$, (4) Compute pole angles, (5) Plot/select LHP poles, (6) Form $H(s)$. This exact sequence matches the official KTU model answer key and earns full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analog Frequency Transformations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT604 - DIGITAL SIGNALS AND PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Analog Frequency Transformations**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 📘 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analog Frequency Transformation** is a mathematical technique used in filter design where the complex frequency variable $S$ of a **normalized lowpass prototype filter** (with cutoff $\Omega_p = 1$ rad/sec) is substituted by a rational function of a new variable $s$, thereby converting the prototype into another filter type — namely **Lowpass (LP), Highpass (HP), Bandpass (BP), or Bandstop (BS)** — while preserving the **magnitude response shape** and the **filter order**.

In the context of KTU's 2024 DSP syllabus (Module 4 — Design of Digital Filters), these transformations form the critical **analog preprocessing stage** that precedes the **Bilinear Transformation**, which then maps the resulting analog filter into the digital (z) domain.

Mathematically, for a prototype transfer function $H_{LP}(S)$:

$$H_{\text{new}}(s) = H_{LP}\bigl(S = f(s)\bigr)$$

where $f(s)$ is the **transformation function** selected based on the desired filter type.

---

## 🌉 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it like a "recipe transformation" in cooking.** 🍳
>
> Imagine you have a perfectly tested recipe for a plain vanilla cake (the **normalized lowpass prototype** with cutoff = 1). Now, suppose you want to make:
> - **Chocolate cake** → Lowpass-to-Lowpass with different cutoff
> - **Coffee cake** → Lowpass-to-Highpass (flipping flavors)
> - **Layered cake with chocolate-coffee-vanilla-chocolate** → Lowpass-to-Bandpass
> - **Donut (hole in the middle)** → Lowpass-to-Bandstop
>
> Instead of starting from scratch, you **substitute ingredients systematically** while keeping the structural ratios intact. The mathematical structure of the recipe (filter order, ripple, roll-off) is preserved — only the frequency "ingredients" change.

This is exactly what analog frequency transformations do: they **re-map the frequency axis** of a known, well-tabulated prototype filter to obtain a new filter with desired cutoff(s), **without re-deriving** the entire filter from specifications.

---

## 🎯 Why This Matters in KTU 2024 DSP

| Aspect | Significance |
|---|---|
| **Design Efficiency** | Avoids solving filter approximations from scratch |
| **Standardization** | Chebyshev, Butterworth, Bessel prototypes are tabulated |
| **Foundation for Digital IIR Design** | Mandatory step before Bilinear/Impulse Invariance methods |
| **Preservation of Properties** | Order $N$ and shape of $|H(j\Omega)|$ are retained |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Benchmark Frequencies (KTU expects you to know these):**
> - Prototype passband edge: $\Omega_p = 1$ rad/sec
> - Stopband edge: $\Omega_s > 1$ rad/sec (depends on filter type)
> - For target filter: $\omega_p, \omega_s$ (or $\Omega_p, \Omega_s$ in rad/sec)

---

## 🧭 The Four Canonical Transformations — Quick Preview

| # | Source | Target | Substitution $S \rightarrow f(s)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | LP | LP | $S = \dfrac{s}{\Omega_c}$ |
| 2 | LP | HP | $S = \dfrac{\Omega_c}{s}$ |
| 3 | LP | BP | $S = \dfrac{s^2 + \omega_l\,\omega_u}{s\,(\omega_u - \omega_l)}$ |
| 4 | LP | BS | $S = \dfrac{s\,(\omega_u - \omega_l)}{s^2 + \omega_l\,\omega_u}$ |

where:
- $\Omega_c$ = desired cutoff frequency (rad/sec)
- $\omega_l, \omega_u$ = lower and upper band-edge frequencies (rad/sec)

> These four transformations are the **backbone of every IIR filter design problem** in KTU's ESE and internals.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🧠 The Underlying Philosophy

The **principal idea** behind analog frequency transformations is the **frequency-axis warping principle**:

$$S = f(s) \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad j\Omega = f(j\omega)$$

When we evaluate the prototype at $S = j\Omega$, the new filter evaluated at $s = j\omega$ must produce the **same magnitude** at the corresponding frequency. That is:

$$\bigl|H_{\text{new}}(j\omega)\bigr| = \bigl|H_{LP}\bigl(f(j\omega)\bigr)\bigr| = \bigl|H_{LP}(j\Omega)\bigr|$$

This implies that the transformation $f(j\omega)$ must be a **pure imaginary function of $\omega$** (so that the $j\Omega$-axis maps to the $j\omega$-axis), ensuring that a stable, causal prototype yields a stable, causal transformed filter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability Preservation Rule (Critical for KTU):**
> If $f(s)$ maps the LHP (left-half plane) of the $s$-plane into the LHP of the new $s$-plane, the transformed filter is **stable**. All four canonical transformations satisfy this.

---

## 📐 Transformation 1: Lowpass → Lowpass (LP → LP)

### Concept
Scales the entire frequency axis by a factor $\Omega_c$, the desired cutoff.

### Substitution Rule
$$S = \frac{s}{\Omega_c}$$

### Frequency Mapping
$$j\Omega = \frac{j\omega}{\Omega_c} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \Omega = \frac{\omega}{\Omega_c}$$

### Properties
- **Order preserved:** $N \rightarrow N$
- **DC gain preserved:** $H(0) \rightarrow H(0)$
- **Passband edge:** $\omega = \Omega_c \rightarrow \Omega = 1$ ✓
- **Design formula:**

$$H_{LP}(s) = H_{LP,\text{proto}}\left(\frac{s}{\Omega_c}\right)$$

### Example
Prototype: $H_{LP}(S) = \dfrac{1}{S^2 + \sqrt{2}\,S + 1}$, target $\Omega_c = 1000$ rad/sec

$$H_{LP}(s) = \frac{1}{\left(\frac{s}{1000}\right)^2 + \sqrt{2}\left(\frac{s}{1000}\right) + 1} = \frac{10^6}{s^2 + 1000\sqrt{2}\,s + 10^6}$$

---

## 📐 Transformation 2: Lowpass → Highpass (LP → HP)

### Concept
Inverts the frequency axis, mapping $\Omega = 0$ (DC) to $\omega = \infty$ and $\Omega = \infty$ to $\omega = 0$.

### Substitution Rule
$$S = \frac{\Omega_c}{s}$$

### Frequency Mapping
$$j\Omega = \frac{\Omega_c}{j\omega} = -j\,\frac{\Omega_c}{\omega} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \Omega = -\frac{\Omega_c}{\omega}$$

The negative sign indicates the **direction of traversal** along the imaginary axis; magnitudes depend only on $\vert\Omega\vert$, so:

$$\vert H_{HP}(j\omega)\vert = \bigl|H_{LP}\bigl(j\Omega_c/\omega\bigr)\bigr|$$

### Properties
- **Order preserved:** $N \rightarrow N$
- **DC gain** $\rightarrow \infty$ (filter blocks DC)
- **Infinite-frequency gain** $\rightarrow H(0)$ (passes high frequencies)
- **Passband edge:** $\omega = \Omega_c \rightarrow \Omega = 1$ ✓

### Design Formula
$$H_{HP}(s) = H_{LP,\text{proto}}\left(\frac{\Omega_c}{s}\right)$$

### Example
Prototype: $H_{LP}(S) = \dfrac{1}{S+1}$, target $\Omega_c = 2000$ rad/sec

$$H_{HP}(s) = \frac{1}{\frac{2000}{s}+1} = \frac{s}{s+2000}$$

---

## 📐 Transformation 3: Lowpass → Bandpass (LP → BP)

### Concept
Stretches the single prototype passband (0 to 1) into a band between $\omega_l$ and $\omega_u$.

### Substitution Rule
$$S = \frac{s^2 + \omega_l\,\omega_u}{s\,(\omega_u - \omega_l)} = \frac{s^2 + \omega_0^2}{B\,s}$$

where:
- $\omega_0 = \sqrt{\omega_l\,\omega_u}$ is the **center (geometric mean) frequency**
- $B = \omega_u - \omega_l$ is the **bandwidth**

### Frequency Mapping
$$j\Omega = \frac{(j\omega)^2 + \omega_0^2}{B\,(j\omega)} = j\,\frac{\omega_0^2 - \omega^2}{B\,\omega}$$

$$\Omega = \frac{\omega^2 - \omega_0^2}{B\,\omega}$$

### Key Frequency Mappings
| Prototype frequency $\Omega$ | Target frequency $\omega$ |
|---|---|
| $0$ (DC) | $\omega_0 = \sqrt{\omega_l\,\omega_u}$ (center) |
| $1$ (passband edge) | $\omega_l$ **or** $\omega_u$ (band edges) |
| $-1$ | $\omega_u$ **or** $\omega_l$ (other band edge) |
| $\infty$ | $0$ **or** $\infty$ (DC and infinity) |

### Order Inflation (CRITICAL for KTU)
> [!WARNING]
> An $N$-th order LP prototype produces a **$2N$-th order BP filter**.
> Example: 3rd order Butterworth LP → 6th order BP filter.

---

## 📐 Transformation 4: Lowpass → Bandstop (LP → BS / Band-Reject)

### Concept
Maps the single prototype passband into a "notch" between $\omega_l$ and $\omega_u$.

### Substitution Rule
$$S = \frac{s\,(\omega_u - \omega_l)}{s^2 + \omega_l\,\omega_u} = \frac{B\,s}{s^2 + \omega_0^2}$$

### Frequency Mapping
$$j\Omega = \frac{B\,(j\omega)}{(j\omega)^2 + \omega_0^2} = j\,\frac{B\,\omega}{\omega_0^2 - \omega^2}$$

$$\Omega = \frac{B\,\omega}{\omega_0^2 - \omega^2}$$

### Key Frequency Mappings
| Prototype frequency $\Omega$ | Target frequency $\omega$ |
|---|---|
| $0$ (DC) | $0$ **or** $\infty$ |
| $1$ (passband edge) | $\omega_l$ **or** $\omega_u$ |
| $\infty$ | $\omega_0$ (NOTCH center) |

### Order Inflation
> [!NOTE]
> Again, an $N$-th order LP prototype → **$2N$-th order BS filter**.

---

## 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Transformation | Substitution $S = f(s)$ | Order Change | Maps $\Omega = 0$ to | Maps $\Omega = 1$ to | Stability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **LP → LP** | $S = \dfrac{s}{\Omega_c}$ | $N \rightarrow N$ | $\omega = 0$ | $\omega = \Omega_c$ | ✓ |
| **LP → HP** | $S = \dfrac{\Omega_c}{s}$ | $N \rightarrow N$ | $\omega = \infty$ | $\omega = \Omega_c$ | ✓ |
| **LP → BP** | $S = \dfrac{s^2 + \omega_l\omega_u}{s(\omega_u - \omega_l)}$ | $N \rightarrow 2N$ | $\omega = \omega_0$ | $\omega = \omega_l$ or $\omega_u$ | ✓ |
| **LP → BS** | $S = \dfrac{s(\omega_u - \omega_l)}{s^2 + \omega_l\omega_u}$ | $N \rightarrow 2N$ | $\omega = 0$ or $\infty$ | $\omega = \omega_l$ or $\omega_u$ | ✓ |

### Additional Useful Identities (for KTU derivations)
- Center frequency: $\omega_0 = \sqrt{\omega_l\,\omega_u}$
- Bandwidth: $B = \omega_u - \omega_l$
- Quality factor: $Q = \dfrac{\omega_0}{B}$
- Quality factors are per-section in BP/BS (important for cascade design)

---

## 🌍 Real-World Utility

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Audio Engineering** | Crossover networks, equalizers (HP/LP/BP/BS filters) |
| **Telecommunications** | Channel selection (BP), DC removal (HP), hum rejection (BS at 50/60 Hz) |
| **Biomedical (ECG/EEG)** | Notch filters at powerline frequency; BP filters for QRS detection |
| **Radar & Sonar** | BP filtering for matched receiver |
| **Control Systems** | Lead-lag compensators (proportional to LP/HP variants) |

# 🔷 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 🧪 Derivation A: LP → HP Transformation (Conceptual Derivation)

### 🎯 Goal
Derive the substitution rule $S = \dfrac{\Omega_c}{s}$ by enforcing the **frequency-mapping constraints**.

### 📝 Given
- Prototype LP filter: $H_{LP}(S)$ with passband edge $\Omega_p = 1$
- Target HP filter: $H_{HP}(s)$ with passband edge $\omega_p = \Omega_c$ (rad/sec)

### 🔢 Step 1 — Write the desired frequency mapping

We require that the prototype's passband edge maps to the target's passband edge:

$$\Omega = 1 \quad \text{at} \quad \omega = \Omega_c$$

### 🔢 Step 2 — Assume a simple inverse-type mapping

We hypothesize that the mapping has the form:

$$S = \frac{K}{s}$$

where $K$ is an unknown constant to be determined.

### 🔢 Step 3 — Evaluate on the imaginary axis

Set $S = j\Omega$ and $s = j\omega$:

$$j\Omega = \frac{K}{j\omega} = -\frac{jK}{\omega}$$

$$\Omega = -\frac{K}{\omega}$$

The sign of $\Omega$ is the *direction* of axis traversal; what matters is the magnitude $\vert\Omega\vert = \dfrac{\vert K \vert}{\omega}$.

### 🔢 Step 4 — Apply the passband-edge condition

$$\vert \Omega \vert = 1 \quad \text{at} \quad \omega = \Omega_c$$

$$1 = \frac{\vert K \vert}{\Omega_c} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \vert K \vert = \Omega_c$$

Hence $K = \Omega_c$ (we take the positive value, as is conventional).

### ✅ Step 5 — Final substitution rule

$$\boxed{S = \frac{\Omega_c}{s}}$$

### 🔢 Step 6 — Verify the DC and high-frequency limits

| Limit | Result | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $\omega \to 0$ | $\vert S \vert \to \infty$ | DC maps to infinite prototype frequency → filter blocks DC |
| $\omega \to \infty$ | $\vert S \vert \to 0$ | High freq maps to DC → filter passes high frequencies ✓ |
| $\omega = \Omega_c$ | $\vert S \vert = 1$ | Cutoff maps to prototype passband edge ✓ |

> All three conditions are satisfied, confirming the substitution rule is correct.

---

## 🧪 Derivation B: LP → BP Transformation (Geometric Derivation)

### 🎯 Goal
Derive $S = \dfrac{s^2 + \omega_0^2}{B\,s}$ from the bandedge-mapping constraints.

### 📝 Given
- Prototype passband: $0 \le \vert\Omega\vert \le 1$
- Target passband: $\omega_l \le \omega \le \omega_u$

### 🔢 Step 1 — Hypothesize the form

A second-order transformation is needed (since one prototype point must map to **two** target points — the lower and upper band edges):

$$S = \frac{s^2 + A}{B\,s}$$

where $A, B > 0$ are constants to be determined.

### 🔢 Step 2 — Evaluate on the imaginary axis

Set $s = j\omega$:

$$j\Omega = \frac{(j\omega)^2 + A}{B\,(j\omega)} = \frac{A - \omega^2}{jB\omega} = j\,\frac{\omega^2 - A}{B\omega}$$

$$\Omega = \frac{\omega^2 - A}{B\omega}$$

### 🔢 Step 3 — Apply the center-frequency condition

The prototype's DC point ($\Omega = 0$) must map to the bandpass **center** frequency $\omega_0$:

$$0 = \frac{\omega_0^2 - A}{B\,\omega_0} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{A = \omega_0^2 = \omega_l\,\omega_u}$$

### 🔢 Step 4 — Apply the bandedge condition

The prototype's passband edge ($\vert\Omega\vert = 1$) must map to **either** bandedge. Take $\omega = \omega_l$:

$$1 = \frac{\omega_l^2 - \omega_0^2}{B\,\omega_l} = \frac{\omega_l^2 - \omega_l\omega_u}{B\,\omega_l} = \frac{\omega_l(\omega_l - \omega_u)}{B\,\omega_l} = \frac{\omega_l - \omega_u}{B}$$

$$B = \omega_u - \omega_l \quad \text{(positive, since } \omega_u > \omega_l\text{)}$$

$$\boxed{B = \omega_u - \omega_l}$$

### ✅ Step 5 — Final substitution rule

$$\boxed{S = \frac{s^2 + \omega_l\,\omega_u}{s\,(\omega_u - \omega_l)}}$$

### 🔢 Step 6 — Verify the four critical mappings

| $\Omega$ | $s$ | Computed $\omega$ | Expected |
|---|---|---|---|
| $0$ | DC of $H_{LP}$ | $\omega_0 = \sqrt{\omega_l\omega_u}$ | center ✓ |
| $+1$ | upper PB edge | $\omega_u$ | upper edge ✓ |
| $-1$ | lower PB edge | $\omega_l$ | lower edge ✓ |
| $\infty$ | infinite freq | $0$ or $\infty$ | extremes ✓ |

> All four conditions are satisfied.

---

## 🧪 Worked Analytical Example — KTU Style

### 📋 Problem
A second-order normalized Butterworth lowpass prototype has transfer function:

$$H_{LP}(S) = \frac{1}{S^2 + \sqrt{2}\,S + 1}$$

Apply the **LP → BP** transformation to obtain a bandpass filter with lower and upper bandedges at $\omega_l = 1000$ rad/sec and $\omega_u = 4000$ rad/sec.

### 🔢 Step 1 — Compute $\omega_0$ and $B$

$$\omega_0 = \sqrt{\omega_l\,\omega_u} = \sqrt{1000 \times 4000} = \sqrt{4 \times 10^6} = 2000 \text{ rad/sec}$$

$$B = \omega_u - \omega_l = 4000 - 1000 = 3000 \text{ rad/sec}$$

### 🔢 Step 2 — Write the substitution

$$S = \frac{s^2 + 2000^2}{3000\,s} = \frac{s^2 + 4 \times 10^6}{3000\,s}$$

### 🔢 Step 3 — Substitute into the prototype

$$H_{BP}(s) = \frac{1}{\left(\dfrac{s^2 + 4 \times 10^6}{3000\,s}\right)^2 + \sqrt{2}\left(\dfrac{s^2 + 4 \times 10^6}{3000\,s}\right) + 1}$$

### 🔢 Step 4 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $(3000\,s)^2 = 9 \times 10^6\,s^2$

$$H_{BP}(s) = \frac{9 \times 10^6\,s^2}{(s^2 + 4 \times 10^6)^2 + 3000\sqrt{2}\,s\,(s^2 + 4 \times 10^6) + 9 \times 10^6\,s^2}$$

### 🔢 Step 5 — Expand the denominator

$$(s^2 + 4 \times 10^6)^2 = s^4 + 8 \times 10^6\,s^2 + 16 \times 10^{12}$$

$$3000\sqrt{2}\,s\,(s^2 + 4 \times 10^6) = 3000\sqrt{2}\,s^3 + 12000\sqrt{2} \times 10^6\,s$$

$$9 \times 10^6\,s^2 = 9 \times 10^6\,s^2$$

### 🔢 Step 6 — Combine like terms in the denominator

$$D(s) = s^4 + 3000\sqrt{2}\,s^3 + (8 \times 10^6 + 9 \times 10^6)\,s^2 + 12000\sqrt{2} \times 10^6\,s + 16 \times 10^{12}$$

$$D(s) = s^4 + 3000\sqrt{2}\,s^3 + 17 \times 10^6\,s^2 + 12\sqrt{2} \times 10^9\,s + 16 \times 10^{12}$$

### ✅ Final Result

$$\boxed{H_{BP}(s) = \frac{9 \times 10^6\,s^2}{s^4 + 3000\sqrt{2}\,s^3 + 17 \times 10^6\,s^2 + 12\sqrt{2} \times 10^9\,s + 16 \times 10^{12}}}$$

### 🔢 Step 7 — Verify the order

- Prototype order: $N = 2$
- Transformed order: $4 = 2N$ ✓ (as predicted by the BP transformation rule)

---

## 🧪 Worked Analytical Example — KTU Style (HP)

### 📋 Problem
Transform the same prototype into a **highpass filter** with cutoff $\Omega_c = 500$ rad/sec.

### 🔢 Step 1 — Apply LP → HP substitution

$$S = \frac{\Omega_c}{s} = \frac{500}{s}$$

### 🔢 Step 2 — Substitute

$$H_{HP}(s) = \frac{1}{\left(\dfrac{500}{s}\right)^2 + \sqrt{2}\left(\dfrac{500}{s}\right) + 1}$$

### 🔢 Step 3 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $s^2$

$$H_{HP}(s) = \frac{s^2}{500^2 + 500\sqrt{2}\,s + s^2} = \frac{s^2}{s^2 + 500\sqrt{2}\,s + 250000}$$

### ✅ Final Result

$$\boxed{H_{HP}(s) = \frac{s^2}{s^2 + 500\sqrt{2}\,s + 250000}}$$

### 🔢 Step 4 — Verify
- At $s = 0$ (DC): $H_{HP}(0) = 0$ ✓ (HP blocks DC)
- At $s \to \infty$: $H_{HP} \to 1$ ✓ (HP passes high frequencies)
- At $s = j500$: $\vert H_{HP}(j500)\vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ ✓ (–3 dB at cutoff)

---

## 🧪 Worked Analytical Example — KTU Style (BS)

### 📋 Problem
Transform the same prototype into a **bandstop filter** with $\omega_l = 1000$ rad/sec and $\omega_u = 4000$ rad/sec.

### 🔢 Step 1 — Compute $\omega_0$ and $B$ (same as BP case)

$$\omega_0 = 2000 \text{ rad/sec}, \quad B = 3000 \text{ rad/sec}$$

### 🔢 Step 2 — Apply LP → BS substitution

$$S = \frac{3000\,s}{s^2 + 4 \times 10^6}$$

### 🔢 Step 3 — Substitute

$$H_{BS}(s) = \frac{1}{\left(\dfrac{3000\,s}{s^2 + 4\times 10^6}\right)^2 + \sqrt{2}\left(\dfrac{3000\,s}{s^2 + 4 \times 10^6}\right) + 1}$$

### 🔢 Step 4 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $(s^2 + 4 \times 10^6)^2$

$$H_{BS}(s) = \frac{(s^2 + 4 \times 10^6)^2}{9 \times 10^6\,s^2 + 3000\sqrt{2}\,s\,(s^2 + 4 \times 10^6) + (s^2 + 4\times 10^6)^2}$$

### 🔢 Step 5 — Expand and simplify

The denominator expands to:

$$D(s) = s^4 + 3000\sqrt{2}\,s^3 + 17 \times 10^6\,s^2 + 12\sqrt{2} \times 10^9\,s + 16 \times 10^{12}$$

The numerator is:

$$N(s) = s^4 + 8 \times 10^6\,s^2 + 16 \times 10^{12}$$

### ✅ Final Result

$$\boxed{H_{BS}(s) = \frac{s^4 + 8 \times 10^6\,s^2 + 16 \times 10^{12}}{s^4 + 3000\sqrt{2}\,s^3 + 17 \times 10^6\,s^2 + 12\sqrt{2} \times 10^9\,s + 16 \times 10^{12}}}$$

### 🔢 Step 6 — Verify
- At $s = 0$ (DC): $H_{BS}(0) = \dfrac{16 \times 10^{12}}{16 \times 10^{12}} = 1$ ✓ (BS passes DC)
- At $s = j2000$ (notch center): numerator $N(j2000) = (j2000)^4 + 8\times 10^6 \times (j2000)^2 + 16 \times 10^{12}$
  $= 16 \times 10^{12} - 8 \times 10^6 \times 4 \times 10^6 + 16 \times 10^{12} = 0$ ✓ (notch at center)
- Order: $N = 2 \rightarrow 2N = 4$ ✓

# 🔷 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 📊 Diagram 1: Role of Analog Frequency Transformation in IIR Filter Design Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Filter Specifications: Ap, As, wp, ws"] --> B["Select Approximation: Butterworth / Chebyshev / Elliptic"]
    B --> C["Determine Prototype Order N"]
    C --> D["Normalize to Unity Cutoff LP Prototype: H_LP of S"]
    D --> E["Apply Analog Frequency Transformation: S = f of s"]
    E --> F1["LP to LP: S = s / Wc"]
    E --> F2["LP to HP: S = Wc / s"]
    E --> F3["LP to BP: S = s^2 + wl*wu divided by s*wu minus wl"]
    E --> F4["LP to BS: S = s*wu minus wl divided by s^2 + wl*wu"]
    F1 --> G["Target Analog Filter: H_target of s"]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H["Apply Bilinear Transform: s = 2*Fs * z minus 1 divided by z plus 1"]
    H --> I["Digital Filter: H_d of z"]
    I --> J["Realization: Direct / Cascade / Parallel"]
```

> 📝 **Reading guide:** The blue-shaded box "Apply Analog Frequency Transformation" is the precise step this module covers. Everything *upstream* of it (specifications, approximation) is covered in Module 3; everything *downstream* (Bilinear transform, realization) is covered in Module 4 / 5.

---

## 📊 Diagram 2: Frequency-Axis Mapping for All Four Transformations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AXIS1["PROTOTYPE FREQUENCY AXIS: OMEGA"]
        O0["OMEGA = 0 (DC)"]
        O1["OMEGA = 1 (Passband Edge)"]
        OINF["OMEGA = INFINITY"]
    end

    subgraph AXIS2["LP to LP TARGET: omega"]
        T1A["omega = 0 (DC)"]
        T1B["omega = Wc (Cutoff)"]
        T1C["omega = INFINITY"]
    end

    subgraph AXIS3["LP to HP TARGET: omega"]
        T2A["omega = 0 (DC) BLOCKED"]
        T2B["omega = Wc (Cutoff)"]
        T2C["omega = INFINITY PASSED"]
    end

    subgraph AXIS4["LP to BP TARGET: omega"]
        T3A["omega = 0 (DC)"]
        T3B["omega = wl (Lower Edge)"]
        T3C["omega = w0 (Center)"]
        T3D["omega = wu (Upper Edge)"]
        T3E["omega = INFINITY"]
    end

    subgraph AXIS5["LP to BS TARGET: omega"]
        T4A["omega = 0 (DC) PASSED"]
        T4B["omega = wl (Lower Edge)"]
        T4C["omega = w0 (Center) NOTCH"]
        T4D["omega = wu (Upper Edge)"]
        T4E["omega = INFINITY PASSED"]
    end

    O0 --> T1A
    O1 --> T1B
    OINF --> T1C

    O0 --> T2C
    O1 --> T2B
    OINF --> T2A

    O0 --> T3C
    O1 --> T3B
    O1 --> T3D
    OINF --> T3A
    OINF --> T3E

    O0 --> T4A
    O0 --> T4E
    O1 --> T4B
    O1 --> T4D
    OINF --> T4C
```

> 📝 **Note the inversion in LP→HP** (DC ↔ ∞) and the **folding in LP→BS** (DC and ∞ both map from OMEGA=0). The bandpass and bandstop mappings create a **bilateral correspondence** between the prototype and the target, which is why order doubles.

---

## 📊 Diagram 3: Pole-Zero Migration Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph P0["PROTOTYPE S-PLANE: N=2 Butterworth"]
        P1["Pole at minus 0.707 plus j 0.707"]
        P2["Pole at minus 0.707 minus j 0.707"]
    end

    subgraph P1PL["LP to LP TARGET: Wc = 1000"]
        Q1["Pole at minus 707 plus j 707"]
        Q2["Pole at minus 707 minus j 707"]
    end

    subgraph P1PH["LP to HP TARGET: Wc = 1000"]
        R1["Zero at s = 0 (order 2)"]
        R2["Pole at minus 707 plus j 707"]
        R3["Pole at minus 707 minus j 707"]
    end

    subgraph P1BP["LP to BP TARGET: wl=1000, wu=4000"]
        S1["Pole pair near w = 1000"]
        S2["Pole pair near w = 4000"]
        S3["Zero pair at origin for BP"]
    end

    subgraph P1BS["LP to BS TARGET: wl=1000, wu=4000"]
        T1["Zero pair on jw axis at w = 2000"]
        T2["Pole pair near w = 1000"]
        T3["Pole pair near w = 4000"]
    end

    P0 --> P1PL
    P0 --> P1PH
    P0 --> P1BP
    P0 --> P1BS
```

> 📝 **Key insight for KTU exams:** Notice how:
> - LP→LP just **scales** the poles radially
> - LP→HP **adds zeros at origin** (one per pole) and **inverts** the pole locations
> - LP→BP and LP→BS **create 2N poles** (conjugate pairs split into two groups)
> - LP→BS **adds zeros on the jω axis** at the notch frequencies

---

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Normalized LP Prototype: H_LP of S"] --> B["Transformation Block: S = f of s"]
    B --> C["Target Analog Filter: H_target of s"]
    C --> D["Bilinear Transform: s equals 2 Fs times z minus 1 over z plus 1"]
    D --> E["Digital Filter: H of z"]
    E --> F["Output y of n"]
    G["Input x of n"] --> E
```

## 📊 Diagram 5: Master Decision Tree — Which Transformation to Apply?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["What is the desired filter type?"] --> Q1{"Passes low frequencies only?"}
    Q1 -->|Yes| A1["Use LP to LP: S = s / Wc"]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{"Passes high frequencies only?"}
    Q2 -->|Yes| A2["Use LP to HP: S = Wc / s"]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{"Passes a band in the middle?"}
    Q3 -->|Yes| A4["Use LP to BP: S = s^2 plus wl wu divided by s times wu minus wl"]
    Q3 -->|No| A5["Use LP to BS: S = s times wu minus wl divided by s^2 plus wl wu"]
    A1 --> DONE["Obtain H_target of s"]
    A2 --> DONE
    A4 --> DONE
    A5 --> DONE
    DONE --> NEXT["Next: Apply Bilinear Transform to get H of z"]
```
