# 🔷 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### ❓ Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**Define analog frequency transformation. State the four standard transformations used to convert a normalized lowpass prototype filter into LP, HP, BP, and BS filters.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
Analog frequency transformation is the technique of substituting the complex frequency variable $S$ of a normalized lowpass prototype filter $H_{LP}(S)$ (with $\Omega_p = 1$ rad/sec) by a rational function of a new variable $s$, to obtain a new filter type (LP, HP, BP, or BS) with desired cutoff frequencies, while preserving the magnitude response shape and filter order.

**The four transformations (2 marks):**

| Transformation | Substitution $S = f(s)$ |
|---|---|
| LP → LP | $S = \dfrac{s}{\Omega_c}$ |
| LP → HP | $S = \dfrac{\Omega_c}{s}$ |
| LP → BP | $S = \dfrac{s^2 + \omega_l\omega_u}{s(\omega_u - \omega_l)}$ |
| LP → BS | $S = \dfrac{s(\omega_u - \omega_l)}{s^2 + \omega_l\omega_u}$ |

---

### ❓ Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Why does the order of a filter double when a lowpass prototype is transformed into a bandpass or bandstop filter using analog frequency transformation?**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 marks)

**Reasoning (3 marks):**

The LP-to-BP (or LP-to-BS) transformation substitutes a **second-order rational function** for $S$ in the prototype transfer function. Since the prototype $H_{LP}(S)$ is an $N$-th order rational function of $S$, and the substitution $S = f(s)$ is itself a ratio of **second-degree polynomials** in $s$, the resulting transfer function $H_{BP}(s) = H_{LP}\bigl(f(s)\bigr)$ is a rational function of degree $2N$ in the variable $s$.

Geometrically, **each pole (and zero) of the prototype generates two poles (and zeros) in the transformed filter** — one for the upper frequency band and one for the lower frequency band. For example, a prototype pole at $S = -a + jb$ maps to two poles in the BP filter, located symmetrically around the geometric center frequency $\omega_0$. This bilateral mapping is what causes the order to double from $N$ to $2N$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For LP→LP and LP→HP transformations, the substitution is a **first-order rational function** (degree 1 in $s$), so the order is **preserved** as $N \to N$.

---

## 📝 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

### 🎯 Question B-A (Choice 1) — Comprehensive 14-Mark Problem

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** A second-order normalized Butterworth lowpass prototype filter has the transfer function:

$$H_{LP}(S) = \frac{1}{S^2 + \sqrt{2}\,S + 1}$$

Apply the **lowpass-to-highpass** transformation to obtain a highpass filter with cutoff frequency $\Omega_c = 1000$ rad/sec. Write down the final transfer function $H_{HP}(s)$ and verify the DC and high-frequency gains. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Starting from the same prototype, apply the **lowpass-to-bandpass** transformation to obtain a bandpass filter with lower bandedge $\omega_l = 1000$ rad/sec and upper bandedge $\omega_u = 9000$ rad/sec. Compute the new order, center frequency, bandwidth, and write the final transfer function $H_{BP}(s)$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: State the LP→HP substitution rule (1 mark)**

$$S = \frac{\Omega_c}{s} = \frac{1000}{s}$$

**Step 2: Substitute into the prototype (2 marks)**

$$H_{HP}(s) = \frac{1}{\left(\dfrac{1000}{s}\right)^2 + \sqrt{2}\left(\dfrac{1000}{s}\right) + 1}$$

**Step 3: Multiply numerator and denominator by $s^2$ (1 mark)**

$$H_{HP}(s) = \frac{s^2}{1000^2 + 1000\sqrt{2}\,s + s^2} = \frac{s^2}{s^2 + 1000\sqrt{2}\,s + 10^6}$$

**Step 4: Final result (1 mark)**

$$\boxed{H_{HP}(s) = \frac{s^2}{s^2 + 1000\sqrt{2}\,s + 10^6}}$$

**Step 5: Verification (2 marks)**

- DC gain: $H_{HP}(0) = \dfrac{0^2}{10^6} = 0$ ✓ (HP blocks DC)
- High-frequency gain: $\lim_{s \to \infty} H_{HP}(s) = \dfrac{s^2}{s^2} = 1$ ✓ (HP passes high frequencies)
- At cutoff $s = j1000$: $\vert H_{HP}(j1000)\vert = \dfrac{10^6}{\sqrt{(10^6-10^6)^2 + (10^6\sqrt{2})^2}} = \dfrac{10^6}{10^6\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ ✓ (–3 dB at cutoff)

---

#### ✅ Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Compute the transformation parameters (2 marks)**

$$\omega_0 = \sqrt{\omega_l\,\omega_u} = \sqrt{1000 \times 9000} = \sqrt{9 \times 10^6} = 3000 \text{ rad/sec}$$

$$B = \omega_u - \omega_l = 9000 - 1000 = 8000 \text{ rad/sec}$$

**Step 2: State the LP→BP substitution (1 mark)**

$$S = \frac{s^2 + 9 \times 10^6}{8000\,s}$$

**Step 3: Compute the new order (1 mark)**

The new order is $2N = 2 \times 2 = 4$.

**Step 4: Substitute into the prototype (2 marks)**

$$H_{BP}(s) = \frac{1}{\left(\dfrac{s^2 + 9 \times 10^6}{8000\,s}\right)^2 + \sqrt{2}\left(\dfrac{s^2 + 9 \times 10^6}{8000\,s}\right) + 1}$$

**Step 5: Multiply through by $(8000s)^2 = 64 \times 10^6\,s^2$ (1 mark)**

$$\boxed{H_{BP}(s) = \frac{64 \times 10^6\,s^2}{s^4 + 8000\sqrt{2}\,s^3 + 82 \times 10^6\,s^2 + 72\sqrt{2} \times 10^9\,s + 81 \times 10^{12}}}$$

**Step 6: Quality factor of the filter (bonus)**

$$Q = \frac{\omega_0}{B} = \frac{3000}{8000} = 0.375$$

> Low Q indicates a wideband bandpass filter.

---

### 🎯 Question B-B (Choice 2) — Alternative 14-Mark Problem

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4 Alternate]**

**(a)** A normalized lowpass Chebyshev Type-I prototype has the transfer function:

$$H_{LP}(S) = \frac{0.5}{(S+0.5)^2 + 0.866^2} = \frac{0.5}{S^2 + S + 1}$$

Apply the **lowpass-to-lowpass** transformation to obtain a lowpass filter with cutoff $\Omega_c = 500$ rad/sec. Write the resulting transfer function and identify the new pole locations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply the **lowpass-to-bandstop** transformation to the same prototype to obtain a bandstop filter with $\omega_l = 2000$ rad/sec and $\omega_u = 8000$ rad/sec. Determine the order, notch center frequency, and final transfer function. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: State the LP→LP substitution (1 mark)**

$$S = \frac{s}{\Omega_c} = \frac{s}{500}$$

**Step 2: Substitute (2 marks)**

$$H_{LP}(s) = \frac{0.5}{\left(\dfrac{s}{500}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{s}{500}\right) + 1}$$

**Step 3: Multiply numerator and denominator by $500^2 = 250000$ (1 mark)**

$$H_{LP}(s) = \frac{0.5 \times 250000}{s^2 + 500\,s + 250000} = \frac{125000}{s^2 + 500\,s + 250000}$$

**Step 4: Final result (1 mark)**

$$\boxed{H_{LP}(s) = \frac{125000}{s^2 + 500\,s + 250000}}$$

**Step 5: Identify new pole locations (2 marks)**

The characteristic polynomial is $s^2 + 500s + 250000$. Solving:

$$s = \frac{-500 \pm \sqrt{500^2 - 4 \times 250000}}{2} = \frac{-500 \pm \sqrt{250000 - 1000000}}{2}$$

$$s = \frac{-500 \pm j\sqrt{750000}}{2} = \frac{-500 \pm j\,866.03}{2}$$

$$s = -250 \pm j\,433.01$$

**Pole magnitude check:** $\sqrt{250^2 + 433.01^2} = \sqrt{62500 + 187500} = \sqrt{250000} = 500$ rad/sec ✓ (matches the new cutoff)

---

#### ✅ Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Compute transformation parameters (1 mark)**

$$\omega_0 = \sqrt{2000 \times 8000} = \sqrt{16 \times 10^6} = 4000 \text{ rad/sec}$$

$$B = 8000 - 2000 = 6000 \text{ rad/sec}$$

**Step 2: State the LP→BS substitution (1 mark)**

$$S = \frac{6000\,s}{s^2 + 16 \times 10^6}$$

**Step 3: State the new order (1 mark)**

Order doubles: $2N = 2 \times 2 = 4$

**Step 4: Substitute (2 marks)**

$$H_{BS}(s) = \frac{0.5}{\left(\dfrac{6000s}{s^2 + 16 \times 10^6}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{6000s}{s^2 + 16 \times 10^6}\right) + 1}$$

**Step 5: Multiply by $(s^2 + 16 \times 10^6)^2$ (1 mark)**

$$H_{BS}(s) = \frac{0.5\,(s^2 + 16 \times 10^6)^2}{36 \times 10^6\,s^2 + 6000\,s\,(s^2 + 16 \times 10^6) + (s^2 + 16 \times 10^6)^2}$$

**Step 6: Expand and combine terms (1 mark)**

Expanding the denominator:

$$D(s) = s^4 + 6000\,s^3 + 52 \times 10^6\,s^2 + 96 \times 10^9\,s + 256 \times 10^{12}$$

$$\boxed{H_{BS}(s) = \frac{0.5\,(s^4 + 32 \times 10^6\,s^2 + 256 \times 10^{12})}{s^4 + 6000\,s^3 + 52 \times 10^6\,s^2 + 96 \times 10^9\,s + 256 \times 10^{12}}}$$

> **Notch center verification:** At $s = j\omega_0 = j4000$, the numerator becomes $0.5 \times ((j4000)^2 + 16 \times 10^6)^2 = 0.5 \times (0)^2 = 0$ ✓ (BS has zero at notch center).

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU Board Exams (Module 4):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply through the entire denominator** when substituting $S = f(s)$. Students often leave the substitution "as is" without clearing the fraction. Always multiply by the LCD to obtain a polynomial in $s$. **(–2 to –3 marks)**
>
> 2. **Not stating the new filter order explicitly.** For LP→BP and LP→BS, the order doubles from $N$ to $2N$. Examiners *will* deduct 1 mark for omitting this statement.
>
> 3. **Confusing $\omega_l$ and $\omega_u$:** The convention is $\omega_l < \omega_u$. Swapping them leads to negative bandwidths, which is mathematically wrong. Always compute $B = \omega_u - \omega_l > 0$.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the prototype's normalization:** The prototype is assumed to have $\Omega_p = 1$ rad/sec. If a prototype with a different cutoff is given, you must first *normalize* it (divide $S$ by the prototype cutoff) before applying the transformation.
>
> 5. **Not simplifying the final answer:** KTU expects a clean, fully expanded polynomial in $s$ in the denominator. Leaving nested fractions loses marks.
>
> 6. **Misapplying the HP rule:** A frequent error is writing $S = s/\Omega_c$ for HP (this is LP-to-LP!). For HP, it is $S = \Omega_c / s$. Memorize: **HP = inversion.**
>
> 7. **Ignoring unit consistency:** If $\omega_l$ and $\omega_u$ are given in **Hz** ($f_l, f_u$), convert to rad/sec using $\omega = 2\pi f$ *before* applying the transformation. Mixing units is a silent killer.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Analog Frequency Transformations**

- ✅ Analog frequency transformations are applied to a **normalized LP prototype** ($\Omega_p = 1$ rad/sec) to obtain LP, HP, BP, or BS filters.

- ✅ The four canonical substitution rules are:
  - **LP→LP:** $S = s / \Omega_c$
  - **LP→HP:** $S = \Omega_c / s$
  - **LP→BP:** $S = (s^2 + \omega_l\omega_u) / [s(\omega_u - \omega_l)]$
  - **LP→BS:** $S = s(\omega_u - \omega_l) / (s^2 + \omega_l\omega_u)$

- ✅ **Order behavior:** LP and HP transformations preserve order ($N \to N$); BP and BS **double** the order ($N \to 2N$).

- ✅ **Key parameters for BP/BS:** $\omega_0 = \sqrt{\omega_l \omega_u}$ (center) and $B = \omega_u - \omega_l$ (bandwidth); $Q = \omega_0 / B$.

- ✅ **Stability:** All four transformations preserve LHP → LHP mapping, so stability is maintained.

- ✅ **Frequency response shape is preserved** (magnitude plot of prototype = magnitude plot of target, with axes relabeled).

- ✅ **Procedure to follow in KTU exams:**
  1. Identify filter type and parameters ($\Omega_c$ or $\omega_l, \omega_u$).
  2. Compute $\omega_0$ and $B$ if needed.
  3. Write the substitution rule explicitly.
  4. Substitute $S = f(s)$ into the prototype $H_{LP}(S)$.
  5. Multiply numerator and denominator by the LCD to clear fractions.
  6. Expand and simplify to polynomial form.
  7. State the new order and verify critical frequency mappings.

- ✅ **This is a precursor step to the Bilinear Transform** — the analog frequency transformation happens *first*, then the resulting $H(s)$ is mapped to $H(z)$ via the bilinear transformation $s = 2F_s \cdot (z-1)/(z+1)$.

- ✅ **Common mistake to avoid:** Forgetting the order doubling in BP/BS cases, or misapplying the HP rule. Write the substitution *before* computing — it forces you to think clearly.

- ✅ **Must-know fact:** The HP transformation introduces **zeros at $s = 0$** in the transfer function (because $S \to \infty$ as $s \to 0$, and the prototype's high-frequency behavior is replicated at DC for the target). The BS transformation introduces **zeros on the $j\omega$-axis** at the notch frequencies.

- ✅ **For the KTU exam:** Always carry units (rad/sec vs Hz) consistently, and *always* state the new order and verify the frequency mappings — this is what differentiates a 7-mark answer from a 14-mark answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Impulse Invariance Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT604 - DIGITAL SIGNALS AND PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Impulse Invariance Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Impulse Invariance Method (IIM)** is a technique used in **IIR Digital Filter Design** to convert a rational **analog (continuous-time) transfer function** $H_a(s)$ into an equivalent **digital (discrete-time) transfer function** $H(z)$ by sampling the analog filter's **impulse response** $h_a(t)$ at uniform time intervals $T$.

Mathematically, the core relationship is defined as:

$$h(n) = T \cdot h_a(nT), \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \dots$$

where $T$ is the **sampling period** (reciprocal of sampling frequency $F_s = 1/T$). The digital transfer function is then obtained via the **Z-transform** of the sampled, scaled impulse response.

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy: The Stroboscopic Photographer

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Sampling a River's Flow"**
>
> Imagine an engineer standing beside a river with a bucket, scooping water every **10 seconds** to measure its instantaneous speed and chemical content. The engineer cannot record the river's behavior *continuously*, but by taking regular **discrete samples**, a faithful *digital record* of the continuous flow is preserved.
>
> In the **Impulse Invariance Method**, the analog impulse response $h_a(t)$ is the *continuous river*, and the *bucket* is the sampler that captures its values at instants $t = 0, T, 2T, 3T, \dots$. The captured sequence $h(n)$ is then used to construct the digital filter. As long as the river's behavior doesn't change too rapidly between samples (i.e., the analog signal is **bandlimited**), the sampled record is a faithful representation.

### 1.3 Why is the Method Called "Impulse Invariance"?

The name originates from the principle that the **digital filter's impulse response** is an *invariant* (scaled) version of the **analog filter's impulse response** at sampled instants. In other words, the discrete-time system's reaction to a unit impulse is the sampled version of the continuous-time system's reaction to a Dirac delta $\delta(t)$.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Sampling Period** $T$ (in seconds): typically chosen such that $F_s = 1/T \geq 2 F_{max}$ by the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem**.
> - **Sampling Frequency** $F_s$ (in Hz): $F_s = 1/T$.
> - **Nyquist Frequency** $\omega_N = \pi/T$ (in rad/sample) — the highest digital frequency representable.
> - The factor $T$ in $h(n) = T \cdot h_a(nT)$ ensures that the **DC gain** $\sum h(n)$ equals $\int h_a(t)\,dt$, preserving the area under the impulse response.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Logic — Why Does It Work?

The **Impulse Invariance Method** is fundamentally built on three pillars:

1. **Linear Mapping of Poles**: Each analog pole $s_k$ in the $s$-plane maps to a digital pole $z_k = e^{s_k T}$ in the $z$-plane via the exponential transformation.
2. **Residue Preservation**: The partial-fraction residues $A_k$ are preserved (with a multiplicative scaling by $T$).
3. **Z-Transform Equivalence**: If $h_a(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{L}} H_a(s)$, then $h(n) = T\cdot h_a(nT) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{Z}} H(z)$.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Procedure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examination Algorithm — Impulse Invariance Method**
>
> **Step 1:** Start with a stable analog transfer function $H_a(s) = \dfrac{B(s)}{A(s)}$.
>
> **Step 2:** Perform **Partial Fraction Expansion (PFE)**:
> $$H_a(s) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \frac{A_k}{s - s_k}$$
> where $s_k$ are the analog poles and $A_k$ are the corresponding residues.
>
> **Step 3:** Identify the analog impulse response:
> $$h_a(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} A_k e^{s_k t} u(t)$$
>
> **Step 4:** Sample and scale:
> $$h(n) = T \cdot h_a(nT) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} T \cdot A_k e^{s_k nT} u(n)$$
>
> **Step 5:** Apply the Z-transform pole-by-pole:
> $$H(z) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \frac{T \cdot A_k}{1 - e^{s_k T} z^{-1}}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is a **high-yield reference** for the Impulse Invariance Method, aligned with the KTU 2024 examination pattern.

| **S. No.** | **Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Description / Use** |
|:----------:|:------------|:---------------------------|:----------------------|
| 1 | Sampling Relation | $h(n) = T \cdot h_a(nT)$ | Maps continuous-time to discrete-time |
| 2 | Direct Mapping Formula | $H(z) = \sum_k \dfrac{T \cdot A_k}{1 - e^{s_k T} z^{-1}}$ | Core formula for IIR filter design |
| 3 | Pole Mapping | $z_k = e^{s_k T}$ | Maps analog pole to digital pole |
| 4 | Frequency Response Relation | $H(e^{j\omega}) = \dfrac{1}{T} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} H_a\!\left(j\tfrac{\omega - 2\pi k}{T}\right)$ | Shows inherent aliasing |
| 5 | Aliasing-Free Condition | $H_a(j\Omega) = 0$ for $\vert \Omega \vert \geq \pi/T$ | Required for distortionless conversion |
| 6 | Z-Transform of Sampled Signal | $\mathcal{Z}\{h_a(nT)\} = \dfrac{1}{T} \sum_{k} H_a\!\left(s - j\tfrac{2\pi k}{T}\right)\bigg\vert_{z=e^{sT}}$ | Spectral replication |
| 7 | Stability Condition | $\text{Re}(s_k) < 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \vert z_k \vert = e^{\text{Re}(s_k)\,T} < 1$ | Stable analog filter $\Rightarrow$ stable digital filter |
| 8 | Scaling of Residues | $A_k^{(d)} = T \cdot A_k$ | Digital residue is analog residue scaled by $T$ |
| 9 | DC Gain Preservation | $H(z)\vert_{z=1} = H_a(s)\vert_{s=0}$ | Ensured by the $T$ scaling factor |
| 10 | Filter Type Suitability | Best for low-pass and bandlimited filters | Avoid for high-pass and band-stop filters |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Audio Equalizers**: Design of digital low-pass and band-pass crossovers for loudspeakers.
> - **Biomedical Signal Processing**: ECG/EEG filtering where analog prototypes (Butterworth, Chebyshev) are converted to digital form.
> - **Control Systems**: Discretization of analog controllers (PID) for embedded DSP implementation.
> - **Telecommunications**: Channel filtering, where analog prototype filters are migrated to DSP hardware (FPGA/DSP processors).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Core Mapping Formula

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theoretical Foundation**: We begin with the analog filter's impulse response and derive the digital filter's transfer function rigorously.

**Starting Point**: Let the analog transfer function be expressed in partial fractions:

$$H_a(s) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \frac{A_k}{s - s_k}$$

**Step A**: Take the inverse Laplace transform of each term to obtain the impulse response. Recall that $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\left\{\dfrac{1}{s - s_k}\right\} = e^{s_k t} u(t)$, hence:

$$h_a(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} A_k \, e^{s_k t} \, u(t)$$

**Step B**: Sample the impulse response at $t = nT$ (i.e., replace $t$ by $nT$):

$$h_a(nT) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} A_k \, e^{s_k nT} \, u(n)$$

**Step C**: Scale by $T$ to preserve the DC gain (this is the *area-preservation* principle of impulse invariance):

$$h(n) = T \cdot h_a(nT) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} T \cdot A_k \, e^{s_k nT} \, u(n)$$

**Step D**: Apply the Z-transform. Recall the Z-transform pair $\mathcal{Z}\{a^n u(n)\} = \dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}$ for $\vert a \vert < 1$. Letting $a_k = e^{s_k T}$:

$$H(z) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} T \cdot A_k \cdot \frac{1}{1 - e^{s_k T} z^{-1}}$$

**Final Result**:

$$\boxed{\,H(z) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \frac{T \cdot A_k}{1 - e^{s_k T} z^{-1}}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical closed-form expression** that KTU examiners expect in the 14-mark derivations.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Frequency Response and Aliasing Relation

**Step E**: The Z-transform of a sampled signal relates to its Laplace transform via the spectral-replication identity:

$$H(z)\big\vert_{z = e^{j\omega}} = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} H_a\!\left(j\frac{\omega - 2\pi k}{T}\right)$$

**Step F**: This infinite summation is the discrete-time Fourier representation of the *periodic replication* of the analog spectrum at intervals of $2\pi/T$. The factor $1/T$ arises from the area-preservation principle.

**Step G**: For **aliasing-free** conversion, the analog filter $H_a(j\Omega)$ must be strictly bandlimited to $\vert \Omega \vert < \pi/T$. In that case, only the $k = 0$ term survives within the principal digital band $\omega \in (-\pi, \pi)$:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{T} H_a\!\left(j\frac{\omega}{T}\right), \quad \vert \omega \vert < \pi$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aliasing**: If the analog filter's spectrum extends beyond $\pi/T$, the replicated copies *overlap*, causing **distortion** in the digital filter's frequency response. This is the **fundamental limitation** of the Impulse Invariance Method.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example (KTU Standard 14-Mark Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem**: Convert the analog filter $H_a(s) = \dfrac{2}{(s+1)(s+2)}$ into a digital filter $H(z)$ using the Impulse Invariance Method with $T = 1$ second.

**Step 1 — Partial Fraction Expansion**:

$$H_a(s) = \frac{2}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{A}{s+1} + \frac{B}{s+2}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the Residues**:

$$A = \left.\frac{2}{s+2}\right|_{s = -1} = \frac{2}{-1 + 2} = \frac{2}{1} = 2$$

$$B = \left.\frac{2}{s+1}\right|_{s = -2} = \frac{2}{-2 + 1} = \frac{2}{-1} = -2$$

**Step 3 — Substituted PFE**:

$$H_a(s) = \frac{2}{s+1} - \frac{2}{s+2}$$

**Step 4 — Identify Analog Impulse Response**:

$$h_a(t) = 2 e^{-t} u(t) - 2 e^{-2t} u(t)$$

**Step 5 — Sample and Scale** (with $T = 1$):

$$h(n) = T \cdot h_a(nT) = 1 \cdot \left[2 e^{-n} - 2 e^{-2n}\right] u(n) = \left(2 e^{-n} - 2 e^{-2n}\right) u(n)$$

**Step 6 — Apply Z-Transform Term-by-Term** (using $\mathcal{Z}\{a^n u(n)\} = \dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}$):

$$H(z) = \frac{2}{1 - e^{-1} z^{-1}} - \frac{2}{1 - e^{-2} z^{-1}}$$

**Step 7 — Simplify to a Rational Form in $z^{-1}$**:

$$H(z) = \frac{2(1 - e^{-2} z^{-1}) - 2(1 - e^{-1} z^{-1})}{(1 - e^{-1} z^{-1})(1 - e^{-2} z^{-1})}$$

Expanding the numerator:

$$\text{Numerator} = 2 - 2 e^{-2} z^{-1} - 2 + 2 e^{-1} z^{-1} = 2(e^{-1} - e^{-2}) z^{-1}$$

Substituting the numerical values $e^{-1} \approx 0.3679$ and $e^{-2} \approx 0.1353$:

$$\text{Numerator} \approx 2 \times 0.2326 \, z^{-1} = 0.4652 \, z^{-1}$$

**Step 8 — Final Digital Transfer Function**:

$$H(z) = \frac{2(e^{-1} - e^{-2}) z^{-1}}{1 - (e^{-1} + e^{-2}) z^{-1} + e^{-3} z^{-2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification of Stability**: Both analog poles $s_1 = -1$ and $s_2 = -2$ have negative real parts, so $e^{s_k T} = e^{-1} \approx 0.368$ and $e^{-2} \approx 0.135$ lie *inside* the unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$. Hence the digital filter is **stable**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Algorithmic Flowchart of the Impulse Invariance Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Analog Transfer Function H_a of s] --> B1[Check Stability of H_a of s]
    B1 --> C1[Perform Partial Fraction Expansion of H_a of s]
    C1 --> D1[Extract Poles s_k and Residues A_k]
    D1 --> E1[Apply Sampling h of n equals T times h_a of nT]
    E1 --> F1[Apply Z Transform Term by Term]
    F1 --> G1[Assemble Digital Transfer Function H of z]
    G1 --> H1{Is Analog Filter Bandlimited to pi by T}
    H1 -- Yes --> I1[Accurate Digital Filter Achieved]
    H1 -- No --> J1[Distortion due to Aliasing Present]
    J1 --> K1[Consider Alternative Method BLT]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A2[Analog Prototype Filter H_a of s] -->|Impulse Response h_a of t| B2[Analog System Identification]
    B2 -->|Sample at nT| C2[Discrete Time Sampler]
    C2 -->|Scaled by T| D2[Digital Impulse Response h of n]
    D2 -->|Z Transform| E2[Digital Filter H of z]
    E2 --> F2[Embedded DSP Implementation]
    F2 --> G2[Real Time Signal Output]
    style A2 fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333
    style E2 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333
    style F2 fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333
```

### 4.3 S-Plane to Z-Plane Pole Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A3[Analog Pole s_k in Left Half Plane] -->|Exponential Map z_k equals e to the power s_k T| B3[Digital Pole z_k Inside Unit Circle]
    A4[Analog Pole on Imaginary Axis] -->|Map| B4[Digital Pole on Unit Circle]
    A5[Analog Pole in Right Half Plane] -->|Map| B5[Digital Pole Outside Unit Circle UNSTABLE]
    style A3 fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#1e8449
    style B3 fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#1e8449
    style A4 fill:#fcf3cf,stroke:#b7950b
    style B4 fill:#fcf3cf,stroke:#b7950b
    style A5 fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#c0392b
    style B5 fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#c0392b
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the Pole-Mapping Diagram**:
> - **Left Half Plane $\rightarrow$ Inside Unit Circle**: Stable analog poles always yield stable digital poles (a key *advantage* of IIM).
> - **Imaginary Axis $\rightarrow$ Unit Circle**: Marginally stable poles (oscillators) lie exactly on the digital stability boundary.
> - **Right Half Plane $\rightarrow$ Outside Unit Circle**: Unstable analog poles remain unstable in the digital domain, preserving the *stability invariant*.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the Impulse Invariance Method of IIR filter design. Why is the method restricted to bandlimited analog filters?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:
- **Definition (1 Mark)**: The Impulse Invariance Method converts an analog filter transfer function $H_a(s)$ to a digital filter $H(z)$ by sampling the analog impulse response $h_a(t)$ at uniform intervals $T$, such that $h(n) = T \cdot h_a(nT)$.
- **Mechanism (1 Mark)**: The digital transfer function is obtained by Z-transforming the sampled impulse response, where each analog pole $s_k$ maps to a digital pole $z_k = e^{s_k T}$.
- **Restriction (1 Mark)**: The method is restricted to bandlimited analog filters because the sampling process causes *spectral replication* (aliasing) at intervals of $2\pi/T$. If $H_a(j\Omega)$ has frequency components above $\pi/T$ (the Nyquist frequency), these components overlap during sampling, distorting the digital frequency response.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
*State the relationship between the analog and digital transfer functions obtained via the Impulse Invariance Method. What is the role of the factor $T$?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:
- **Relationship (2 Marks)**: For a partial-fraction-expanded analog filter $H_a(s) = \sum_k \dfrac{A_k}{s - s_k}$, the digital transfer function is:
$$H(z) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \frac{T \cdot A_k}{1 - e^{s_k T} z^{-1}}$$
- **Role of $T$ (1 Mark)**: The factor $T$ ensures *area preservation* between the continuous and discrete impulse responses, i.e., $\sum h(n) = \int h_a(t)\,dt$. This guarantees that the DC gain $H(z=1) = H_a(s=0)$, maintaining the filter's magnitude response at zero frequency.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice 1**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** *Derive the relationship between the analog and digital transfer functions using the Impulse Invariance Method. Start from the partial-fraction expansion of $H_a(s)$ and clearly state the pole-mapping formula.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *An analog filter has the transfer function $H_a(s) = \dfrac{3}{(s+1)(s+3)}$. Using the Impulse Invariance Method with $T = 0.5$ second, obtain the digital filter $H(z)$. Comment on its stability.* **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

**Step 1**: Let $H_a(s) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \dfrac{A_k}{s - s_k}$ be the partial-fraction form.

**Step 2**: Inverse Laplace transform gives $h_a(t) = \sum_k A_k e^{s_k t} u(t)$.

**Step 3**: Sampling at $t = nT$ and scaling by $T$ yields $h(n) = T \cdot \sum_k A_k e^{s_k nT} u(n)$.

**Step 4**: Applying Z-transform term-wise, and using $\mathcal{Z}\{a^n u(n)\} = \dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}$, we obtain:

$$H(z) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \frac{T \cdot A_k}{1 - e^{s_k T} z^{-1}}$$

**Pole-Mapping Formula**: Each analog pole $s_k$ is mapped to a digital pole $z_k = e^{s_k T}$, and the residue is scaled to $T \cdot A_k$.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

**Step 1 — PFE**:

$$H_a(s) = \frac{3}{(s+1)(s+3)} = \frac{A}{s+1} + \frac{B}{s+3}$$

**Step 2 — Residues**:

$$A = \left.\frac{3}{s+3}\right|_{s = -1} = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5$$

$$B = \left.\frac{3}{s+1}\right|_{s = -3} = \frac{3}{-2} = -1.5$$

So $H_a(s) = \dfrac{1.5}{s+1} - \dfrac{1.5}{s+3}$.

**Step 3 — Apply IIM formula** with $T = 0.5$:

$$H(z) = \frac{1.5 \times 0.5}{1 - e^{-0.5} z^{-1}} - \frac{1.5 \times 0.5}{1 - e^{-1.5} z^{-1}}$$

$$H(z) = \frac{0.75}{1 - e^{-0.5} z^{-1}} - \frac{0.75}{1 - e^{-1.5} z^{-1}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Values**: $e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065$ and $e^{-1.5} \approx 0.2231$.

$$H(z) = \frac{0.75}{1 - 0.6065 z^{-1}} - \frac{0.75}{1 - 0.2231 z^{-1}}$$

**Step 5 — Stability Check**:
- Analog poles: $s_1 = -1$, $s_2 = -3$ (both with $\text{Re}(s) < 0$ — analog filter is stable).
- Digital poles: $z_1 = e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065$, $z_2 = e^{-1.5} \approx 0.2231$. Both have magnitudes $< 1$, so they lie **inside the unit circle**.

**Conclusion**: The digital filter is **stable** because stable analog poles always map to interior digital poles under impulse invariance (since $\vert e^{s_k T}\vert = e^{\text{Re}(s_k) T} < 1$ when $\text{Re}(s_k) < 0$).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice 2**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Retest Paper]**

**(a)** *Explain the aliasing problem in the Impulse Invariance Method. Under what conditions is the method free from aliasing?* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *The analog transfer function of a filter is $H_a(s) = \dfrac{2}{(s+1)(s+2)}$. Design a digital filter using the Impulse Invariance Method with $T = 0.5$ second, and verify the DC gain preservation.* **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

**Aliasing Explanation (5 Marks)**:
The Z-transform of a uniformly sampled signal is related to the Laplace transform by the spectral-replication identity:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} H_a\!\left(j\frac{\omega - 2\pi k}{T}\right)$$

This means that the digital filter's spectrum is a *sum of infinitely many replicated* analog spectra, centered at frequencies $\omega = 0, \pm 2\pi, \pm 4\pi, \dots$. In the principal digital band $\vert \omega \vert < \pi$, *only* the $k=0$ replica survives **iff** the analog filter is strictly bandlimited to $\vert \Omega \vert < \pi/T$. If $H_a(j\Omega)$ has content beyond $\pi/T$, adjacent replicas *overlap* and *add*, producing a distorted digital spectrum — this is **aliasing**.

**Aliasing-Free Conditions (2 Marks)**:
1. The analog filter $H_a(j\Omega)$ must be *strictly bandlimited*: $H_a(j\Omega) = 0$ for $\vert \Omega \vert \geq \pi/T$.
2. The sampling frequency must satisfy $F_s \geq 2 F_{max}$, where $F_{max}$ is the highest frequency component in the analog signal/filter.
3. In practice, the method works well for **low-pass** and **band-pass** filters with *steep roll-off*, but is unsuitable for **high-pass** and **band-stop** filters.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Step 1 — PFE**: $H_a(s) = \dfrac{2}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \dfrac{A}{s+1} + \dfrac{B}{s+2}$.

$$A = \left.\frac{2}{s+2}\right|_{s=-1} = 2, \quad B = \left.\frac{2}{s+1}\right|_{s=-2} = -2$$

**Step 2 — IIM with $T = 0.5$**:

$$H(z) = \frac{2 \times 0.5}{1 - e^{-0.5} z^{-1}} - \frac{2 \times 0.5}{1 - e^{-1.0} z^{-1}}$$

$$H(z) = \frac{1.0}{1 - 0.6065 z^{-1}} - \frac{1.0}{1 - 0.3679 z^{-1}}$$

**Step 3 — DC Gain Verification**:

For the analog filter at $s = 0$:

$$H_a(0) = \frac{2}{(0+1)(0+2)} = \frac{2}{2} = 1$$

For the digital filter at $z = 1$ (i.e., $\omega = 0$):

$$H(1) = \frac{1.0}{1 - 0.6065} - \frac{1.0}{1 - 0.3679} = \frac{1.0}{0.3935} - \frac{1.0}{0.6321}$$

$$H(1) \approx 2.541 - 1.582 = 0.959 \approx 1$$

The small discrepancy is due to rounding of $e^{-0.5}$ and $e^{-1.0}$. With exact values, $H(z=1) = H_a(s=0) = 1$, confirming **DC gain preservation** by the $T$ scaling factor.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $T$ scaling factor**: Many students write $h(n) = h_a(nT)$ without the $T$. This loses **2–3 marks** in derivations, as DC gain preservation is a key KTU requirement.
>
> 2. **Incorrect PFE residue calculation**: Sign errors in evaluating $A_k = \left.\dfrac{N(s)}{D'(s)}\right\vert_{s = s_k}$ are the **#1 source of mistakes** in 14-mark problems. Always re-verify by recombining the partial fractions.
>
> 3. **Skipping stability analysis**: KTU examiners explicitly ask for stability comments. Failing to mention that $\vert z_k \vert = e^{\text{Re}(s_k) T} < 1$ for stable analog filters results in **loss of 1–2 marks**.
>
> 4. **Using IIM for high-pass filters**: Students often blindly apply the method to high-pass designs. Examiners *deliberately* test this — note that **IIM is unsuitable for high-pass** due to severe aliasing, and the **Bilinear Transformation (BLT)** is preferred instead.
>
> 5. **Not simplifying to a single rational expression**: KTU expects the final $H(z)$ in a single $\dfrac{b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2} + \dots}{1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2} + \dots}$ form for full marks, not just a sum of first-order sections.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Impulse Invariance Method**

- **Core Formula** (memorize verbatim): $H(z) = \sum_k \dfrac{T \cdot A_k}{1 - e^{s_k T} z^{-1}}$
- **Pole-Mapping Rule**: $s_k \rightarrow z_k = e^{s_k T}$; residues scale as $A_k^{(d)} = T \cdot A_k$.
- **Stability Invariant**: Stable analog filter ($\text{Re}(s_k) < 0$) **always** maps to a stable digital filter.
- **Aliasing**: The frequency response satisfies $H(e^{j\omega}) = \dfrac{1}{T} \sum_k H_a\!\left(j\tfrac{\omega - 2\pi k}{T}\right)$ — *infinite spectral replicas*.
- **Aliasing-Free Condition**: $H_a(j\Omega) = 0$ for $\vert \Omega \vert \geq \pi/T$.
- **Best-Use Cases**: Low-pass and band-pass filters with **strict bandlimitation**.
- **Avoid For**: High-pass, band-stop, and any filter with significant energy near/above the Nyquist frequency.
- **DC Gain Preservation**: $H(z=1) = H_a(s=0)$ — guaranteed by the $T$ scaling factor.
- **Compared to BLT**: IIM preserves the *time-domain impulse response* (useful for matched filters) but suffers aliasing; BLT preserves the *frequency-domain mapping* without aliasing but warps frequencies nonlinearly.
- **Typical KTU Question Pattern**: (a) Derive the IIM formula for 7 marks, (b) Apply to a second-order analog prototype and verify stability for 7 marks.
- **Always remember to mention** the limitations (aliasing) and the stability check ($\vert z_k \vert < 1$) in your exam answers — they carry **bonus credit** with KTU examiners.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bilinear Transformation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEVT604 - DIGITAL SIGNALS AND PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bilinear Transformation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Bilinear Transformation** (also called the **Tustin's Method** or **Tustin Approximation**) is a conformal mapping technique used in Digital Signal Processing (DSP) to convert a continuous-time (analog) transfer function $H_a(s)$ into an equivalent discrete-time (digital) transfer function $H(z)$. It is the most widely used method in KTU-evaluated IIR digital filter design problems.

The transformation is defined by the mapping equation:

$$s = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{z - 1}{z + 1}$$

where:
- $s = \sigma + j\Omega$ is the complex variable of the **s-plane** (analog domain).
- $z$ is the complex variable of the **z-plane** (digital domain).
- $T$ is the **sampling period** (in seconds), the inverse of the sampling frequency $f_s$, i.e., $T = 1/f_s$.

Equivalently, the inverse mapping (z to s) is given by:

$$z = \frac{1 + (sT/2)}{1 - (sT/2)} = \frac{2 + sT}{2 - sT}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 — IIR Filter Design):** The Bilinear Transformation is the *only* mandatory method students must master for converting analog prototypes (Butterworth / Chebyshev) into digital IIR filters. Impulse Invariance and other methods are optional extensions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are translating a **road map drawn on a flat sheet of paper** (analog/s-domain) into a **Google Maps street-view layout** (digital/z-domain). The flat paper map shows the entire terrain in one continuous view, while Google Maps slices the same terrain into discrete, navigable segments.

The Bilinear Transformation is like the **mathematical GPS** that tells you: *"For every point on the flat paper, here is exactly which discrete tile on Google Maps it corresponds to."* However, because the Earth is curved and the paper is flat, the scale is not perfectly uniform — streets near the **equator (low frequencies)** look almost identical, but streets near the **poles (high frequencies)** get stretched/squished. This stretching effect in DSP is called **frequency warping**, and we fix it using a technique called **pre-warping**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Frequency Warping in Plain English:** The Bilinear Transformation squeezes the entire infinite analog frequency range $\Omega \in [0, \infty)$ into a finite digital frequency range $\omega \in [0, \pi]$. This is great for stability, but it distorts the frequency axis in a non-linear way. The fix is **pre-warping** critical (edge) frequencies before designing the analog prototype.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value/Unit | Significance |
|----------|--------|---------------------|--------------|
| Sampling Period | $T$ | **seconds (s)** | $T = 1/f_s$ |
| Sampling Frequency | $f_s$ | **Hertz (Hz)** | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist) |
| Digital Frequency | $\omega$ | **radians/sample** | Range: $[-\pi, \pi]$ |
| Analog Frequency | $\Omega$ | **radians/second** | Range: $[0, \infty)$ |
| Warping Constant | $2/T$ | **radians/second** | Governs mapping scale |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The Bilinear Transformation works by first applying the **trapezoidal rule of numerical integration** to convert a continuous-time differential equation into a discrete-time difference equation. The derivation chain is:

1. **Start with the analog integrator** $\frac{1}{s}$ in the s-domain, which represents integration in continuous time.
2. **Apply the Trapezoidal Rule** for numerical integration:
   
   $$y(n) = y(n-1) + \frac{T}{2}[x(n) + x(n-1)]$$
   
3. **Take the Z-Transform** of both sides:
   
   $$Y(z) = z^{-1}Y(z) + \frac{T}{2}[X(z) + z^{-1}X(z)]$$
   
4. **Solve for the ratio** $Y(z)/X(z)$ (the discrete-time equivalent of the integrator):
   
   $$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{T}{2} \cdot \frac{1 + z^{-1}}{1 - z^{-1}} = \frac{T}{2} \cdot \frac{z + 1}{z - 1}$$
   
5. **Invert and identify the s-to-z mapping:** Since the analog integrator was $\frac{1}{s}$, we equate:
   
   $$\frac{1}{s} = \frac{T}{2} \cdot \frac{z + 1}{z - 1} \implies s = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{z - 1}{z + 1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind It:** The trapezoidal rule is a 2nd-order accurate numerical integration method, so the resulting digital filter preserves the analog filter's magnitude response shape far better than lower-order approximations.

## 2.2 Properties of the Bilinear Transformation

| Property | Mathematical Statement | Engineering Implication |
|----------|------------------------|-------------------------|
| **One-to-One Mapping** | Every point in s maps to a unique point in z, and vice-versa | No ambiguity; every analog filter has exactly one digital counterpart |
| **Stability Preservation** | Left-half s-plane ($\sigma < 0$) maps strictly to the interior of the unit circle ($\vert z \vert < 1$) | Stable analog filter $\Rightarrow$ Stable digital filter (always!) |
| **Frequency Warping** | $\Omega = \frac{2}{T} \tan\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)$ | Non-linear; high frequencies are compressed; **pre-warping is mandatory** for IIR design |
| **No Aliasing** | The mapping is one-to-one; no frequency folding occurs | Spectrum is preserved without periodic replication |
| **Imaginary Axis Mapping** | $s = j\Omega$ axis maps to $\vert z \vert = 1$ (unit circle) | Passband/Stopband edges are preserved as boundaries |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table is your KTU board-exam survival kit for Bilinear Transformation problems. Memorize it.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | When to Use |
|---|--------------|----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Direct Mapping** | $s = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{z-1}{z+1}$ | Substitute into $H_a(s)$ to get $H(z)$ |
| 2 | **Inverse Mapping** | $z = \frac{2 + sT}{2 - sT}$ | Used for stability analysis; mapping verification |
| 3 | **Frequency Warping** | $\Omega = \frac{2}{T} \tan\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)$ | Compute analog $\Omega$ from digital $\omega$ |
| 4 | **Inverse Warping** | $\omega = 2 \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\Omega T}{2}\right)$ | Compute digital $\omega$ from analog $\Omega$ |
| 5 | **Pre-warping Formula** | $\Omega_p = \frac{2}{T} \tan\left(\frac{\omega_p}{2}\right)$ | Substitute digital specs into analog prototype |
| 6 | **Substitution Form (z⁻¹)** | $s = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}}$ | Avoids dealing with positive powers of $z$ — **KTU-preferred form** |
| 7 | **Stability Criterion** | $\sigma < 0 \iff \vert z \vert < 1$ | Verifying filter stability after transformation |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Where This is Used in the Real World

- **Audio Equalizers and Crossovers:** Used in parametric EQs (e.g., Baxandall, Linkwitz-Riley) where low/high/shelf filters are designed in analog and then digitized for software plugins (Logic Pro, Ableton).
- **Biomedical Signal Processing:** ECG, EEG, and EMG monitors digitize analog bandpass filters via the Bilinear Transformation for embedded DSP chips.
- **Telecommunications:** Digital receivers (SDR — Software Defined Radio) implement matched filters and channel-select filters using this method.
- **Control Systems:** When digital controllers emulate analog PID controllers, the Bilinear Transformation (Tustin's method) is the industry standard to avoid aliasing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation #1: Frequency Warping Relationship

We begin by substituting $z = e^{j\omega}$ (a point on the unit circle) and $s = j\Omega$ (a point on the imaginary axis) into the transformation:

$$s = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{z - 1}{z + 1}$$

### Step 1 — Substitute $z = e^{j\omega}$ into the numerator and denominator:

$$z - 1 = e^{j\omega} - 1$$

$$z + 1 = e^{j\omega} + 1$$

### Step 2 — Apply Euler's Formula: $e^{j\omega} = \cos\omega + j\sin\omega$:

$$e^{j\omega} - 1 = \cos\omega - 1 + j\sin\omega$$

$$e^{j\omega} + 1 = \cos\omega + 1 + j\sin\omega$$

### Step 3 — Factor out $e^{j\omega/2}$ from both terms (a standard trig trick):

$$e^{j\omega} - 1 = e^{j\omega/2}\left(e^{j\omega/2} - e^{-j\omega/2}\right) = e^{j\omega/2} \cdot 2j\sin(\omega/2)$$

$$e^{j\omega} + 1 = e^{j\omega/2}\left(e^{j\omega/2} + e^{-j\omega/2}\right) = e^{j\omega/2} \cdot 2\cos(\omega/2)$$

### Step 4 — Form the ratio and cancel common terms:

$$\frac{z - 1}{z + 1} = \frac{e^{j\omega/2} \cdot 2j\sin(\omega/2)}{e^{j\omega/2} \cdot 2\cos(\omega/2)} = j\tan(\omega/2)$$

### Step 5 — Multiply by $2/T$ and equate to $s = j\Omega$:

$$s = \frac{2}{T} \cdot j\tan\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)$$

$$j\Omega = j \cdot \frac{2}{T}\tan\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)$$

### Step 6 — Equate the imaginary parts to obtain the warping relation:

$$\boxed{\Omega = \frac{2}{T} \tan\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** We moved from the abstract s-to-z mapping to a direct relationship between analog frequency $\Omega$ and digital frequency $\omega$. The $\tan(\cdot)$ non-linearity is what causes frequency warping.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation #2: Design of a Digital Lowpass Butterworth Filter via Bilinear Transformation

> **Problem Statement (KTU Standard Style):** Design a single-pole digital Butterworth lowpass filter with passband edge $\omega_p = 0.2\pi$ rad/sample and sampling frequency $f_s = 100$ Hz using the Bilinear Transformation.

### Step 1 — Identify Given Parameters:

$$\omega_p = 0.2\pi = 0.6283 \text{ rad/sample}, \quad f_s = 100 \text{ Hz}, \quad T = 1/f_s = 0.01 \text{ s}$$

### Step 2 — Pre-warp the passband edge frequency to obtain the analog equivalent:

Apply the pre-warping formula $\Omega_p = \frac{2}{T}\tan(\omega_p/2)$:

$$\Omega_p = \frac{2}{0.01} \tan\left(\frac{0.2\pi}{2}\right) = 200 \cdot \tan(0.1\pi)$$

$$\tan(0.1\pi) = \tan(18°) = 0.3249$$

$$\Omega_p = 200 \times 0.3249 = 64.98 \text{ rad/s}$$

### Step 3 — Determine the analog Butterworth filter order:

For a single-pole Butterworth lowpass filter, the magnitude-squared response is:

$$\vert H_a(j\Omega) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{1 + (\Omega / \Omega_c)^{2N}}$$

With $N = 1$ and $\Omega_c = \Omega_p$:

$$H_a(s) = \frac{\Omega_p}{s + \Omega_p} = \frac{64.98}{s + 64.98}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Bilinear Transformation:

Substitute $s = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}} = 200 \cdot \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}}$:

$$H(z) = \frac{64.98}{200 \cdot \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}} + 64.98}$$

### Step 5 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $(1 + z^{-1})$:

$$H(z) = \frac{64.98(1 + z^{-1})}{200(1 - z^{-1}) + 64.98(1 + z^{-1})}$$

### Step 6 — Expand the denominator:

$$\text{Denominator} = 200 - 200z^{-1} + 64.98 + 64.98z^{-1} = 264.98 - 135.02z^{-1}$$

### Step 7 — Write the final transfer function:

$$\boxed{H(z) = \frac{64.98(1 + z^{-1})}{264.98 - 135.02z^{-1}} = \frac{0.2452(1 + z^{-1})}{1 - 0.5095z^{-1}}}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** At each step, we kept the chain of substitutions explicit. The bilinear transformation was applied **after** designing the analog filter, which is the **correct KTU procedure** — never substitute s directly into a digital specification without pre-warping.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Bilinear vs. Impulse Invariance

| Parameter | Bilinear Transformation | Impulse Invariance |
|-----------|------------------------|---------------------|
| **Mapping Nature** | One-to-one (bijective) | Many-to-one (aliasing possible) |
| **Stability** | Always preserved | Preserved if analog filter is bandlimited |
| **Frequency Response** | Distorted at high frequencies (warping) | Faithful at low frequencies |
| **Aliasing** | **None** | Possible at high frequencies |
| **Pre-warping Needed?** | **Yes (mandatory)** | No |
| **Best Suited For** | All filter types (LP, HP, BP, BS) | **Only** bandlimited lowpass/highpass |
| **KTU Preference** | **Primary method** | Supplementary method |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Bilinear Transformation Design Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Digital Filter Specifications wp, ws, Ap, As]:::startNode
    B[Pre-warp Critical Frequencies using Omega equals 2 over T times tan of omega over 2]:::processNode
    C[Design Analog Prototype H sub a of s using Butterworth or Chebyshev]:::processNode
    D[Apply Bilinear Substitution s equals 2 over T times z minus 1 over z plus 1]:::processNode
    E[Simplify Algebraic Expression to obtain H of z]:::processNode
    F[Verify Stability Poles must lie inside unit circle]:::checkNode
    G[Output: Final Digital Filter H of z]:::endNode
    H[Adjust Filter Order N and Re-iterate]:::feedbackNode

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F -->|Stable| G
    F -->|Unstable| H
    H --> C

    classDef startNode fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#0d3a66,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef processNode fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#a04a00,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef checkNode fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#0d5a0d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef endNode fill:#d62728,stroke:#7a1414,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef feedbackNode fill:#9467bd,stroke:#5a3a7a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: s-plane to z-plane Mapping Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph sPlane["S PLANE Continuous Time Domain"]
        A1[Imaginary Axis sigma equals 0]:::sAxis
        A2[Left Half Plane sigma less than 0 Stable]:::lhp
        A3[Right Half Plane sigma greater than 0 Unstable]:::rhp
    end

    subgraph zPlane["Z PLANE Discrete Time Domain"]
        B1[Unit Circle]:::zAxis
        B2[Interior of Unit Circle Stable]:::lhp
        B3[Exterior of Unit Circle Unstable]:::rhp
    end

    A1 -->|Maps to| B1
    A2 -->|Maps to| B2
    A3 -->|Maps to| B3

    classDef sAxis fill:#17becf,stroke:#0a6a75,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef lhp fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#0d5a0d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef rhp fill:#d62728,stroke:#7a1414,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    B[Bilinear Transform s equals 2 over T times z minus 1 over z plus 1]:::transform

    D[Pre-warp Stage Omega p equals 2 over T times tan of omega p over 2]:::prewarp

    A --> D
    D -->|Compensated Specs| B
    B --> C
```
